blocks:
Ayahs

بَرَآءَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّم مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١)
[This is] a [declaration of] repudiation by Allah and His Apostle [addressed] to the polytheists with whom you had made a treaty: (1)
فَسِيحُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِى ٱللَّهِ ۙ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْزِى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢)
Travel [unmolested] in the land for four months, but know that you cannot frustrate Allah, and that Allah shall disgrace the faithless. (2)

1- The Names of This Surah

Commentators have mentioned many names for this Surah, reaching up to ten names, the most famous of which are "Bara'at" (Dissociation), "Tawbah" (Repentance), and "Fadihah" (The Exposer). Each of these names has a clear reason. The name "Bara'at" was given to this Surah because it begins with the dissociation and immunity of Allah and His Messenger from the treaty-breaking polytheists.

It is called "Tawbah" because "repentance" is frequently mentioned within this Surah. The naming as "Fadihah" is due to the fact that various verses within it led to the disgrace and exposure (fadihah) of the hypocrites and the unveiling of their hidden deeds.

2- The History of the Surah's Revelation

This Surah is the last, or among the last, of the Surahs revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Medina, and as mentioned, it consists of 129 verses.

The beginning of its revelation is considered to be in the ninth year of the Hijrah. A study of the Surah's verses shows that a portion of it was revealed before the Battle of Tabuk, a portion during the preparations for the battle, and another part after the return from the battle.

From the beginning of the Surah until verse 28 was revealed before the arrival of the Hajj season. As the explanation will follow, God willing, the opening verses of this Surah, which concerned the status of the remaining polytheists, were proclaimed to the people during the Hajj ceremonies by the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام.

3- The Content of the Surah

Since this Surah was revealed during the peak of Islam's rise in the Arabian Peninsula and after the breaking of the last resistance of the polytheists, its content holds special importance and contains sensitive passages.

A significant part of it concerns the remaining polytheists and idolaters, the severance of ties with them, and the annulment of treaties they had with the Muslims (due to their repeated violations of these treaties), so that the remnants of idolatry would be forever cleared from the Islamic environment.

Furthermore, as Islam expanded and the ranks of the enemies were broken, some individuals changed their outward appearance and embedded themselves within the ranks of the Muslims to strike a fatal blow to Islam at an opportune moment. Another major part of this Surah speaks of these hypocrites and their fate, issuing stern warnings to the Muslims and enumerating the signs of the hypocrites.

Another section of this Surah pertains to the importance of Jihad in the way of Allah, for at this sensitive juncture, negligence of this vital subject would have led to weakness, retreat, or the defeat of the Muslims.

Another important part of this Surah, serving as a completion of previous discussions, speaks of the deviation of the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) from the truth of Monotheism, and the deviation of their scholars from their duty of leadership and enlightenment.

Additionally, in other verses, in connection with the discussions on Jihad, it invites the Muslims to unity and the tightening of their ranks. It severely rebukes and blames the defectors—the weak and lazy individuals who shirked the duty of Jihad under various pretexts—and conversely, it praises and lauds the early Emigrants (Muhajirin) and other sincere believers.

Since the expanding Islamic society at that time had developed various needs that had to be addressed, it mentions discussions on Zakat, the prohibition of hoarding and amassing wealth, the necessity of seeking knowledge, and the obligation of educating the ignorant.

In addition to the above topics, other subjects such as the story of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله migration, the issue of the sacred months in which fighting is prohibited, the matter of taking Jizyah from minorities, and similar themes are raised as appropriate.

4- Why Does This Surah Not Have "Bismillah"?

The answer to this question is provided by the manner in which this Surah begins. This Surah actually starts with a declaration of war against treaty-breaking enemies, an expression of dissociation and immunity, and the adoption of a firm and harsh approach toward them. It reflects the wrath of Allah toward this group, and thus it is not compatible with "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ" (In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful), which is a sign of peace, friendship, and love, and an expression of God's attributes of Grace and Mercy.

This matter has been narrated in a tradition from Ali عليه السلام. [^1] Some also believe that since this Surah is effectively a continuation of Surah Al-Anfal—as Surah Al-Anfal discusses treaties and this Surah discusses the annulment of the treaties of the treaty-breakers—therefore, "Bismillah" was not mentioned between the two.

[^1]: - The late Tabarsi narrates from Ali (ع) as follows: "

لم تنزل‌بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌ على رأس سورة برائة، لان بسم اللَّه للامان و الرحمة و نزلت برائة لرفع الامان و السيف فيه‌

!" (Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim was not revealed at the head of Surah Bara'at, because Bismillah is for security and mercy, while Bara'at was revealed to remove security and [it contains] the sword!)

A tradition has also been narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام regarding this matter, [^1] and there is no obstacle to both reasons being the cause for omitting "Bismillah"—one mentioned in the first narration and the other in the second.

5- The Virtue and Constructive Effect of This Surah

In Islamic traditions, special importance is given to the recitation of this Surah and Surah Al-Anfal. Among these, we read in a tradition from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام who said: "

من قرأ برائة و الانفال فى كل شهر لم يدخله نفاق ابدا و كان من شيعة امير المؤمنين حقا

" (Whoever recites Surah Bara'at and Al-Anfal every month, hypocrisy shall never enter him, and he will be among the true followers of the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام).

We have said many times that the extraordinary importance given in traditions to the recitation of various Surahs does not mean that reciting without reflection and action will cause these effects to appear miraculously. For instance, one who recites the words of Bara'at and Al-Anfal without the slightest understanding of their meaning will not become a stranger to hypocrisy or be placed in the ranks of true Shiites. Rather, these are in truth references to the content of the Surah and its constructive effect on the education of the individual and society, which is impossible without understanding the meaning and being prepared for action. Since the ranks of true believers and hypocrites, and the main lines of their lives, are clearly defined in these two Surahs, the path is made completely clear for those who are people of action, not just words. Therefore, their recitation, combined with understanding the content and implementing it in life, has this extraordinary effect. Those who look at the Quran and its luminous verses as a mere incantation are, in truth, strangers to the spirit of this educational and human-building Book.

[^1]: - The late Tabarsi narrates from Imam Sadiq (ع) that he said: "

الانفال و برائة واحدة

" (Al-Anfal and Bara'at are one).

The importance of the various points mentioned in this Surah is so great that it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "Surah Bara'at and Tawhid were revealed to me accompanied by seventy thousand ranks of angels, each recommending the importance of these two Surahs!"

6- A Historical Fact That Some Try to Conceal

Almost all commentators and historians agree that when this Surah—or its opening verses—was revealed and annulled the treaties the polytheists had with the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله initially gave this command to Abu Bakr to read to the general public in Mecca during the Hajj. Then He took it back and gave it to Ali عليه السلام, and Ali عليه السلام was commissioned to proclaim it, which he did to all the people during the Hajj ceremonies. Although the details and elaborations are narrated differently, paying attention to the following points can clarify the truth for us:

1- Ahmad ibn Hanbal, the famous leader of the Sunnis, narrates in his book "Musnad" from Ibn Abbas that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent a certain person (referring to Abu Bakr, as clear from future narrations) and gave him Surah Al-Tawbah (to proclaim to the people during Hajj). Then He sent Ali عليه السلام after him, took it from him, and said: "

لا يذهب بها الا رجل منى و انا منه‌

" (No one should take this except a man who is from me and I am from him). [^1]

2- He also narrates in the same book from Anas ibn Malik that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent Surah Bara'at with Abu Bakr, but when he reached "Dhu al-Hulayfah" (another name for Masjid al-Shajarah, located one league from Medina), He said:

[^1]: - Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 1, p. 331, Egypt edition.

" لا يبلغها الا انا او رجل من اهل بيتى فبعث بها مع على‌

" (No one should proclaim this except myself or a man from my household. Then He sent it with Ali عليه السلام). [^1]

3- In the same book, through another chain of narration from Ali عليه السلام, it is narrated that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent Surah Bara'at with him, Ali عليه السلام said: "O Messenger of Allah! I am not a [professional] orator or speaker." The Prophet said: "There is no choice; either I must take it or you." Ali عليه السلام said: "Since it is so, I will take it." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "

انطلق فان اللَّه يثبت لسانك و يهدى قلبك‌

" (Go, for Allah will make your tongue firm and guide your heart). Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله placed His hand on Ali's عليه السلام mouth (so that by its blessing his tongue would become eloquent and fluent). [^2]

4- Al-Nasa'i, the famous Sunni leader, in the book "Khasa'is," narrates from Zayd ibn Subay' from Ali عليه السلام that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent Surah Bara'at with Abu Bakr to the people of Mecca, then He sent him (Ali عليه السلام) after him and said: "Take the letter from him and go to Mecca." Ali عليه السلام reached Abu Bakr on the way and took the letter from him. Abu Bakr returned saddened and said to the Prophet: "Has something been revealed concerning me?" (that you removed me from this task). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "No,

الا انى امرت ان ابلغه انا او رجل من اهل بيتى‌

" (Except that I was commanded that either I myself should proclaim it or a man from my household should proclaim it). [^3]

5- Through another chain, he narrates from Abdullah ibn Arqam that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent Surah Bara'at with Abu Bakr. When he had traveled part of the way,

[^1]: - Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 3, p. 212.

[^2]: - Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 1, p. 150.

[^3]: - Khasa'is al-Nasa'i, p. 28.

He sent Ali and took the Surah from him and he (Ali) took it (to Mecca). Abu Bakr felt a kind of distress in his heart (and came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله). The Prophet said:

" لا يؤدى عنى الا انا او رجل منى‌

" (No one performs [this duty] on my behalf except myself or a man from me). [^1]

6- The famous scholar Ibn Kathir, in his commentary, narrates from Ahmad ibn Hanbal from Hanash from Ali عليه السلام that when ten verses of Surah Bara'at were revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He called Abu Bakr and sent him to recite these verses to the people of Mecca. Then He sent for me and said: "Go after Abu Bakr, and wherever you reach him, take the letter from him..." Abu Bakr returned to the Prophet and asked if something had been revealed about him. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "No,

و لكن جبرئيل جاءني فقال لن يؤدى الا انت او رجل منك‌

" (But Gabriel came to me and said: None shall perform [this] except you or a man from you). [^2]

7- Ibn Kathir narrated the exact same meaning from Zayd ibn Yasigh. [^3]

8- The same Sunni scholar brought this Hadith through another chain from Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Husayn ibn Ali (Imam al-Baqir) عليه السلام in his commentary. [^4]

9- Allamah Ibn al-Athir, another Sunni scholar, in "Jami' al-Usul," narrates from Tirmidhi from Anas ibn Malik that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent Surah Bara'at with Abu Bakr, then He called him and said:

لا ينبغى لاحد ان يبلغ هذه الا رجل من اهلى، فدعا عليا فاعطاه اياه‌

" (It is not fitting for anyone to proclaim this except a man from my family. Then He called Ali عليه السلام and gave it to him). [^5]

[^1]: - Khasa'is al-Nasa'i, p. 28.

[^2] and [^3]: - Tafsir Ibn Kathir, Vol. 2, p. 322.

[^5]: - Jami' al-Usul, Vol. 9, p. 475.

10- Muhibb al-Din al-Tabari, a Sunni scholar, in the book "Dhakha'ir al-Uqba," narrates from Abu Sa'id or Abu Hurayrah: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله appointed Abu Bakr to oversee the Hajj. When he reached the land of "Dajnan," he heard the sound of Ali's عليه السلام camel and recognized it. He came to him and said: "Why have you come?"

He said: "It is good; the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has sent Surah Bara'at with me." When Abu Bakr returned (and expressed concern over the change in this mission), the Prophet said:

لا يبلغ عنى غيرى او رجل منى يعنى عليا

: "No one shall proclaim on my behalf except myself or a man from me," meaning Ali عليه السلام. [^1]

In other narrations, it is explicitly stated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله entrusted His own special camel to Ali to ride to Mecca and proclaim this invitation. Midway, when Abu Bakr heard the sound of the camel, he recognized it.

This Hadith, along with what was mentioned above, essentially conveys one point: that the camel was the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله own special camel, which He had placed at Ali's عليه السلام disposal at that time because his mission was a significant one.

In many other famous Sunni books, this Hadith is narrated sometimes as musnad (with a complete chain) and sometimes as mursal (with an incomplete chain), and it is among the Hadiths whose essence no one disputes.

According to some narrations from Sunni sources, after Abu Bakr was removed from the position of proclaiming these verses, he came to Mecca as the Amir al-Hajj (Leader of the Pilgrimage) and oversaw the Hajj affairs.

Explanation and Analysis

This Hadith clearly proves a great virtue for Ali عليه السلام.

[^1]: - Dhakha'ir al-Uqba, p. 69.

Unfortunately, like other Hadiths of this nature, it has been treated with unkindness. A group strives to either completely invalidate its value or diminish its importance, and they have chosen several ways to do this:

1- Sometimes, like the author of "Al-Manar," they have selected from among the Hadiths related to this event only those that speak of Abu Bakr's supervision of the Hajj ceremonies, while remaining silent regarding the taking of Surah Bara'at from him and the words the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله spoke about Ali عليه السلام!

While the silence of some Hadiths on this subject is no reason to ignore all those Hadiths that discuss it. A research-oriented approach requires considering all Hadiths, even if they go against one's inclinations and preconceptions.

2- At other times, they have attempted to weaken the chain of some Hadiths, such as those ending with "Simak" and "Hanash" (as the aforementioned commentator did).

However, this Hadith does not have only one or two chains, and its narrator is not limited to "Simak" and "Hanash"; rather, it appears through numerous chains in their reliable books.

3- And at times, they have resorted to astonishing justifications regarding the text of the Hadith, such as saying that if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله gave the mission of proclaiming the Surah to Ali عليه السلام, it was because, according to Arab custom, the annulment of treaties must be proclaimed by the person himself or one of his family members.

While, firstly, it is explicitly stated in numerous chains that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Gabriel brought me this command," or "I was given such a mission."

Secondly, in some of the chains of these Hadiths mentioned above, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: "If you do not do this, I must undertake it myself." Was there no Abbas, the Prophet's uncle, or any other relative among the Muslims such that if Ali عليه السلام did not go, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله Himself would have to act?

Thirdly, they have provided no evidence for the claim that Arab custom was such; it appears to be a guess and conjecture to justify the above Hadith according to their own desire.

Fourthly, in some reliable chains of this Hadith, the phrase "لا يذهب بها الا رجل منى و انا منه" (No one shall take it except a man who is from me and I am from him) or similar phrases appear, showing that the Prophet considered Ali عليه السلام as Himself and Himself as him (the same meaning found in the Verse of Mubahala).

From what we have presented, we conclude that if prejudices and preconceptions are set aside, the Prophet, through this action, distinguished the superiority of Ali's عليه السلام status over all the companions. "Indeed, this is the proclamation."

بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ إِلَى الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (1) فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللَّهِ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ مُخْزِي الْكافِرِينَ (2)

Commentary

The Treaties of the Polytheists are Annulled

In the environment of the Islamic call, there were various groups with whom the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله dealt according to their stances:

One group had no treaty with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had no obligation toward them either.

Other groups had signed non-aggression treaties with the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله at "Hudaybiyyah" and similar places. Some of these treaties had a fixed term, while others did not.

Among these, some tribes that had treaties with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله broke their pacts unilaterally and without any justification by openly cooperating with the enemies of Islam, or they plotted to eliminate the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, such as the Jews of "Bani Nadir" and "Bani Qurayzah." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله acted firmly against them and expelled them all from Medina. However, some treaties were still in force, including both fixed-term and open-ended ones.

The first verse under discussion announces to all polytheists (idolaters) that: Any treaty they had with the Muslims will be annulled. It says: "[This is] a declaration of dissociation from Allah and His Messenger to those with whom you made a treaty among the polytheists." (بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ إِلَى الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ).

Then He grants them a four-month grace period to reflect during this time and clarify their situation. After the expiration of the four months, they must either abandon the practice of idolatry or prepare for battle. He says: "So travel freely throughout the land for four months" (but after four months, the situation will change) (فَسِيحُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَرْبَعَةَ أَشْهُرٍ). [^1] "But know that you cannot escape Allah, and you cannot go beyond the realm of His power" (وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللَّهِ).

"And know also that Allah will eventually disgrace and shame the polytheistic and idol-worshipping disbelievers." (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ مُخْزِي الْكافِرِينَ).

[^1]: - "Sihu" is from the root "Siyahat," meaning travel accompanied by peace and a grace period.

Here, two points must be noted

1- Is the Unilateral Annulment of a Treaty Valid?

We know that in Islam, extraordinary importance is given to the issue of fulfilling covenants and adhering to treaties—even toward disbelievers and enemies. Nevertheless, the question arises: how does the Quran command the unilateral annulment of the polytheists' treaty?

The answer to this question becomes clear by considering the following points:

Firstly, as explicitly stated in verses 7 and 8 of this same Surah (a few verses later), this annulment was not without cause. Rather, signs and indications of treaty-breaking had appeared from them, and they were ready, if able, to strike a fatal blow to the Muslims without the slightest regard for the treaties they held. It is entirely logical that if a person sees an enemy preparing to break a treaty and the signs of it are sufficiently visible in their actions, they should declare the treaty annulled before being caught off guard and rise against them.

Secondly, regarding treaties imposed on a people or nation due to specific circumstances, which they find themselves forced to accept, what is the obstacle to unilaterally annulling such treaties once they gain power and ability? The practice of idolatry was neither a religion nor a rational school of thought; rather, it was a superstitious, illusory, and dangerous method that eventually had to be removed from human society. If the power and strength of the idolaters in the Arabian Peninsula were so great at the beginning that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was forced to submit to peace and a treaty with them, this does not mean He must remain faithful to such an imposed treaty—which is contrary to logic, reason, and wisdom—once He has gained strength. This is exactly like a great reformer appearing among cow-worshippers in some country, starting a vast campaign to dismantle this program, and when placed under pressure, being forced to sign a non-aggression pact with them. But when he finds enough followers, he rises to sweep away these decayed thoughts and declares his treaty with them annulled.

Therefore, we see that this ruling was specific to the polytheists, while the People of the Book and other nations around the Arabian Peninsula who had treaties of some kind with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had their treaties respected until the end of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life.

Furthermore, we see that the annulment of the polytheists' treaty was not done by surprise. Instead, they were given a four-month grace period, and this matter was brought to everyone's attention at the center of the public gathering of Hijaz—the day of Eid al-Adha next to the House of Kaaba—so they would have sufficient opportunity for further thought and reflection. Perhaps they would abandon this superstitious practice, which is the source of backwardness, division, ignorance, and malice. Allah never wished to catch them off guard or deprive them of the chance to think. Even if they were not ready to accept Islam, they would have enough time to prepare forces for their own defense so as not to be involved in an unfair war! If the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not have an educational perspective and regard for human principles, He should never have awakened the enemy by giving a four-month grace period and providing them with enough time to prepare forces and military readiness. Rather, He should have attacked them without warning on a specific day after the unilateral annulment of the treaty and dismantled their establishment.

For this reason, we see that many idolaters utilized this four-month grace period and, through further study of Islamic teachings, returned to its embrace.

2- When Did These Four Months Begin?

There is discussion among commentators in answering this question, but what appears from the literal sense of the above verses is that their beginning was from the time this proclamation was read to the general public. We know that the day of its reading was the day of Eid al-Adha, the tenth of the month of Dhu al-Hijjah. Therefore, its end was considered the tenth of the month of Rabi' al-Awwal of the following year.

A tradition narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام also confirms this point. [^1]

Ayahs

وَأَذَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلنَّاسِ يَوْمَ ٱلْحَجِّ ٱلْأَكْبَرِ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ۙ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ فَإِن تُبْتُمْ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ وَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِى ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٣)
[This is] an announcement from Allah and His Apostle to all the people on the day of the greater Hajj: that Allah and His Apostle repudiate the polytheists: If you repent that is better for you; but if you turn your backs [on Allah], know that you cannot frustrate Allah, and inform the faithless of a painful punishment (3)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّم مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنقُصُوكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَمْ يُظَـٰهِرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَدًا فَأَتِمُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلَىٰ مُدَّتِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤)
(barring the polytheists with whom you have made a treaty, and who did not violate any [of its terms] with you, nor backed anyone against you. So fulfill the treaty with them until [the end of] its term. Indeed Allah loves the Godwary). (4)

Commentary

Those Whose Treaties Are Respected

In these verses, the subject of the annulment of treaties with the polytheists is once again addressed with greater emphasis, and it even specifies the date of its announcement, stating: "And an announcement from Allah and His Messenger to the people on the day of the Great Hajj that Allah is disassociated from the polytheists, and [so is] His Messenger" (وَ أَذانٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ إِلَى النَّاسِ يَوْمَ الْحَجِّ الْأَكْبَرِ أَنَّ اللَّهَ بَرِي‌ءٌ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ وَ رَسُولُهُ‌) [1]. In truth, by making this public declaration in the land of Mecca and on that great day, Allah intends to close all avenues of excuse-making for the enemy and to silence the tongues of detractors and mischief-makers, so they may not say, "They caught us off guard and attacked us unfairly."

It is noteworthy that the expression "to the people" (إِلَى النَّاسِ‌) instead of "to the polytheists" indicates that it was necessary to convey this message to all people present in Mecca on that day, so that non-polytheists would also be witnesses to this matter.

Then, addressing the polytheists themselves, He endeavors to guide them through both encouragement and warning. First, He says: "So if you repent, it is better for you" (فَإِنْ تُبْتُمْ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ‌).

This means that accepting the creed of monotheism is to your benefit, the benefit of your society, and for your own worldly life and the Hereafter.

[1]: The phrase "And an announcement..." (وَ أَذانٌ) is conjoined to the phrase "A declaration of immunity from Allah..." (بَراءَةٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ). Other possibilities have been suggested regarding the syntax of this sentence, but what we have stated appears most evident.

If you reflect deeply, all your disorders will be set right under its light; it is not that it brings any profit to Allah or the Prophet.

Next, He warns the prejudiced and stubborn opponents: "But if you turn away, then know that you will not escape Allah" (وَ إِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ غَيْرُ مُعْجِزِي اللَّهِ‌). That is, if you turn away from this command which guarantees your own prosperity, know that you can never frustrate Allah or escape the realm of His power.

At the end of this verse, He issues a warning to those who resist with obstinacy, saying: "And give tidings of a painful punishment to those who disbelieve" (وَ بَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ‌).

As we previously mentioned, this unilateral annulment of treaties with the polytheists was specific to those from whom signs of readiness for treaty-breaking had appeared. Therefore, in the next verse, He exempts one group, saying: "Excepted are those with whom you made a treaty among the polytheists and who then did not fail you in anything and did not support anyone against you" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَنْقُصُوكُمْ شَيْئاً وَ لَمْ يُظاهِرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَحَداً).

Regarding this group, "So fulfill for them their treaty until their term" (فأَتِمُّوا إِلَيْهِمْ عَهْدَهُمْ إِلى‌ مُدَّتِهِمْ‌).

"Indeed, Allah loves the righteous" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ‌)—those who avoid any form of treaty-breaking or transgression.

Key Points

1- What is the "Great Hajj" (Hajj al-Akbar)?

There is discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of the "Day of the Great Hajj." However, what is gathered from many narrations transmitted through both the Ahl al-Bayt and Sunni sources is that it refers to the tenth day of Dhu al-Hijjah, the Day of Eid al-Adha, or in other words, "Yawm al-Nahr" (the Day of Sacrifice).

The fact that the four-month period ended on the tenth of Rabi' al-Thani, according to what is found in Islamic sources, is another proof for this matter. Furthermore, on the day of Eid al-Adha, the main parts of the Hajj rituals actually conclude, and thus, the term "Day of Hajj" can be applied to it.

As for why it is called "Great" (Akbar), it is because in that year, all groups, including Muslims and idolaters (according to the ancient custom they had), participated in the Hajj ceremonies. However, this practice was completely abolished in subsequent years.

In addition to the above interpretation, which also appears in Islamic narrations [1], there is another interpretation: that it refers to the Hajj rituals themselves, in contrast to the "Umrah" rituals, which are called the "Lesser Hajj" (Hajj al-Asghar).

This interpretation also appears in some narrations, and there is no obstacle to both

[1]: In Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, it is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who said: "It was only called the Great [Hajj] because it was a year in which both Muslims and polytheists performed Hajj, and no polytheist performed Hajj after that year." (Vol. 2, p. 184).

reasons simultaneously being the cause for this naming. [1]

2- The Four Articles Announced on That Day

Although the Quran mentions the declaration of Allah's disassociation from the polytheists in a general manner, it is understood from Islamic narrations that Ali عليه السلام was commissioned to announce four matters to the people:

  1. The annulment of the treaties of the polytheists.
  2. Their lack of right to participate in the Hajj ceremonies the following year.
  3. The prohibition of performing Tawaf naked, which was common among the polytheists until then.
  4. The prohibition of polytheists entering the House of Allah. [2]

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that Ali عليه السلام delivered a sermon during the Hajj ceremonies of that year and said:

"No naked person shall perform Tawaf around the House, and no polytheist shall perform Hajj to the House. Whoever has a treaty with a fixed term, it shall be respected until its term; and whoever has no fixed term, his term shall be four months."

[1]: In the same commentary, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that in response to a question from one of his companions regarding "the Day of the Great Hajj," he said: "It is the Day of Sacrifice (Yawm al-Nahr), and the Lesser [Hajj] is the Umrah." (Vol. 2, p. 186).

[2]: In some narrations, the fourth subject is mentioned as "the non-entry of polytheists into Paradise."

In some other narrations, the fourth subject refers to the non-entry of idolaters into the Kaaba.

3- Who Had Fixed-Term Treaties?

From the accounts of historians and some commentators, it is understood that they were a group from the tribes of "Bani Kinanah" and "Bani Damrah," who had nine months remaining of their non-aggression treaty. Since they had remained faithful to the terms of the treaty and had not participated in supporting the enemies of Islam, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله remained faithful to their treaty until the end of its term. [1] Others have considered a tribe from "Bani Khuza'ah" as part of this group whose treaties had a fixed term. [2]

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, under the verse being discussed.

[2]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 10, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

فَإِذَا ٱنسَلَخَ ٱلْأَشْهُرُ ٱلْحُرُمُ فَٱقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدتُّمُوهُمْ وَخُذُوهُمْ وَٱحْصُرُوهُمْ وَٱقْعُدُوا۟ لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ ۚ فَإِن تَابُوا۟ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَخَلُّوا۟ سَبِيلَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥)
Then, when the sacred months have passed, kill the polytheists wherever you find them, capture them and besiege them, and lie in wait for them at every ambush. But if they repent, and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, then let them alone. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)
وَإِنْ أَحَدٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ ٱسْتَجَارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّىٰ يَسْمَعَ كَلَـٰمَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ أَبْلِغْهُ مَأْمَنَهُۥ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦)
If any of the polytheists seeks asylum from you, grant him asylum until he hears the Word of Allah. Then convey him to his place of safety. That is because they are a people who do not know. (6)

Commentary

Severity Combined with Lenience!

Here, the duty of the Muslims after the expiration of the grace period for the polytheists—namely, four months—is explained. It issues the most severe command regarding them, stating:

"When the sacred months have passed, kill the idolaters wherever you find them" (فَإِذَا انْسَلَخَ الْأَشْهُرُ الْحُرُمُ فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ) [^1]. Then it says: "Capture them and besiege them" (وَ خُذُوهُمْ).

"And surround them" (وَ احْصُرُوهُمْ).

"And lie in wait for them at every turn and block the roads against them" (وَ اقْعُدُوا لَهُمْ كُلَّ مَرْصَدٍ) [^2]. Here, four harsh commands are observed regarding them: "blocking the roads, besieging, capturing, and finally, killing." It appears that these four matters are not presented as a choice of preference; rather, considering the conditions of the environment, time, place, and the individuals involved, whichever of these actions is deemed appropriate must be implemented. If sufficient pressure can be applied through only captivity, siege, and blocking their paths, then that route should be taken.

And if there is no remedy but killing, then killing them is permitted.

This severity is because the program of Islam was to uproot idolatry from the face of the earth. As we have previously pointed out, the issue of religious freedom—meaning the absence of compulsion for followers of other religions to accept Islam—is limited to the Heavenly religions and the People of the Book, such as the Jews and Christians, and does not include idolaters. This is because idolatry is not a religion or a creed to be respected; rather, it is a degradation, a superstition, a deviation, and a disease that must be uprooted at any cost.

However, this severity and harshness do not mean that the path of return is closed to them. Rather, at any moment they wish, they can change their direction. Therefore, it immediately adds: "If they repent and return toward the Truth, establish prayer, and pay the zakat, then let them go their way" and do not trouble them (فَإِنْ تابُوا وَ أَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ آتَوُا الزَّكاةَ فَخَلُّوا سَبِيلَهُمْ).

In this case, they will not have the slightest difference from other Muslims and will share in all laws and rights with them.

"For Allah is Forgiving and Merciful," and He does not turn away from His door anyone who returns to Him (إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Then, He completes this subject in the next verse with another command, so that no doubt remains that the goal of Islam in this instruction is the universalization of Monotheism and the religion of truth and justice, not colonialism, exploitation, or the seizing of the property or lands of others. He says:

"And if any one of the polytheists seeks asylum from you, grant him asylum so that he may hear the word of Allah" (وَ إِنْ أَحَدٌ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ اسْتَجارَكَ فَأَجِرْهُ حَتَّى يَسْمَعَ كَلامَ اللَّهِ).

This means to treat him with the utmost tranquility and give them the opportunity for thought and reflection so they may freely examine the content of your invitation, and if the light of guidance shines upon their hearts, they may accept it. Then He adds: "Then escort him to his place of safety" so that no one troubles him along the way (ثُمَّ أَبْلِغْهُ مَأْمَنَهُ).

Finally, He states the reason for this constructive command: "That is because they are a people who do not know" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

Therefore, if the doors of gaining awareness are opened to them, it is hoped that they will emerge from idolatry—which is born of ignorance—and step onto the path of Monotheism and God, which is the product of knowledge and wisdom.

It is narrated in both Shi'a and Sunni sources that after the cancellation of their treaty, one of the polytheists said to Ali عليه السلام: "O son of Abu Talib, if one of us, after the passing of these four months, wishes to meet the Prophet and discuss matters with him or hear the word of Allah, will he be in safety?" Ali عليه السلام replied: "Yes, for Allah has said: 'And if any one of the polytheists seeks asylum from you, grant him asylum...'" [^1]. In this way, the extraordinary strictness derived from the first verse is balanced by the lenience employed in the second verse. This is the method of education: to always mix severity with lenience and create a healing elixir from them.

[^1]: "انسلخ" (insalakha) is from the root "انسلاخ" (insilakh) meaning to go out or depart, and its origin is from "سلخ الشاة" (salkh al-shat), meaning to skin a sheep. [^2]: "مرصد" (marsad) is from the root "رصد" (rasad) meaning a path or an ambush point.

Several points should be noted here

1- What is meant by "the sacred months" here?

Although commentators have discussed this extensively, given the preceding verses, it appears that what is meant is the same four-month grace period stipulated for the polytheists. Its beginning was the 10th of Dhu al-Hijjah in the 9th year of the Hijra, and its end was the 10th of Rabi' al-Thani of the following year. This interpretation has been accepted by many researchers, and more importantly, it has been explicitly stated in numerous narrations [^2].

2- Are prayer and zakat conditions for the acceptance of Islam?

From the above verses, it might initially seem that for the repentance of the polytheists to be accepted, the performance of prayer and zakat is also necessary. For this reason, some Sunni jurists have taken the abandonment of prayer and zakat as evidence of disbelief (kufr).

However, the truth is that the mention of these two great Islamic commands is intended so that in all cases where the claim of Islam appears doubtful—as was often the case with the polytheists of that day—the performance of these two great Islamic duties serves as a sign of their Islam.

Alternatively, it means that they should accept prayer and zakat as two divine laws, submit to them, and recognize them officially, even if there is some deficiency in their practical execution. This is because we have abundant evidence that merely abandoning prayer or zakat does not place a person in the ranks of the disbelievers, even though his Islam is very incomplete.

Of course, if the abandonment of zakat is in the form of an uprising against the Islamic government, it would be a cause for disbelief, but that is another discussion unrelated to our topic.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 106; Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 15, p. 226. [^2]: In Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, under the verse in question, several hadiths are narrated in this regard.

3- Faith is born of knowledge

From the above verses, it is also understood that the major factor in lack of faith is ignorance, and the primary source of faith is knowledge and awareness. Therefore, for the guidance and direction of people, sufficient facilities for study and thought must be placed at their disposal so they can find the path of Truth, rather than accepting Islam blindly or out of imitation.

Ayahs

كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ عَهْدٌ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَعِندَ رَسُولِهِۦٓ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتُّمْ عِندَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ فَمَا ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱسْتَقِيمُوا۟ لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٧)
How shall the polytheists have any [valid] treaty with Allah and His Apostle?! (Barring those with whom you made a treaty at the Holy Mosque; so long as they are steadfast with you, be steadfast with them. Indeed Allah loves the Godwary.) (7)
كَيْفَ وَإِن يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ لَا يَرْقُبُوا۟ فِيكُمْ إِلًّا وَلَا ذِمَّةً ۚ يُرْضُونَكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَتَأْبَىٰ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٨)
How? For if, they get the better of you, they will observe toward you neither kinship nor covenant. They please you with their mouths while their hearts spurn you; and most of them are transgressors. (8)
ٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٩)
They have sold the signs of Allah for a paltry gain, and have barred [the people] from His way. Evil indeed is what they have been doing. (9)
لَا يَرْقُبُونَ فِى مُؤْمِنٍ إِلًّا وَلَا ذِمَّةً ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُعْتَدُونَ (١٠)
They observe toward a believer neither kinship nor covenant, and it is they who are the transgressors. (10)

Commentary

Transgressing Covenant-Breakers!

As we observed in the previous verses, Islam annulled the treaties with the polytheists and idolaters—except for a specific group—granting them only a four-month respite to make their decision. In the verses under discussion, the reason and cause for this action are explained. First, using a rhetorical question, it says:

"How can there be for the polytheists a treaty in the sight of Allah and with His Messenger?" (كَيْفَ يَكُونُ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ عَهْدٌ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَ عِنْدَ رَسُولِهِ).

This means that with these actions and their numerous violations, they should not expect the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to remain unilaterally faithful to their treaties.

Immediately after, it excludes a group that did not participate with the other polytheists in wrongful acts and covenant-breaking, saying: "Except those with whom you made a treaty at the Sacred Mosque" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ عِنْدَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ).

"So as long as they are true to you, be true to them" (فَمَا اسْتَقامُوا لَكُمْ فَاسْتَقِيمُوا لَهُمْ).

"For Allah loves the righteous [who guard against evil] and those who avoid any form of covenant-breaking" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ).

In the following verse, this same subject is expressed with greater clarity and emphasis, again using a rhetorical question: "How [can there be a treaty] while, if they gain dominance over you, they regard neither kinship with you nor a covenant?" (كَيْفَ وَ إِنْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ لا يَرْقُبُوا فِيكُمْ إِلًّا وَ لا ذِمَّةً).

"Ill" (إِلّ) means kinship, though some have interpreted it as a covenant or treaty. In the first case, the meaning is that although the Quraysh were kin to the Prophet and a group of Muslims, when they themselves show the slightest disregard for this matter and do not respect the sanctity of kinship, how can they expect the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the Muslims to observe it regarding them? In the second case, it serves as an emphasis for "Dhimmah" (ذِمَّة), which also means a covenant or treaty.

Raghib, in his book Al-Mufradat, considers the root of this word to be from "Alil" (اليل), meaning brilliance, because firm covenants and close kinships possess a special brilliance.

Then the Quran adds that you should never be deceived by their pleasing words and seemingly beautiful expressions, because: "They satisfy you with their mouths, but their hearts refuse" (يُرْضُونَكُمْ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ وَ تَأْبى‌ قُلُوبُهُمْ).

Their hearts are filled with malice, vengefulness, cruelty, hard-heartedness, and indifference toward covenants and kinship, even if they express friendship and affection with their tongues.

At the end of the verse, it points to the root cause of this issue, saying: "And most of them are defiant and disobedient" (وَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ فاسِقُونَ).

In the next verse, it explains one of the signs of their defiance and disobedience as follows: "They have exchanged the signs of Allah for a small price, and for their transient and insignificant material interests, they barred people from His way" (اشْتَرَوْا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا فَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ).

It is mentioned in a narration that Abu Sufyan prepared a feast and invited a group of people to a banquet to incite their enmity against the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) through this means.

Some commentators have considered the above verse to refer to this story, but it appears that the verse has a broad meaning that encompasses this incident and other actions of the idolaters who disregarded Allah's signs to preserve their material and fleeting interests.

Then it says: "Evil indeed is what they used to do" (إِنَّهُمْ ساءَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

They both deprived themselves of salvation, guidance, and happiness, and became obstacles in the path of others; and what action is worse than a person carrying both his own burden of sin and the burden of others?

In the last verse under discussion, it once again emphasizes the previous discourse: "Regarding a believer, they do not observe any kinship or covenant if they gain the upper hand" (لا يَرْقُبُونَ فِي مُؤْمِنٍ إِلًّا وَ لا ذِمَّةً).

"For they are indeed the transgressors" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُعْتَدُونَ).

Not only regarding you, but regarding anyone they have power over, they will commit transgression.

Although the content of the above verse emphasizes the discussion in the previous verses, it contains a difference and an addition: in the previous verses, the talk was about the companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the Muslims who were around him, but in this verse, the talk is about any individual with faith. That is, you have no specific status in their eyes; rather, anyone who is a believer and a follower of the creed of monotheism is their enemy, and they observe nothing regarding him. Thus, they are in reality enemies of faith and truth. This is similar to what the Quran says regarding some past nations: "And they resented them not except because they believed in Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Praiseworthy" (وَ ما نَقَمُوا مِنْهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ) (Surah Al-Buruj, Verse 8).

Two points must be noted here

1- There is discussion among commentators as to which group is intended by the exception in the phrase "Except those with whom you made a treaty at the Sacred Mosque" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ عاهَدْتُمْ عِنْدَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ). However, considering the previous verses, it appears that the intended groups are those tribes that remained faithful to their covenant, such as the "Banu Damrah," "Banu Khuzaymah," and their like.

In fact, this sentence serves as an emphasis on the previous verses, indicating that Muslims must be vigilant to distinguish these groups from the covenant-breakers.

As for the phrase "with whom you made a treaty at the Sacred Mosque," it may be because during the Peace of Hudaybiyyah—when the Muslims made a treaty with the polytheists of Quraysh in the land of Hudaybiyyah, fifteen miles from Mecca in the sixth year of the Hijra—other groups of Arab polytheists, such as the tribes mentioned above, joined this treaty and entered into a non-aggression pact with the Muslims. However, the polytheists of Quraysh broke their covenant and subsequently embraced Islam during the conquest of Mecca in the eighth year. But the affiliated groups did not become Muslim, nor did they break the treaty.

Since the land of Mecca encompasses a vast area (up to about 48 miles) around it, all these regions are considered part of the "Sacred Mosque" (Masjid al-Haram). As we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 196, regarding the Hajj al-Tamattu' and its rulings: "This is for those whose family is not present at the Sacred Mosque" (ذلِكَ لِمَنْ لَمْ يَكُنْ أَهْلُهُ حاضِرِي الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ). According to the explicit narrations and the fatwas of jurists, the rulings of Hajj al-Tamattu' apply to those whose distance from Mecca is more than 48 miles. Therefore, there is no obstacle to the Peace of Hudaybiyyah, which took place 15 miles from Mecca, being mentioned as "at the Sacred Mosque" (عِنْدَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ).

As for the claim by some commentators that the above exception refers to the polytheists of Quraysh—whose treaty made at Hudaybiyyah the Holy Quran respected—this seems incorrect.

Firstly, the covenant-breaking of the polytheists of Quraysh was certain and established; if they were not covenant-breakers, then who was?

Secondly, the Peace of Hudaybiyyah pertains to the sixth year of the Hijra, whereas in the eighth year, after the conquest of Mecca, the polytheists of Quraysh accepted Islam. Therefore, the above verses, which were revealed in the ninth year of the Hijra, cannot be referring to them.

2- As previously stated, the meaning of the above verses is not that their mere intention to break the covenant upon reaching power is the justification for the unilateral annulment of the treaty. Rather, they had demonstrated this mindset practically many times: whenever they had the chance, they would strike a heavy blow against the Muslims without regard for the treaty. This extent is sufficient for the annulment of a treaty.

Ayahs

فَإِن تَابُوا۟ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۗ وَنُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (١١)
Yet if they repent and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, then they are your brethren in faith. We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge. (11)
وَإِن نَّكَثُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ عَهْدِهِمْ وَطَعَنُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ فَقَـٰتِلُوٓا۟ أَئِمَّةَ ٱلْكُفْرِ ۙ إِنَّهُمْ لَآ أَيْمَـٰنَ لَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَنتَهُونَ (١٢)
But if they break their pledges after their having made a treaty and revile your religion, then fight the leaders of unfaith—indeed, they have no [commitment to] pledges,—maybe they will desist. (12)
أَلَا تُقَـٰتِلُونَ قَوْمًا نَّكَثُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ وَهَمُّوا۟ بِإِخْرَاجِ ٱلرَّسُولِ وَهُم بَدَءُوكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۚ أَتَخْشَوْنَهُمْ ۚ فَٱللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَوْهُ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٣)
Will you not make war on a people who broke their pledges and resolved to expel the Apostle, and opened [hostilities] against you initially? Do you fear them? But Allah is worthier of being feared by you, should you be faithful. (13)
قَـٰتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ وَيُخْزِهِمْ وَيَنصُرْكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَيَشْفِ صُدُورَ قَوْمٍ مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٤)
Make war on them so that Allah may punish them by your hands and humiliate them, and help you against them, and heal the hearts of a faithful folk, (14)
وَيُذْهِبْ غَيْظَ قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَيَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٥)
and remove rage from their hearts, and Allah turns clemently to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise (15)

Commentary

Why Do You Fear Fighting the Enemy?

One of the techniques of eloquence and rhetoric is to repeat highly important matters using various expressions for emphasis and to ensure the subject is firmly established. Since the issue of dealing the final blow to the body of idolatry in the Islamic environment and uprooting its last remains was of great significance, the Holy Quran once again presents the previous points with fresh phrasing in the above verses. New points are also included which take the matter out of the form of mere repetition—even if it were a permissible repetition.

First, It says: "But if they repent, establish prayer, and give zakat, then they are your brothers in religion" (فَإِنْ تابُوا وَ أَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ آتَوُا الزَّكاةَ فَإِخْوانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ‌).

And at the end of the verse, It adds: "And We detail the verses for a people who know" (وَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

In the previous verses, the discussion was that if they repent and perform the Islamic duties of prayer and zakat, do not obstruct them (فَخَلُّوا سَبِيلَهُمْ‌). However, here He says: "they are your brothers in religion," meaning they will have no difference from other Muslims in terms of respect and affection, just as there is no difference between brothers.

This is more effective in preparing the spirit, mind, and emotions of the polytheists to accept Islam; in one stage, It recommends non-interference, and in the next stage, It commands the "rights of a brother" regarding them.

"But if they continue their breach of promise, trample upon their covenant, defame your religion, and continue their malicious propaganda, then fight the leaders of disbelief" (وَ إِنْ نَكَثُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ عَهْدِهِمْ وَ طَعَنُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ فَقاتِلُوا أَئِمَّةَ الْكُفْرِ). "For indeed, their oaths are of no value" (إِنَّهُمْ لا أَيْمانَ لَهُمْ‌).

It is true that they entered into a non-aggression pact with you, but this pact, with its repeated violations and their readiness for future breaches, holds no credibility or value at all.

"So that, in view of this severity and considering that the path of return is open to them, they may regret their actions and desist" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَنْتَهُونَ‌).

In the next verse, to incite the Muslims and remove any weakness, fear, or doubt regarding this vital matter from their spirits and minds, It says: "Will you not fight a people who broke their oaths and determined to expel the Messenger?" (أَ لا تُقاتِلُونَ قَوْماً نَكَثُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ وَ هَمُّوا بِإِخْراجِ الرَّسُولِ‌). You did not initiate the combat or the cancellation of the treaty to be worried or uneasy; rather, "they were the ones who started [the conflict] with you the first time" (وَ هُمْ بَدَؤُكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ).

And if the hesitation of some of you in fighting them is due to fear, this fear is completely misplaced: "Do you fear them? But Allah has more right that you should fear Him, if you are [truly] believers" (أَ تَخْشَوْنَهُمْ فَاللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَنْ تَخْشَوْهُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

In the following verse, He gives a promise of certain victory to the Muslims and says: "Fight them; Allah will punish them by your hands" (قاتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ اللَّهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ‌). Not only will He punish them, but He will also "disgrace them" (وَ يُخْزِهِمْ‌).

"And He will give you victory over them" (وَ يَنْصُرْكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ‌).

In this way, He heals the hearts of a group of believers who were under severe pressure and torture by this cold-hearted group and had given sacrifices in this path, "and He places a balm upon the wounds of their hearts through this means" (وَ يَشْفِ صُدُورَ قَوْمٍ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Some commentators have said that "a believing people" refers to the believers of the "Bani Khuza'ah" tribe, upon whom a group of idolaters from the "Bani Bakr" tribe treacherously fell and caught them off guard. Others have said it refers to a group of people from Yemen who accepted Islam but were harassed and tortured by the idolaters when they came to Mecca.

However, it is not unlikely that this expression includes all those who were in some way under the pressure and torture of the idolaters and whose hearts were filled with grief because of them.

In the next verse, It adds that in the light of your victory and their defeat, He "removes the fury of their [the believers'] hearts" (وَ يُذْهِبْ غَيْظَ قُلُوبِهِمْ‌).

This sentence might be an emphasis for the previous phrase (وَ يَشْفِ صُدُورَ قَوْمٍ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌), or it might be distinct from it. The previous phrase could indicate that in the light of Islam's victory, hearts that had been throbbing and distressed for years for the sake of Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were healed. The second sentence indicates that hearts filled with distress due to the loss of loved ones and relatives and enduring various types of harassment and torture find their peace with the killing of the cold-hearted enemies.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah accepts the repentance of whom He wills (and deems appropriate)" (وَ يَتُوبُ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

"And Allah is aware of the intentions of those who repent, and the commands He has given regarding them as well as the oath-breakers are wise" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ‌).

Additionally, the final sentences indicate that some of them may enter through the door of repentance in the future; it must be noted that Allah accepts their repentance and severity against them is no longer permissible. It is also a glad tiding that such individuals will come toward the Muslims in the future, and divine success will encompass them due to their spiritual readiness.

Generally, some commentators have considered these recent verses to be among the unseen reports of the Quran and signs of the truthfulness of the Prophet's call, because what the Quran stated therein has indeed come to pass.

Key Points

1- There is debate among commentators regarding who this group refers to. Some point to the Jews, some to nations that would clash with Muslims in the future, such as the Persian and Roman empires, some to the pagans of the Quraysh, and some to certain individuals who became Muslim and then apostatized.

However, the apparent meaning of the verses clearly testifies that the subject is that same group of polytheists and idolaters who at that time ostensibly had a non-aggression pact with the Muslims but had practically violated their covenant; they were groups of polytheists around Mecca or other parts of the Hijaz.

The possibility that it refers to the Jews is very remote, as all the discussions in these verses revolve around the polytheists.

Similarly, it is impossible that it refers to the Quraysh tribe, because the Quraysh and their leader Abu Sufyan ostensibly accepted Islam in the 8th year of the Hijrah after the conquest of Mecca, while the Surah under discussion was revealed in the 9th year.

Also, the possibility that it refers to the Persian and Roman empires is very far from the context of the verses, as the verses speak of a current conflict, not future ones, and furthermore, they had not expelled the Prophet from his homeland.

The possibility that it refers to apostates is also extremely remote, as history does not show a powerful group of apostates at that time whom the Muslims were intended to fight. Moreover, the word "ayman" (plural of yamin, meaning oaths) and the word "ahd" (covenant) apparently refer to the non-aggression pact, not the acceptance of Islam (take note).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 7, p. 307

If we see in some Islamic narrations that this verse is applied to the instigators of the Battle of Jamal (the Nakithin) and the like, it is not because the verses were revealed about them, but rather the goal is that the spirit of the verse and its ruling are applicable to the "Nakithin" and similar groups that would emerge later.

The only remaining question is: if the reference is to those same groups of oath-breaking idolaters mentioned in the previous verses, why does It use the expression "وَ إِنْ نَكَثُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ" ("And if they break their oaths") when these groups had already practically broken them?

The answer is that the mentioned phrase means: if they continue their breach of promise and do not desist from their actions, you must fight them. This is similar to what we say in the meaning of "اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ" ("Guide us to the straight path"), which means: O Allah, keep us consistently on the straight path and continue our guidance.

The evidence for this is that the mentioned phrase (إِنْ نَكَثُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ‌) is placed in contrast to the phrase (فَإِنْ تابُوا ...) ("But if they repent..."), meaning it is one of two cases: either they repent, give up polytheism and idolatry, and come to the way of God, or they continue on their path. In the first case, they are your brothers, and in the second case, you must fight them!

2- It is noteworthy that in the above verses, It does not say fight the disbelievers, but rather says rise in Jihad against their leaders and chiefs (أَئِمَّةَ الْكُفْرِ). This indicates that the masses follow their leaders and chiefs; your target should always be them. You must block the sources of misguidance, deviation, oppression, and corruption, and burn the roots. As long as they exist, fighting their followers is of no use. Additionally, this expression is considered a kind of high-mindedness, nobility of spirit, and encouragement for the Muslims—that your main opponents are them; prepare yourselves to fight them, not their minor subordinates!

Strangely, some have considered this expression to refer to Abu Sufyan and his like among the elders of the Quraysh, while a group of them were killed at Badr and the rest (like Abu Sufyan) ostensibly accepted Islam after the conquest of Mecca and were positioned in the ranks of the Muslims at the time of the verse's revelation, so fighting them would have been meaningless.

Today, this important command of the Quran remains in full force; to eliminate oppression, corruption, colonialism, and exploitation, the stance must be against the leaders and chiefs of these groups, and dealing with ordinary individuals is fruitless (take note!).

3- The expression "فَإِخْوانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ‌" ("then they are your brothers in religion") mentioned in the verses above is the most delicate expression that can be stated regarding the equality of individuals in a society with the strongest emotional bonds. This is because the clearest and closest emotional bond in humans that enjoys complete equality is the bond between two brothers.

But alas, class divides and ethnic and racial idols have once again destroyed this Islamic brotherhood which was the envy of all enemies. Yesterday's brothers are today lined up against each other in an unbelievable manner, and sometimes they slaughter one another in a way that no enemy would do to his foe—and this is one of the secrets of our backwardness today!

4- From the phrase "أَ تَخْشَوْنَهُمْ‌" ("Do you fear them?"), it can be generally inferred that among the Muslims, there was a group that dreaded this command of Jihad, either because of the power and strength of the enemy or because they felt breaking a treaty is a sin.

The Quran explicitly answers them: you should not fear these weak humans; rather, you should fear disobeying the command of the Lord. Furthermore, the fear of being an oath-breaker is misplaced because they were the ones who initially provided the grounds for breaking the covenant and took the lead.

5- The phrase "هَمُّوا بِإِخْراجِ الرَّسُولِ‌" ("they determined to expel the Messenger") apparently refers to the issue of expelling the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from "Mecca" during the migration to Medina. They first intended that, and then their intention changed to the assassination of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله left Mecca that same night by Allah's command. In any case, the mention of this subject is not as a sign of their breach of promise, but as a recounting of a painful memory of the crimes of the idolaters, in which both the Quraysh and other tribes participated; otherwise, the idolaters' breach of promise had been made manifest through other means.

6- Among the surprising matters is that some followers of the school of Determinism (Jabriyyah) have used the verse "قاتِلُوهُمْ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ اللَّهُ بِأَيْدِيكُمْ‌" ("Fight them; Allah will punish them by your hands") to argue for their school of thought. However, if we clear our minds of prejudices, the above verse does not have the slightest indication of their objective. It is exactly like when we go to one of our friends to perform a task and say, "I hope Allah rectifies this matter through your hand." The meaning of this statement is not that you are forced to do this task; rather, the meaning is that Allah has placed a power at your disposal and given you a pure intention so that by using them, you can perform this task with free will.

Ayah

أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تُتْرَكُوا۟ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا رَسُولِهِۦ وَلَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلِيجَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٦)
Do you suppose that you will be let off while Allah has not yet ascertained those of you who wage jihad and those who do not take anyone as [their] confidant besides Allah and His Apostle and the faithful? Allah is well aware of what you do. (16)

Commentary

In this verse, Allah encourages the Muslims toward Jihad through another approach, drawing their attention to their heavy responsibility in this regard. You must not imagine that everything will be set right merely by claiming faith; rather, the sincerity of your intention, the truthfulness of your speech, and the reality of your faith are clarified through struggle against the enemies—a struggle that is sincere and free from any form of hypocrisy.

First, He says: "Do you think that you will be left alone?"

And that you will not be placed in the field of testing, while those among you who have striven (in Jihad), and those who have not taken any intimate friend and confidant other than Allah, His Messenger صلى الله عليه وآله, and the believers, have not yet been distinguished? (أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَنْ تُتْرَكُوا وَ لَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا مِنْكُمْ وَ لَمْ يَتَّخِذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَ لا رَسُولِهِ وَ لَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌ وَلِيجَةً) [1]

The word "Walijah" (ولِيجة) is derived from the root "Wuluj" (ولوج), meaning "to enter." It refers to someone who is a confidant (keeper of secrets) and a manager of one's affairs; its meaning is nearly identical to the term "Bitanah" (بطانة).

In reality, the above sentence reminds the Muslims of two points: that matters are not settled and the character of individuals is not clarified merely by the profession of faith. Rather, people are tested through two means of examination:

First, Jihad in the way of Allah to erase the traces of polytheism and idolatry; and second, abandoning every kind of relationship and cooperation with the hypocrites and enemies. The first drives out the external enemies, and the second drives out the internal ones.

The phrase "لَمَّا يَعْلَمِ اللَّهُ" (while Allah has not yet known), similar to what is seen in other verses of the Quran, actually means "it has not yet been realized." (In other words, the negation of "knowledge" is a negation of the "known object," and this type of expression is usually used for emphasis). Otherwise, according to rational proofs and the explicit text of numerous verses of the Quran, Allah has been and will be aware of everything.

This verse is, in fact, similar to the first verse of Surah Al-Ankabut, where it says: "أَ حَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَنْ يُتْرَكُوا أَنْ يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَ هُمْ لا يُفْتَنُونَ" (Do people think that they will be left alone because they say: "We believe," and will not be tested?).

And as we mentioned in the commentary of Surah Al-Imran, divine trials are not for discovering an unknown matter, but rather they mean "nurturing" and are intended to bring talents into bloom and to reveal the inner secrets of individuals.

At the end of the verse, as a warning and emphasis, He says: "And Allah is Aware of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ).

Lest some imagine that Allah is unaware of their secret connections with the hypocrites and enemies; rather, He knows everything well and will treat His servants accordingly.

From the style of the verse's expression, it appears that among the Muslims of that day, there were individuals who had recently entered the environment of Islam and lacked the spiritual readiness for Jihad. This discourse is about them; otherwise, the true Mujahideen had already clarified their status many times in the fields of Jihad up to that day.

[^1]: "Am" (أَمْ) is a conjunction used to join one interrogative sentence to another, and in this way, it conveys the meaning of a question. However, it always follows another interrogation, and in the above verse, it is joined to the sentence "أَ لا تُقاتِلُونَ..." (Will you not fight...) which appeared in verse 13.

Ayahs

مَا كَانَ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يَعْمُرُوا۟ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ شَـٰهِدِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِم بِٱلْكُفْرِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ وَفِى ٱلنَّارِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٧)
The polytheists may not maintain Allah’s mosques while they are witness to their own unfaith. Their works have failed, and they shall remain in the Fire [forever]. (17)
إِنَّمَا يَعْمُرُ مَسَـٰجِدَ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَأَقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَى ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَلَمْ يَخْشَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۖ فَعَسَىٰٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَن يَكُونُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (١٨)
Only those shall maintain Allah’s mosques who believe in Allah and the Last Day, and maintain the prayer, and give the zakat, and fear no one except Allah. They, hopefully, will be among the guided. (18)

Commentary

The Construction of Masajid is Not Within Everyone's Competence

Among the issues that could have been raised by some after the cancellation of the treaty with the polytheists and the decree of Jihad against them was: why should we drive away this vast group and not allow them to enter Masjid al-Haram for the Hajj ceremonies, while their participation in these rites is a source of prosperity from every perspective? This includes the physical prosperity of the structure of Masjid al-Haram through the significant contributions they made to this work, as well as spiritual prosperity in terms of increasing the congregation around the House of Allah!

The above verses respond to such vain and baseless thoughts, and in the first verse, it explicitly states:

"It is not for the polytheists to maintain the mosques of Allah while they witness against themselves to disbelief" (مَا كَانَ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَنْ يَعْمُرُوا مَسَاجِدَ اللَّهِ شَاهِدِينَ عَلَىٰ أَنْفُسِهِمْ بِالْكُفْرِ).

Their testimony against their own disbelief is evident both through their words and through their actions; even their manner of worship and Hajj ceremonies bear witness to this fact.

Then, referring to the reason and philosophy behind this ruling, it says: "Because of their lack of faith, their deeds are nullified and go to waste, and they hold the slightest weight or value before Allah" (أُولَٰئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ).

And for this reason, "they shall abide in the Fire forever" (وَفِي النَّارِ هُمْ خَالِدُونَ).

Therefore, neither their efforts for the construction and maintenance of Masjid al-Haram or the like have any value, nor does their massive gathering around the Kaaba.

Allah is Pure and Sanctified, and His House must also be pure and clean; the hands of the defiled must be completely severed from the House of Allah and the Masajid.

In the next verse, to complete this discourse, it mentions the conditions for those who maintain the Masajid and centers of worship, stating five important conditions:

"The mosques of Allah are only to be maintained by those who believe in Allah and the Last Day" (إِنَّمَا يَعْمُرُ مَسَاجِدَ اللَّهِ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ). This refers to the first and second conditions, which are of a foundational and creedal nature. Without them, no pure, worthy, or sincere action can stem from a human being; rather, even if it appears worthy outwardly, it will be inwardly contaminated with various impure motives.

Then it points to the third and fourth conditions, saying: "and establish prayer and give zakat" (وَأَقَامَ الصَّلَاةَ وَآتَى الزَّكَاةَ).

This means that one's faith in Allah and the Last Day should not remain merely at the level of a claim, but must be confirmed by pure deeds. Both the connection with Allah must be firm—by performing the prayer correctly—and the connection with Allah's creation—by paying the zakat.

Finally, it mentions the last condition, saying: "and fear none but Allah" (وَلَمْ يَخْشَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ).

His heart is filled with the love of Allah and he feels responsibility only toward His command. He considers weak servants too insignificant to have any influence on his destiny, the destiny of his society, his future, his victory and progress, and ultimately, on the prosperity of the center of his worship.

And at the end, it adds: "It is expected that these [who possess such qualities] will be among the guided" and reach their goal, striving in the construction and maintenance of the Masajid of Allah and benefiting from its great results (فَعَسَىٰ أُولَٰئِكَ أَنْ يَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُهْتَدِينَ).

Here, several points should be noted

1- What is meant by "Maintenance" (Imran)?

Does maintaining the Masajid mean the prosperity of the building and its facilities, or does it mean congregating and participating in it? Some commentators have chosen only one of these two aspects in interpreting "maintaining the Masajid" in the above verse, while the concept of this word is broad and encompasses all these matters.

Polytheists and idolaters have neither the right to participate in the Masajid nor to repair or construct their buildings; all these matters must be carried out by the hands of Muslims.

Furthermore, it is inferred from these verses that Muslims should not accept gifts and contributions from polytheists—indeed, from all non-Islamic sects—for the construction of their Masajid. For although the first verse speaks only of polytheists, the second verse, which begins with the word "Innama" (Only), restricts the maintenance of Masajid to Muslims.

From this, it becomes clear that the trustees and guardians of Masajid must also be chosen from among the purest individuals. It is not right that impure and defiled individuals—due to their wealth and riches, or because of their status or social influence, as has unfortunately become common in many places—be appointed over these centers of worship and Islamic gatherings.

Rather, all impure hands must be shortened from all these sacred centers.

From the day a group of tyrannical rulers or contaminated and sinful wealthy people took to building Masajid and Islamic centers, their spirit, spirituality, and constructive programs were distorted. This is why we see many such Masajid taking the form of "Masjid al-Dirar" (the Mosque of Harm)!

2- Sincere Action Stems Only from Faith

Some might think: what is the harm in using the capital of non-Muslims for the construction and maintenance of these centers?

But those who say this do not pay attention to this fundamental point: that Islam everywhere considers a righteous deed to be the fruit of the tree of faith. Action is always a reflection of a person's intentions and beliefs, and it always takes on their shape and color. It is impossible for impure intentions to produce a pure action and show a useful result, because action is the reflection of intention.

3- Brave Guardians

The phrase "and fear none but Allah" (لَمْ يَخْشَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ) shows that the construction, maintenance, and preservation of Masajid is not possible except under the shadow of bravery and courage. These sacred Islamic centers become hubs for human-building and high-level educational classes only when they have brave founders and guardians—those who fear no one but Allah, are not influenced by any position or power, and implement no program therein except Divine programs.

4- Is Only Masjid al-Haram Intended?

Some commentators have considered the above verses to be specific to "Masjid al-Haram," while the wording of the verse is general, and there is no evidence for this restriction. Although "Masjid al-Haram," which is the greatest Islamic mosque, stands in the first rank and was primarily in mind on the day the verses were revealed, this does not justify restricting the concept of the verses.

5- The Importance of Building Masajid

Regarding the importance of building a Masjid, numerous hadiths have reached us through both the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) and Sunni sources, showing the extraordinary importance of this work.

It is narrated from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) that he said: "Whoever builds a Masjid, even if it be the size of a bird's nest, Allah will build for him a house in Paradise." [^1]

And in another hadith narrated from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family): "Whoever lights a lamp in a Masjid, the angels and the bearers of the Throne will continue to seek forgiveness for him as long as the light of that lamp shines in that Masjid." [^2]

But today, what is of greater importance is the spiritual maintenance and prosperity of the Masajid. In other words, more than the importance we give to building the Masjid, we must give importance to building the individuals who are the people of the Masjid, its guardians, and its protectors.

The Masjid must be a hub for every kind of Islamic movement and constructive activity in the field of public awareness and awakening, purifying the environment, and preparing Muslims to defend Islamic heritage!

In particular, care must be taken that the Masjid becomes a center for faithful youth, not just a center for retirees and the disabled. The Masjid must be a hub for the most active sectors of society, not a center for idle, lethargic, and drowsy individuals!

[^1]: This hadith is mentioned in the book Wasa'il, Chapter 8 of the Chapters on the Rulings of Masajid, and also in Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 10, p. 213, narrated from Ibn Abbas.

[^2]: Kitab al-Mahasin, p. 57, as quoted in Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 1, p. 108.

Ayahs

۞ أَجَعَلْتُمْ سِقَايَةَ ٱلْحَآجِّ وَعِمَارَةَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ كَمَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَجَـٰهَدَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ لَا يَسْتَوُۥنَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٩)
Do you regard the providing of water to Hajj pilgrims and the maintenance of the Holy Mosque as similar [in worth] to someone who has faith in Allah and [believes in] the Last Day and wages jihad in the way of Allah? They are not equal with Allah, and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (19)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (٢٠)
Those who have believed and migrated, and waged jihad in the way of Allah with their possessions and persons have a greater rank near Allah, and it is they who are the triumphant. (20)
يُبَشِّرُهُمْ رَبُّهُم بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَرِضْوَٰنٍ وَجَنَّـٰتٍ لَّهُمْ فِيهَا نَعِيمٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٢١)
Their Lord gives them the good news of His mercy and [His] pleasure, and for them there will be gardens with lasting bliss, (21)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٢٢)
to remain in them forever. With Allah indeed is a great reward. (22)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verses, various narrations have been cited in both Sunni and Shi'a books. Among them, what appears to be most authentic is presented below.

The famous Sunni scholar, Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani, narrates from Buraydah that Shaybah and Abbas were each boasting to the other.
