They were engaged in this conversation when Ali عليه السلام passed by them and asked, "What are you boasting about?" Abbas said, "I have been given a privilege that no one else possesses, and that is the matter of providing water to the pilgrims of the House of Allah (Siqayat al-Hajj)."

Shaybah said, "I am the maintainer of al-Masjid al-Haram (and the key-bearer of the Kaaba)." Ali عليه السلام said, "Despite my respect for you both, I must say that notwithstanding my young age, I possess an honor that you do not." They asked, "What honor is that?!"

He replied, "I fought with the sword in Jihad until you believed in Allah and His Messenger صلى الله عليه وآله."

Abbas rose in anger and, dragging his cloak, went to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (to complain), saying, "Do you not see how Ali speaks to me?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said, "Call Ali." When he came before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He asked, "Why did you speak to your uncle (Abbas) in this manner?"

Ali عليه السلام submitted, "O Messenger of Allah! If I have upset him, it was by stating a truth. Before the word of truth, let whoever wishes be upset, and whoever wishes be pleased!" Gabriel descended and said, "O Muhammad! Your Lord sends you greetings and says: Recite these verses to them: 'أَ جَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِ‌...'"

"Do you consider the providing of drinking water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of al-Masjid al-Haram as [equal to the worth of] one who believes in Allah and the Last Day and strives in the cause of Allah? They are not equal..." [1] This same narration, with the same theme or slight variations, has been reported in numerous Sunni books, such as the Tafsir of Tabari and Tha'labi, Asbab al-Nuzul by al-Wahidi, Tafsir al-Khazin of Baghdadi, Ma'alim al-Tanzil by Allamah Baghawi, Manaqib of Ibn al-Maghazili, Jami' al-Usul by Ibn al-Athir, Tafsir al-Fakhr al-Razi, and other books. [2] In any case, the aforementioned Hadith is among the well-known and famous narrations that even biased individuals have acknowledged, and we shall speak further on this after completing the commentary on these verses.

Commentary

The Criterion of Honor and Virtue

Although these verses have a specific occasion of revelation, they simultaneously complement the discussion of the preceding verses, and many such instances exist in the Quran.

In the first verse, It says: "Do you consider the providing of drinking water to the pilgrims and the maintenance of al-Masjid al-Haram as [equal to the worth of] one who believes in Allah and the Last Day and strives in the cause of Allah? They are not equal in the sight of Allah. And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people." (أَ جَعَلْتُمْ سِقايَةَ الْحاجِّ وَ عِمارَةَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ كَمَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَ جاهَدَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لا يَسْتَوُونَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ‌ وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

"Siqayah" is both an infinitive meaning "to give water" and a noun meaning the "tool" or "vessel" used for giving water (as seen in Surah Yusuf, verse 70), and also a large container or basin into which water is poured. In al-Masjid al-Haram, between the Well of Zamzam and the Kaaba, there is a place known as "Siqayat al-Abbas." It seems a large vessel was placed there from which pilgrims would take water.

History indicates that before Islam, the office of "Siqayat al-Hajj" was ranked alongside the office of the key-bearer of the Kaaba and was considered one of the most important positions.

The necessity and intense need of the pilgrims for water during the Hajj days—especially in that dry, scorching, and water-scarce land where the weather is hot most of the year—gave this matter (Siqayat al-Hajj) a special importance. Naturally, whoever oversaw this position enjoyed a unique status because his service to the pilgrims was considered a vital service.

Similarly, the "key-bearing" and the maintenance and development of al-Masjid al-Haram, which was considered the holiest and greatest religious center even during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), evoked extraordinary respect for the person or persons in charge of it.

Despite all this, the Holy Quran declares that faith in Allah and Jihad in His way are superior and higher than all these deeds!

In the next verse, as an emphasis and explanation, It says: "Those who have believed and emigrated and striven in the cause of Allah with their wealth and their lives are greater in rank in the sight of Allah." (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ هاجَرُوا وَ جاهَدُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً عِنْدَ اللَّهِ‌).

"And it is those who are the attainers [of success]." (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ‌).

In the following verse, It says that Allah grants them three great blessings in exchange for these three important deeds (Faith, Emigration, and Jihad): 1- "Their Lord gives them good tidings of mercy from Him" and makes them benefit from it (يُبَشِّرُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنْهُ‌). 2- "He grants them His pleasure and approval" (wa Ridwan). 3- "He grants them gardens in which for them is enduring pleasure." (وَ جَنَّاتٍ لَهُمْ فِيها نَعِيمٌ مُقِيمٌ‌).

In the next verse, for further emphasis, It adds: "Abiding therein forever." (خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً). "Indeed, with Allah is a great reward" which He grants to His servants in return for their deeds (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ‌).

Two points must be noted here

1- Distortion of History:

As we read in the occasion of revelation for the above verses, according to the narration cited in many of the most famous Sunni books, these verses were revealed regarding Ali عليه السلام and the statement of his virtues, even though their concept is general and broad (and we have repeatedly stated that occasions of revelation do not limit the concepts of the verses).

However, since some Sunni commentators are reluctant to establish significant virtues for Ali عليه السلام—even though they consider him their fourth great leader—it seems they fear that if they submit to the evidence proving the extraordinary merits of Ali عليه السلام, the Shi'a population might rise against them and place them in a predicament as to why they prioritized others over Ali عليه السلام. Consequently, they often turn a blind eye to historical realities and, as much as possible, attempt to criticize the chains of transmission (isnad) of such Hadiths. If they find no room to attack the chain, they try to distort its meaning. Such prejudices, unfortunately, continue even in our era, and even some of their intellectual scholars have not remained free from this.

I do not forget a conversation I had with one of the Sunni scholars. When the discussion turned to such Hadiths, he made a strange statement. He said, "In my opinion, the Shi'a can prove all the principles and branches of their school from 'our sources and books,' because there are enough Hadiths favoring the Shi'a school in our books! But to rid himself of all these sources at once, he said, 'In my opinion, our predecessors were gullible people and included every Hadith they heard in their books, and we cannot simply accept what they wrote!'" (Of course, his statement included their Sahih books and primary authoritative Musnads as well!).

I told him, "The scholarly method is not for a person to accept a school beforehand based on a series of inheritances and then consider every Hadith that agrees with it as authentic and every Hadith that does not align with it as a product of the 'gullibility of the predecessors,' even if it is a reliable Hadith. How good it would be if, instead of this way of thinking, you chose another path: first empty yourself of any inherited belief, then sit before logical evidence, and only then choose a belief!"

Observe carefully why and for what reason the famous and well-known Hadiths that report the high status of Ali عليه السلام and prove his superiority over others have been treated with such unkindness, or rather attacked with a barrage of objections, and sometimes consigned to oblivion as if these Hadiths have no external existence at all!! Given what we have said above, let us examine the words of the famous commentator, the author of Al-Manar:

In the occasion of revelation for the above verses, he has completely set aside the famous narration mentioned earlier and considered another narration authentic that does not align with the content of the verses at all—one that should be discarded as a Hadith contradicting the Quran. It is the Hadith narrated from Nu'man ibn Bashir, who says: "I was sitting near the pulpit of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله among a group of companions. One of them said, 'After Islam, I do not consider any deed higher than providing water to the pilgrims.' Another said, 'Maintaining al-Masjid al-Haram is higher than any deed.' A third said, 'Jihad in the way of Allah is better than what you said.' Umar forbade them from arguing and said, 'Do not raise your voices near the pulpit of the Messenger of Allah'—and that day was Friday—'but when I have performed the Friday prayer, I will go to the Messenger of Allah and ask him about the matter you differed upon.'" (After the prayer, he went to the Messenger of Allah and asked), and at this point, the above verses were revealed. [1]

This narration is incompatible with the verses under discussion in several ways, and we know that any narration that contradicts the Quran must be cast aside, because:

Firstly: In the above verses, the comparison is not between "Jihad" and "Siqayat al-Hajj" and "Maintenance" of al-Masjid al-Haram. Rather, on one side of the comparison are "Siqayat al-Hajj" and "Maintenance," and on the other side are "Faith in Allah and the Last Day and Jihad." This shows that individuals were comparing those deeds they had performed during the Age of Ignorance with faith and Jihad, and the Quran explicitly states these two are not equal—not a comparison of "Jihad" with "Maintenance" and "Siqayat al-Hajj" (note carefully).

Secondly: The phrase "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" shows that the deeds of the first group were coupled with wrongdoing (zulm). This is the case if they occurred in a state of polytheism (shirk), as the Quran says: "Indeed, shirk is a great wrongdoing." [2] If the comparison were between "Faith" and "Siqayat al-Hajj coupled with faith and Jihad," the phrase "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" would have no meaning.

Thirdly: The second verse under discussion, which says those who believed, emigrated, and strived have a higher rank, implies they are superior to those who did not have faith, emigration, and Jihad. This does not reconcile with the Hadith of Nu'man, because the interlocutors according to that Hadith were all believers and perhaps had participated in emigration and Jihad.

Fourthly: In the preceding verses, the discussion was about the polytheists' attempt to maintain mosques ("It is not for the polytheists to maintain the mosques of Allah..."), and the verses under discussion, which follow them, pursue the same subject. This shows that the maintenance of al-Masjid al-Haram and Siqayat al-Hajj in a state of polytheism is the subject of these verses, which does not align with Nu'man's narration.

The only point that might be raised against these arguments is that the expression "greater in rank" (a'zamu darajatan) indicates that both sides of the "comparison" are good deeds, even if one is superior to the other.

However, the answer to this is clear, because the superlative form (ism al-tafdil) is often used in cases where one side of the comparison possesses virtue and the other side is "zero." For example, it is often said "Arriving late is better than never arriving." The meaning of this is not that "never arriving, crashing, and being destroyed" is a good thing but "arriving late" is better; or as we read in the Quran: "And settlement is best" (Surah An-Nisa, 128).

"Settlement is better than war." The meaning of this sentence is not that war is a good thing. Or we read: "And a believing slave is better than a polytheist" (Surah Al-Baqarah, 221). Does a polytheist have any "good" or virtue? And in this very Surah At-Tawbah, verse 108, we read: "A mosque founded on righteousness from the first day is more worthy for you to stand in." (Than Masjid al-Dirar, the mosque built by hypocrites to cause division).

This is while we know that worship in Masjid al-Dirar has no worthiness at all. Similar expressions are abundant in the Quran, Arabic speech, and other languages.

From the sum of what has been said, we conclude that the narration of "Nu'man ibn Bashir," because it contradicts the content of the Quran, must be set aside. What aligns with the apparent meaning of the verses is the famous Hadith we mentioned at the beginning under the occasion of revelation, and this is a virtue for the great leader of Islam, Ali عليه السلام.

May Allah keep us all steadfast in following the truth and following such leaders, and grant us open eyes, ears, and a mind free from prejudice.

2- From the above verses, it is understood that the station of "Ridwan" (Pleasure/Approval), which is among the greatest gifts and stations Allah grants to believers and mujahideen, is something other than the gardens of Paradise and its eternal blessings, and other than the vast mercy of the Lord. (The explanation of this subject will come, Allah willing, under verse 72 of this same Surah in the commentary on the phrase "And approval from Allah is greater").

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 10, p. 215. [^2]: Surah Luqman, verse 13. [1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [2]: For more explanation regarding this Hadith and its source specifications, refer to the book Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 3, pp. 122 to 127.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَكُمْ وَإِخْوَٰنَكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ إِنِ ٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْكُفْرَ عَلَى ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُم مِّنكُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٢٣)
O you who have faith! Do not befriend your fathers and brothers if they prefer faithlessness to faith. Those of you who befriend them—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (23)
قُلْ إِن كَانَ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَآؤُكُمْ وَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُكُمْ وَعَشِيرَتُكُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلٌ ٱقْتَرَفْتُمُوهَا وَتِجَـٰرَةٌ تَخْشَوْنَ كَسَادَهَا وَمَسَـٰكِنُ تَرْضَوْنَهَآ أَحَبَّ إِلَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَجِهَادٍ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ فَتَرَبَّصُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٢٤)
Say, ‘If your fathers and your sons, your brethren, your spouses, and your kinsfolk, the possessions that you have acquired, the business you fear may suffer, and the dwellings you are fond of, are dearer to you than Allah and His Apostle and to waging jihad in His way, then wait until Allah issues His edict, and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (24)

Commentary

Everything Sacrificed for the Goal and for Allah

The final temptation and excuse that might have arisen for a group of Muslims regarding the command to fight the idolaters—and according to some commentaries, did indeed arise—was their concern that, on one hand, among the polytheists and idolaters were their close relatives and kin. Sometimes a father had become Muslim while the son remained in polytheism; at other times, the sons had chosen the path of Monotheism while the fathers remained in the darkness of Shirk. The same applied to brothers, spouses, and the wider clan and tribe. If they were to fight all polytheists, they would have to renounce their own relatives and tribe!

On the other hand, their capital and trade were largely in the hands of the polytheists, whose coming and going to Mecca brought prosperity to their commerce.

Thirdly, they possessed comfortable and relatively well-built houses in Mecca which, in the event of a conflict with the polytheists, might face destruction, or lose their value and utility if the Hajj ceremonies were suspended by the polytheists.

The above verses address such individuals and provide a clear, decisive response. First, it says:

"O you who have believed, do not take your fathers or your brothers as allies [and supporters] if they prefer disbelief over belief" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا آبَاءَكُمْ وَإِخْوَانَكُمْ أَوْلِيَاءَ إِنِ اسْتَحَبُّوا الْكُفْرَ عَلَى الْإِيمَانِ).

Then, as an emphasis, it adds: "And whoever of you allies with them, then those are the wrongdoers" (وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَأُولَئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ).

What injustice is greater than for a person, by forming bonds of friendship with strangers and enemies of the Truth, to wrong both themselves, the society to which they belong, and the Messenger of Allah!

In the following verse, due to the extraordinary importance of the subject, the same matter is expressed with more detail, emphasis, and warning. Addressing the Prophet, He says:

"Say, [O Muhammad], 'If your fathers, your sons, your brothers, your wives, your relatives, wealth which you have obtained, commerce wherein you fear decline, and dwellings with which you are pleased are more beloved to you than Allah and His Messenger and jihad in His cause, then wait until Allah executes His command'" (قُلْ إِنْ كَانَ آبَاؤُكُمْ وَأَبْنَاؤُكُمْ وَإِخْوَانُكُمْ وَأَزْوَاجُكُمْ وَعَشِيرَتُكُمْ وَأَمْوَالٌ اقْتَرَفْتُمُوهَا وَتِجَارَةٌ تَخْشَوْنَ كَسَادَهَا وَمَسَاكِنُ تَرْضَوْنَهَا أَحَبَّ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَجِهَادٍ فِي سَبِيلِهِ فَتَرَبَّصُوا حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَ اللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِ).

And since preferring these matters over the pleasure of Allah and Jihad is a form of disobedience and manifest defiance (fisq), and those enamored by the glitter of material life are not worthy of divine guidance, He adds at the end of the verse: "And Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people" (وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفَاسِقِينَ). In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim al-Qummi, it is narrated:

"When the Commander of the Faithful (Ali عليه السلام) announced that no polytheist should enter the Sacred Mosque after that, the Quraysh became deeply distressed and said: 'Our trade is gone, our families are lost, and our houses are ruined.' Then Allah revealed: 'Say (O Muhammad): If your fathers...'"

In the verses above, the main outlines of true faith are drawn, distinguishing it from faith tainted by polytheism and hypocrisy. The boundary between true believers and those weak in faith is clearly defined. It explicitly states that if the eight assets of material life—four of which relate to the closest kin (fathers, sons, brothers, and spouses), one to the social group and tribe, another to accumulated wealth and capital, another to the prosperity of trade and business, and finally one to comfortable dwellings—are more precious and valuable to a person than Allah, His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Jihad, and obedience to His command, to the extent that they are unwilling to sacrifice them for the religion, then it is clear that true and complete faith has not been realized.

The reality and spirit of faith, with all its values, manifest only when one does not hesitate in such sacrifice and renunciation.

Furthermore, those who are not ready for such sacrifice are actually wronging themselves and their society. They will eventually fall into the very things they fear; for a nation that is not ready for such sacrifices at the crossroads of history and moments of destiny will sooner or later face defeat. The very relatives, wealth, and capital for which they abandoned Jihad out of attachment will be endangered and will be destroyed in the clutches of the enemy.

Two points must be noted here

1- What we read in the above verses does not mean severing the bonds of friendship and affection with relatives, ignoring economic assets, or encouraging the abandonment of human emotions. Rather, the meaning is that at the crossroads, the love for wife, children, wealth, status, house, and family must not prevent the execution of Allah's command or the inclination toward Jihad, nor should it hinder a person from their sacred goal.

Therefore, if a person is not at such a crossroads, observing both is necessary for them. In verse 15 of Surah Luqman, regarding idol-worshipping parents, we read: "But if they endeavor to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them but accompany them in [this] world with appropriate kindness" (وَإِنْ جَاهَدَاكَ عَلَىٰ أَنْ تُشْرِكَ بِي مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا وَصَاحِبْهُمَا فِي الدُّنْيَا مَعْرُوفًا).

2- One interpretation of the phrase "then wait until Allah executes His command" (فَتَرَبَّصُوا حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَ اللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِ) is what we mentioned above: a threat from Allah to those who prioritize their material interests over His pleasure. Since this threat is expressed in general terms, its effect is greater and more terrifying. It is exactly like a person saying to a subordinate: "If you refuse to do your duty, I too will do what I must."

There is another possibility in interpreting this phrase: Allah is saying, "If you are not ready for such sacrifice, Allah will grant the command of victory and triumph to His Prophet through a way He knows, and He will help him in a manner He wills." This is similar to what we read in verse 54 of Surah al-Ma'idah: "O you who have believed, whoever of you should revert from his religion - Allah will bring forth [in place of them] a people He will love and who will love Him..." (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِينِهِ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ).

The Past and Present Depend on This Command

3- Some might think that what appears in the verses above is specific to the early Muslims and belongs to past history, but this is a great mistake. These verses encompass not only yesterday but also the today and tomorrow of Muslims.

If they do not possess firm faith and readiness for Jihad, sacrifice, and—if necessary—migration (Hijrah); if they prioritize their material interests over Allah's pleasure and withhold sacrifice due to excessive attachments to wife, children, wealth, and the luxuries of life, their future is dark. Not only the future, but they will be in danger today as well. All their past heritage and honors will vanish, their vital resources will fall into the hands of others, and life will lose its meaning for them, for "Life is faith and Jihad in the shadow of faith!" The above verses should be taught as a motto to all Muslim children and youth, so that the spirit of sacrifice, gallantry, and faith is revived in them, enabling them to safeguard their heritage.

Ayahs

لَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى مَوَاطِنَ كَثِيرَةٍ ۙ وَيَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ ۙ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ فَلَمْ تُغْنِ عَنكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُم مُّدْبِرِينَ (٢٥)
Allah has certainly helped you in many situations, and on the day of Hunayn, when your great number impressed you, but it did not avail you in any way, and the earth became narrow for you in spite of its expanse, whereupon you turned your backs [to flee]. (25)
ثُمَّ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَعَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَأَنزَلَ جُنُودًا لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا وَعَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٦)
Then Allah sent down His composure upon His Apostle and upon the faithful, and He sent down hosts you did not see, and He punished the faithless, and that is the requital of the faithless. (26)
ثُمَّ يَتُوبُ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٧)
Then Allah shall turn clemently after that to whomever He wishes. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (27)

Commentary

A Vast Multitude Alone Accomplishes Nothing

In the preceding verses, we saw that Allah invites the Muslims to all-encompassing sacrifice in the path of Jihad and the uprooting of shirk (polytheism) and idol-worship. He issues a stern warning to those whose souls are so consumed by love for wives, children, kinsfolk, and wealth that they are unwilling to engage in sacrifice and Jihad.

Following that, in the verses under discussion, He points to a crucial matter that every leader must bring to the attention of his followers at sensitive moments:

If the love of wealth and children prevents a group of weak-faith individuals from the great Jihad they faced against the polytheists, the group of true believers should not allow worry to enter their hearts. This is because Allah did not leave them alone on those days when their numbers were few (such as the battlefield of Badr), nor did the vast multitude avail them on the day when their numbers were eye-catching (such as the battlefield of Hunayn). Rather, in every situation, it was Allah’s help and His assistance that brought about their victory.

Therefore, in the first verse, He says: "Allah has already helped you on many battlefields" (لَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ اللَّهُ فِي مَواطِنَ كَثِيرَةٍ). Mawatin is the plural of mawtin, meaning a place a person chooses for permanent or temporary residence. However, one of its meanings is "battlefields," fitting because warriors reside there for a short or long duration.

Then He adds: "and on the day of Hunayn, when your great numbers impressed you" (وَ يَوْمَ حُنَيْنٍ إِذْ أَعْجَبَتْكُمْ كَثْرَتُكُمْ).

The number of the Islamic army in this battle has been recorded as twelve thousand, and some have written ten thousand or eight thousand; however, the famous and authentic narrations confirm twelve thousand. Such a number was unprecedented in any Islamic battle until that day, to the extent that some Muslims boastfully said: "We shall never be defeated today!" (لن نغلب اليوم).

But as we shall mention, God willing, in the description of the Ghazwa (Battle) of Hunayn, this vast multitude—a group of whom were new converts and undisciplined—caused the army to flee and suffer an initial defeat. Ultimately, however, Allah’s grace saved them.

This initial defeat was such that the Quran adds: "and the earth, despite its vastness, became straitened for you" (وَ ضاقَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ).

"Then you turned back in flight" (ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ).

At this moment, when the army of Islam had scattered around the land of Hunayn, and only a small group remained with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was severely concerned and distressed by their flight, "Then Allah sent down His tranquility (Sakina) upon His Messenger and upon the believers" (ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى‌ رَسُولِهِ وَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ). "And He sent down forces which you did not see" to strengthen and aid you (وَ أَنْزَلَ جُنُوداً لَمْ تَرَوْها).

As we mentioned under the verses related to the "Ghazwa of Badr," the descent of this invisible divine army was to consolidate and strengthen the spirit of the Muslims and to create a force of steadfastness and endurance in their souls and hearts, not that the angels and unseen forces physically participated in the fighting [1]. Finally, He describes the ultimate result of the Battle of Hunayn: "And He punished those who disbelieved" (some were killed, some captured, and a group fled so far that they were out of reach of the Islamic army).

[1]: For further explanation, refer to this same volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under verses 9 to 12 of Surah Al-Anfal.

(وَ عَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

"And that is the recompense of the disbelievers" (وَ ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الْكافِرِينَ).

Yet, despite this, He left the doors of repentance and return open to the captives and those who fled from among the disbelievers, should they wish to return to Allah and accept the religion of truth. Thus, in the final verse under discussion, He says: "Then Allah will accept the repentance of whom He wills after that" (and whom He deems worthy and ready for true repentance) (ثُمَّ يَتُوبُ اللَّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ). The phrase yatubu (accepts repentance), mentioned in the present/future tense (Mudari') which denotes continuity, implies that the doors of repentance and return remain open to them.

"For Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." He never closes the doors of repentance to anyone, nor does He make anyone despair of His vast mercy (وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Now that the concise commentary of the verses has become clear, attention must be paid to the important points contained within this discussion:

[Points to be noted here]

1- "The Instructive Battle of Hunayn"

"Hunayn" is a land near the city of Ta'if, and because this battle took place there, it became known as the Ghazwa of Hunayn, and in the Quran, it is referred to as "the day of Hunayn." Its other names are the Ghazwa of Awtas and the Ghazwa of Hawazin (Awtas being the name of a land in that vicinity, and Hawazin being the name of one of the tribes involved in the battle against the Muslims).

This "Ghazwa" began when, according to Ibn al-Athir in Al-Kamil, the great tribe of Hawazin heard of the conquest of Mecca. Their leader, Malik ibn Awf, gathered them and told them that Muhammad might fight them after the conquest of Mecca, and they said it is better to strike first before he battles us.

When this news reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, he ordered the Muslims to prepare to move toward the land of Hawazin [1]. Although there is almost no disagreement among historians regarding the general flow of this battle, various narrations exist regarding its details that are not entirely consistent. What we summarize below is according to the narration brought by the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan.

It was at the end of the month of Ramadan or in the month of Shawwal in the eighth year of the Hijra that the leaders of the Hawazin tribe gathered with Malik ibn Awf, bringing their wealth, children, and women with them so that no one would think of fleeing during the clash with the Muslims. In this manner, they entered the land of Awtas.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله tied the great banner of the army and gave it to Ali عليه السلام, and all those who were standard-bearers for sections of the Islamic army for the conquest of Mecca moved toward the field of Hunayn with those same banners by the Prophet's command. The Prophet learned that Safwan ibn Umayya had a large number of coats of mail in his possession; he sent for him and requested one hundred coats of mail as a loan. Safwan asked: "Is it truly a loan or a seizure?" The Prophet

[1]: Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh, Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 2, p. 261.

صلى الله عليه وآله said: "It is a loan that we guarantee and will return safely." Safwan gave one hundred coats of mail as a loan to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and personally accompanied him.

Two thousand Muslims who had accepted Islam at the conquest of Mecca, plus ten thousand soldiers of Islam who had come with the Prophet for the conquest of Mecca—totaling twelve thousand—moved toward the battlefield.

Malik ibn Awf, who was a daring and courageous man, ordered his tribe to break their sword sheaths and lie in ambush in the mountain crevices, surrounding valleys, and among the trees along the path of the Islamic army. He ordered them to launch a sudden attack and crush the army when the Muslims arrived there in the darkness of early morning.

He added: "Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله has not yet faced real fighting men to taste the flavor of defeat!"

When the Prophet performed the morning prayer with his companions, he ordered the descent into the land of Hunayn. It was at this moment that the Hawazin army suddenly showered the Muslims with arrows from every side. The group at the front of the army (among whom were the new converts from Mecca) fled, and this caused the rest of the army to panic and flee.

At this point, Allah left them to themselves against the enemies and temporarily withdrew His support because they were arrogant about their vast numbers, and the signs of defeat became apparent among them.

However, Ali عليه السلام, who was the standard-bearer of the army, stood with a small number against the enemy and continued to fight.

(At this time, the Prophet was in the heart of the army). Abbas, the Prophet's uncle, and a few others from the Banu Hashim—not exceeding nine people in total, with the tenth being Ayman, the son of Umm Ayman—surrounded the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

As the front of the army passed by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله while fleeing and retreating, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered Abbas, who had a loud and resonant voice, to immediately climb a nearby hill and shout to the Muslims: "O assembly of Muhajirun and Ansar! O companions of Surah Al-Baqarah! O people of the Pledge of the Tree (Bay'at al-Shajarah)! Where are you fleeing? Here is the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله!"

When the Muslims heard Abbas's voice, they returned, saying: "Labbayk, Labbayk!" (Here I am!). The Ansar, in particular, were the first to return, and they launched a fierce attack from every side against the enemy army. With the help of the Lord, they continued their advance such that the Hawazin tribe scattered terrifyingly in every direction, and the Muslims pursued them continuously.

About one hundred of the enemy army were killed, their wealth fell to the Muslims as spoils, and a group was taken captive [1]. At the end of this historical account, we read that after the war ended, representatives of the Hawazin tribe came to the Prophet and accepted Islam. The Prophet showed them great kindness, and even Malik ibn Awf, their leader and elder, accepted Islam. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned his wealth and captives to him and appointed him as the leader of the Muslims of his tribe.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 5, pp. 17-19.

In truth, the major factor in the Muslims' initial defeat, besides the arrogance they felt due to their large numbers, was the presence of two thousand new converts, among whom were naturally some hypocrites, some seeking war booty, and some aimless individuals. Their flight affected the rest.

The factor of the final victory was the steadfastness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Ali عليه السلام, and a small group of companions, along with the remembrance of previous covenants, faith in Allah, and attention to His special support.

2- Who Fled?

There is no doubt that in this field, the vast majority fled at the beginning. The number of those who remained is recorded as ten in the above narration, some say only four, and some say a maximum of about one hundred.

Since, according to some famous narrations, the first caliphs were also among those who fled, some Sunni commentators try to present this flight as a natural occurrence.

The author of Al-Manar says here: "When the shower of enemy arrows targeted the Muslims, the group that had joined the Islamic army from Mecca—among whom were hypocrites, weak-faith individuals, and booty-seekers—fled and turned their backs to the field. The rest of the army 'naturally' became anxious and distressed; they too fled according to 'habit' and not out of fear! This is a natural matter; when one group flees, the rest become unsettled without realizing it. Therefore, their flight did not mean abandoning the help of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or leaving him in the hands of the disbelievers, which would merit Allah's wrath!" [1] We offer no commentary on this statement and leave the judgment to the readers.

It is also necessary to mention that in Sahih Bukhari, the most authoritative Sunni source, when discussing the rout and flight of the Muslims in this field, it narrates:

"Suddenly Umar ibn al-Khattab was among the people, and I said: 'What is the matter with the people?' He said: 'The command of Allah.' Then the people returned to the Messenger of Allah..." [2]

However, if we set aside prejudices and pay attention to the Quran, we see that it does not distinguish between groups among those who fled; rather, it censures them all equally for turning their backs.

We do not know what difference there is between the phrase "ثُمَّ وَلَّيْتُمْ مُدْبِرِينَ" (Then you turned back in flight) which we read in the above verses, and the other phrase in verse 16 of Surah Al-Anfal, where it says: "And whoever turns his back to them on such a day, unless maneuvering for war or joining a company, has certainly incurred the wrath of Allah" (وَ مَنْ يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ إِلَّا مُتَحَرِّفاً لِقِتالٍ أَوْ مُتَحَيِّزاً إِلى‌ فِئَةٍ فَقَدْ باءَ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ).

Therefore, if we place these two verses side by side, it is proven that the Muslims on that day—except for a small group—committed a great error, though they later repented and returned.

3- Faith and Tranquility (Sakina)

"Sakina" is originally from the root sukun, meaning a state of calm and assurance that removes all doubt, hesitation, fear, and terror from a person, making them steadfast in the face of difficult and complex events. "Sakina" has a close relationship with faith; that is, it is born of faith. When believers remember the infinite power of Allah and consider His grace and mercy, a wave of hope arises in their hearts. The fact that we see "Sakina" interpreted as faith in some narrations [1] and as a "heavenly breeze" in the form of a human in others [2] all returns to this same meaning.

In the Holy Quran, Surah Al-Fath, verse 4, we read: "It is He who sent down tranquility into the hearts of the believers that they would increase in faith along with their [present] faith" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدادُوا إِيماناً مَعَ إِيمانِهِمْ).

In any case, this extraordinary psychological state is a divine and heavenly gift in the light of which a person absorbs the most difficult events and feels a world of calm and steadfastness within themselves.

It is noteworthy that the Quran in the verses under discussion does not say: "Then Allah sent down His tranquility upon His Messenger and upon you," even though all the preceding sentences are in the form of direct address using the pronoun "you" (kum). Instead, it says "upon the believers" (عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ),

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 114. [2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 201.

indicating that the hypocrites and those who were seekers of the world on the battlefield of Jihad had no share of this "Sakina" and tranquility; this gift is granted only to the people of faith.

We read in narrations: This heavenly breeze was with the Prophets and Messengers of Allah [1]. For this reason, in events where anyone else would lose control, they possessed a calm spirit, a firm resolve, an iron will, and were unshakable.

The descent of "Sakina" upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the field of Hunayn—as we said—was to remove the anxiety that had gripped the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله due to the flight of the multitude; otherwise, he was as firm and steadfast as a mountain in this scene, as were Ali عليه السلام and a small group of Muslims!

4- In the above verses, reference is made to Allah's help to the Muslims in "many battlefields" (mawatin kathira). Regarding the number of battles in which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله personally fought, or was with the Muslims but did not personally fight, as well as the fields where the Islamic army faced the enemy but the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was not present, there is much discussion among historians. However, from some narrations reached to us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it is understood that their number reached eighty.

It is narrated in the book Al-Kafi that one of the Abbasid caliphs had vowed that if he were saved from poisoning, he would give "much wealth" (malun kathir) to the poor. When he recovered, the jurists around him disagreed on the amount, and none had a clear proof. Finally, he asked the ninth Imam, "Hazrat Muhammad ibn Ali al-Naqi" عليه السلام.

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 112.

He said: "'Much' is eighty." When they asked for the reason, the Imam pointed to the above verse and said: "We counted the number of battlefields of Islam and disbelief in which the Muslims were victorious, and the number was eighty." [1]

5- A point that is of utmost necessity for Muslims to pay attention to today is to gain experience from events such as the incident of "Hunayn." They must know that the multitude of their numbers and their vast population should never be a source of arrogance. A vast multitude alone accomplishes nothing; the important issue is the existence of disciplined, faithful, and determined individuals, even if they are a small group. Just as a small group changed the fate of the Battle of Hunayn after the vast multitude—untested and undisciplined—had caused rout and defeat. The important thing is that individuals be nurtured with the spirit of faith, endurance, and sacrifice such that their hearts become the center of divine "Sakina," standing firm and calm like a mountain against the harshest storms of life.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلَا يَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ ٱلْحَرَامَ بَعْدَ عَامِهِمْ هَـٰذَا ۚ وَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ عَيْلَةً فَسَوْفَ يُغْنِيكُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ إِن شَآءَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٨)
O you who have faith! The polytheists are indeed unclean: so let them not approach the Holy Mosque after this year. Should you fear poverty, Allah will enrich you out of His grace, if He wishes. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (28)

Commentary

Polytheists Have No Right to Enter Masjid al-Haram

We have stated that one of the four commands conveyed by Ali عليه السلام to the people of Mecca during the Hajj ceremonies in the ninth year of the Hijra was that, from the following year, no polytheist (mushrik) would have the right to enter Masjid al-Haram or perform the circumambulation (Tawaf) of the Kaaba. The above verse refers to this subject and its underlying philosophy.

First, it says: "O you who have believed, indeed the polytheists are unclean; so let them not approach Masjid al-Haram after this, their [final] year" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ بَعْدَ عامِهِمْ هذا).

Is this verse evidence for the ritual impurity (najasa) of polytheists in the technical jurisprudential (fiqhi) sense or not?

There is discussion among jurists and commentators, and to investigate the meaning of the verse, it is necessary to first examine the word "najas" (نَجَس).

"Najas" (rhyming with hawas) has an infinitive meaning, and it is also used as an adjective for emphasis and exaggeration.

Raghib in the book Al-Mufradat says regarding the meaning of this word: "Najasa" and "najas" mean any kind of filth, and it is of two types: one is physical filth, and the other is internal (spiritual) filth.

And Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan says: Anything that human nature finds repulsive is called "najas."

For this reason, this term is used in many cases where the concept of ritual impurity or physical contamination does not exist. For example, the Arabs call ailments that are difficult to treat "najas"; base and wicked individuals are described with this word; and the aging and exhaustion of the body is also called "najas."

From this, it becomes clear that based on this verse alone, one cannot judge whether the application of the word "najas" to polytheists is because their physical bodies are contaminated—like the contamination of blood, urine, or wine—or because they possess a type of internal pollution due to the belief in idolatry. Thus, this verse cannot be used as the sole argument to prove the ritual impurity of disbelievers; rather, other evidences must be sought.

Then, in response to short-sighted individuals who claimed that if the polytheists were barred from Masjid al-Haram, their business and trade would lose prosperity and they would become poor and destitute, it says: "And if you fear poverty, Allah will enrich you from His favor if He wills" (وَ إِنْ خِفْتُمْ عَيْلَةً فَسَوْفَ يُغْنِيكُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ إِنْ شاءَ). Just as He enriched them in the most excellent way, and with the expansion of Islam during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, a flood of pilgrims to the House of God moved toward Mecca. This continues to this day, and Mecca—which geographically is in the most unsuitable conditions, situated among dry mountains and waterless, barren rocky lands—has become a very prosperous city and a major center of exchange and trade.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Wise" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ).

Every command He gives is based on wisdom, and He is fully aware and informed of its future results.

Ayah

قَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَلَا بِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ ٱلْحَقِّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا۟ ٱلْجِزْيَةَ عَن يَدٍ وَهُمْ صَـٰغِرُونَ (٢٩)
Fight those who do not have faith in Allah nor [believe] in the Last Day, nor forbid what Allah and His Apostle have forbidden, nor practise the true religion, from among those who were given the Book, until they pay the tribute out of hand, degraded. (29)

Commentary

Our Duty Toward the People of the Book

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the duty of Muslims toward "idolaters." The verse under discussion and the subsequent verses clarify the obligation of Muslims regarding the "People of the Book" (Ahl al-Kitab).

In these verses, Islam effectively establishes a set of intermediary laws for them, positioned between "Muslims" and "polytheists." This is because the People of the Book bear a resemblance to Muslims in that they follow a heavenly religion, yet in another sense, they resemble polytheists. For this reason, Islam does not permit their killing, whereas it allowed it regarding the idolaters who persisted in resistance; this was because the program for idolaters was to uproot polytheism from the face of the earth.

However, Islam allows for reconciliation with the People of the Book only if they are willing to live peacefully with Muslims as a healthy religious minority, respect Islam, and refrain from instigating actions against Muslims or spreading anti-Islamic propaganda. Another sign of their submission to this type of peaceful coexistence is their acceptance of "Jizyah," which is a form of poll tax. They are to pay a modest annual amount under this title to the Islamic government, the limits and conditions of which will be specified in future discussions, God willing.

Otherwise, Islam issues the permission to struggle and fight against them. The reason for this firm stance is clarified through three phrases within the verse under discussion.

First, it says: "Fight those who do not believe in Allah or the Last Day" (قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَلَا بِالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ). But how is it that the People of the Book, such as the Jews and Christians, do not believe in Allah and the Day of Resurrection, while we outwardly see that they accept both God and the Hereafter? This is because their faith is mixed with superstitions and baseless notions.

Regarding faith in the Origin and the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid), firstly, a group of Jews—as will be mentioned in subsequent verses—considered "Ezra" ('Uzayr) to be the son of God, and Christians generally believe in the divinity of Christ and the Trinity (three gods).

Secondly, as also pointed out in future verses, they were entangled in polytheism in worship; they practically worshipped their scholars and religious leaders, sought forgiveness for sins—which belongs exclusively to Allah—from them, and recognized divine laws that those leaders had distorted.

As for their belief in the Hereafter, it is a distorted belief; for as inferred from their words, they limit the Resurrection to a purely spiritual one. Therefore, both their faith in the Origin and the Hereafter is flawed.

Then, it points to their second characteristic: they do not submit to Divine prohibitions and do not consider forbidden what Allah and His Messenger have forbidden (وَلَا يُحَرِّمُونَ مَا حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ).

It is possible that "His Messenger" refers to Moses عليه السلام and Jesus عليه السلام, as they are not practically faithful even to the prohibitions of their own religions, and they commit many acts that were forbidden in the law of Moses عليه السلام or Jesus عليه السلام. Not only do they commit them, but they sometimes even rule them to be permissible!

It is also possible that "His Messenger" refers to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. This would mean that the command for Jihad against them was issued because they do not submit to what Allah has forbidden through the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and commit all sorts of sins.

This second possibility seems more likely, and evidence for it is verse 33 of this same Surah, the commentary of which will follow shortly, where it says: "It is He who has sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ).

Furthermore, when the word "His Messenger" (رسوله) is used absolutely in the Quran, it refers to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Moreover, if the reference were to their own prophets, it should not have been in the singular form, but rather in the dual or plural, as they had their own messenger or messengers, just as it appears in Surah Yunus, verse 13: "And their messengers came to them with clear proofs" (وَجَاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ). (Similar expressions are seen in other verses of the Quran as well).

It might be argued that in this case, the verse would be stating the obvious, as it is clear that non-Muslims do not accept all the prohibitions of the Islamic faith.

However, it must be noted that the purpose of mentioning these traits is to state the reason why Jihad against them is permissible. That is, Jihad is allowed because they have not accepted Islamic prohibitions and are tainted by many sins; if they resist and cease to be a healthy minority, they can be fought.

Finally, it points to their third characteristic, saying: "And [they] do not acknowledge the religion of truth" (وَلَا يَدِينُونَ دِينَ الْحَقِّ).

Again, regarding this phrase, the two previous possibilities exist, but it is apparent that "the religion of truth" refers to the religion of Islam, which is mentioned a few verses later.

Mentioning this phrase after mentioning their lack of belief in Islamic prohibitions is a case of mentioning the general after the specific. That is, it first points to their being tainted by many prohibitions, as this pollution is particularly conspicuous: addiction to wine, usury, eating pork, and committing many forms of sexual licentiousness that are increasing and spreading among them day by day.

Then it says that fundamentally, they do not submit to the religion of truth; meaning their religions have deviated from the original path, they have consigned many truths to oblivion, and have replaced them with a mass of superstitions. For this reason, they must either accept the evolutionary revolution of Islam and reconstruct their religious intellectual world, or at the very least, exist as a healthy minority alongside Muslims and accept the conditions of peaceful coexistence.

After mentioning these three attributes, which in reality justify the struggle against them, it says: "This ruling is concerning those who were given the Scripture" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ).

The word "min" (from) here is what is termed "bayaniyyah" (explanatory) rather than "tab'idiyyah" (partitive). In other words, the Quran says that all followers of previous heavenly books have (unfortunately) fallen into these religious deviations, and this ruling applies to all of them.

Then, it states the difference between them and the polytheists and idolaters in one phrase, saying: "This struggle will continue until they pay the Jizyah while they are in a state of submission" (حَتَّىٰ يُعْطُوا الْجِزْيَةَ عَنْ يَدٍ وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ).

"Jizyah" is derived from the root "jaza" (recompense), meaning the money taken from non-Muslims who come under the protection of the Islamic government. This naming is because they pay it as a "recompense for the protection of their property and lives" to the Islamic government. (This is what is inferred from the words of Raghib in the book Al-Mufradat). "Saghir" is from the root "saghar" (smallness), meaning one who is content with being small/humble. What is meant in the above verse is that the payment of Jizyah must be as a sign of humility before the law of Islam and the Quran; in other words, it should be a sign of peaceful coexistence and acceptance of the status of a healthy and respected minority before the governing majority.

The fact that some commentators have interpreted it as humiliation, insult, degradation, or mockery of the People of the Book is neither supported by the linguistic meaning of the word, nor is it consistent with the spirit of Islamic teachings, nor does it align with other instructions received regarding the manner of treating religious minorities.

Another noteworthy point is that although only "Jizyah" is mentioned among the conditions of "Dhimmah" (protection) in the above verse, the expression "while they are in a state of submission" (وَهُمْ صَاغِرُونَ) is a brief allusion to the other conditions of Dhimmah. From this, it is understood that they should not, for instance, engage in propaganda against Muslims in an Islamic environment, nor cooperate with their enemies, nor create obstacles in the path of their progress, as these matters are inconsistent with the spirit of humility, submission, and cooperation.

What is Jizyah?

"Jizyah" is a type of Islamic poll tax applied to individuals, not to property or land; in other words, it is an "annual per capita tax."

Some believe its original root is non-Arabic, taken from "Kezit," an ancient Persian word meaning a tax collected to strengthen the army. However, many believe this word is purely Arabic and, as previously mentioned, is derived from the root "jaza," because the said tax is the recompense for the security that the Islamic government provides for religious minorities.

"Jizyah" existed before Islam as well. Some believe the first person to collect "Jizyah" was Anushirvan, the Sassanid king. Even if this is not certain, at the very least, Anushirvan was someone who took Jizyah from his own nation; from everyone over twenty and under fifty years of age who were not government employees, he collected a poll tax of 12, 8, 6, or 4 dirhams per person, depending on their status.

The primary philosophy of this tax has been described as follows: defending the existence, independence, and security of a country is the duty of all its citizens. Therefore, whenever a group practically rises to perform this duty, and another group cannot join the ranks of soldiers due to being occupied with business and trade, the duty of the second group is to pay the expenses of the warriors and guardians of security in the form of an annual poll tax.

We have evidence that confirms this philosophy regarding Jizyah both before the Islamic era and during the Islamic era.

The age group of Jizyah payers in the era of Anushirvan just mentioned (between twenty and fifty years) is clear evidence of this, as this age group actually pertained to those who had the power to carry weapons and participate in maintaining the security and independence of the country, but paid Jizyah instead due to their occupation with trade.

Another proof is that in Islam, Jizyah is not required of Muslims, because Jihad is obligatory for all, and when necessary, everyone must be present on the battlefield against the enemy. However, since religious minorities are exempt from participating in Jihad, they must pay Jizyah instead, so that in this way they may have a share in maintaining the security of the Islamic country in which they live peacefully.

Furthermore, the exemption of children of religious minorities, as well as women, the elderly, and the blind, from the ruling of Jizyah is another proof of this subject.

From what has been said, it becomes clear that Jizyah is merely a form of financial assistance paid by the People of the Book in exchange for the responsibility that Muslims undertake to ensure the security of their lives and property.

Therefore, those who have considered Jizyah a type of "right of conquest" have not paid attention to its spirit and philosophy. They have failed to notice the fact that when the People of the Book become "Ahl al-Dhimmah" (protected people), the Islamic government is duty-bound to protect them from any aggression or harassment.

Considering that in exchange for paying Jizyah, besides benefiting from immunity and security, they have no obligation regarding participation in the battlefield or any defensive and security matters, it becomes clear that their responsibility toward the Islamic government is far less than that of Muslims.

That is, by paying a negligible amount per year, they enjoy all the benefits of the Islamic government and become equal to Muslims, while they are not in the midst of events or facing dangers.

Among the clear proofs confirming this philosophy is that in the treaties concluded between Muslims and the People of the Book regarding Jizyah during the era of Islamic rule, it was explicitly stated that the People of the Book are obliged to pay Jizyah, and in return, Muslims are obliged to ensure their security; even if enemies from outside rise to oppose or harass them, the Islamic government will defend them.

These treaties are numerous; as an example, we provide one below, which is the treaty that "Khalid ibn al-Walid" concluded with the Christians around the "Euphrates."

The text of the treaty is as follows:

"This is a document from Khalid ibn al-Walid to Saluba ibn Nastuna and his people: I have made a covenant with you for Jizyah and protection. You shall have protection and defense, and as long as we defend you, we have the right to Jizyah; otherwise, we have no right. Written in the year twelve [A.H.] in [the month of] Safar." [^1]

Interestingly, we read that whenever there was a failure in protecting them, the Jizyah was returned to them or not collected from them at all! It is also necessary to note that Jizyah does not have a fixed amount; its rate depends on the ability of the payers. However, what is gathered from Islamic history is that usually a modest amount was set in this regard. This amount was sometimes no more than about one dinar per year, and sometimes it was even stipulated in the treaties that the payers were obliged to pay Jizyah according to their ability.

From the sum of what has been said, the various objections and propaganda spread regarding this Islamic ruling will vanish, and it is proven that this is a just and logical ruling.

[^1]: Quoted from Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 10, p. 294.

Ayahs

وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ٱبْنُ ٱللَّهِ وَقَالَتِ ٱلنَّصَـٰرَى ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ قَوْلُهُم بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ ۖ يُضَـٰهِـُٔونَ قَوْلَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٣٠)
The Jews say, ‘Ezra is the son of Allah,’ and the Christians say, ‘Christ is the son of Allah.’ That is an opinion that they mouth, imitating the opinions of the faithless of former times. May Allah assail them, where do they stray?! (30)
ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَـٰنَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَسِيحَ ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَمَآ أُمِرُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا ۖ لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٣١)
They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah, and also Christ, Mary’s son; though they were commanded to worship only the One God, there is no god except Him; He is far too immaculate to have any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (31)
يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُطْفِـُٔوا۟ نُورَ ٱللَّهِ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَيَأْبَى ٱللَّهُ إِلَّآ أَن يُتِمَّ نُورَهُۥ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٢)
They desire to put out the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah is intent on perfecting His light though the faithless should be averse. (32)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ (٣٣)
It is He who has sent His Apostle with the guidance and the religion of truth that He may make it prevail over all religions, though the polytheists should be averse. (33)

Commentary

The Idol Worship of the People of the Book

In the preceding verses, following the discussion regarding the polytheists, the annulment of their treaties, and the necessity of dismantling the system of idol worship, reference was made to the status of the "People of the Book." It was noted that they too must live in peaceful coexistence with Muslims under certain conditions; otherwise, they must be fought.

In the verses under discussion, the similarity between the People of the Book—specifically the Jews and Christians—and the polytheists and idolaters is explained. This is to clarify that if a degree of strictness has been applied to the People of the Book, it is due to their deviation from Monotheism (Tawhid) and their inclination toward a form of "polytheism in belief" and a form of "polytheism in worship."

First, it says: "The Jews said: 'Ezra (Uzair) is the son of Allah'!" (وَ قالَتِ الْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ابْنُ اللَّهِ).

"And the Christians said: 'The Messiah is the son of Allah'!" (وَ قالَتِ النَّصارى‌ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ اللَّهِ).

"This is a statement they utter with their mouths, and no reality is hidden within it" (ذلِكَ قَوْلُهُمْ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ).

"This talk of theirs resembles the speech of the disbelievers of old" (يُضاهِؤُنَ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ).

"May Allah destroy them [and afflict them with His curse and distance them from His mercy]; how they are deluded and how they distort the truths" (قاتَلَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ).

Here, several points must be noted

1- Who is "Uzair"?

"Uzair" in the Arabic language is the same as "Ezra" in the Jewish language. Since Arabs usually make changes to foreign names when adopting them—especially sometimes converting them into the "diminutive" form (tasghir) to express affection—they transformed "Ezra" into "Uzair." This is similar to how the original name of "Jesus," which is "Yeshua," and "John," which is "Yohanan," were altered upon being transferred into Arabic, becoming "Isa" and "Yahya" [1]. In any case, "Uzair" or "Ezra" holds a special position in Jewish history, to the extent that some attribute the foundation of the national identity and the brilliance of this people's history to him. In reality, he rendered a great service to this faith. This occurred when, during the event of Nebuchadnezzar (Bakht al-Nasr), the King of Babylon, the state of the Jews was completely shattered by him; their cities fell to Nebuchadnezzar's soldiers, their Temple was destroyed, their book, the Torah, was burned, their men were killed, and their women and children were taken captive and moved to Babylon, where they remained for about a century.

[^1]: The purpose of "diminution" (tasghir), as mentioned in literary discussions, is this: to express a small type of something, a specific form is made from the original noun, such as "rajul" (man), whose diminutive is "rujayl" (meaning a small man). However, sometimes using this word is not due to smallness but to express affection for a person or thing, just as a person expresses affection toward his child.

Then, when Cyrus, the King of Persia, conquered Babylon, Ezra, who was one of the Jewish elders at that time, came to him and interceded for them. Cyrus agreed that the Jews could return to their cities and that the Torah should be rewritten.

At this time, he rewrote the Torah according to what remained in his memory from the sayings of the Jewish predecessors.

For this reason, the Jews consider him one of the saviors and revivers of their faith and therefore hold extraordinary respect for him [1]. This subject led a group of Jews to choose the title "Son of Allah" (ibn Allah) for him. Although it is understood from some narrations, such as the narration in Al-Ihtijaj by Tabarsi, that they applied this title as a mark of respect for Uzair, we read in that same narration: When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked them: "If you respect Uzair for his great services and call him by this name, then why do you not give this name to Moses عليه السلام, who served you far more than Uzair?" They were left speechless and had no answer to this question [2]. But whatever the case, this naming had surpassed mere respect in the minds of a group; as is the way of the common folk, they naturally interpreted it in its literal sense and truly imagined him to be the son of God, because he had both saved them from displacement and wandering and had organized their faith by rewriting the Torah.

Of course, not all of them held such a belief, but it is understood from the Quran that this way of thinking existed among a group of them, especially those who lived during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 9, p. 253 and Al-Manar, Vol. 10, p. 322. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 205.

This is evidenced by the fact that it is nowhere recorded in history that they denied this attribution or raised an outcry upon hearing the above verse; had it been otherwise, they would certainly have shown a reaction.

From what we have said, the answer to this question becomes clear: Today, such a belief does not exist among the Jews, and no one considers Uzair the son of God; nevertheless, why has the Quran attributed such a thing to them?

The explanation is: It is not necessary for all Jews to have held this belief; it is certain enough that at the time of the revelation of the Quranic verses, there was a group among the Jews with these beliefs. This is proven by the fact that they never denied the aforementioned attribution and only justified it according to narrations, presenting the naming of Uzair as "Son of Allah" as a form of respect—a point on which they were rendered helpless when faced with the Prophet's objection as to why they did not accord this respect to Moses himself.

In any case, whenever a belief is attributed to a people, it is not necessary for all of them to be unanimous in it; rather, it is sufficient that a significant number of them hold such a belief.

2- The Messiah was not the son of God

Regarding the Christians, there is no doubt that they consider "the Messiah" to be the literal son of God. They do not apply this name merely as respect or protocol, but in its literal sense, and they explicitly state in their books that applying this name to anyone other than the Messiah in its literal sense is not permissible. There is no doubt that this is one of the innovations of the Christians. As we stated in Volume 4, pages 220-233: The Messiah never made such a claim; he only introduced himself as a servant and messenger of God. Fundamentally, it makes no sense for the father-son relationship, which is specific to the material world and the realm of contingencies, to be established between Allah and anyone else.

3- Borrowing these superstitions from others

The Holy Quran states in the above verse that in these deviations, they resemble the polytheists of old.

This is an indication that they imitated them, as some of those polytheists considered certain gods to be the father-god, some the son-god, and even some the mother-god or spouse. In the roots of the beliefs of the ancient idolaters of India, China, and Egypt, such thoughts are seen, which later infiltrated the Jews and Christians.

In the present era, a group of researchers has taken to comparing the contents of the "Two Testaments" (the Torah, the Gospel, and related books) with the beliefs of Buddhists and Brahmins, seeking the roots of the contents of these books among their beliefs. It is noteworthy that many of the teachings of the Gospel and the Torah correspond with the superstitions of Buddhists and Brahmins; even many of the stories and parables present in the Gospel are identical to those found in those two faiths.

If researchers have only recently arrived at this thought, the Quran alluded to this reality fourteen centuries ago in the verse above.

4- The phrase "قاتَلَهُمُ اللَّهُ" (May Allah destroy/fight them), although originally meaning that Allah should fight them or kill them, as Tabarsi narrated in Majma' al-Bayan from Ibn Abbas, this phrase is an allusion to a curse, meaning: May Allah distance them from His mercy.

In the next verse, it points to their practical polytheism (as opposed to polytheism in belief), or in other words, "polytheism in worship," saying: "The Jews and Christians took their scholars and monks as lords besides Allah" (اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَ رُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ).

And also: "They accepted the Messiah, son of Mary, as a deity" (وَ الْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ).

"Ahbar" is the plural of "hibr," meaning a scholar or learned person, and "ruhban" is the plural of "rahib," referring to individuals who chose to reside in monasteries for the purpose of renouncing the world and engaging in worship.

There is no doubt that the Jews and Christians did not prostrate before their scholars and monks, nor did they perform prayers, fasting, or other acts of worship for them. However, since they placed themselves in unconditional obedience to them, even considering it mandatory to implement decrees that were contrary to the command of Allah, the Quran has interpreted this blind and illogical following as "worship."

This meaning is found in a narration from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليهما السلام, where they said:

"By Allah, they did not fast for them nor did they pray for them, but they (the leaders) made lawful for them what was forbidden, and forbade for them what was lawful, so they followed them and thus worshipped them without realizing it." [1]

In another hadith, it is mentioned that Adi ibn Hatim said: I came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله while there was a gold cross around my neck. He said to me:

"O Adi! Cast away this idol from your neck!" I did so, then I drew closer and heard him reciting this verse: "اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَ رُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً..." When he finished the verse, I said: "We never worshipped our leaders!" He said: "Is it not the case that they make the lawful of Allah forbidden and the forbidden of Allah lawful, and you follow them?" I said: "Yes, it is so." He said: "That is their worship." [1]

The reason for this is clear, because legislation belongs exclusively to Allah, and no one besides Him has the right to make something lawful or forbidden for the people or to establish a law. The only thing humans can do is to discover the laws of the Lord and apply them to the required instances.

Therefore, if someone undertakes to legislate against divine laws, and someone recognizes it and accepts it without question, they have attributed the status of God to someone other than God, and this is a type of practical polytheism and idol worship—or in other words, the worship of other than Allah.

Evidence suggests that the Jews and Christians granted their leaders such authority to occasionally change divine laws at their discretion, and even now, the issue of "absolution of sins" is common among Christians, where they confess sins before a priest and he says: "I have forgiven you!" [2]

Another point to be noted is that since the type of worship and devotion of the Christians toward "Jesus" differed from the Jews' worship toward their leaders—one truly considered the Messiah the son of God, while the others were described as worshipping their leaders due to unconditional obedience—the above verse has distinguished between the two and separated their accounts, saying: "اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَ رُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ".

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse and Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 209. [^2]: In Volume 2, page 484 of Tafsir Nemouneh, we have also provided explanations regarding the "worship of humans" by the People of the Book.

Then it separates the Messiah, saying: "وَ الْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ," and this shows that in the expressions of the Quran, all subtleties are observed.

At the end of the verse, it emphasizes that all this human-worship is an innovation and a fabricated matter: "And they were never commanded to choose multiple gods for themselves; rather, they were commanded to worship only one Deity" (وَ ما أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا إِلهاً واحِداً).

"A Deity besides whom none is worthy of worship" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

"A Deity who is glorified far above what they associate with Him" (سُبْحانَهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ).

An Instructive Lesson

The Holy Quran, in the above verse, gives a very valuable lesson to all its followers and points out one of the most sublime concepts of Monotheism (Tawhid), stating:

No Muslim has the right to accept the unconditional obedience of any human, for this act is equivalent to worshipping him. All obedience must fall within the framework of obedience to Allah, and following a human command is permissible only as long as it does not oppose the laws of Allah—regardless of who this person is or what position they hold.

For unconditional obedience is equivalent to worship, and it is a form of idol worship and servitude. Unfortunately, however, Muslims, by distancing themselves from this important Islamic instruction and setting up human idols, have become afflicted with divisions, fragmentations, colonialisms, and exploitations. Until these idols are broken and set aside, they should not expect their disorders to be resolved.

Fundamentally, this type of idol worship is more dangerous than the idol worship of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) where they prostrated before stone and wood, because those soulless idols never colonized their worshippers. However, humans who turn into "idols" chain their followers in the shackles of captivity through their despotism and cause them to suffer all kinds of degradation and misery.

In the third verse under discussion, a beautiful analogy is made for the desperate and futile efforts of the Jews, Christians, and all opponents of Islam, even the polytheists. It says: "They want to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah has willed that this divine light shall continue to grow more widespread and perfect until it encompasses the whole world, and all the inhabitants of the world benefit from its radiance, even if the disbelievers dislike it" (يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُطْفِؤُا نُورَ اللَّهِ بِأَفْواهِهِمْ وَ يَأْبَى اللَّهُ إِلَّا أَنْ يُتِمَّ نُورَهُ وَ لَوْ كَرِهَ الْكافِرُونَ).

Here, several points must be noted

1- In this verse, the religion of God, the Holy Quran, and the teachings of Islam are likened to light and radiance. We know that light is the source of life, movement, growth, and prosperity on earth, and the origin of every kind of beauty.

Islam is also a movement-generating religion that drives human society forward on the path of perfections and is the source of every good and blessing.

The efforts and strivings of the enemies are likened to blowing and puffing with the mouth. How ridiculous it is for a person to want to extinguish a great light like the light of the sun by puffing? To visualize the insignificance of their efforts, no expression seems more eloquent than this. In reality, the strivings of a helpless creature against the infinite Will and the boundless Power of the Truth will be nothing other than this.

2- The issue of extinguishing the light of Allah is mentioned in two places in the Quran: one is the verse above, and the other is verse 8 of Surah al-Saff. In both cases, it is mentioned as a critique of the efforts of the enemies of Islam, but a slight difference in expression is seen between these two verses. In the verse under discussion, it says "يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُطْفِؤُا" (they want to extinguish), while in Surah al-Saff it says "يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِؤُا" (they want [in order] to extinguish). Certainly, this difference in expression points to a subtle point.

Raghib in Al-Mufradat, explaining the difference between these two expressions, says: The first verse refers to extinguishing without preliminaries, but the second verse refers to extinguishing coupled with resorting to preliminaries and means. That is, whether they rise to extinguish the light of Truth without using preliminaries or by resorting to various means, they will face defeat.

3- The word "yaba" (يأبى) is from the root "iba'" (إباء), meaning intense refusal and prevention of something. This expression establishes the certain Will and Intention of the Lord for the completion and advancement of the religion of Islam and is a source of encouragement and hope for all Muslims regarding the future of this faith—provided that Muslims are true Muslims!

The Future in the Realm of Islam!

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, it gives the glad tidings of Islam becoming global to the Muslims, thereby completing the discussion of the previous verse that the desperate efforts of the enemies of Islam lead nowhere. It states with clarity:

"He is the One Who sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth to make it prevail over all religions, even if the polytheists dislike it" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى‌ وَ دِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَ لَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُشْرِكُونَ).

By "guidance" is meant the clear proofs and manifest arguments present in the religion of Islam. By "religion of truth" is meant this very faith whose principles are truth, whose branches are truth, and ultimately whose history, documents, records, results, and outcomes are also truth. Without doubt, a religion whose content is truth and whose proofs, documents, and history are clear must eventually prevail over all religions.

With the passage of time, the advancement of science and knowledge, and the ease of communication, realities will reveal their faces from behind the veils of poisonous propaganda. The obstacles that the opponents of truth place in its path will be crushed, and thus the religion of truth will encompass everywhere, even if the enemies of truth do not want it and do not spare any kind of sabotage, for their movement is a movement against the course of history and against the laws of creation!

Here, several points must be noted.

1- What is meant by guidance and the religion of truth?

The fact that the Quran says in the above verse: "أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدى‌ وَ دِينِ الْحَقِ" seems to point to the reason for Islam's victory over all the religions of the world. For when the content of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call was guidance, and intellect testified to it in every instance, and also when its principles and branches were in accord with truth, supportive of truth, and seeking truth, such a religion naturally prevails over all the religions of the world.

It is narrated from an Indian scholar that he spent some time studying and researching the various religions of the world. Finally, after much study, he chose Islam and wrote a book in English titled "Why I Became a Muslim?" in which he clarified the advantages of Islam over all religions.

Among the most important issues that caught his attention is his statement:
