"Islam is the only religion that has a fixed and preserved history. He wonders how Europe has chosen a religion for itself whose bringer they have elevated above the status of a human and made their God, while it has no documented and acceptable history." [1]

A study and review of the statements of those who have accepted Islam and left their former faith shows that they were influenced by the extraordinary simplicity, the reasoned nature, and the strength of the principles and branches of this faith and its human issues—issues that are purged of every kind of superstition and from which the light of "truth" and "guidance" shines forth.

2- Logical victory or victory of power?

There is a discussion among commentators as to how Islam prevails over all religions and what form this victory will take. Some have considered this victory to be only a logical and argumentative victory, saying that this has already been achieved, because Islam, from the perspective of logic and reasoning, is not comparable to existing religions.

However, an examination of the usage of the root "izhar" (لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ ...) in Quranic verses shows that this root mostly appears in the sense of physical victory and outward power. As we read in the story of the "Companions of the Cave": "إِنَّهُمْ إِنْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ يَرْجُمُوكُمْ": "If they (Decius and his gang) prevail over you, they will stone you" (Al-Kahf, verse 20). And also regarding the polytheists, we read: "كَيْفَ وَ إِنْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ لا يَرْقُبُوا فِيكُمْ إِلًّا وَ لا ذِمَّةً": "How [can there be a treaty] while, if they prevail over you, they do not observe regarding you any kinship or covenant?" (At-Tawbah, 8). It is obvious that victory in these cases is not a logical victory, but a practical and objective victory. In any case, it is more correct to consider the aforementioned victory and prevalence as an all-encompassing victory, as it is more consistent with the concept of the verse, which is absolute from every perspective. That is, a day will come when Islam will prevail over all the religions of the world both in terms of logic and reasoning and in terms of outward influence and governance, overshadowing them all.

3- The Quran and the Uprising of the Mahdi (عليه السلام)

The above verse, which appears identically and with these very words in Surah al-Saff and is repeated with a slight difference in Surah al-Fath, gives news of a major event whose importance necessitated this repetition. It gives news of the globalization of Islam and this religion becoming world-encompassing.

Although some commentators have taken the victory discussed in this verse to mean a regional and limited victory that took place for Islam and Muslims during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or in the times following it, given that there is no condition or restriction in the verse and it is absolute from every perspective, there is no reason to limit its meaning. The concept of the verse is the all-encompassing victory of Islam over all the religions of the world, and the meaning of this statement is that eventually, Islam will encompass the entire globe and prevail over the whole world.

There is no doubt that at present, this matter has not been realized, but we know that this certain promise of God is gradually being realized. The speed of Islam's progress in the world, the recognition of this religion in various European countries, its rapid influence in America and Africa, the conversion to Islam of many scientists, and the like, all show that Islam is moving toward becoming global.

However, according to various narrations found in Islamic sources, the completion of this program will be when "the Mahdi (عليه السلام)" appears and realizes the program of the globalization of Islam.

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan narrates from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse:

"That will be at the time of the emergence of the Mahdi, so there will remain no one except that he acknowledges Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)."

"The promise in this verse will be fulfilled at the time of the appearance of the Mahdi from the progeny of Muhammad; on that day, there will be no one on earth except that he confesses to the truth of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله."

And also in the same commentary, it is narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"There will not remain on the face of the earth a house of mud or fur but that Allah will cause the word of Islam to enter it!"

And also in the book Ikmal al-Din by Saduq, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse:

"By Allah, its interpretation has not yet been sent down, and its interpretation will not be sent down until the Qa'im emerges. When the Qa'im emerges, there will not remain a disbeliever in Allah the Almighty." [1]

Other hadiths with the same theme have been narrated from the leaders of Islam.

A group of commentators has also mentioned this interpretation under the verse.

However, it is surprising that the author of Al-Manar not only did not accept the above interpretation here but, as appropriate, entered into a discussion regarding the hadiths of the Mahdi عليه السلام. With a specific prejudice he holds against the Shi'a—never missing an opportunity for unfair attacks—he completely denied the hadiths related to "the Mahdi" and considered them contradictory and unacceptable! This was under the assumption that the belief in the existence of the Mahdi belongs only to the Shi'a or those inclined toward Shi'ism! Furthermore, he considered the belief in the existence of the Mahdi to be a factor for stagnation and backwardness.

For this reason, we are compelled to present a very condensed discussion regarding the narrations related to the appearance of the Mahdi عليه السلام and also a discussion regarding the effects of this belief on the progress of Islamic society and the struggle against oppression and corruption. This is to clarify that whenever prejudice enters, science and knowledge flee through the other door, and how the aforementioned commentator, despite having significant information on Islamic issues, has seen some clear truths upside down due to this weakness (intense prejudice).

Islamic Narrations Regarding the Appearance of the Mahdi (عليه السلام)

Although many books have been written by Sunni scholars and Shi'a scholars regarding the hadiths related to the uprising of the Mahdi, in our opinion, nothing is more eloquent and at the same time more condensed than the letter sent by a group of scholars from the Hijaz in response to one of the questioners. Therefore, we present the exact translation of it below for the esteemed readers.

But first, we remind you that:

The narrations related to the uprising of the Mahdi عليه السلام are such that no Islamic researcher, following any of the groups or schools of thought, can deny their "succession" (tawatur).

So far, many books have been written in this field, and their authors have unanimously accepted the authenticity of the hadiths related to the global reformer, i.e., "the Mahdi عليه السلام." Only a very few individuals, such as Ibn Khaldun and Ahmad Amin al-Misri, have doubted the issuance of these reports from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. We have evidence that their motive in this was not the weakness of the reports, but rather they thought that the narrations related to "the Mahdi" عليه السلام contained matters that cannot be easily believed, either because they could not separate correct hadiths from incorrect ones or they did not understand their interpretation.

In any case, it is necessary first of all to present the question and answer recently published by the "Muslim World League" (Rabitat al-Alam al-Islami)—which is under the influence of the most extremist Islamic factions, namely the Wahhabis—for the dear readers. This is to clarify that the issue of the appearance of the Mahdi عليه السلام is a matter upon which there is consensus among Muslims. In our opinion, the necessary evidence has been collected in this short treatise in such a way that no one has the power to deny it, and if the strict Wahhabis have also submitted to it, it is for this very reason.

About a year ago, a person named "Abu Muhammad" from Kenya asked a question regarding the appearance of "the Awaited Mahdi (عليه السلام)" from the Muslim World League. The Secretary-General of the "League," Muhammad Salih al-Qazzaz, in the response he sent to him—while explicitly stating that Ibn Taymiyyah, the founder of the Wahhabi school, also accepted the hadiths related to the appearance of the Mahdi عليه السلام—sent him the text of a treatise prepared by five famous current scholars of the Hijaz in this field.

In this treatise, after mentioning the name of "the Mahdi" عليه السلام and "his place of appearance," namely Mecca, we read:

"...At the time of the appearance of corruption in the world and the spread of disbelief and oppression, Allah, through him (the Mahdi عليه السلام), will fill the world with justice and equity just as it was filled with oppression and tyranny...

He is the last of the 'Twelve Rightly Guided Caliphs' whom the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله gave news of in the books of 'Sahih'.

The hadiths related to the Mahdi have been narrated by many of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, including:

Uthman ibn Affan, Ali ibn Abi Talib, Talhah ibn Ubaydullah, Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, Qurrah ibn Asas al-Mazani, Abd Allah ibn al-Harith, Abu Hurayrah, Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, Jabir ibn Abd Allah, Abu Umamah, Jabir ibn Majid, Abd Allah ibn Umar, Anas ibn Malik, Imran ibn Husayn, and Umm Salamah.

These are twenty of those who narrated the reports of the Mahdi, and besides them, many other individuals exist.

Many statements have also been narrated from the companions themselves in which the appearance of the Mahdi عليه السلام is discussed, which can also be placed in the rank of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله narrations. For this issue is not among those about which one can say something through ijtihad (therefore, they too naturally heard this matter from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله)."

Then it adds:

Both the above hadiths narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the testimony and witness of the companions—which here have the status of hadith—have appeared in many famous Islamic books and primary texts of hadith, including "Sunan," "Ma'ajim," and "Masanid." Among them:

Sunan Abu Dawud, Sunan Tirmidhi, Ibn Majah, Ibn Amr al-Dani, Musnad Ahmad, Ibn Ya'la, al-Bazzar, Sahih al-Hakim, Ma'ajim al-Tabarani (Great and Middle), al-Ruyani, al-Daraqutni, Abu Nu'aym in Akhbar al-Mahdi, al-Khatib in Tarikh Baghdad, Ibn Asakir in Tarikh Dimashq, and others.

Then it adds:

Some Islamic scholars have authored specific books in this field, including:

Abu Nu'aym in Akhbar al-Mahdi, Ibn Hajar al-Haythami in Al-Qawl al-Mukhtasar fi Alamat al-Mahdi al-Muntazar, al-Shawkani in Al-Tawdih fi Tawatur ma ja'a fi al-Muntazar wa al-Dajjal wa al-Masih, Idris al-Iraqi al-Maghribi in the book Al-Mahdi, Abu al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Mu'min al-Maghribi in the book Al-Wahm al-Maknun fi al-Radd ala Ibn Khaldun.

And the last person to write a detailed discussion in this field is the director of the Islamic University of Medina, who discussed it in several issues of the university's magazine.

It further adds:

A number of great figures and Islamic scholars, both ancient and modern, have also explicitly stated in their writings that the hadiths in the field of the Mahdi are at the level of succession (tawatur) [and are in no way deniable], including:

Al-Sakhawi in the book Fath al-Mughith, Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Safawini in Sharh al-Aqidah, Abu al-Hasan al-Abiri in Manaqib al-Shafi'i, Ibn Taymiyyah in his book of fatwas, al-Suyuti in Al-Hawi, Idris al-Iraqi in his work on the Mahdi, al-Shawkani in the book Al-Tawdih fi Tawatur ma ja'a fi al-Muntazar... Muhammad Ja'far al-Kattani in Nazm al-Tanathur, Abu al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Mu'min in Al-Wahm al-Maknun...

At the end of the discussion, it says: (Only) Ibn Khaldun wanted to criticize the hadiths related to the Mahdi using the baseless and fabricated hadith that says: "There is no Mahdi except Jesus," but the great leaders and scholars of Islam have rejected his statement, especially Ibn Abd al-Mu'min, who wrote a special book on his statement that was published thirty years ago in the East and West.

The preservers of hadith and the great scholars of hadith have also explicitly stated that the hadiths of the Mahdi عليه السلام include "Sahih" and "Hasan" hadiths, and the total of them is "Mutawatir" (successive).

Therefore, belief in the appearance of the Mahdi is obligatory (upon every Muslim), and this is considered part of the beliefs of the People of the Sunnah and the Community (Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah), and none but the ignorant, the uninformed, or the innovator denies it!"

Director of the Office of the Islamic Fiqh Council, Muhammad Muntasir al-Kattani.

Constructive Effects of Awaiting the Appearance of the Mahdi (عليه السلام)

In the previous discussion, we learned that this belief is not an imported aspect of Islamic teachings.

Rather, it is among the most certain topics taken from the founder of Islam himself, and all Islamic sects are unanimous in this field, and the hadiths in this regard are successive.

Now let us turn to the consequences of this "awaiting" (intizar) in the current state of Islamic societies.

Let us see whether faith in such an appearance immerses a person in such visionary thoughts that they become heedless of their current situation and submit to any conditions?

Or is this belief truly a kind of call to uprising and the building of the individual and society?

Does it create movement or stagnation?

Is it responsibility-generating or a cause for fleeing from responsibilities?! And finally, is it a sedative or an awakener?

But before explaining and examining these questions, attention to one point is absolutely necessary: that the most constructive instructions and the most sublime concepts, whenever they fall into the hands of the uninformed, the unworthy, or the exploitative, may be so distorted that they give a result exactly opposite to the original goal and move in a direction against it. This has many examples, and the issue of "awaiting," as we shall see, is among these issues.

In any case, to avoid any error in calculation in these types of discussions, one must, so to speak, take the water from the source so that the potential pollutions of the streams and canals along the way do not affect it.

That is, in the discussion of "awaiting," we go directly to the primary Islamic texts and examine the various tones of the narrations that emphasize the issue of "awaiting" to become aware of the original goal.

Now, pay close attention to these few narrations:

1- Someone asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: What do you say about someone who possesses the guardianship (wilayah) of the leaders and awaits the appearance of the government of truth, and dies in this state?

The Imam عليه السلام replied:

"He is in the same position as one who was with the Qa'im in his tent—then he remained silent for a moment—then he said: He is like one who was with the Messenger of Allah!" [1]

This same theme has been narrated in many reports with different expressions.

2- In some: "In the position of one striking with his sword in the way of Allah." 3- In others: "Like one who fought alongside the Messenger of Allah with his sword!" 4- In others: "In the position of one sitting under the banner of the Qa'im." 5- In others: "In the position of the mujahid in front of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله)." 6- And in others: "In the position of one who was martyred with the Messenger of Allah."

These seven comparisons mentioned regarding the awaiting of the appearance of the Mahdi عليه السلام in these six narrations clarify the reality that a type of relationship and similarity exists between the issue of "awaiting" on one hand, and "jihad" and struggle against the enemy in its ultimate form on the other (pay attention).

[^1]: Mahasin al-Barqi according to the narration in Bihar, old edition, Vol. 13, p. 136.

7- In numerous narrations, awaiting such a government is introduced as the highest form of worship.

This theme is narrated in some hadiths from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and in some from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام. In a hadith, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"The best of my Ummah's deeds is awaiting the relief (faraj) from Allah, the Mighty and Sublime." [1]

And in another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The best worship is awaiting the relief." [2]

This hadith, whether we take "awaiting the relief" in the broad sense of the word or in the specific sense, i.e., awaiting the appearance of the great global reformer, clarifies the importance of awaiting in our subject of discussion.

These expressions all indicate that awaiting such a revolution is always coupled with a vast and far-reaching jihad. Keep this in mind as we go to the concept of awaiting and then draw a conclusion from the total.

The Concept of Awaiting

"Awaiting" (intizar) is usually said of the state of someone who is dissatisfied with the current situation and strives to create a better situation.

For example, a patient who awaits recovery, or a father who awaits the return of his son from a journey, are dissatisfied with the illness and the separation from the son and strive for a better situation.

Similarly, a merchant who is dissatisfied with a turbulent market and awaits the subsiding of an economic crisis has these two states: "alienation from the current situation" and "striving for a better situation."

Therefore, the issue of awaiting the government of truth and justice of "the Mahdi" and the uprising of the global reformer is in reality composed of two elements: the element of "negation" and the element of "affirmation." The element of negation is the same alienation from the current situation, and the element of affirmation is desiring a better situation.

If these two aspects settle deeply in the human soul, they will become the source of two branches of far-reaching actions.

These two branches of actions are: abstaining from any cooperation and harmony with the factors of oppression and corruption, and even struggling and clashing with them on one hand; and self-building, self-help, and acquiring physical, spiritual, material, and spiritual readiness for the formation of that single global and popular government on the other.

If we look closely, we see that both parts are constructive and factors for movement, awareness, and awakening.

With attention to the original concept of "awaiting," the meaning of the numerous narrations we quoted above regarding the reward and result of the work of the awaiters is well understood. Now we understand why true awaiters are sometimes counted as those who are in the tent of Imam Mahdi عليه السلام or under his banner, or like one who strikes with the sword in the way of Allah, or is drenched in his own blood, or has become a martyr.

Are these not indications of the different stages and degrees of struggle in the way of truth and justice that are proportionate to the amount of readiness and the degree of awaiting of individuals?

That is, just as the level of sacrifice of the mujahideen in the way of God and their roles differ, awaiting, self-building, and readiness also have completely different degrees, each of which resembles one of those in terms of "preliminaries" and "results." Both are jihad and both require readiness and self-building. Someone who is placed in the tent of the leader of such a government—meaning they are in the center of the command headquarters of a global government—cannot be a heedless, uninformed, and indifferent person. That place is not for everyone; it is for individuals who truly possess the merit for such a position and importance.

Similarly, someone who has a weapon in hand and fights against the opponents of the government of peace and justice in front of the leader of this revolution must have extensive spiritual, intellectual, and combat readiness.

For more awareness of the real effects of awaiting the appearance of the Mahdi, pay attention to the following explanation:

Awaiting Means Full Readiness

If I am an oppressor and a tyrant, how is it possible for me to be awaiting someone whose sword's prey is the blood of the oppressors?

If I am polluted and impure, how can I await a revolution whose first flame catches the hem of the polluted?

An army that awaits a great jihad increases the combat readiness of its personnel, breathes a revolutionary spirit into them, and corrects any point of weakness.

For the nature of "awaiting" is always proportionate to the goal we are awaiting.

Awaiting the arrival of an ordinary traveler from a journey.

Awaiting the return of a very dear friend.

Awaiting the arrival of the season for picking fruit from the tree and harvesting the crop.

Each of these awaitings is mixed with a type of readiness. In one, the house must be prepared and means of hospitality provided; in the other, the necessary tools, the sickle, and...

Now, consider that those who await the uprising of a great global reformer are, in fact, awaiting a revolution, a transformation, and a transition that is the most extensive and fundamental of human revolutions throughout the history of mankind.

It is a revolution that, unlike previous revolutions, is not regional in nature; rather, it is both universal and all-encompassing, and it includes all affairs and aspects of human life—it is a political, cultural, economic, and moral revolution.

The First Philosophy: Individual Self-Building

Such a transformation, before anything else, requires prepared and valuable human elements who can carry the heavy burden of such extensive reforms in the world. This, in the first instance, necessitates raising the level of thought, awareness, and spiritual and intellectual readiness to cooperate in implementing that magnificent program. Narrow-mindedness, short-sightedness, crooked thinking, envy, childish and irrational disputes, and generally any kind of hypocrisy and division are incompatible with the status of "true awaiters."

The important point is that a true awaiter of such an important program can never play the role of a spectator; he must certainly place himself in the ranks of the revolutionaries from this very moment.

Faith in the results and the ultimate outcome of this transformation never allows him to be in the ranks of the opponents, and being in the ranks of the supporters requires possessing "pure deeds," an even purer spirit, and enjoying sufficient "courage" and "awareness."

If I am corrupt and dishonest, how can I count the days in anticipation of a system in which corrupt and dishonest individuals have no role whatsoever, but will rather be rejected and despised?

Is this anticipation not enough to purify my spirit and thought, and to wash my body and soul from the filth of pollution?

An army that awaits a liberating Jihad certainly enters a state of full alert; it acquires the weaponry suitable for such a battlefield and builds the necessary trenches. It raises the combat readiness of its personnel.

It strengthens the morale of its individuals and keeps the flame of love and longing for such a struggle alive in the heart of every single one of its soldiers. An army that does not possess such readiness is never truly in a state of anticipation, and if it claims to be, it is lying.

Anticipation for a global reformer means full intellectual, moral, material, and spiritual readiness for the reformation of the entire world. Think about how constructive such a readiness is!

The reformation of the entire earth and ending all injustices and disorders is no joke; it cannot be a simple task. Readiness for such a great goal must be proportionate to it; that is, it must be as vast and deep as the goal itself!

To realize such a revolution, men who are very great, determined, very powerful and invincible, extraordinarily pure and high-minded, completely ready, and possessing the necessary deep insight are required.

And self-building for such a goal necessitates implementing the deepest moral, intellectual, and social programs. This is the meaning of true anticipation. Can anyone say that such anticipation is not constructive?

The Second Philosophy: Social Self-Help

True awaiters, at the same time, have a duty not to focus solely on themselves but to be mindful of one another's condition. In addition to reforming themselves, they must strive to reform others, because the magnificent and heavy program they await is not an individual program.

It is a program in which all elements of the revolution must participate; the work must be collective and universal. Efforts and endeavors must be coordinated, and the depth and breadth of this coordination must be as grand as the global revolutionary program they anticipate.

In a vast field of collective struggle, no individual can remain oblivious to the state of others; rather, he is duty-bound to reform any weak point he sees anywhere, to repair any vulnerable position, and to strengthen any weak or powerless part. This is because without the active and coordinated participation of all fighters, implementing such a program is impossible.

Therefore, true awaiters, in addition to striving for their own reformation, consider it their duty to reform others as well.

This is another constructive effect of awaiting the uprising of a global reformer, and this is the philosophy behind all those virtues enumerated for the true awaiters.

The Third Philosophy: True Awaiters Do Not Dissolve into the Corruption of the Environment

Another important effect of awaiting the Mahdi is not dissolving into the corruptions of the environment and not surrendering to pollutions.

To explain: when corruption becomes pervasive and draws the majority or a large group into pollution, sometimes pure individuals find themselves in a difficult psychological deadlock—a deadlock originating from despair over reforms.

Sometimes they think that the matter is past recovery and there is no longer any hope for reform, and that striving and effort to keep oneself pure is futile. This hopelessness and despair might gradually draw them toward corruption and conformity with the environment, making them unable to maintain themselves as a righteous minority against an unhealthy majority, fearing that not "blending in with the crowd" would lead to disgrace! The only thing that can breathe "hope" into them, invite them to resistance and self-restraint, and prevent them from dissolving into the corrupt environment is the hope for ultimate reform. Only in this case will they not give up their efforts to preserve their purity and reform others.

And if we see that in Islamic teachings, despair of forgiveness is considered one of the greatest sins, and uninformed individuals might wonder why despair of Allah's mercy is regarded as so significant—even more significant than many other sins—the philosophy is actually this: a sinner who despairs of mercy sees no reason to think of compensation or at least to stop continuing the sin. His logic is: "Now that the water has passed over my head, what does it matter if it is one fathom or a hundred? Since I am already disgraced in the world, the world's sorrow is nothing! There is no color darker than black; the end is Hell, which I have already bought for myself, so what else should I fear?!" and similar logics...

But when the window of hope is opened for him—hope for the Lord's pardon, hope for a change in the existing situation—it becomes a turning point in his life and invites him to stop on the path of sin and return toward purity and reform.

For this reason, hope can always be recognized as an effective educational factor for corrupt individuals. Likewise, righteous individuals caught in corrupt environments cannot preserve themselves without hope.

The result is that awaiting the appearance of a reformer—where the more corrupt the world becomes, the greater the hope for His appearance—has an increasing psychological effect on the believers and insures them against the powerful waves of corruption. They do not become despaired by the expansion of the scope of environmental corruption; rather, according to the principle: "When the promise of union draws near * The fire of love becomes sharper," they see the attainment of the goal before them, and their effort to fight corruption or preserve themselves is pursued with greater longing and love.

From the sum of the previous discussions, we conclude that the sedative effect of anticipation occurs only if its concept is distorted or perverted—just as a group of opponents have perverted it and a group of supporters have distorted it. However, if it is implemented in its true sense within society and the individual, it will be an important factor for education, self-building, dynamism, and hope.

Among the clear evidences confirming this subject is that under the verse: (وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ...) "Allah has promised those who have faith among you and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the land..." it has been narrated from the great leaders of Islam that the meaning of this verse is:

"هُوَ الْقَائِمُ وَ أَصْحَابُهُ" "He is the Qa'im and his companions." [1]

And in another Hadith, we read: (نَزَلَتْ فِي الْمَهْدِيِّ): "This verse was revealed concerning the Mahdi (عليه السلام)."

In this verse, the Mahdi and his companions are introduced as (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ) "those who have faith and do righteous deeds." Therefore, the realization of this global revolution is impossible without a firm faith that dispels any kind of weakness, abjectness, and inability, and without righteous deeds that open the way for the reformation of the world. And those who await such a program must both raise the level of their awareness and faith and strive to reform their deeds.

Only such people can give themselves the glad tidings of accompanying Him in His government—not those who cooperate with oppression and tyranny, nor those who are strangers to faith and righteous deeds, nor the cowardly and abject individuals who, due to weakness of faith, are afraid of everything, even their own shadows.

And not the sluggish, indifferent, and idle individuals who sit idly by, choosing silence in the face of the corruptions of their environment and society, making not the slightest effort or struggle in the path of fighting corruption.

This is the constructive effect of the uprising of the Mahdi in Islamic society.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar (old edition), Vol. 13, p. 14.

Ayahs

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْأَحْبَارِ وَٱلرُّهْبَانِ لَيَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوَٰلَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ ٱلذَّهَبَ وَٱلْفِضَّةَ وَلَا يُنفِقُونَهَا فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٣٤)
O you who have faith! Indeed many of the scribes and monks wrongfully eat up the people’s wealth, and bar [them] from the way of Allah. Those who treasure up gold and silver, and do not spend it in the way of Allah, inform them of a painful punishment (34)
يَوْمَ يُحْمَىٰ عَلَيْهَا فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ فَتُكْوَىٰ بِهَا جِبَاهُهُمْ وَجُنُوبُهُمْ وَظُهُورُهُمْ ۖ هَـٰذَا مَا كَنَزْتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ فَذُوقُوا۟ مَا كُنتُمْ تَكْنِزُونَ (٣٥)
on the day when these shall be heated in hellfire and therewith branded on their foreheads, their sides, and their backs [and told]: ‘This is what you treasured up for yourselves! So taste what you have treasured!’ (35)

Commentary

Hoarding (Kanz) is Forbidden

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the polytheistic actions of the Jews and Christians, who attributed a form of divinity to their scholars. The verse under discussion states: not only do they lack the status of divinity, but they are also unfit for the leadership of the people; the best evidence for this was their various transgressions.

Here, addressing the Muslims, it says:

"O you who have believed, indeed many of the scholars and the monks consume the wealth of people unjustly and avert [them] from the way of Allah" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْأَحْبارِ وَ الرُّهْبانِ لَيَأْكُلُونَ أَمْوالَ النَّاسِ بِالْباطِلِ وَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ).

It is noteworthy that, as is the custom of the Quran, it does not apply this ruling to all Jewish scholars and monks, but rather, by using the expression "many" (كثيراً), it effectively excludes the righteous minority. Such precision is observed in other verses of the Quran as well, as we have previously noted.

As for how they consumed people's wealth pointlessly and without justification—or in the Quranic expression, through "falsehood" (باطل)—this is mentioned more or less in other verses, and parts of it are recorded in history.

One way was that they concealed the truths of the teachings of the religion of Prophet Jesus (عليه السلام) and Prophet Moses (عليه السلام) so that people would not turn to the new religion (Islam), ensuring their interests were not jeopardized and their gifts were not cut off, as alluded to in verses 41, 79, and 174 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Another way was that by taking "bribes" from the people, they turned truth into falsehood and falsehood into truth, issuing void judgments in favor of the powerful and influential, as mentioned in verse 41 of Surah Al-Ma'idah.

Another of their illegitimate sources of income was that in the name of "selling paradise" or "forgiving sins," they took enormous sums from the people, selling paradise and forgiveness—which belong exclusively to Allah—to the public. This caused a great uproar in the history of Christianity and sparked many debates and conflicts!

As for their obstructing the way of Allah, it is clear, for they distorted the divine verses or kept them hidden to protect their own interests. Furthermore, they accused anyone they saw as opposing their status and interests, and by establishing "Inquisition Courts," they tried them in the worst manner and condemned and punished them most severely.

Had they not truly committed such acts and sacrificed their followers to their own greed and whims, many more groups today would have accepted the true religion, Islam, with heart and soul. Therefore, it can be boldly said: the sin of millions of humans who remain in the darkness of disbelief lies upon their shoulders!

Even now, what measures do the institutions of the Church and the Jews not take to alter world public opinion regarding Islam, and what strange and horrific slanders do they not attribute to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)?

This subject is so extensive that a group of enlightened Christian scholars have explicitly confessed that the traditional method of the Church in its unchivalrous struggle against Islam is one of the reasons Westerners remain ignorant of this pure religion.

Then, in connection with the discussion of the worldliness of the Jewish and Christian leaders, the Quran mentions a general law regarding hoarders of wealth, saying: "And those who hoard gold and silver and spend it not in the way of Allah—give them tidings of a painful punishment" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَ الْفِضَّةَ وَ لا يُنْفِقُونَها فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

"Yaknizun" (يکنزون) is from the root "Kanz" (كنز), meaning "treasure," which originally refers to gathering the parts of something together. Thus, a fleshy camel is called "Kinaz al-Lahm." Subsequently, it was applied to the gathering, keeping, and hiding of wealth or valuable objects.

Therefore, its concept includes gathering, keeping, and sometimes hiding.

"Dhahab" (ذهب) means "gold" and "fiddah" (فضة) means "silver." Some linguists (as quoted by Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan) have offered an interesting observation regarding these two words: they say "gold" is called "dhahab" because it quickly leaves one's hand and has no permanence (the root "dhahab" in Arabic means "to go"), and "silver" is called "fiddah" because it is quickly scattered and dispersed (the root "infidad" means "dispersion"). To understand the state of such wealth, these names alone are sufficient!

From the day human societies were formed, the issue of exchanging various products among humans was prevalent. Everyone offered their surplus agricultural, livestock, and other products for sale. However, in the beginning, exchanges were always in the form of barter (commodity for commodity), as money had not been invented. Since barter created many difficulties—for instance, individuals might want to sell their surplus but did not need anything else at that moment, yet wished to convert it into something they could use to acquire desired goods whenever they chose—the invention of "money" was proposed.

The emergence of "silver" and, more importantly, "gold" realized this idea, and these two metals formed low-value and high-value currency, respectively. Through them, the circulation of transactions gained more significant and noticeable momentum.

Therefore, the primary philosophy of money is the more complete and rapid circulation of the wheels of economic exchange. Those who hide money in the form of "treasure" (kanz) not only cause economic stagnation and harm the interests of society but their action is directly contrary to the philosophy of the emergence of money.

The above verse explicitly prohibits the hoarding of wealth and the creation of treasures, commanding Muslims to employ their wealth in the way of Allah and for the benefit of Allah's servants, and to strictly avoid amassing, storing, and removing it from the circulation of transactions. Otherwise, they must expect a painful punishment.

This painful punishment is not only the severe penalty of the Day of Resurrection but also includes the harsh punishments of this world that afflict both the poor and the rich due to the disruption of economic balance and the emergence of class differences.

If in the past the people of the world were not properly acquainted with the importance of this Islamic command, today we can well perceive it, for the disorders that have afflicted humanity due to the hoarding of wealth by a group of selfish and ignorant individuals—manifesting as riots, wars, and bloodshed—are hidden from no one.

To what extent does the accumulation of wealth count as "Kanz"?

There is a discussion among commentators regarding the above verse: does any kind of gathering of wealth beyond the necessities of life count as "Kanz" and is it forbidden according to the verse?

Or was this ruling specific to the beginning of Islam before the revelation of the ruling on Zakat and was subsequently lifted with the revelation of the Zakat ruling?

Or is it that, fundamentally, what is obligatory is paying the annual Zakat and nothing else; therefore, whenever a person gathers wealth and regularly pays its Islamic tax, i.e., Zakat, every year, they will not be subject to the above verse?

In many narrations found in both Shi'a and Sunni sources, the third interpretation is seen. For example, in a hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read:

"Any wealth whose Zakat you have paid is not Kanz." [^1] We also read: When the above verse was revealed, it became difficult for the Muslims, and they said, "With this ruling, none of us can store anything for our children or secure their future..." Finally, they asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and he said:

"Indeed, Allah has not made Zakat obligatory except to purify what remains of your wealth, and He only ordained inheritances from wealth that remains after you." [^2] This means that if the gathering of wealth were completely forbidden, the law of inheritance would have no subject.

[^1] and [^2]: Al-Manar, Vol. 10, p. 404.

In the book Amali by Sheikh [Tusi], the same meaning is narrated from the Prophet: "Whoever pays the Zakat of his wealth, its remainder is not Kanz." [^1] However, we see other narrations in Islamic sources whose content, at first glance, does not seem consistent with the above interpretation. Among them is a hadith narrated in Majma' al-Bayan from Ali (عليه السلام) who said:

"Whatever exceeds four thousand [dirhams]—which apparently refers to one year's expenses—is Kanz, whether its Zakat is paid or not; and whatever is less than that is considered 'nafaqah' (living expenses). Therefore, give tidings of a painful punishment to the hoarders of wealth." [^2] And in the book Al-Kafi, it is narrated from Mu'adh ibn Kathir that he said: I heard Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) saying: "Our followers (Shi'a) are currently free to spend what they have in their hands in the way of good (and the remainder is lawful for them), but when our 'Qa'im' rises, he will prohibit all treasures and accumulated wealth so that all is brought to him, to use it for help against the enemy; and this is the meaning of Allah's words in His Book: 'And those who hoard gold and silver...'" [^3] In the biography of Abu Dharr, it is also repeatedly mentioned in many books that he would recite the above verse before Mu'awiyah in Sham every morning and evening, shouting loudly: "Give tidings to the hoarders of treasures of branding on the foreheads, branding on the flanks, and branding on the backs forever, until the heat circulates in their insides!" [^1] Also, Abu Dharr's argumentation before Uthman using the above verse shows that he believed the verse was not exclusive to those who withhold Zakat, but included others as well.

From examining the collection of the above hadiths along with the verse itself, one can conclude that in normal and ordinary conditions—that is, at times when society is not in a dire or dangerous state and people enjoy an ordinary life—paying Zakat is sufficient, and the remainder is not considered Kanz. (Of course, it should be noted that fundamentally, by observing Islamic standards and regulations regarding income, wealth does not accumulate to an extraordinary degree, for Islam has placed so many conditions and restrictions upon it that acquiring such wealth is often impossible).

However, in extraordinary times and when the preservation of the interests of the Islamic society requires it, the Islamic government can place a limit on the accumulation of wealth (as we read in the narration of Ali (عليه السلام)) or demand all the stores and reserves of the people to preserve the existence of the Islamic society (as mentioned in the narration of Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the time of the rising of the Qa'im, which, considering the reason mentioned in that narration, includes other times as well, for he says: "so he may use it against his enemy"). But we repeat that this matter is solely at the disposal of the Islamic government, and it is the one that can make such a decision in times of necessity (take note).

As for the story of Abu Dharr, it may refer to this very issue: that in those days, the Islamic society was in such dire need that hoarding wealth was contrary to the interests of society and the preservation of its existence.

Or, Abu Dharr's view was directed at the funds of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) that were in the hands of Uthman and Mu'awiyah; and we know that such funds, in the presence of the deserving and the needy, cannot be stored for a moment, but must be given to their owners, and the issue of Zakat is not relevant here at all.

Particularly, all Islamic histories, both Shi'a and Sunni, testify that Uthman gave massive amounts of the public treasury to his relatives, and Mu'awiyah built a palace from it that revived the legend of the Sassanid palaces; Abu Dharr had every right to remind them of this verse!

Abu Dharr and Socialism

We know that one of the major criticisms leveled against the third Caliph is the issue of the violent exile of Abu Dharr to the harsh land of "Rabadhah," which led to the death of this great companion and devoted mujahid of the path of Islam—the same person about whom it was narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "The sky has not shaded, nor has the earth carried upon itself, anyone more truthful than Abu Dharr."

We also know that Abu Dharr's disagreement with Uthman was not over a desire for wealth or status, for he was a man who was pious and detached in every respect. Rather, the source of the disagreement was solely the third Caliph's squandering of the public treasury and his unaccountable bestowals and gifts to his kinsmen and relatives.

Abu Dharr was very strict in financial matters, especially where they concerned the "Bayt al-Mal," and he wanted all Muslims to follow the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) method in this regard. However, we know that in the era of the third Caliph, the course of affairs was different.

In any case, when the explicit and decisive words of this great companion became hard for the third Caliph to bear, he first sent him to Sham. But this time, Abu Dharr rose even more explicitly and decisively against the actions of Mu'awiyah, to the point that Ibn Abbas says Mu'awiyah wrote to Uthman: "If you have any need for Sham, return Abu Dharr, for if he remains in Sham, this region will be lost to you."

Uthman wrote a letter and summoned Abu Dharr, and according to some histories, he ordered Mu'awiyah to send him with harsh guards who would drive him toward Medina day and night without a moment's rest, such that Abu Dharr became ill upon arriving in Medina. Since his presence in Medina was also intolerable for the caliphate apparatus, he was sent to Rabadhah and passed away there.

Those who wish to defend the third Caliph in this matter sometimes accuse Abu Dharr of having socialist beliefs, claiming he considered all wealth to belong to God and denied private ownership!!

This is a very strange slander. Since the Quran, with total clarity, has respected private ownership under certain conditions, and since Abu Dharr was among the closest individuals to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and was raised in the lap of the Quran, and there was no one more truthful than him under the sky, how can such an attribution be made to him?

The remote desert dwellers knew this Islamic ruling; they had heard the verses regarding trade, inheritance, and the like. Is it believable that the closest student of the Prophet was ignorant of this ruling?

Is it anything other than that stubborn partisans, to exonerate the third Caliph and, even more strangely, to exonerate Mu'awiyah's apparatus, have cast such a slander upon him, and a group still follows it blindly?! Yes, Abu Dharr, inspired by the verses of the Quran, especially the verse of "Kanz," believed—and explicitly expressed this belief—that the Islamic public treasury should not become the private property of individuals, and one should not be lavishly generous with these funds, in which the poor and needy have a right and which must be used to strengthen Islam and the interests of Muslims, nor should the legend of the palaces of "Khosrow" and "Caesar" be revived.

Furthermore, Abu Dharr believed that in those days when a group of Muslims were in severe hardship, the wealthy of the population should also be content with a simpler life and spend their wealth in the way of Allah.

If Abu Dharr had a sin, it was this; but mercenary historians, the Umayyads, and sycophantic, flattering, and religion-selling narrators have cast such unworthy slanders upon him to distort the image of this mujahid man.

Another "sin" of Abu Dharr was that he had a special love and affection for the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). This sin alone was enough for the Umayyad liars to use their hellish power to tarnish Abu Dharr's reputation. But his character was so pure, and his truthfulness and awareness of Islamic issues so clear, that he exposed all these liars! Among the strange lies attributed to Abu Dharr here to exonerate the third Caliph is what Ibn Sa'd narrates in Al-Tabaqat, saying: A group of the people of Kufa said to Abu Dharr while he was in Rabadhah: "This man (i.e., Uthman) did all these things to you; are you willing to raise a banner so that we may rise in battle against him under it?" Abu Dharr said: "No, if Uthman were to send me from the East to the West, I would be obedient to his command!" [^1] These liars paid no attention to the fact that if he were so submissive to the Caliph's command, he would not have troubled him so much that his presence in Medina was a heavy burden on the Caliph's heart, such that he could not tolerate him at all.

Even stranger than that is the statement made by the author of Al-Manar under this very verse, while referring to the incident of Abu Dharr, saying that the story of Abu Dharr shows how much freedom of opinion existed in the era of the companions (especially Uthman)! And that scholars were respected! And the Caliphs were affectionate! To the extent that Mu'awiyah did not dare say anything to Abu Dharr but wrote to his superior, the Caliph, and asked for his orders! Truly, what things does prejudice not do? Was exile to the hot, dry, and scorching land of Rabadhah—a land of death and fire—an example of respect for freedom of thought and affection for scholars? Was handing this great companion over to death considered evidence of freedom of belief? If Mu'awiyah, out of fear of the flood of public opinion, did not single-handedly plot against Abu Dharr, is that evidence that he respected him?

And again, among the wonders of this story is that the defenders of the Caliph say:

The exile of Abu Dharr was carried out according to the legal principle of "prioritizing the prevention of harm over the acquisition of benefit," for although Abu Dharr's presence in Medina had great benefits and people benefited greatly from his knowledge, Uthman believed that his staying in Medina, due to his inflexible and harsh way of thinking regarding wealth, would be a source of harm; therefore, he overlooked the benefits of his presence and sent him out of Medina. And since both Abu Dharr and Uthman were mujtahids, no criticism applies to the action of either! [^1] Truly, we do not know what harm would have resulted from Abu Dharr's presence in Medina?

Is returning the people to the Sunnah of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) a harm?

Why did Abu Dharr not criticize the first and second Caliphs, who did not have Uthman's programs in financial matters?

Was returning the people to the financial programs of early Islam a source of corruption?

Did the exile of Abu Dharr and the cutting off of his truth-telling tongue become a source of reform?

Did the continuation of Uthman's work, especially in financial matters, not lead to a massive explosion of which he himself became a victim?

Was this the harm, and leaving it the benefit?! But what can be done? When prejudice enters through one door, logic flees through the other.

In any case, the way and path of this great companion are not hidden from any fair researcher, and there is no logical way to exonerate the third Caliph from the harassment he inflicted upon Abu Dharr.

The Punishment of the Hoarders!

In the next verse, it refers to one of the punishments for such individuals in the other world, saying: "The Day when it will be heated in the fire of Hell and seared therewith will be their foreheads, their flanks, and their backs" (يَوْمَ يُحْمى‌ عَلَيْها فِي نارِ جَهَنَّمَ فَتُكْوى‌ بِها جِباهُهُمْ وَ جُنُوبُهُمْ وَ ظُهُورُهُمْ).

And at that moment, the angels of punishment will say to them: "This is what you hoarded for yourselves and turned into Kanz, and did not spend in the way of Allah on the needy" (هذا ما كَنَزْتُمْ لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ).

"Now taste what you used to hoard" and realize its grim consequences (فَذُوقُوا ما كُنْتُمْ تَكْنِزُونَ).

This verse once again emphasizes the truth that human actions do not perish but remain, and it is they that will be manifested before man in the other world, becoming the source of his joy and happiness or his pain and suffering.

As for why, among all the body parts, only the "forehead," "back," and "flank" are mentioned in the above verse, there is discussion among commentators. However, it is narrated from Abu Dharr that he used to say: "This is because the scorching heat penetrates the space behind these three points and encompasses their entire being (until the heat circulates in their insides)." [^1] It has also been said that this is because they reacted to the needy with these three members: sometimes they would frown (forehead), sometimes they would turn away and deviate as a sign of indifference (flank), and sometimes they would turn their backs to them! Therefore, these three points of their bodies are branded with their hoards of gold and silver.

At the end of this discussion, it is appropriate to mention a literary point present in the verse: we read "يَوْمَ يُحْمى‌ عَلَيْها" (the Day when it will be heated upon them), meaning on that day, fire is poured over the coins so they become hot and scorching. Usually, in such cases, the word "upon" (على) is not used; for example, it is said "the iron is heated."

This change in expression might be to indicate the extraordinary burning nature of the coins, because if a coin is thrown into a fire, it does not become as hot and scorching as if it were placed under the fire and fire were poured over it. The Quran does not say the coins are placed in the fire, but says they are placed under the fire so they become well-melted and scorching. This is a vivid expression that recounts the intensity of the punishment for such cold-hearted hoarders of wealth.

Ayahs

إِنَّ عِدَّةَ ٱلشُّهُورِ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱثْنَا عَشَرَ شَهْرًا فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ يَوْمَ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ مِنْهَآ أَرْبَعَةٌ حُرُمٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ ۚ فَلَا تَظْلِمُوا۟ فِيهِنَّ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۚ وَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ كَآفَّةً كَمَا يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ كَآفَّةً ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣٦)
Indeed the number of months with Allah is twelve months in Allah’s Book, the day when He created the heavens and the earth. Of these, four are sacred. That is the upright religion. So do not wrong yourselves during them. Fight all the polytheists, just as they fight you all, and know that Allah is with the Godwary. (36)
إِنَّمَا ٱلنَّسِىٓءُ زِيَادَةٌ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ ۖ يُضَلُّ بِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُحِلُّونَهُۥ عَامًا وَيُحَرِّمُونَهُۥ عَامًا لِّيُوَاطِـُٔوا۟ عِدَّةَ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ فَيُحِلُّوا۟ مَا حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ زُيِّنَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ أَعْمَـٰلِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٧)
Indeed nasee is an increase in unfaith, whereby the faithless are led [further] astray. They allow it in one year and forbid it another year, so as to fit in with the number, which Allah has made inviolable, thus permitting what Allah has forbidden. Their evil deeds appear to them as decorous, and Allah does not guide the faithless lot. (37)

Commentary

A Forced Ceasefire

Since this Surah contains detailed discussions regarding the war with the polytheists, the two verses under discussion refer to another regulation of Islamic war and Jihad, which is the respect for the Sacred Months.

First, it states: "Indeed, the number of months with Allah is twelve [lunar] months in the register of Allah [from] the day He created the heavens and the earth" (إِنَّ عِدَّةَ الشُّهُورِ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ اثْنا عَشَرَ شَهْراً فِي كِتابِ اللَّهِ يَوْمَ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ). The expression "Book of Allah" (كتاب الله) may refer to the Holy Quran or other Divine scriptures; however, considering the phrase "the day He created the heavens and the earth," it is more appropriate to interpret it as the "Book of Creation" and the universe. In any case, from the day the solar system took its current form, years and months existed. A year consists of one complete revolution of the Earth around the Sun, and a month consists of one complete revolution of the Moon around the Earth, which repeats 12 times each year.

This is, in fact, a valuable natural and unchangeable calendar that provides a natural order to the lives of all human beings and accurately regulates their historical calculations. This is considered one of Allah's great blessings to mankind, as we have discussed in detail under verse 189 of Surah Al-Baqarah (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَهِلَّةِ قُلْ هِيَ مَواقِيتُ لِلنَّاسِ وَ الْحَجِ).

Then it adds: "Of these, four are sacred," in which any kind of war and battle is forbidden (مِنْها أَرْبَعَةٌ حُرُمٌ).

Some commentators believe the prohibition of war in these four months dates back to the time of Ibrahim (Abraham) the Friend of Allah (عليه السلام), which remained in force as a tradition even during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) among the Arabs, although they would sometimes change the order of these months according to their whims and desires. However, in Islam, they are permanently fixed and unchangeable: three are consecutive—Dhu al-Qa'dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, and Muharram—and one is separate, which is the month of Rajab. In the words of the Arabs, there are three "Sard" (consecutive) and one "Fard" (solitary).

It is necessary to mention that the prohibition of war in these months applies only if the war is not imposed upon the Muslims by the enemy. In such a case, Muslims must undoubtedly rise and not remain idle, for the sanctity of the Sacred Month was not violated by them, but rather by the enemy (as explained under verse 194 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

To emphasize, it then says: "That is the correct religion [the firm and unchangeable law]" (ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ), not the incorrect custom prevalent among the Arabs who shifted them according to their whims.

It can be inferred from some narrations [1] that the prohibition of war in these four months existed not only in the religion of Ibrahim but also in Judaism, Christianity, and other Divine religions. The phrase "That is the correct religion" (ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ) might also point to this fact—that it has existed as a fixed law in all religions from the beginning.

Then it says: "So do not wrong yourselves therein" (فَلا تَظْلِمُوا فِيهِنَّ أَنْفُسَكُمْ) by violating their sanctity and making yourselves subject to worldly punishments and the penalties of the Hereafter.

However, since the prohibition of Jihad in these four months could potentially be exploited by enemies and make them bold in attacking Muslims, the next phrase adds: "And fight against the polytheists collectively as they fight against you collectively" (وَ قاتِلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ كَافَّةً كَما يُقاتِلُونَكُمْ كَافَّةً).

This means that although they are polytheists and idolaters—and polytheism is a source of division—they nevertheless fight you in a single rank. You, who are monotheists and worshippers of the One God—and monotheism is the religion of unity—are more deserving of maintaining unity against the enemy and standing in a single rank like an iron wall before them.

Finally, it says: "And know that Allah is with the righteous [those who fear Him]" (وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَ الْمُتَّقِينَ). If you are pious and implement the principles of Islamic teachings precisely, Allah guarantees your victory, for He is with the righteous.

In the second verse under discussion, it refers to an incorrect pre-Islamic tradition, namely the issue of "Nasi'" (postponing or changing the order of the Sacred Months), saying: "Indeed, the postponing [of a Sacred Month] is an increase in disbelief" (إِنَّمَا النَّسِي‌ءُ زِيادَةٌ فِي الْكُفْرِ). "By which those who have disbelieved are led [further] astray" (يُضَلُّ بِهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

"They make it lawful one year and unlawful another year to conform to the number of months Allah has made sacred" (يُحِلُّونَهُ عاماً وَ يُحَرِّمُونَهُ عاماً لِيُواطِؤُا عِدَّةَ ما حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ). That is, whenever they removed one of the Sacred Months, they would substitute another month in its place to complete the count of four months!

While through this ugly and ridiculous act, they completely destroyed the philosophy behind the prohibition of the Sacred Months and made Allah's command a plaything for their desires. Surprisingly, they were very pleased and satisfied with this action, because "the evil of their deeds has been made fair-seeming to them" (زُيِّنَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ أَعْمالِهِمْ).

Through satanic whisperings—as will be explained—they altered the Sacred Months, and strangely, they considered it a form of management for their life and livelihood or progress in war and military readiness. They would say that a long period of ceasefire reduces military skill, so a fire must be lit!

Allah leaves those people who are not worthy of guidance to themselves and withdraws His guidance from them, for "Allah does not guide the disbelieving people" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكافِرِينَ).

Key Points

1- The Philosophy of the Sacred Months

The prohibition of war in these four months was one of the ways to end long-term wars and a means to invite peace and tranquility. When combatants lay down their arms for four months of the year, and the clashing of swords or the whistling of bullets falls silent, providing an opportunity for reflection and thought, the probability of ending the war becomes very high.

Continuing an action is always different from restarting it after it has ceased; the latter is far more difficult. We do not forget how much effort was made during the twenty-year Vietnam War to establish a twenty-four-hour ceasefire at the start of the Christian New Year or similar occasions. Yet, Islam declares a four-month ceasefire for its followers every year, which is itself a sign of Islam's peace-loving spirit.

However, as we mentioned, if the enemy seeks to exploit this Islamic law and violate the sanctity of the Sacred Months, Muslims are permitted to retaliate in kind.

2- The Concept and Philosophy of "Nasi'" in the Age of Ignorance

"Nasi'" (نسیء), on the pattern of "Kathir," is derived from the root "Nasa'" (نسا), meaning to postpone (and the word itself can be a verbal noun or an infinitive). Transactions where the payment of money is delayed are called "Nasiah" (credit).

In the pre-Islamic Age of Ignorance, the Arabs would sometimes postpone one of the Sacred Months; for example, they would choose the month of Safar instead of Muharram. The ceremony for this was such that one of the leaders of the "Bani Kinanah" tribe, during the Hajj rituals in the land of Mina, would stand before a relatively large gathering and, upon the people's request, would declare: "I have postponed the month of Muharram this year and chosen Safar in its place!" It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that the first person to establish this tradition was "Amr ibn Luhay," while others say it was "Qalammas," a man from Bani Kinanah.

According to some, the philosophy behind this was that enduring three consecutive Sacred Months (Dhu al-Qa'dah, Dhu al-Hijjah, and Muharram) was sometimes difficult for them. They imagined it weakened the fighting spirit and caused stagnation for the soldiers, as the pre-Islamic Arabs had an extraordinary fondness for "plunder, bloodshed, and war." In fact, "war and plunder" formed a part of their very lives, and enduring a three-month consecutive ceasefire was unbearable for them. Therefore, they tried to at least separate Muharram from these three months.

It is also possible that when Dhu al-Hijjah fell in the summer, the Hajj became difficult for them. We know that Hajj and its rituals were not merely a matter of worship for the pre-Islamic Arabs; rather, this great ceremony, remembered from the time of Ibrahim (عليه السلام), was considered a massive congress that boosted their trade and economy, and they reaped various benefits from this gathering. Thus, they would change the timing of Dhu al-Hijjah at will and substitute another month when the weather was more favorable. It is possible that both philosophies are correct.

In any case, this act caused the fires of war to remain burning, the philosophy of the Sacred Months to be trampled upon, and the Hajj rituals to become a plaything in the hands of individuals and a means for their material interests.

The Quran considers this an "increase in disbelief," because in addition to their polytheism and "doctrinal disbelief," they committed "practical disbelief" by violating this command. Specifically, they committed two forbidden acts: they made what Allah had forbidden lawful, and they made what Allah had made lawful forbidden.

3- Unity of Word Against the Enemy

In the above verses, the Quran commands that when fighting the enemy, Muslims should battle them collectively and in a single rank. From this command, it is understood that they should coordinate even their political, cultural, economic, and military struggles against them. Only under the shadow of such unity, which stems from the spirit of Islamic Monotheism (Tawhid), do they have the ability to achieve victory against the enemy. This is the very command that has long been forgotten and is considered one of the causes of the decline of Muslims.

4- How Do Evil Deeds Appear Beautiful?

The human conscience, when intact, distinguishes well between good and evil. However, when one knowingly steps onto the path of sin and wrongdoing, the light of conscience grows dimmer and dimmer until the ugliness and vileness of sin gradually vanish.

If one continues this path, eventually evil deeds appear beautiful to them, and beautiful deeds appear ugly! This is exactly what is referred to in the above verses and other verses of the Quran.

Sometimes the "beautification of evil deeds" is attributed to Satan, as in Surah An-Nahl, verse 63: "So Satan made their deeds fair-seeming to them" (فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ). Sometimes it is mentioned in the passive voice, as in the verse above, where the agent could be the whisperings of Satan or the rebellious self (Nafs). Sometimes it is attributed to "partners" (i.e., idols), as in Surah Al-An'am, verse 137. And sometimes it is even attributed to Allah, as in Surah An-Naml, verse 4: "Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter—We have made their deeds fair-seeming to them" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ).

We have stated many times that attributing such matters to Allah is because these are the consequences of the person's own actions, and the properties of all things are in Allah's hands, as He is the Cause of all causes. We have also said that such attributions do not contradict the issue of human free will and choice in any way.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 122.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَا لَكُمْ إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمُ ٱنفِرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ٱثَّاقَلْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ أَرَضِيتُم بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ فَمَا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ (٣٨)
O you who have faith! What is the matter with you that when you are told: ‘Go forth in the way of Allah,’ you sink heavily to the ground? Are you pleased with the life of this world instead of the Hereafter? But the wares of the life of this world compared with the Hereafter are but insignificant. (38)
إِلَّا تَنفِرُوا۟ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا وَيَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّوهُ شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٣٩)
If you do not go forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment, and replace you with another people, and you will not hurt Him in the least, and Allah has power over all things. (39)

Circumstances of Revelation

It has been narrated from Ibn Abbas and others that the above verses were revealed concerning the Battle of Tabuk, at the time when the Prophet returned from Ta'if to Medina and prepared the people for battle against the Romans.

It is mentioned in Islamic narrations that the Prophet usually did not clarify his military objectives and final goals to the Muslims before the start of a war, so that Islamic military secrets would not fall into the hands of enemies. However, in the case of Tabuk, because the matter took a different form, he explicitly announced beforehand: "We are going to fight the Romans." This was because fighting the Eastern Roman Empire was not a simple task like fighting the polytheists of Mecca or the Jews of Khaybar; the Muslims had to be fully prepared and engage in self-building for this great confrontation.

Furthermore, the distance between Medina and the land of the Romans was very great, and above all, it was the summer season, the time of heat, and the harvest of grain and fruits.

All these factors combined made going to the battlefield exceptionally difficult for the Muslims, to the extent that some showed hesitation and doubt in responding to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call!

The above verses were revealed, warning the Muslims in a decisive and crushing tone, declaring the danger and preparing them for this great battle. [1]

Commentary

Once Again, Moving Toward the Field of Jihad

As we mentioned in the circumstances of revelation, the above verses refer to the events of the Battle of Tabuk.

"Tabuk" is a region between Medina and Sham (Greater Syria) which is now considered the border of the country of Saudi Arabia, and in those days, it was considered near the territory of the Eastern Roman Empire which dominated the Levant. [2]

[1]: This circumstance of revelation has been brought in summary by many commentators such as al-Tabari in Majma' al-Bayan, Fakhr al-Razi in al-Tafsir al-Kabir, and al-Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani.

[2]: The distance from Tabuk to Medina is recorded as 610 kilometers and to Sham as 692 kilometers.

This event took place in the ninth year of the Hijra, about one year after the Conquest of Mecca. Since the confrontation in this field was with one of the superpowers of the world at that time, and not with a small or large Arab group, a group of Muslims were terrified of participating in this field. Therefore, the ground was completely ready for the poisonous propaganda and whispers of the hypocrites; they spared no effort to weaken the morale of the believers.

The season for picking fruit and harvesting crops had arrived, and for a people with a limited agricultural and pastoral life, these days were considered fateful, as their welfare for the entire year depended on them.

The long distance and the heat of the weather, as we mentioned, also contributed to these deterrent factors.

At this point, heavenly revelation came to the aid of the people, and the verses of the Quran were revealed one after another to counter these negative factors.

In the first verse under discussion, the Quran invites the people to Jihad with the utmost intensity. Sometimes it speaks to them in the language of encouragement, sometimes in the language of blame and reproach, and sometimes in the language of threat, approaching from every door to prepare them.

First, It says: "O you who have believed, what is [the matter] with you that, when it is said to you, 'Go forth in the cause of Allah,' you adhere heavily to the earth?" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَا لَكُمْ إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمُ انْفِرُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ اثَّاقَلْتُمْ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ).

"Aththaqaltum" (اثَّاقَلْتُمْ) is derived from the root "thiql" (ثقل) meaning heaviness, and the phrase "you adhere heavily to the earth" is an allusion to the desire to remain in the homeland and not move toward the field of Jihad, or an allusion to the inclination toward the material world and clinging to the glitz and glamour of the world. In any case, this was the state of a group of weak-faith Muslims, not all of them, nor the true Muslims and lovers of Jihad in the way of Allah.

Then, with reproachful words, It says: "Are you satisfied with the life of this world—this lowly, fleeting, and unstable life—rather than the expansive and eternal life of the Hereafter?" (أَرَضِيتُمْ بِالْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا مِنَ الْآخِرَةِ).

"While the enjoyments and comforts of the life of this world are but a negligible thing compared to the life of the Hereafter" (فَمَا مَتَاعُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ).

How can a rational person submit to such a loss-making exchange? And how can one lose an exceptionally precious commodity for the sake of obtaining a meager and low-value one?

Then, the matter is raised beyond a reproachful tone and takes the form of a serious threat, saying: "If you do not go forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment" (إِلَّا تَنْفِرُوا يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَاباً أَلِيماً).

And if you think that by your withdrawal and turning your backs on the field of Jihad, the wheel of Islam's progress will stop and the light of God's religion will be extinguished, you are gravely mistaken, because: "He will replace you with another people—believing, determined, and obedient to His command" (وَيَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْماً غَيْرَكُمْ); a group that is different from you in every respect—not only their personalities but their faith, will, courage, and obedience are different from yours.

"And you will not harm Him at all" through this (وَلَا تَضُرُّوهُ شَيْئاً).

This is a reality, not an imaginary conversation or a far-fetched wish, because "Allah is over all things competent," and whenever He wills the victory of His pure religion, He will undoubtedly bring it to fruition (وَاللَّهُ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Here, several points should be noted

1- In the two verses above, the issue of Jihad is emphasized through seven ways: First, through addressing the believers. Second, through the command to move to the field of Jihad. Third, by the expression "in the way of Allah" (فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ). Fourth, the rhetorical question regarding exchanging the Hereafter for the world. Fifth, the threat of "painful punishment."

Sixth, the threat that He will remove you from the scene and put others in your place. And seventh, by drawing attention to the infinite power of God and that your sluggishness does not cause any harm to the progress of affairs; rather, any harm will befall yourselves.

2- It is clearly understood from the above verses that the attachment of the Mujahideen to the life of this world makes them sluggish in the matter of Jihad. True Mujahideen must be pious, ascetic people who are indifferent to worldly glitz. Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام), in the course of a supplication he makes for the border guards of Islamic countries, says:

"O Allah! Make them forget, when they meet the enemy, the mention of their deceptive world, and erase from their hearts the thoughts of tempting wealth" (so that they may fight for You with hearts overflowing with Your love).

Truly, if we have a correct understanding of the state of the world and the Hereafter and the nature of these two lives, we would know that this one is so limited and insignificant compared to that one that it does not count. In a hadith narrated from the Prophet in this regard, we read:

"By Allah, the world in comparison to the Hereafter is only like one of you putting his finger into the sea and then lifting it; let him see what [amount of water] he returns with!"

3- Regarding who is meant by the "people" (قَوْماً) whom Allah referred to in the above verses, some commentators have said: "the Persians," and some have said it refers to the people of "Yemen," each of whom had a very large share in the progress of Islam with their exceptional courage. Some consider it an allusion to those who accepted Islam after the revelation of these verses and showed devotion to it with heart and soul.

Ayah

إِلَّا تَنصُرُوهُ فَقَدْ نَصَرَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِذْ أَخْرَجَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ثَانِىَ ٱثْنَيْنِ إِذْ هُمَا فِى ٱلْغَارِ إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ لَا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَنَا ۖ فَأَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُۥ عَلَيْهِ وَأَيَّدَهُۥ بِجُنُودٍ لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا وَجَعَلَ كَلِمَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلسُّفْلَىٰ ۗ وَكَلِمَةُ ٱللَّهِ هِىَ ٱلْعُلْيَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٤٠)
If you do not help him, then Allah certainly helped him when the faithless expelled him, as one of two [refugees], when the two of them were in the cave, he said to his companion, ‘Do not grieve; Allah is indeed with us.’ Then Allah sent down His composure upon him, and strengthened him with hosts you did not see, and He made the word of the faithless the lowest; and the word of Allah is the highest; and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (40)

Commentary

Allah did not leave His Prophet alone in the most critical moments.

In the preceding verses, as mentioned, the issue of Jihad against the enemy was emphasized through several means, including the point that you should not imagine that if you withdraw from Jihad and from aiding the Prophet, his work and the cause of Islam will come to a halt.

The verse under discussion pursues this subject, stating: "If you do not aid him, Allah—who aided him in the most difficult situations and the most complex conditions in a miraculous manner—is capable of supporting him again" (إِلَّا تَنْصُرُوهُ فَقَدْ نَصَرَهُ اللَّهُ) [^1]. This refers to the time when the polytheists of "Mecca" had hatched a dangerous conspiracy to destroy the Prophet. As explained in the commentary of verse 30 of Surah Al-Anfal, the final decision, after extensive preliminary meetings, was that a large number of swordsmen from various Arab tribes would surround the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) house at night and attack him collectively in the morning, putting him to the sword in his bed.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), having been informed of this plot by Allah's command, prepared to leave "Mecca" and migrate to "Medina." However, to ensure the Quraysh disbelievers would not capture him, he first took refuge in the "Cave of Thawr," located south of Mecca in the opposite direction of the road to Medina. During this journey, "Abu Bakr" was also accompanying the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

The enemies exerted great effort to find the Prophet but returned disappointed and hopeless. After staying in the "cave" for three days and nights and ensuring the enemy's withdrawal, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) moved toward Medina by a bypass route at night. After several days and nights, he reached Medina safely, and a new chapter in the history of Islam began.

The above verse refers to one of the most critical moments of this historical journey, saying: "Allah aided His Prophet at the time when the disbelievers drove him out" (إِذْ أَخْرَجَهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

Of course, the disbelievers' intention was not merely to drive him out of "Mecca," but rather they had decided to kill him. However, since the result of their actions was the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) departure from Mecca, this act is attributed to them.

Then it says: "This was while he was the second of two" (ثانِيَ اثْنَيْنِ), referring to the fact that there was no one with him except one person, which demonstrates his ultimate loneliness in this perilous journey; and his traveling companion was Abu Bakr.

"When the two took refuge in the cave, namely the Cave of Thawr" (إِذْ هُما فِي الْغارِ).

At that moment, fear and anxiety overtook the Prophet's companion, and the Prophet comforted him, "and said: Do not grieve, for Allah is with us" (إِذْ يَقُولُ لِصاحِبِهِ لا تَحْزَنْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ مَعَنا).
