"At this moment, Allah sent down the spirit of tranquility and assurance—which He would descend upon His Prophet in sensitive and dangerous moments—upon him" (فَأَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ).

"And He aided him with soldiers that you could not see" (وَ أَيَّدَهُ بِجُنُودٍ لَمْ تَرَوْها).

These unseen forces may refer to the angels who protected the Prophet during this fearful and dangerous journey, or those who rushed to his aid in the battles of "Badr," "Hunayn," and the like.

"And ultimately, He made the plan, goal, and school of the disbelievers the lowest, while the plan and word of Allah became the highest" (وَ جَعَلَ كَلِمَةَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا السُّفْلى‌ وَ كَلِمَةُ اللَّهِ هِيَ الْعُلْيا). This indicates that their conspiracies were shattered, their superstitious creed was folded away, the light of Allah became manifest everywhere, and victory in all aspects was granted to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) of Islam.

Why should it not be so, while "Allah is both All-Mighty and All-Wise"? With His wisdom, He shows the paths of victory to His Prophet, and with His power, He aids him (وَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

[^1]: From a grammatical perspective, this sentence has an omitted part and was originally: "If you do not aid him, Allah will aid him..." because a past tense verb whose meaning has occurred in the past cannot serve as the result of a conditional statement unless it is a past tense verb that carries a future/present meaning.

The Story of the Companion of the Cave

The event of "Abu Bakr's" companionship with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) on this journey and the subtle references made to this subject in the above verse have sparked various discussions among Shia and Sunni commentators.

Some have taken the path of excess (exaggeration) and others the path of neglect. "Fakhr al-Razi," in his commentary, has attempted with a particular bias to derive twelve virtues for "Abu Bakr" from the above verse, and to increase the number, he has woven together unrelated matters to such an extent that addressing their explanation might be considered a waste of time.

Others insist that several rebukes can be inferred from the verse.

First, it must be seen whether the word "Sahib" (companion) is evidence of virtue. Apparently, it is not, because linguistically "Sahib" means "associate" or "traveling companion" in an absolute sense, regardless of whether this associate is a good person or a bad one. As we read in verse 37 of Surah Al-Kahf regarding the story of those two men, one of whom was a believer and monotheist and the other a disbeliever and polytheist: "قالَ لَهُ صاحِبُهُ وَ هُوَ يُحاوِرُهُ أَ كَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ مِنْ تُرابٍ": "His companion said to him while he was conversing with him: 'Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust?'"

Some also insist that the pronoun "Alayhi" (upon him) in the phrase "فَأَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَيْهِ" (Allah sent down His tranquility upon him) refers to "Abu Bakr," arguing that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) did not need tranquility and peace; therefore, the descent of tranquility was for his companion (Abu Bakr).

However, considering the subsequent phrase which says: "وَ أَيَّدَهُ بِجُنُودٍ لَمْ تَرَوْها" (and He aided him with invisible soldiers), and considering the unity of the referent of the pronouns, it becomes clear that the pronoun "Alayhi" also refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). It is a mistake to imagine that "Sakina" (tranquility) is only related to cases of grief and sorrow; rather, we repeatedly read in the Quran that Sakina descended upon the person of the Prophet when he was in difficult and complex conditions. For instance, in verse 26 of this same Surah regarding the Battle of "Hunayn," we read: "ثُمَّ أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى‌ رَسُولِهِ وَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ" (Then Allah sent down His tranquility upon His Messenger and upon the believers). Similarly, in verse 26 of Surah Al-Fath, we read: "فَأَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلى‌ رَسُولِهِ وَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ," while in the preceding sentences of these two verses, there is no mention of grief or sorrow, but rather mention of the complexity of the situation.

In any case, the verses of the Quran show that the descent of Sakina occurred during severe hardships, and undoubtedly the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was going through difficult moments in the "Cave of Thawr."

Even more strange is that some have said the phrase "أَيَّدَهُ بِجُنُودٍ لَمْ تَرَوْها" (He aided him with soldiers you did not see) refers to "Abu Bakr"!! This is while the entire discussion of this verse revolves around Allah's aid to the Prophet, and the Quran wants to clarify that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is not alone, and if you do not aid him, Allah will aid him. How could one abandon the person who is the central focus of the entire discussion and turn to someone who is mentioned only as a subordinate? This claim shows that biases have prevented even paying attention to the meaning of the verse.

Ayahs

ٱنفِرُوا۟ خِفَافًا وَثِقَالًا وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٤١)
Go forth, whether [armed] lightly or heavily, and wage jihad with your possessions and persons in the way of Allah. That is better for you, should you know. (41)
لَوْ كَانَ عَرَضًا قَرِيبًا وَسَفَرًا قَاصِدًا لَّٱتَّبَعُوكَ وَلَـٰكِنۢ بَعُدَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلشُّقَّةُ ۚ وَسَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَوِ ٱسْتَطَعْنَا لَخَرَجْنَا مَعَكُمْ يُهْلِكُونَ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (٤٢)
Were it an accessible gain or a short journey, they would have surely followed you; but the distance seemed too far to them. Yet they will swear by Allah: ‘If we could, we would have surely gone forth with you.’ They [merely] destroy themselves. Allah knows that they are indeed liars. (42)

Commentary

The Mentality of the Greedy and Indolent

We have stated that the Battle of "Tabuk" held an exceptional status and was accompanied by conditions that were entirely difficult and complex. For this reason, a group of those weak in faith or the hypocrites hesitated to participate in this field. In the preceding verses, Allah rebuked a group of believers, asking why they showed heaviness and reluctance when the command for Jihad was issued. He stated that the command for Jihad is for your own benefit; otherwise, Allah is capable of replacing irresolute and indolent people with a courageous, faithful, and determined nation. In fact, even without them, He is capable of protecting His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), just as He protected him in the story of the "Cave of Thawr" and "Laylat al-Mabit."

It is remarkable that a few "spider webs" spun across the mouth of the "cave" caused the thoughts of the stubborn and rebellious enemies to deviate, leading them to turn back from the entrance of the cave while the Prophet of Allah remained safe. When Allah can alter the course of human history with a few spider webs, what need does He have for the help of this person or that, such that they should act with pretension? In reality, all these commands are for their own perfection and progress, not to fulfill any need of Allah.

Following this discourse, He once again invites the believers comprehensively toward Jihad and rebukes those who are negligent.

First, He says: "Go forth, whether light or heavy" (انْفِرُوا خِفافاً وَ ثِقالًا).

"Khifaf" is the plural of "khafif" (light) and "thiqal" is the plural of "thaqil" (heavy). These two words possess a comprehensive meaning that encompasses all human conditions. That is, whether you are young or old, single or married, with few dependents or many, rich or poor, free from troubles or entangled in them, whether you have crops, gardens, and trade or not—in any case, in every state and in every situation, it is mandatory upon you that when the command for Jihad is issued, you all respond to this liberating call, turn your eyes away from every other task, take up arms, and move toward the battlefield.

There is no evidence for the claim made by some commentators who have limited these two words to only one of the aforementioned meanings; rather, each of them is one of the instances of this broad concept.

Then He adds: "And strive with your wealth and your lives in the cause of Allah" (وَ جاهِدُوا بِأَمْوالِكُمْ وَ أَنْفُسِكُمْ).

This means an all-encompassing and pervasive Jihad, because they were facing a powerful enemy considered one of the superpowers of that time, and without such effort, victory was impossible.

However, so that no one mistakenly thinks these sacrifices benefit Allah, He says: "That is better for you, if you only knew" (ذلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ).

Meaning, if you knew that Jihad is the key to loftiness and honor, and the eliminator of weakness and humiliation.

If you knew that no nation in the world will reach true freedom and justice without Jihad.

And if you knew that the way to attain the pleasure of Allah, eternal happiness, and all kinds of Divine blessings and favors lies in this sacred public movement and all-out sacrifice!

Then, He turns the discussion toward the sluggish, lazy, and weak-of-faith individuals who resorted to all kinds of excuses to avoid presence in this great arena, and He says to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Had it been a near gain and an easy journey, they would have followed you" to reach the goods of this world, and they would have rushed to sit at such a ready table (لَوْ كانَ عَرَضاً قَرِيباً وَ سَفَراً قاصِداً لَاتَّبَعُوكَ) [1]. "But the distance was too far for them," so they show weakness and bring forth excuses (وَ لكِنْ بَعُدَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الشُّقَّةُ) [2]. What is strange is that they do not suffice with mere excuses, but rather "they will soon swear by Allah: 'If we could have, we would have gone forth with you'" (وَ سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِاللَّهِ لَوِ اسْتَطَعْنا لَخَرَجْنا مَعَكُمْ).

And they claim: "If you see us not coming to this field, it is due to inability, entanglement, and lack of power."

"They destroy themselves with these actions and these lies" (يُهْلِكُونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ).

"And Allah knows that they are liars" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ). They are fully capable, but because there is no "rich and easy table" and a grueling, troublesome program lies ahead, they resort to false oaths.

This issue was not limited to the Battle of "Tabuk" and the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). In every society, there exists a group of "lazy" or "hypocritical, greedy, and opportunistic" people who are always waiting for the moments of victory and results to arrive, so they can push themselves into the front row, shout loudly, tear their collars in feigned passion, and present themselves as the first mujahideen, the superior fighters, and the most compassionate individuals, in order to benefit from the fruits of others' victories without any hardship!

[1]: "Arad" (عرض) means something accidental that vanishes quickly and has no permanence; it is usually applied to the material favors of the world. "Qasid" (قاصد) means easy and simple, as it originally comes from the root "qasd" (intention), and ordinary people direct their intentions toward easy matters.

[2]: "Shuqqah" (شقة) means rocky lands or long, distant paths, the crossing of which is accompanied by hardship (mashaqqat).

Yet this same group of "passionate mujahideen" and "compassionate fighters," when difficult events arise, each flees in a different direction and fabricates excuses to justify their flight. One has become ill, another's child has fallen into a sickbed, the third's family is preoccupied with childbirth, the fourth's eyes lack sufficient vision, the fifth is busy preparing the preliminaries, and so on!

However, it is necessary for vigilant individuals and enlightened leaders to identify this group from the beginning, and if they are not reformable, to expel them from their ranks!

Ayahs

عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنكَ لِمَ أَذِنتَ لَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكَ ٱلَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا۟ وَتَعْلَمَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٤٣)
May Allah excuse you! Why did you grant them leave [to stay behind] before those who told the truth were evident to you and you had ascertained the liars? (43)
لَا يَسْتَـْٔذِنُكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ أَن يُجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٤)
Those who believe in Allah and the Last Day do not ask you for leave [exempting them] from waging jihad with their possessions and their persons, and Allah knows best the Godwary. (44)
إِنَّمَا يَسْتَـْٔذِنُكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَٱرْتَابَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِى رَيْبِهِمْ يَتَرَدَّدُونَ (٤٥)
Only those seek a leave [of exemption] from you who do not believe in Allah and the Last Day, and whose hearts are in doubt, so they waver in their doubt. (45)

Commentary

Strive to Recognize the Hypocrites

From the above verses, it is understood that a group of hypocrites came to the Prophet and, after presenting various excuses and even taking oaths, requested that he excuse them from participating in the battlefield of "Tabuk," and the Prophet granted them permission.

In the first verse under discussion, Allah addresses His Prophet with a form of reproach and says: "May Allah forgive you! Why did you give them leave [to stay behind] until those who were truthful were evident to you and you knew the liars?" (عَفَا اللَّهُ عَنْكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ).

"Why did you not wait so that those who speak the truth could be distinguished from those who lie," and so that you might realize their true nature? (حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكَ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَ تَعْلَمَ الْكاذِبِينَ).

There is a discussion among commentators regarding whether the above reproach—which is coupled with the declaration of the Lord's forgiveness—is evidence that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) permission was a wrong act, or merely a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla), or neither.

Some have gone to such extremes, and even spoke boldly and disrespectfully regarding the holy status of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), that they considered the above verse as evidence for the possibility of him committing a sin or disobedience. At the very least, they failed to observe the etiquette that the Great Allah maintained toward His Prophet in this expression—where He first speaks of "forgiveness" and then "questions" him—and they have fallen into a strange misguidance.

The fair assessment is that there is no evidence in this verse for the commission of any sin by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), even in the literal appearance of the verse. This is because all indications show that whether the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) gave them permission or not, this group of hypocrites would not have participated in the field of "Tabuk." Even if they had participated, they would not have solved any problems for the Muslims; rather, they would have added to their troubles, as we read a few verses later: "Had they gone forth with you, they would have added to you nothing but confusion" (لَوْ خَرَجُوا فِيكُمْ ما زادُوكُمْ إِلَّا خَبالًا). That is, if they had moved with you, they would have done nothing but cause evil, corruption, slander, tale-bearing, and the creation of discord!

Therefore, no interest of the Muslims was lost by the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) permission. The only thing involved here was that if the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had not given them permission, their cover would have been blown sooner and the people would have become acquainted with their true nature earlier. However, this matter was not such that its loss would constitute a sin. Perhaps it can only be called tark al-awla (leaving the better), in the sense that while the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) granting of permission under those circumstances and in the face of the hypocrites' oaths and insistence was not a bad act, withholding permission would have been even better so that this group could be identified sooner.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that the said reproach and address have a metaphorical or idiomatic aspect, and even tark al-awla was not involved. Rather, the purpose was to describe the hypocritical spirit of the hypocrites through a subtle and metaphorical expression.

This point can be clarified with an example: Suppose an oppressor wants to slap your child's face, and one of your friends grabs his hand. You would not only not be upset by this action but would also be happy. However, to prove the inner ugliness of the opponent, you say to your friend in a reproachful tone: "Why did you not let him slap so that all people would recognize this cold-hearted hypocrite?!" Your goal with this statement is only to prove his cold-heartedness and hypocrisy, which has appeared in the guise of reproaching the defending friend.

Another point remaining in the interpretation of the verse is: Did the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) not know the hypocrites, such that Allah says: "You should not have given them leave so that their condition would become clear to you"?

Answer

The answer to this question is that, firstly, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) did not have knowledge of this group's condition through ordinary means, and "knowledge of the unseen" (ilm al-ghayb) is not sufficient for judging matters; rather, their status must be clarified through ordinary evidence. Secondly, the goal was not only for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to know, but perhaps the goal was for all Muslims to become aware, even though the speech is addressed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Then, He proceeds to describe one of the signs of the believers and the hypocrites, saying: "Those who believe in Allah and the Last Day do not ask leave of you to be excused from striving with their wealth and their lives" (لا يَسْتَأْذِنُكَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ أَنْ يُجاهِدُوا بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ).

Rather, when the command for Jihad is issued, they hasten toward it without hesitation or lethargy. Their very faith in Allah and their responsibilities toward Him, and faith in the Court of Resurrection, invites them to this path and closes the way to making excuses and seeking pretexts.

"And Allah is well aware of the righteous, and He is fully cognizant of their intentions and deeds" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِالْمُتَّقِينَ).

Then He says: "Only those ask leave of you [to stay behind] who do not believe in Allah and the Last Day" (إِنَّما يَسْتَأْذِنُكَ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ).

Then, to emphasize their lack of faith, He says: "And whose hearts are in doubt" (وَ ارْتابَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ).

"So in their doubt they waver"—sometimes taking a step forward and sometimes turning back, constantly living in bewilderment and confusion (فَهُمْ فِي رَيْبِهِمْ يَتَرَدَّدُونَ). Although the above attributes are mentioned in the present continuous form, the purpose is to describe the characteristics and state of the hypocrites and believers, and there is no difference between past, present, and future in this context.

In any case, the believers, in the light of their faith, possess a firm and unbreakable resolve; they have seen the path clearly, their destination is known, and their goal is specified. For this reason, they move forward with a steadfast will, without doubt or hesitation, and with firm steps.

But the hypocrites, because their goal is dark and unspecified, are caught in bewilderment and confusion, and are always looking for an excuse to escape the burden of responsibilities.

These two signs were not exclusive to the "believers" and "hypocrites" of the early Islamic era and the battlefield of "Tabuk"; rather, even today, "true believers" can be distinguished from "false claimants" by these two traits: the believer is brave and determined, while the hypocrite is cowardly, fearful, bewildered, and an excuse-maker!

Ayahs

۞ وَلَوْ أَرَادُوا۟ ٱلْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا۟ لَهُۥ عُدَّةً وَلَـٰكِن كَرِهَ ٱللَّهُ ٱنۢبِعَاثَهُمْ فَثَبَّطَهُمْ وَقِيلَ ٱقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ (٤٦)
Had they desired to go forth, they would have surely made some preparations for it; but Allah was averse to arouse them, so He held them back, and it was said [to them], ‘Be seated with those who sit back.’ (46)
لَوْ خَرَجُوا۟ فِيكُم مَّا زَادُوكُمْ إِلَّا خَبَالًا وَلَأَوْضَعُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَكُمْ يَبْغُونَكُمُ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ وَفِيكُمْ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لَهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٧)
Had they gone forth with you, they would have only added to your troubles, and they would have surely spread rumours in your midst, seeking to cause sedition among you. They have some spies among you, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers. (47)
لَقَدِ ٱبْتَغَوُا۟ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ مِن قَبْلُ وَقَلَّبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأُمُورَ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَظَهَرَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ (٤٨)
They certainly sought to cause sedition earlier, and upset the matters for you, until the truth came and Allah’s command prevailed, much as they were averse. (48)

Commentary

Their Absence is Better Than Their Presence!

In the first of the aforementioned verses, another sign of their falsehood and lies is expressed. In fact, it completes the discussion from the previous verses where it stated: وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ (And Allah knows that they are surely liars). It says: "If they were truthful and ready to participate in Jihad, and were only waiting for your permission, they should have prepared all the means of Jihad, including weapons, mounts, and whatever is within their power." However, no such preparation is seen in them (وَ لَوْ أَرادُوا الْخُرُوجَ لَأَعَدُّوا لَهُ عُدَّةً).

"These are dark-hearted and faithless individuals whom Allah disliked to have participate in the glorious field of Jihad; therefore, He withheld His success (Tawfiq) from them and held them back from moving" (وَ لكِنْ كَرِهَ اللَّهُ انْبِعاثَهُمْ فَثَبَّطَهُمْ) [^1]. There is a discussion among commentators as to who the speaker of these words is: Is it Allah, or the Prophet, or their own inner selves?

However, it appears to be a creative (Takwini) decree arising from their dark and polluted inner state, necessitated by their corrupt beliefs and evil deeds. It is often seen that the requirements of a situation are expressed in the form of a command or prohibition. From the above verse, it is clearly understood that every action and intention has a consequence that inevitably affects the person, and not everyone possesses the merit and worthiness to step into great tasks and the path of Allah. Allah grants this success to those in whom He finds purity of intention, readiness, and sincerity.

[^1]: "Thabbatahum" is from the root "Tathbit," meaning to halt and prevent from performing a task.

In the next verse, it points to the reality that the non-participation of such individuals in the field of Jihad is not only not a cause for regret, but perhaps a cause for joy. This is because they would not only fail to solve any problem, but with their spirit of hypocrisy, lack of faith, and moral deviation, they would become the source of new problems.

In truth, it teaches the Muslims a great lesson: never think of increasing the "blackness of the army" (sheer numbers) and quantity, but rather think of selecting sincere and faithful individuals, even if their numbers are few. This was a lesson for the Muslims of yesterday, today, and tomorrow.

First, it says: "If they had set out with you toward the field of Jihad (Tabuk), their first sinister effect would have been that they would have added nothing to you except doubt and anxiety" (لَوْ خَرَجُوا فِيكُمْ ما زادُوكُمْ إِلَّا خَبالًا).

"Khabal" means anxiety and doubt, "Khabal" (rhyming with Ajal) means "insanity," and "Khabl" (rhyming with Tabl) means "the corruption of limbs."

Therefore, their presence with that corrupt spirit, coupled with doubt, hypocrisy, and cowardice, has no effect other than creating hesitation and suspicion and producing corruption within the army of Islam.

Furthermore, "they would have hurried to infiltrate the ranks of the army to create hypocrisy, division, and to break the bonds of unity" (وَ لَأَوْضَعُوا خِلالَكُمْ يَبْغُونَكُمُ الْفِتْنَةَ) [^1]. Then it warns the Muslims to be careful: "There are weak-faith individuals in the corners of your assembly who are quickly influenced by the words of this hypocritical group" (وَ فِيكُمْ سَمَّاعُونَ لَهُمْ).

"Samma'" refers to someone who has a high tendency to accept and listen, believing any word without investigation or scrutiny.

Therefore, the duty of the strong-faith Muslims is to watch over this weak group, lest they become prey for the wolf-like hypocrites.

There is also the possibility that "Samma'" means a spy and tale-bearer; meaning that among you are some individuals who spy for the group of hypocrites.

And at the end of the verse, it says: "Allah knows all the wrongdoers." Those who commit injustice against themselves or society, whether openly or secretly, are not hidden from the view of His knowledge (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِالظَّالِمِينَ).

[^1]: "Awda'u" is from the root "Ida'," meaning speed in movement, and here it means accelerating infiltration into the army of Islam. "Fitnah" here means division and discord.

In the next verse, it warns the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that this is not the first time this hypocritical group has engaged in poisoning minds and destruction. You must remember that they committed such acts in the past as well, and even now they use every opportunity to reach their goal. It says: "This group of hypocrites previously sought to create division and scattering among you" (لَقَدِ ابْتَغَوُا الْفِتْنَةَ مِنْ قَبْلُ).

This refers to the story of the "Battle of Uhud," where Abdullah ibn Ubayy and his companions turned back halfway and abandoned their support for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or it refers to other instances where they plotted against the person of the Prophet or individual Muslims, which Islamic history has recorded.

"They turned matters upside down for you," devising plans to disrupt the affairs of the Muslims, hold them back from Jihad, and leave you alone (وَ قَلَّبُوا لَكَ الْأُمُورَ).

But none of these conspiracies and efforts led anywhere; they were all neutralized and their arrows hit stone. "Finally, victory and triumph arrived and the Truth became manifest" (حَتَّى جاءَ الْحَقُّ وَ ظَهَرَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ).

"While they were displeased by your progress and victory" (وَ هُمْ كارِهُونَ).

However, the will and desire of servants can have the least effect against the will and providence of the Lord. Allah willed to make you victorious, to spread Your religion throughout the world, and to remove obstacles whatever they may be, and He ultimately did so.

But the important point is to know that what is stated in the above verses, like other contents of the Quran, is not exclusive to the era and time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In every society, a group of hypocrites always exists who try to disrupt people's thoughts at sensitive and decisive moments through poisoning minds and tale-bearing, to destroy the spirit of unity, and to sow the seeds of doubt and hesitation in minds. But if the society is vigilant, surely with the help of the Lord—Who has promised victory to His friends—all their plans will be neutralized and their conspiracies will be nipped in the bud, provided that they strive sincerely and watch these internal enemies with alertness and precision.

Ayah

وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ ٱئْذَن لِّى وَلَا تَفْتِنِّىٓ ۚ أَلَا فِى ٱلْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا۟ ۗ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌۢ بِٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٩)
Among them there are some who say, ‘Give me leave, and do not put me to temptation.’ Look! They have already fallen into temptation and indeed hell besieges the faithless. (49)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators has narrated that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was preparing the Muslims for the Battle of Tabuk and calling them to mobilize, one of the leaders of the "Bani Salmah" tribe named "Jadd bin Qays," who was among the ranks of the hypocrites, came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "If you permit, let me not be present in this battlefield, for I have a strong passion for women; especially if my eyes fall upon the Roman girls, I might lose my heart and be captivated (fitnah) by them! And thus, I might abandon the fight!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله gave him permission.

At this moment, the above verse was revealed, condemning that person's action. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله turned to a group from "Bani Salmah" and asked, "Who is your leader?" They replied, "Jadd bin Qays, but he is a stingy and cowardly man." He صلى الله عليه وآله said, "What disease is worse than the disease of stinginess?" Then He said, "Your leader is that fair-skinned youth, Bishr bin Bara" (who was a man of great generosity and cheerfulness).

Commentary

The Excuse-Making Hypocrites

The above occasion of revelation shows that whenever a human wants to shirk a responsibility, he creates excuses for himself using any means, just like the excuse the hypocrite Jadd bin Qays fabricated to avoid presence in the field of Jihad—claiming that beautiful Roman women might steal his heart, causing him to abandon the war and fall into a "religious complication" (shari'i issue)!

The words of Jadd bin Qays remind me of the statement of an official of a tyrant who used to say: "If we do not put the people under pressure, the salary we receive is religiously problematic for us!" Meaning, to escape this "problem," they must commit oppression and injustice against God's creation.

In any case, here the Quran addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and, in response to such disgraced excuse-seekers, says: "And among them is he who says, 'Permit me [to leave] and do not subject me to fitnah (temptation by beautiful Roman women and girls)!'" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ ائْذَنْ لِي وَ لا تَفْتِنِّي).

There is also a possibility in the commentary and occasion of revelation of the verse that he (Jadd bin Qays) wanted to excuse himself from Jihad using the pretext of his wife, children, and unprotected wealth.

However, in any case, the Quran says in response to him: "Unquestionably, into fitnah (trial and sin and opposition to God's command) they have [already] fallen. And indeed, Hell will encompass the disbelievers" (أَلا فِي الْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا وَ إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ).

This means that while they use hollow excuses—claiming they might become tainted by sin later—they are currently situated in the heart of sin, and Hell has already surrounded them. They trample upon the explicit command of Allah and His Prophet regarding the mobilization for Jihad, lest they fall into a "religious doubt"!

Here, several points should be noted

1- One of the ways to recognize the group of hypocrites in any society is to scrutinize their style of reasoning and the excuses they bring for abandoning necessary duties. The nature of these excuses clearly reveals their inner reality.

They often cling to a series of trivial, insignificant, and sometimes ridiculous and laughable subjects to ignore important and general matters. To deceive the believers, they imagine they are using the believers' own intellectual alphabet, bringing up religious issues and the commands of Allah and the Prophet, while they are drowning in sin and have taken up swords to strike at the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and His religion.

2- Commentators have various discussions regarding the interpretation of the phrase "وَ إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ" (And indeed, Hell will encompass the disbelievers). Some say this phrase is an allusion to the fact that the factors and causes for entering Hell—namely their sins—have surrounded them.

Others have said that this is among the certain events of the future which are expressed in the "past" or "present" tense, meaning that Hell will certainly encompass them in the future.

However, the possibility also exists to interpret the sentence in its literal sense and say that Hell exists right now and is located within the inner reality of this world, and they are currently in the midst of Hell, even if the command for it to take effect has not yet been issued. Just as Paradise also exists right now and encompasses everyone within the inner reality of this world; however, since the people of Paradise have an affinity with Paradise, they will be connected to it, and since the people of Hell have an affinity with Hell, they are with Hell. [^1]

Ayahs

إِن تُصِبْكَ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْكَ مُصِيبَةٌ يَقُولُوا۟ قَدْ أَخَذْنَآ أَمْرَنَا مِن قَبْلُ وَيَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُمْ فَرِحُونَ (٥٠)
If some good should befall you, it upsets them; but if an adversity befalls you, they say, ‘We had already taken our precautions in advance,’ and they go away boasting. (50)
قُل لَّن يُصِيبَنَآ إِلَّا مَا كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَنَا هُوَ مَوْلَىٰنَا ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (٥١)
Say, ‘Nothing will befall us except what Allah has ordained for us. He is our master, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust.’ (51)
قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنَآ إِلَّآ إِحْدَى ٱلْحُسْنَيَيْنِ ۖ وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ أَن يُصِيبَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِعَذَابٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦٓ أَوْ بِأَيْدِينَا ۖ فَتَرَبَّصُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّتَرَبِّصُونَ (٥٢)
Say, ‘Do you not await one of the two excellent things to happen to us? But we await that Allah shall visit on you a punishment, from Him, or by our hands. So wait! We too are waiting along with you.’ (52)

Commentary

The above verses point to one of the characteristics of the hypocrites and their signs, completing the discussion regarding the traits of the hypocrites found in the preceding and succeeding verses.

First, it says: "If good fortune touches you, it distresses them" (إِنْ تُصِبْكَ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ).

Whether this good or blessing is victory over the enemy, war spoils, or any other form of progress.

This distress is proof of their internal enmity and lack of faith. How is it possible for someone to have the slightest share of faith and yet be saddened by the victory of the Prophet of Allah or even an ordinary believer?

"But if a misfortune befalls you" and you become entangled in a problem, they say with joy: "We took our precautions beforehand," and we made the necessary decisions to save ourselves from this precipice! (وَ إِنْ تُصِبْكَ مُصِيبَةٌ يَقُولُوا قَدْ أَخَذْنا أَمْرَنا مِنْ قَبْلُ).

"And they turn away while they are rejoicing" at your defeat, misfortune, or distress when they return to their homes (وَ يَتَوَلَّوْا وَ هُمْ فَرِحُونَ).

These blind-hearted hypocrites exploit every opportunity for their own benefit and boast of their intellect and insight, claiming it was their wisdom and planning that led them not to participate in such-and-all field, and that the problems which befell others due to a lack of intellect did not affect them. They speak in such a way as if they are bursting with joy!

But you, O Prophet, respond to them in two ways—a crushing and logical response. First, "Say: Nothing will ever befall us except what Allah has ordained for us; He is our Protector," and our Wise and Merciful Guardian, who ordains nothing for us except what is good and beneficial (قُلْ لَنْ يُصِيبَنا إِلَّا ما كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَنا هُوَ مَوْلانا). Indeed, "and in Allah let the believers put their trust" (وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ).

They love only Him, seek help only from Him, prostrate at His threshold, and their support and refuge is none but Him.

This is a great mistake that the hypocrites are trapped in; they imagine that with their small intellects and weak thoughts, they can predict all problems and events and are independent of Allah's grace and mercy. They do not realize that their entire existence is like a blade of straw before a great storm of events, or like a small drop of water in a scorching desert on a summer day; if Divine grace is not a companion and helper, the weak human can achieve nothing.

"And you, O Prophet, give them this response as well: What do you expect for us except one of the two best things (goodness and happiness)?" Either we crush the enemies and return victorious from the battlefield, or we are killed and drink the draught of martyrdom with pride. Whichever occurs is welcome, for it is a source of pride and the light of our eyes (قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنا إِلَّا إِحْدَى الْحُسْنَيَيْنِ).

But conversely, "we expect for you one of two misfortunes, miseries, and calamities: either Allah will afflict you with a punishment from Himself in this world and the next, or you will be humiliated and destroyed at our hands" (وَ نَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ أَنْ يُصِيبَكُمُ اللَّهُ بِعَذابٍ مِنْ عِنْدِهِ أَوْ بِأَيْدِينا).

"So wait; we too are waiting with you." You wait for our misfortune, and we sit waiting for your misery! (فَتَرَبَّصُوا إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ مُتَرَبِّصُونَ).

Here, several points should be noted

1- Our Destiny and Our Efforts

There is no doubt that our destiny, as far as it relates to our work, effort, and striving, is in our own hands. The verses of the Quran explicitly state this subject, such as: "And that there is not for man except that [good] for which he strives" (Surah An-Najm, Verse 39) (وَ أَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى‌) and "Every soul, for what it has earned, will be retained" (Surah Al-Muddaththir, Verse 38) (كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ), among other verses. (Though the impact of striving and effort is also among the Divine norms and by His command).

However, outside the circle of our striving and effort, and where it is beyond the realm of our power, the hand of Destiny is the sole ruler. Whatever has been decreed according to the law of causality—which leads back to the Will, Knowledge, and Wisdom of the Lord—will be fulfilled.

Nevertheless, the believers and God-worshippers who believe in His Knowledge, Wisdom, Grace, and Mercy consider all these decrees to be in accordance with the "Best System" and the welfare of the servants. Everyone has a destiny proportionate to the merits they have acquired.

A hypocritical, cowardly, lazy, and scattered population is doomed to perish, and this fate is certain for them. But a believing, aware, united, and determined population has no fate but victory.

Based on what has been said, it becomes clear that the above verses neither contradict the principle of free will and choice, nor are they evidence for a deterministic fate for humans or the ineffectiveness of efforts and strivings.

2- In the Dictionary of Believers, Failure Does Not Exist.

In the last verse under discussion, we encounter a remarkably firm logic in which the primary secret of all the victories of the early Muslims is hidden. If the Prophet of Islam had no teaching or instruction other than this, it would have been sufficient to guarantee the victory of his followers: it completely erased the concept of failure and frustration from their souls and proved to them that in any case, you are victorious. If you are killed, you are victorious; and if you kill the enemy, you are still victorious.

You have two paths before you; whichever you take, you will reach the intended destination. There are absolutely no dead ends or precipices in your path. One path leads toward martyrdom, which is the pinnacle of pride for a person of faith and the highest blessing imaginable for a human—to strike a deal with God, giving one's life and purchasing an eternal and everlasting life in the proximity of the Lord and amidst His indescribable blessings.

The other path is victory over the enemy, crushing his diabolical power, and cleansing the human living environment from the evil of oppressors, tyrants, and the corrupt. This too is a great grace and a definite honor.

A soldier who enters the battlefield with this spirit never entertains the thought of fleeing or turning his back to the enemy. He fears no one and nothing; fear, terror, anxiety, and doubt find no way into his being. An army composed of such soldiers will be an invincible army! Such a spirit can only be nurtured through Islamic teachings. Today, if through correct education and training, this logic once again manifests in the souls of Muslims, they will compensate for their setbacks and defeats.

Those who study and investigate the causes of the progress of the early Muslims and the causes of today's backwardness—and sometimes consider this subject an unsolvable enigma—would do well to come and reflect a little on the above verse to find the answer to this question clearly within it.

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, when it speaks of the two failures of the hypocrites, it explains them and states them one by one. But when it speaks of the two victories of the believers, it leaves it concise and passes over it, as if it is so clear, obvious, and manifest that there is no need for explanation or statement at all. This is a beautiful and subtle point of eloquence used in the verse.

3- The Perpetual Traits of the Hypocrites

Once again, we emphasize that we should not study these verses as the presentation of a historical issue related to the past; rather, we must know that it is a lesson for our today, yesterday, and tomorrow, and for all humanity! No society is usually free of a group of hypocrites, whether few or many, and their traits are almost identical and uniform.

They are ignorant individuals who are simultaneously selfish and arrogant, attributing to themselves extraordinary intellect and insight.

They are always in pain and torment from the comfort of the people, and happy and laughing at their distress.

They always live amidst a mass of vain imaginations, doubt, uncertainty, and bewilderment; for this reason, they take one step forward and one step back.

In contrast to them, the true believers are happy with the happiness of the people and share in their grief. They never boast of their own knowledge and insight, and never consider themselves independent of Divine grace. They have hearts overflowing with the love of God, and in this path, they fear no event.

Ayahs

قُلْ أَنفِقُوا۟ طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا لَّن يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنكُمْ ۖ إِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٥٣)
Say, ‘Spend willingly or unwillingly, it shall never be accepted from you; for you are indeed a transgressing lot.’ (53)
وَمَا مَنَعَهُمْ أَن تُقْبَلَ مِنْهُمْ نَفَقَـٰتُهُمْ إِلَّآ أَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَبِرَسُولِهِۦ وَلَا يَأْتُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ إِلَّا وَهُمْ كُسَالَىٰ وَلَا يُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا وَهُمْ كَـٰرِهُونَ (٥٤)
Nothing stops their charities from being accepted except that they have no faith in Allah and His Apostle and do not perform the prayer but lazily, and do not spend but reluctantly. (54)
فَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٥٥)
So let not their wealth and children impress you: Allah only desires to punish them with these in the life of this world, and that their souls may depart while they are faithless. (55)

Commentary

These verses point to another set of characteristics of the hypocrites and the outcome of their deeds, clarifying how their actions are soulless and ineffective, yielding them no benefit. Since among righteous deeds, spending in the way of Allah (Zakat in its broad sense) and prayer (the bond between the creation and the Creator) hold a special status, the verses specifically highlight these two aspects.

First, it says: "O Prophet, tell them: Whether you spend in the way of Allah willingly and by choice, or reluctantly and out of compulsion or personal and social considerations, in any case, it will never be accepted from you hypocrites" (قُلْ أَنْفِقُوا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً لَنْ يُتَقَبَّلَ مِنْكُمْ) [^1]. Then, pointing to the reason, it states: "For you have been a rebellious people" (إِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ). Your intentions are tainted, your deeds are impure, and your hearts are dark; and Allah only accepts a deed that is pure and originates from a pure and God-fearing person.

It is clear that "fisq" (rebellion/disobedience) here is not a simple or ordinary sin, for a person might commit a sin yet still perform a sincere deed. Rather, it refers to disbelief (kufr) and hypocrisy, or the fact that their spending is contaminated with ostentation and showing off.

Furthermore, there is no obstacle to "fisq" in the above sentence carrying its broad meaning, encompassing both possibilities, as the subsequent verse will further clarify this point.

In the next verse, it once again explains the non-acceptance of their spending and says:

[^1]: The phrase "انفقوا" (spend), although in the imperative form, carries a conditional meaning. That is: "If you spend, whether by choice or compulsion, in both cases it will not be accepted."

"Nothing prevented their spending from being accepted from them except that they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger" (وَ ما مَنَعَهُمْ أَنْ تُقْبَلَ منهم نَفَقاتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ بِرَسُولِهِ). Any deed that is not coupled with faith in Allah and Monotheism is not accepted in the presence of God.

The Quran repeatedly emphasizes that the condition for the acceptance of righteous deeds is faith. Even if a deed originates from faith, but after some time the doer adopts the path of disbelief, his deed becomes "habat" (nullified), destroyed, and ineffective (we have discussed this in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 69 to 72).

After mentioning the non-acceptance of their financial spending, it refers to the state of their worship, saying: "And they do not come to prayer except lazily and with distress and heaviness" (وَ لا يَأْتُونَ الصَّلاةَ إِلَّا وَ هُمْ كُسالى).

Just as "they do not spend except reluctantly and under compulsion" (وَ لا يُنْفِقُونَ إِلَّا وَ هُمْ كارِهُونَ).

In reality, their spending is not accepted for two reasons: first, because it stems from disbelief and lack of faith; and second, because it is done reluctantly and under compulsion.

Similarly, their prayer is not accepted for two reasons: first, due to disbelief; and second, because it is performed with laziness and reluctance.

While the above sentences describe the state of the hypocrites regarding the lack of results from their deeds, they actually express another of their signs. This means that true believers can be well-recognized by the vitality they possess during worship and the desire and inclination they show toward righteous deeds, which they hasten toward sincerely.

Just as the state of the hypocrites can be discerned from the manner in which they perform their deeds, for they usually undertake good work with reluctance, discouragement, distress, and aversion, as if someone has forcibly taken their hand and is dragging them toward a good deed.

Naturally, the deeds of the first group, because they spring from the love of Allah and are accompanied by devotion, observe all etiquettes and regulations. However, the deeds of the second group, because they are out of reluctance and lack of interest, are deficient, broken, and soulless. Therefore, their differing motivations give two different forms to their actions.

In the final verse, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Let not their wealth or their children impress you" (فَلا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوالُهُمْ وَ لا أَوْلادُهُمْ), and do not think how they, despite being hypocrites, have been granted all these divine blessings.

For these are blessings only in appearance, but in reality, "Allah only intends to punish them through these things in the life of this world, and that their souls should depart while they are disbelievers" (إِنَّما يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ بِها فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ تَزْهَقَ أَنْفُسُهُمْ وَ هُمْ كافِرُونَ).

In fact, they are punished through this wealth and children (economic and human power) in two ways: First, such individuals usually have unrighteous children and unblessed wealth that become a source of pain and suffering in the life of this world. Day and night, they must toil for children who are a source of shame and distress, and struggle to preserve wealth acquired through sin. On the other hand, because they are ultimately attached to this wealth and these children and do not believe in the vast and blissful abode of the Hereafter and the world after death, letting go of all this wealth is so difficult for them that they stake their faith upon it and die in a state of disbelief, passing away in the most agonizing condition.

Wealth and children, if pure and righteous, are a blessing, happiness, and a source of comfort and ease; but if impure and unrighteous, they are suffering and a painful torment.

Key Points

  1. Some ask how it is said at the beginning of the first verse, "Whether you spend by choice or compulsion, it will not be accepted," while at the end of the second verse it is explicitly stated that they do not spend except reluctantly. Do these two not contradict each other?

The answer becomes clear by noting one point: the beginning of the first verse is actually a "conditional proposition." That is, "If you spend out of obedience or compulsion, in whatever shape or form, it will not be accepted." We know that a conditional proposition does not prove the existence of the condition; meaning, even if they were to spend out of desire and choice, it would be useless because they are faithless.

However, the end of the second verse is the statement of an "external reality," which is that they always spend reluctantly (take note).

  1. Another lesson that can be drawn from the above verses is that one should not be deceived solely by people's prayer and fasting. For the hypocrites both prayed and outwardly spent in the way of Allah. Rather, one must distinguish hypocritical prayers and spending from the pure and constructive deeds of true believers. Incidentally, with precision and scrutiny, this can often be recognized even in the outward appearance of the deed.

And we read in a Hadith: "Do not look at the length of a man's bowing (ruku') and prostration (sujud), for that is something he has become accustomed to, and if he were to leave it, he would feel uneasy. Rather, look at the truthfulness of his speech and his fulfillment of trusts." (Because truthfulness and trustworthiness stem from faith, whereas habitual bowing and prostration are compatible even with disbelief and hypocrisy).

Ayahs

وَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ إِنَّهُمْ لَمِنكُمْ وَمَا هُم مِّنكُمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ يَفْرَقُونَ (٥٦)
They swear by Allah that they belong to you, but they do not belong to you. Rather, they are a frightened lot. (56)
لَوْ يَجِدُونَ مَلْجَـًٔا أَوْ مَغَـٰرَٰتٍ أَوْ مُدَّخَلًا لَّوَلَّوْا۟ إِلَيْهِ وَهُمْ يَجْمَحُونَ (٥٧)
If they could find a refuge, or a hideout, or a hole [to creep into], they would turn to it in frantic haste. (57)

Commentary

Another Sign of the Hypocrites

In the above verses, another action and state of the hypocrites is clearly depicted. It says: "They swear by Allah that they are indeed of you" (وَ يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّهُمْ لَمِنْكُمْ).

"While they are not of you, nor do they agree with you in anything; rather, they are a people who are intensely afraid," and out of extreme fear, they conceal their disbelief and profess faith lest they be caught (وَ ما هُمْ مِنْكُمْ وَ لكِنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ يَفْرَقُونَ).

"Yafraqūn" (يفرقون) is derived from the root "faraq" (rhyming with "shafaq"), meaning intensity of fear and dread. Al-Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat that this root originally means separation and scattering; it is as if they are so terrified that it feels as though their hearts are about to scatter and shatter apart.

In reality, because they have no internal support, they are always afflicted by great fear and horror. Because of this fear and horror, they never reveal what is in their hearts. Since they do not fear Allah, they fear everything and live in a constant state of terror, whereas true believers possess a specific tranquility and courage under the shadow of faith.

In the next verse, their intense malice, enmity, and hatred toward the believers are reflected in a short but very expressive and eloquent phrase. It says: "They are such that if they could find a refuge (like a strong fortress), or access to caves in the mountains, or could find a way underground, they would hasten toward it with the utmost speed" so that they could distance themselves from you and be able to manifest their grudge and enmity (لَوْ يَجِدُونَ مَلْجَأً أَوْ مَغاراتٍ أَوْ مُدَّخَلًا لَوَلَّوْا إِلَيْهِ وَ هُمْ يَجْمَحُونَ).

"Malja’" (ملجأ) means a place of refuge, such as strong castles or the like.

"Maghārāt" (مغارات) is the plural of "maghārah," meaning "cave."

"Muddakhalan" (مدخلاً) means hidden paths, such as tunnels dug underground through which one enters a place.

"Yajmahūn" (يجمحون) is from the root "jamāḥ," meaning a rapid and intense movement that nothing can stop, similar to the movement of rebellious and headstrong horses that cannot be halted; for this reason, such a horse is called "jamūḥ."

In any case, this is one of the most eloquent expressions the Quran uses regarding the fear and horror of the hypocrites, or their malice and hatred. If they could find an escape route in the mountains or on the earth, they would distance themselves from you out of fear or enmity. However, because they have kinsfolk, property, and wealth in your environment, they are forced to grit their teeth and remain!

Ayahs

وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَلْمِزُكَ فِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ فَإِنْ أُعْطُوا۟ مِنْهَا رَضُوا۟ وَإِن لَّمْ يُعْطَوْا۟ مِنْهَآ إِذَا هُمْ يَسْخَطُونَ (٥٨)
There are some of them who blame you regarding [the distribution of] the charities: if they are given from them, they are pleased, but if they are not given from them, behold, they are displeased. (58)
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ رَضُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ سَيُؤْتِينَا ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ رَٰغِبُونَ (٥٩)
[It would have been better] if they had been pleased with what Allah and His Apostle gave them, and had said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us; Allah and His Apostle will give to us out of His grace. Indeed to Allah do we eagerly turn.’ (59)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentary al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated from Sahih Bukhari, Nasa'i, and a group of others that the Prophet ﷺ was busy distributing some assets (from spoils of war or the like) when a man from the tribe of "Bani Tamim" named "Dhu al-Khuwaysirah" arrived and shouted:

"O Messenger of Allah! Be just!" The Prophet ﷺ replied: "Woe to you! If I am not just, then who will be just?!" Umar shouted: "O Messenger of Allah, give me permission to strike his neck!" The Prophet ﷺ said: "Leave him be! He has companions such that you would consider your own prayers and fasting insignificant compared to theirs; yet, they flee from the religion just as an arrow flies from a bow!"

At this moment, the above verses were revealed, offering counsel to such individuals.

Commentary

Irrational Egoists

In the first verse above, another trait of the hypocrites is mentioned: they are never satisfied with what is rightfully theirs. They constantly expect to benefit as much as possible from the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) or public interests, whether they are entitled to it or not. Their friendship and enmity revolve around these very interests; they are pleased with whoever fills their pockets and displeased with whoever denies them for the sake of justice and the rights of others.

Right and justice have no meaning in their lexicon; or if they do, a "just" person is one who gives them more, and an "oppressor" is one who withholds the rights of others from them! In other words, they lack any social character and possess only an individualistic personality confined within the framework of their own interests, viewing everything solely from this perspective.

Therefore, He says: "And among them are some who criticize you concerning the distribution of charities" and say you have not observed justice (وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَلْمِزُكَ فِي الصَّدَقاتِ).

But in reality, it is such that they look only to their own benefits: "If they are given a share of it, they are pleased" and happy, and they consider you an executor of justice even if they are not entitled to it (فَإِنْ أُعْطُوا مِنْها رَضُوا).

"But if they are not given from it, they become enraged" and accuse you of injustice (وَ إِنْ لَمْ يُعْطَوْا مِنْها إِذا هُمْ يَسْخَطُونَ).

"But if they had been satisfied with what Allah and His Messenger gave them and said, 'Sufficient for us is Allah; Allah will soon give us of His bounty, and [so will] His Messenger; indeed, to Allah we are hopeful,' [it would have been better for them]" (وَ لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ رَضُوا ما آتاهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ وَ قالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللَّهُ سَيُؤْتِينَا اللَّهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَ رَسُولُهُ إِنَّا إِلَى اللَّهِ راغِبُونَ).

Are such people not found in Islamic societies today?

Are all people content with their legitimate rights? Do they consider someone just if he gives them only the amount they are entitled to?

Certainly, the answer to these questions is negative. To our great regret, there are still many who consider their personal interests as the scale for measuring truth and justice. They are not content with their own rights, and if someone wishes to grant everyone—especially the deprived—their legitimate rights, their outcry reaches the heavens.

Therefore, it is not necessary to turn the pages of history to recognize hypocrites; a look around us, and even a look at ourselves, can reveal our own state and that of others.

O Lord! Revive the spirit of faith within us! Put to death the thoughts of Satan and hypocrisy within us! And grant us the success to build ourselves in such a way that we are content with our own rights—neither encroaching upon others nor perceiving justice as the usurpation of the rights of others. May we always be seekers of justice and executors of justice! ...

Ayah

۞ إِنَّمَا ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتُ لِلْفُقَرَآءِ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ عَلَيْهَا وَٱلْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَفِى ٱلرِّقَابِ وَٱلْغَـٰرِمِينَ وَفِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۖ فَرِيضَةً مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٦٠)
The charities are only for the poor and the needy, and those employed to collect them, and those whose hearts are to be reconciled, and for [the freedom of] the slaves and the debtors, and in the way of Allah, and for the traveller. [This is] an ordinance from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (60)

Commentary

The Distribution of Zakat and Its Nuances

Two distinct periods are observed in Islamic history: the Meccan period, where the efforts of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims were devoted to the education and training of individuals, instruction, and propagation; and the Medinan period, in which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله embarked on the formation of an "Islamic Government" and the implementation of Islamic teachings through this righteous administration.

Undoubtedly, one of the most fundamental and necessary matters when forming a government is the establishment of a "Bayt al-Mal" (Public Treasury), through which the economic needs of the government are met—needs that exist in every government without exception.

For this reason, one of the first actions the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله took in Medina was the establishment of the Bayt al-Mal, one of whose sources was "Zakat." According to the well-known view, this ruling was legislated in the second year of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله Hijra.

Of course, as we will mention later, God willing, the ruling of Zakat had been revealed previously in Mecca, but not in the form of an obligatory collection for the Bayt al-Mal; rather, the people themselves took the initiative to pay it. However, in Medina, the command for its collection and "centralization" was issued by Allah in verse 103 of Surah At-Tawbah.

The verse under discussion, which was certainly revealed after the verse mandating the collection of Zakat (even though it is mentioned earlier in the Quran's arrangement), explains the various uses of Zakat.

Interestingly, the verse begins with the word "Innama" (Only), which signifies restriction. This indicates that certain selfish or ignorant individuals expected to receive a share of the Zakat without any entitlement; the word "Innama" serves as a rejection of all such claims. This meaning was also reflected in the two verses preceding this one, where some criticized the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for not giving them a share of the Zakat, becoming angry if deprived of it, yet expressing satisfaction when they received it.

In any case, the above verse clearly explains the actual expenditures of Zakat, putting an end to all misplaced expectations, and summarizes them into eight categories:

1- "The Poor" (Fuqara): First, It says: "Zakat is only for the poor" (إِنَّمَا الصَّدَقاتُ لِلْفُقَراءِ).

2- "The Needy" (Masakin) (وَ الْمَساكِينِ). The difference between "Faqir" (poor) and "Miskin" (needy) is a discussion that will follow at the end of the commentary on this verse.

3- "The Administrators" and collectors of Zakat (وَ الْعامِلِينَ عَلَيْها). This group consists of the employees and staff who strive for the collection of Zakat and the management of the Islamic Bayt al-Mal. What is given to them is effectively their wage or salary; therefore, poverty is by no means a condition for this group.

4- "Those whose hearts are to be reconciled" (Mu'allafatu Qulubuhum): Meaning those who do not have a strong spiritual motivation to advance Islamic goals, but through financial encouragement, their hearts can be won over and their affection gained (وَ الْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ). A further explanation regarding this group will follow.

5- "Freeing Slaves" (وَ فِي الرِّقابِ). This means a portion of the Zakat is allocated to the struggle against slavery and to ending this anti-human practice. As we have stated elsewhere, Islam's program regarding slaves is one of "gradual liberation," the ultimate result of which is the freeing of all slaves without facing undesirable social reactions. Allocating a portion of Zakat to this cause constitutes a part of this program.

6- "Paying the debts of the debtors" and those who have fallen under the burden of debt without crime or fault and are unable to repay it (وَ الْغارِمِينَ).

7- "In the cause of Allah" (وَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ). As we will point out at the end of the verse, this refers to all paths that lead to the expansion and strengthening of the Divine religion, including Jihad, propagation, and the like.

8- "The Wayfarer" (Ibn al-Sabil). Meaning travelers who, for some reason, are stranded on their journey and do not have sufficient provisions or means of transport to reach their destination. Even if they are not poor or destitute individuals, they have fallen into such a state due to theft, illness, loss of property, or other causes. Such persons must be assisted through Zakat to the extent necessary to reach their destination.

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis on the aforementioned expenditures, He says: "This is an obligation from Allah" (فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللَّهِ). Undoubtedly, this obligation is calculated, precise, and fully encompasses the interests of the individual and society, for "Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ).

Several points must be noted here

1- The Difference Between "Faqir" and "Miskin"

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether "Faqir" and "Miskin" have the same meaning and are mentioned in the verse for emphasis—making the categories of Zakat seven—or whether they have two distinct meanings.

The vast majority of commentators and jurists have accepted the second possibility. However, even among the proponents of this view, there is much debate regarding the interpretation of these two words. What seems closer to the truth is that "Faqir" refers to someone who has a financial deficiency in their life, even if they are engaged in a trade and never ask anyone for help. "Miskin," however, is someone whose need is more severe and who is unable to work, and for this reason, asks others for help.

Evidence for this is, first, the linguistic root of "Miskin," which is derived from "Sukun" (stillness), as if such a person has become stationary and grounded due to the severity of poverty.

Secondly, observing the usage of these two words in the Quran supports the above meaning. For instance, in Surah Al-Balad, verse 16, we read: "Or a needy person (Miskin) lying in the dust" (أَوْ مِسْكِيناً ذا مَتْرَبَةٍ). In Surah An-Nisa, verse 8, we read: "And when the relatives and the orphans and the needy (Masakin) are present at the division [of inheritance], give them something of it" (وَ إِذا حَضَرَ الْقِسْمَةَ أُولُوا الْقُرْبى‌ وَ الْيَتامى‌ وَ الْمَساكِينُ فَارْزُقُوهُمْ). From this expression, it is understood that "Masakin" refers to the beggars who sometimes attend such occasions.

In Surah Al-Qalam, verse 24, we read: "No needy person (Miskin) shall enter it upon you today" (أَنْ لا يَدْخُلَنَّهَا الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِسْكِينٌ), which refers to beggars.

Furthermore, the expression "feeding the Miskin" or "the food of the Miskin" in numerous verses of the Quran shows that "Masakin" are hungry individuals who are in need of even a single meal.

On the other hand, from some instances of the word "Faqir" in the Quran, it is clearly understood that dignified individuals who never ask for help but suffer from financial deficiency are included in this concept. This is seen in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 273: "[Charity is] for the poor (Fuqara) who are restricted in the way of Allah... The ignorant one thinks them wealthy because of their self-restraint" (لِلْفُقَراءِ الَّذِينَ أُحْصِرُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ... يَحْسَبُهُمُ الْجاهِلُ أَغْنِياءَ مِنَ التَّعَفُّفِ).

Beyond all this, in a narration reported by Muhammad ibn Muslim from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام or Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that they were asked about the "Faqir" and the "Miskin." He replied: "The Faqir is the one who does not ask, and the Miskin is in a more difficult state than him, the one who asks." [^1] The same content is narrated in another Hadith from Abu Basir from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, and both are explicit regarding the aforementioned concept.

Of course, some indications might suggest otherwise, but when all available evidence is considered, it becomes clear that the truth is as stated above.

2- Is it Necessary to Divide Zakat into Eight Equal Parts?

Some commentators or jurists believe that the literal meaning of the verse is that the Zakat of property must be divided into eight equal shares, each spent on its respective category, unless the amount of Zakat is so negligible that it cannot be divided into eight significant shares.

However, the overwhelming majority of jurists hold that the eight categories mentioned are the permissible avenues for spending Zakat, and dividing it equally among them is not obligatory.

The definitive practice (Sira) of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, and their companions also confirms this. Furthermore, considering that Zakat is an Islamic tax and the Islamic government is responsible for collecting it from the people, and the goal of its legislation is to meet the various needs of the Islamic society, its distribution among these eight categories naturally depends on social necessities on one hand and the discretion of the Islamic government on the other.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 6, p. 144, Chapters on the Entitled Recipients of Zakat, Chapter 1, Hadith 2.

3- When was Zakat Made Obligatory?

From various verses of the Quran, including Surah Al-A'raf verse 156, Surah An-Naml verse 3, Surah Luqman verse 4, and Surah Fussilat verse 7—all of which are Meccan Surahs—it is understood that the ruling of the obligation of Zakat was revealed in Mecca, and Muslims were duty-bound to perform this Islamic obligation. However, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to Medina and established the foundation of the Islamic government, naturally requiring the formation of a Bayt al-Mal, he was commissioned by Allah to personally collect the Zakat from the people (rather than them spending it according to their own desire and discretion).

The noble verse: "Take, [O Muhammad], from their wealth a charity..." (Surah At-Tawbah 103) was revealed at this time, and it is well-known that this was in the second year of the Hijra. Subsequently, the expenditures of Zakat were precisely explained in the verse under discussion, which is verse 60 of Surah At-Tawbah. It is not surprising that the legislation for collecting Zakat is in verse 103 while the mention of its expenditures—which is said to have been revealed in the ninth year of the Hijra—is in verse 60, because we know that the verses of the Quran were not compiled according to the chronological order of revelation, but were placed in appropriate positions by the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

4- Who are the "Mu'allafatu Qulubuhum"?

What is understood from the expression "Mu'allafatu Qulubuhum" is that one of the expenditures of Zakat is for those to whom Zakat is given to create affinity and affection. But does this refer to the disbelievers and non-Muslims who are encouraged through financial aid to secure their cooperation in Jihad? Or does it also include Muslims with weak faith?

As we have stated in jurisprudential discussions, the concept of the verse, as well as some narrations related to this matter, is broad. It encompasses all those whose affection is sought for the benefit of Islam and Muslims through financial encouragement, and there is no reason to restrict it only to disbelievers.

5- The Role of Zakat in Islam

Considering that Islam did not emerge merely as an ethical, philosophical, or creedal school, but as a "comprehensive religion" in which all material and spiritual needs are anticipated; and considering that Islam, from the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, was accompanied by the establishment of a government; and also considering that Islam pays special attention to supporting the deprived and fighting class disparity, it becomes clear that the role of the Bayt al-Mal, and Zakat as one of its sources of income, is among the most important roles.

There is no doubt that every society has individuals who are incapacitated, ill, orphans without guardians, the disabled, and the like, who must be supported.

Furthermore, to preserve its existence against enemy aggression, it needs mujahid soldiers whose expenses are paid by the government. Likewise, the employees of the Islamic government, the judges, as well as the media apparatus and religious centers, each require expenditures that cannot be managed without a regular and reliable financial backing.

For this reason, in Islam, the issue of Zakat—which is effectively a type of "tax on income and production" and a "tax on stagnant wealth"—holds a special importance, to the extent that it is placed alongside the most important acts of worship. In many instances, it is mentioned together with prayer (Salah) and is even considered a condition for the acceptance of prayer! We even read in Islamic narrations that if the Islamic government demands Zakat from a person or persons and they resist the government and refuse, they are considered apostates. If advice does not benefit them, the use of military force against them is permissible. The story of the "People of Riddah" (the group that refused to pay Zakat after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Caliph of the time fought them, a struggle even Ali عليه السلام endorsed and in which he personally served as a standard-bearer) is famous in Islamic history.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever withholds a Qirat of Zakat is neither a believer nor a Muslim, and has no dignity!" [^1] It is noteworthy that it is understood from the narrations that the "limits" and "amount" of Zakat have been so precisely determined in Islam that if all Muslims were to pay the Zakat of their wealth correctly and completely, not a single poor or deprived individual would remain throughout the Islamic lands.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "If people paid the Zakat of their wealth, no Muslim would remain poor and needy!... People do not become poor, needy, hungry, or naked except because of the sins of the wealthy!" [^2] It is also understood from the narrations that the payment of Zakat causes the preservation of the principle of ownership and the strengthening of its foundations, such that if people forget this important Islamic principle, the gap and distance between groups will become so great that even the wealth of the rich will be endangered.

In a Hadith from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام, we read: "Fortify your wealth with Zakat." [^1] The same content has been narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in other Hadiths.

For more information on these narrations, refer to chapters one, three, four, and five of the Chapters on Zakat in Volume 6 of Wasa'il al-Shia.

6- The final point that requires attention is that in the verse under discussion, the word "Lam" (for) is mentioned for four groups (إِنَّمَا الصَّدَقاتُ لِلْفُقَراءِ وَ الْمَساكِينِ وَ الْعامِلِينَ عَلَيْها وَ الْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ), and this expression usually signifies "ownership."

However, for the other four groups, the word "Fi" (in/for) is used (وَ فِي الرِّقابِ وَ الْغارِمِينَ وَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ ابْنِ السَّبِيلِ), and this expression is usually for explaining "expenditure." [^2] Among commentators, there is discussion regarding this "difference in expression." Some believe that the first four groups become the owners of the Zakat, while the second four groups do not become owners; it is only permissible for Zakat to be spent on them.

Others believe that this difference in expression points to another nuance, which is that the second four groups have a greater entitlement to Zakat, because the word "Fi" signifies "containment" (Zarfiyyah), as if these four groups are the "vessel" for Zakat and Zakat is their "content," whereas the first groups are not so.

However, we have chosen another possibility here: that the six groups "the poor, the needy, the administrators, those whose hearts are reconciled, the debtors, and the wayfarer"—who are mentioned without "Fi"—are similar and are coupled with one another. The other two groups, "freeing slaves" and "in the cause of Allah," which are expressed with the word "Fi," have a special status. Perhaps this difference in expression is because the six groups can become owners of the Zakat, and Zakat can be paid directly to them (even the debtors and those unable to pay their debts, provided we are certain they will spend it on paying their debt).

But the two groups do not become owners of the Zakat, and it cannot be paid to them; rather, it must be spent on their behalf. For example, slaves must be purchased and freed using Zakat; it is clear that they do not become owners of the Zakat in this case. Similarly, cases falling under the heading "in the cause of Allah," such as the cost of Jihad, providing weapons, or building mosques and religious centers and the like—none of these are owners of the Zakat, but rather the recipients of its expenditure.

In any case, this difference in expression shows how calculated the expressions of the Quran are.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 6, p. 20, Chapter 4, Hadith 9. [^2]: Wasa'il, Vol. 6, p. 4 (Chapter 1, Hadith 6—from the Chapters on Zakat). [^1]: Wasa'il, Vol. 6, p. 6 (Chapter 1, Hadith 11—from the Chapters on Zakat). [^2]: It should be noted that "Fi" is explicitly mentioned in two cases and is implied by conjunction in the other two, just as "Lam" is mentioned in one case and the rest are conjoined to it.

Ayah

وَمِنْهُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ ۚ قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَّكُمْ يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَيُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٦١)
Among them are those who torment the Prophet, and say, ‘He is an ear.’ Say, ‘An ear that is good for you. He has faith in Allah and trusts the faithful, and is a mercy for those of you who have faith.’ As for those who torment the Apostle of Allah, there is a painful punishment for them. (61)

Occasion of Revelation: This is a Virtue, Not a Flaw!

Several occasions of revelation have been mentioned for the above verse, which are not dissimilar to one another. Among them, it is said that this verse was revealed regarding a group of hypocrites who were sitting together and speaking unseemly words about the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. One of them said: "Do not do this, for we fear it might reach Muhammad's ears, and he might speak ill of us (and incite the people against us)."

One of them, named "Julas," said: "It does not matter; we will say whatever we wish, and if it reaches his ears, we will go to him and deny it, and he will accept it from us. For Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله is a gullible and naive person who believes whatever anyone says." At this moment, the above verse was revealed and responded to them:

Commentary

As is understood from the content of this verse, it speaks of an individual or individuals who were annoying the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله with their words, saying: "He is a gullible and naive person" (وَ مِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ وَ يَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ).

"Udhun" (أُذُن) literally means "ear," but this word is also applied to individuals who listen excessively to people's talk and are, so to speak, "all ears" or "gullible."

In reality, they were portraying one of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله points of strength—which is absolutely necessary in a leader—as a point of weakness. They were oblivious to the fact that a beloved leader must show the utmost grace and affection, accept people's excuses as much as possible, and not tear away the veil from their faults (unless doing so leads to exploitation).

Therefore, the Quran immediately adds: "Tell them: if the Prophet lends an ear to your words and accepts your excuses, and in your opinion, he is 'all ears,' this is for your own good!" (قُلْ أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ لَكُمْ).

For in this way, He preserves your honor, does not crush your personality, does not wound your feelings, and strives through this method to maintain affection, harmony, and unity among you. Whereas, if He were to immediately lift the veils and expose the liars, it would cause you great trouble. Furthermore, the reputation of many would be swiftly destroyed, the path of return and repentance would be closed to them, and tainted individuals who were capable of guidance would find themselves placed in the ranks of the wicked and would distance themselves from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

A kind and compassionate leader, who is simultaneously mature and wise, must understand everything, yet He must act as if He does not notice many things, so that those worthy of upbringing may be nurtured and not flee from His school, and so that people's secrets are not exposed.
