There is also the possibility in the meaning of the verse that Allah says in response to the critics: "It is not that He lends an ear to all talk; rather, He lends an ear to words that are to your benefit and advantage. That is, He hears the Divine revelation, listens to useful suggestions, and accepts the apologies of individuals in cases where it is to their benefit and the benefit of society." [1] Then, so that the critics do not exploit this statement or use it as a pretext, He adds: "He believes in Allah and His commands, and He listens to the words of the true believers, accepts them, and acts upon them" (يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَ يُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

This means that in reality, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has two types of programs: one is the program of maintaining appearances and preventing the tearing of veils, and the other is at the stage of action. In the first stage, He listens to everyone's words and does not outwardly deny them; but in the stage of action, His only attention is toward Allah's commands and the suggestions and words of the true believers. A realistic leader must be such, and securing the interests of society is not possible except through this path. Therefore, He immediately says: "He is a mercy for those of you who believe" (وَ رَحْمَةٌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ).

A question may be raised here: in some verses of the Quran, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is a "mercy to the worlds" (رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ) (Al-Anbiya, 107).

However, the verse under discussion says He is a mercy for the believers. Is that "generality" consistent with this "specification"? By paying attention to one point, the answer to this question becomes clear:

Mercy has degrees and levels, one of which is "potentiality and capacity," and another level is "actuality."

For example, rain is a Divine mercy, meaning this capacity and worthiness exist in all its drops to be a source of good, blessing, growth, and life. However, certainly, the manifestation and emergence of the effects of this mercy occur only in prepared and receptive lands. Therefore, we can say both that all raindrops are "mercy," and it is also correct to say these drops are a source of "mercy" in receptive and prepared lands. The first sentence refers to the stage of "requirement and potentiality," and the second sentence refers to the stage of "existence and actuality."

Regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He is potentially a source of mercy for all the worlds, but He is actually so specifically for the believers.

The only thing that remains here is that those who distress the Prophet عليه السلام with these words and criticize Him should not imagine they will remain without punishment. It is true that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has a duty toward them to face them with His nobility and vastness of spirit and refrain from exposing them, but the meaning of this is not that they will remain without penalty for these actions of theirs. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "And those who annoy the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله shall have a painful punishment" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ رَسُولَ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

[^1]: In fact, according to the first interpretation, "Udhunu Khayrin" (أُذُنُ خَيْرٍ), which is a possessive construction (Mudaf and Mudaf ilayh), is of the type where the described is added to the attribute. According to the second interpretation, it is of the type where the attribute is added to the object. According to the first possibility, the meaning of the phrase becomes: "He is a good listener for you," and according to the second possibility, its concept is: "He hears useful and good words for your benefit, not just any words."

Ayahs

يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ لِيُرْضُوكُمْ وَٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ أَحَقُّ أَن يُرْضُوهُ إِن كَانُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٦٢)
They swear to you by Allah, to please you; but Allah and His Apostle are worthier that they should please Him, should they be faithful. (62)
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ مَن يُحَادِدِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَأَنَّ لَهُۥ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدًا فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخِزْىُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٦٣)
Do they not know that whoever opposes Allah and His Apostle, there awaits him the Fire of hell, to remain in it [forever]? That is the great disgrace. (63)

Occasion of Revelation

From the statements of some commentators, it is understood that the above verse complements the previous verse and was naturally revealed regarding the same occasion. However, another group of commentators has narrated a different occasion of revelation for these two verses, which is as follows: When verses were revealed censuring those who stayed behind from the Battle of Tabuk, one of the hypocrites said: "By Allah, these people are our best and our nobles; if what Muhammad says about them is true, then these people are worse than beasts." One of the Muslims heard this and said: "By Allah, what he says is the truth, and you are worse than a beast!" This reached the ears of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; he sent for the hypocrite and asked him why he had said such a thing. He swore an oath that he had not said it. The believer who was his opponent and had reported the matter to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "O Allah, You Yourself confirm the truthful and belie the liar." The above verses were revealed and clarified their situation.

Commentary

The Self-Righteous Appearance of the Hypocrites!

One of the signs of the hypocrites and their ugly and ominous deeds, which the Quran repeatedly mentions, is that to cover their true faces, they would deny many of their wrongdoings. By resorting to false oaths, they sought to deceive the people and make them pleased with them.

In the above verses, the Holy Quran pulls back the curtain from this ugly act, both disgracing them and making the Muslims aware not to be influenced by such false oaths.

First, It says: "They swear by Allah to you to please you" (يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللَّهِ لَكُمْ لِيُرْضُوكُمْ).

It is clear that their goal in these oaths is not to state the truth, but rather they want to change the appearance of reality in your eyes through deception and trickery to reach their objectives. Otherwise, if their goal were truly to please the sincere believers, it would be more necessary for them to please Allah and His Messenger, while through their actions, they have severely displeased Allah and the Prophet.

Therefore, the Quran says: "If they are truthful and have faith, it is more fitting that they should please Allah and His Messenger!" (وَ اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُرْضُوهُ إِنْ كانُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ). It is noteworthy that in the above sentence, since "Allah" and "the Messenger" are mentioned, the pronoun should normally be in the dual form, yet a singular pronoun has been used (referring to the pronoun in "يرضوه"). This expression is, in fact, an indication that the pleasure of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is not separate from the pleasure of Allah; he only desires what Allah desires. In other words, this is an allusion to the reality of "Unity of Actions" (Tawhid al-Af'ali), for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has no independence of his own before Allah, and his pleasure and wrath all lead back to Allah; everything is for Him and in His way.

It is narrated in some traditions that during the time of the Prophet, a man said during his speech: "Whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger has succeeded, and whoever disobeys them (using the dual pronoun) has gone astray."

When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله heard this expression—where he placed Allah and the Prophet in the same rank by using a dual pronoun—he became upset and said: "What a bad speaker you are! Why did you not say: 'and whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger'?" (rather than using the dual pronoun and saying "disobeys those two"). [1]

In the next verse, He severely threatens such hypocritical individuals and says: "Do they not know that whoever opposes and acts against Allah and His Messenger, for him is the fire of Hell, to abide therein forever?" (أَ لَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّهُ مَنْ يُحادِدِ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ فَأَنَّ لَهُ نارَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِداً فِيها).

Then, for emphasis, He adds: "That is the great disgrace" (ذلِكَ الْخِزْيُ الْعَظِيمُ).

"Yuhadid" (يُحادِدِ) is from the root "Muhaddah" and the base "Hadd," which means the side or the limit of something. Since enemies and opponents stand on the opposite side, this term (Muhaddah) has also come to mean enmity and opposition, just as in everyday conversation we use the word "side" (tarafiyat) to mean "opposition and enmity."

[1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, under the verse.

Ayahs

يَحْذَرُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ أَن تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ تُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ قُلِ ٱسْتَهْزِءُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مُخْرِجٌ مَّا تَحْذَرُونَ (٦٤)
The hypocrites are apprehensive lest a surah should be sent down against them, informing them about what is in their hearts. Say, ‘Go on deriding. Allah will indeed bring out what you are apprehensive of.’ (64)
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّمَا كُنَّا نَخُوضُ وَنَلْعَبُ ۚ قُلْ أَبِٱللَّهِ وَءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَرَسُولِهِۦ كُنتُمْ تَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٦٥)
If you question them [regarding their conduct], they will surely say, ‘We were just gossiping and amusing ourselves.’ Say, ‘Were you deriding Allah, His signs, and His apostles? (65)
لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ قَدْ كَفَرْتُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ ۚ إِن نَّعْفُ عَن طَآئِفَةٍ مِّنكُمْ نُعَذِّبْ طَآئِفَةًۢ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (٦٦)
Do not make excuses. You have disbelieved after your faith.’ If We forgive a group among you, We will punish another group, for they have been guilty. (66)

Occasion of Revelation

Several occasions of revelation have been narrated for the above verses, all of which relate to the actions of the hypocrites following the Battle of Tabuk.

Among them is that a group of hypocrites held a secret meeting to plot the assassination of the Prophet. Their plan was to ambush him in one of the mountain passes on the way back from the Battle of Tabuk while remaining incognito, startle the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) camel so it would bolt, and thereby kill him. Allah informed His Prophet of this plot. He commanded a group of Muslims to be on guard and disperse them. When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) reached that 'aqaba (mountain pass), Ammar was holding the reins of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) mount, and Hudhayfah was driving it from behind. At that moment, the group of hypocrites, who had apparently covered their faces, approached. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to Hudhayfah: "Strike the faces of their mounts and drive them away." Hudhayfah did so.

When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) passed the pass safely, he asked Hudhayfah: "Did you recognize them?" He replied: "No, I did not recognize any of them." Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) enumerated all their names for him. Hudhayfah said: "Since this is the case, why do You not send a group to kill them?" He replied: "I do not like the Arabs to say that once Muhammad became victorious over his enemies, he turned to killing his own companions!" This occasion of revelation has been narrated from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) and appears in numerous books of Hadith and Tafsir.

In another occasion of revelation, we read: A group of hypocrites, upon observing the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) position against the enemy in Tabuk, said mockingly: "This man imagines he will conquer the palaces of Sham (Syria) and the mighty fortresses of the Romans; such a thing is impossible, impossible!" Allah informed His Prophet of this incident. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) ordered that the path of this group be blocked, then he called them and rebuked them, saying: "You said such and such." They offered excuses, saying: "We had no [ill] intention; we were just jesting and joking," and they swore oaths to this effect!

Commentary

Another Dangerous Plot by the Hypocrites

In the preceding verses, we saw how the hypocrites perceived strengths as weaknesses and campaigned on them to create division among the Muslims. In the verses under discussion, another part of their programs and methods is mentioned.

From the first verse, it is understood that to repel the danger of the hypocrites from the Prophet, Allah would occasionally lift the veil from their secrets and introduce them to the community so that the Muslims would remain alert and not fall into their traps, and so that the hypocrites themselves would realize their position and restrain themselves. For this reason, they often lived in a state of fear and dread. The Quran refers to this situation, saying:

"The hypocrites fear lest a Surah should be sent down against them, informing them of what is in their hearts" (يَحْذَرُ الْمُنافِقُونَ أَنْ تُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُورَةٌ تُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ).

Yet, strangely, due to the intensity of their stubbornness and enmity, they still did not cease their mockery and derision regarding the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) actions. Therefore, at the end of this verse, Allah tells His Prophet: "Say to them: Mock as you wish, but know that Allah will bring to light what you fear, and He will disgrace you!" (قُلِ اسْتَهْزِؤُا إِنَّ اللَّهَ مُخْرِجٌ ما تَحْذَرُونَ).

Of course, the phrase istahzi'u (mock) is a command for the purpose of threat, similar to when a person says to his enemy: "Do as much sabotage and harassment as you have the power to do; I will answer them all at once." Such expressions are mentioned in the context of a threat.

Furthermore, it should be noted that it is inferred from the above verse that the hypocrites were aware in their hearts of the truthfulness of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) call and knew well His connection with Allah. However, despite this, due to stubbornness, prejudice, and enmity toward the truth, instead of submitting to Him, they engaged in sabotage. For this reason, the Quran says: the hypocrites feared that verses would be revealed against them, exposing their hidden thoughts.

It is also necessary to note that the phrase tunazzala 'alayhim (sent down upon them) does not mean that such verses were revealed to the hypocrites; rather, it means they were about them and against them, even though they were revealed to the person of the Prophet.

In the next verse, it points to another of the hypocrites' programs, saying: "If you ask them why they said such an improper thing or performed such a wrong act, they say: We were only jesting and playing, and in reality, we had no [ill] intention!" (وَ لَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ إِنَّما كُنَّا نَخُوضُ وَ نَلْعَبُ) [1]. In fact, this was a strange escape route; they would hatch plots and spread poison with the intention that if their secret was not revealed and their sinister goal was achieved, they would have reached their serious objective. But if the curtains were pulled back and their secret was exposed, they would hide themselves under the mask of "jesting and joking," and with this excuse and pretext, escape punishment from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the people.

The hypocrites of today and the hypocrites of every era, who have uniform programs, make extensive use of this method. Sometimes they even raise the most serious matters in the guise of simple jests and jokes; if they reach their goal, all the better, otherwise, by labeling it a joke, they escape the clutches of punishment.

But the Quran answers them with a decisive and crushing expression, commanding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Say: Was it Allah, and His signs, and His Messenger, that you were mocking and taking as a joke?" (قُلْ أَ بِاللَّهِ وَ آياتِهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ). That is to say, can one joke with everything, even with Allah, the Prophet, and the verses of the Quran?! Are these matters, which are among the most serious issues, subject to joking?! Is the matter of startling the camel and the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) fall from that dangerous pass something that can be covered under the mask of a joke? Or is the mockery of divine verses and the Prophet’s news of future victories a matter to be played with?! All of this testifies that they had dangerous goals which they placed under these covers.

Then He commands the Prophet to explicitly tell these hypocrites: "Make no excuses" (لا تَعْتَذِرُوا), for "you have disbelieved after your belief" (قَدْ كَفَرْتُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ). This expression shows that the aforementioned group was not in the ranks of the hypocrites from the beginning, but rather in the ranks of the weak-of-faith believers, and after the aforementioned incident, they took the path of disbelief.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the above sentence that this group was in the ranks of the hypocrites before this as well, but since they had not committed an overt wrong, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Muslims were duty-bound to treat them as believers. However, when the veil was lifted after the event of the Battle of Tabuk and their disbelief and hypocrisy became public, they were warned that from then on, they would not be in the ranks of the believers.

Finally, He ends the verse with this sentence: "If We forgive a party of you, We will punish another party because they were criminals" (إِنْ نَعْفُ عَنْ طائِفَةٍ مِنْكُمْ نُعَذِّبْ طائِفَةً بِأَنَّهُمْ كانُوا مُجْرِمِينَ).

The fact that He says He will punish a group because of their crime and sin is evidence that the "forgiven group" are individuals who have washed away the traces of crime and sin from their existence with the water of repentance.

In future verses, such as verse 74, there is also evidence for this matter.

Numerous narrations have been recorded under this verse indicating that some of these hypocrites, whose state is described in the above verses, regretted their actions and repented, while others remained in their ways (for more explanation, refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 239).

[1]: "Khawd" (خوض), as stated in lexicons, means entering water gradually; then, as a metaphor, it is applied to entering into various affairs. However, in the Quran, it mostly means entering or starting ugly and evil deeds or speech.

Ayahs

ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتُ بَعْضُهُم مِّنۢ بَعْضٍ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَقْبِضُونَ أَيْدِيَهُمْ ۚ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٦٧)
The hypocrites, men and women, are all alike: they bid what is wrong and forbid what is right; and are tight-fisted. They have forgotten Allah, so He has forgotten them. The hypocrites are indeed the transgressors. (67)
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْكُفَّارَ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ هِىَ حَسْبُهُمْ ۚ وَلَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٦٨)
Allah has promised the hypocrites, men and women, and the faithless, the Fire of hell, to remain in it [forever]. That suffices them. Allah has cursed them, and there is a lasting punishment for them. (68)
كَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ كَانُوٓا۟ أَشَدَّ مِنكُمْ قُوَّةً وَأَكْثَرَ أَمْوَٰلًا وَأَوْلَـٰدًا فَٱسْتَمْتَعُوا۟ بِخَلَـٰقِهِمْ فَٱسْتَمْتَعْتُم بِخَلَـٰقِكُمْ كَمَا ٱسْتَمْتَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُم بِخَلَـٰقِهِمْ وَخُضْتُمْ كَٱلَّذِى خَاضُوٓا۟ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٦٩)
[Hypocrites! Your case is] similar to those who were before you, who were more powerful than you and more abounding in wealth and children: they enjoyed their share [of worldly existence]; you too enjoy your share, just like those who were before you enjoyed their share, and you have gossiped [impiously] as they gossiped. They are the ones whose works have failed in this world and the Hereafter; and it is they who are the losers. (69)
أَلَمْ يَأْتِهِمْ نَبَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَعَادٍ وَثَمُودَ وَقَوْمِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَأَصْحَـٰبِ مَدْيَنَ وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَـٰتِ ۚ أَتَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٧٠)
Has there not come to them the account of those who were before them—the people of Noah, ‘Ad, and Tham£d, and the people of Abraham, the inhabitants of Midian, and the towns that were overturned? Their apostles brought them manifest proofs. So it was not Allah who wronged them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves. (70)

Commentary

Signs of the Hypocrites

In these verses, the discussion continues regarding the hypocrites, their behavior, and their characteristics.

However, in the first verse under discussion, a general point is made: the spirit of hypocrisy may appear in various forms and manifest in different faces that might not initially attract attention. Specifically, the manifestation of the spirit of hypocrisy in a "man" may differ from that in a "woman." Yet, one must not be deceived by the changing faces of hypocrisy among the hypocrites; rather, with careful observation, it becomes clear that they all share a series of traits that constitute their common denominator. Therefore, He says:

"The hypocrite men and hypocrite women are of one another" (الْمُنافِقُونَ وَ الْمُنافِقاتُ بَعْضُهُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ).

Then, He proceeds to mention five of their characteristics:

First and second: "They enjoin what is 'wrong' and forbid what is 'right'" (يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمُنْكَرِ وَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمَعْرُوفِ).

This is exactly the opposite of the program of the true believers, who constantly strive to reform society and cleanse it from pollution and corruption through "enjoining what is right" and "forbidding what is wrong." The hypocrites constantly try to ensure that corruption spreads everywhere and that virtue and goodness are uprooted from society, so they can better achieve their sinister goals in such a polluted environment.

Third: They are not generous; rather, "they close their hands." They neither spend in the way of Allah, nor do they rush to help the deprived, nor do their relatives or acquaintances benefit from their financial assistance (وَ يَقْبِضُونَ أَيْدِيَهُمْ).

It is clear that since they do not believe in the Hereafter or the results and rewards of "spending" (in charity), they are extremely stingy in bestowing wealth. Although they may spend vast amounts of money to achieve their sinister motives or give out of ostentation, they never do such a thing out of sincerity and for the sake of Allah.

Fourth: All their actions, words, and behavior show that "they have forgotten Allah," and likewise, their life situation shows that "Allah has also forgotten them regarding His blessings, success, and favors"—meaning He has treated them with the treatment of one forgotten. The effects of these two types of forgetfulness are evident throughout their lives (نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ).

It is obvious that attributing "forgetfulness" to Allah does not mean actual forgetting; rather, it is a metaphor for the fact that He treats them like a forgotten person, meaning He grants them no share of His mercy and success.

This type of expression is even seen in everyday speech; for example, we say: "Since you forgot your duty, we will also forget you when paying the wages and rewards," meaning we will not give you any wages or rewards.

This meaning has also been repeatedly mentioned in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام [1]. It is noteworthy that the subject of the Lord's forgetfulness is linked to their forgetfulness with the "Fa of consequence" (فاء تفريع); meaning their forgetfulness regarding the Divine command and His Pure Essence results in Allah depriving them of His favors, and this is the consequence of their own actions.

Fifth: "The hypocrites are the rebellious (Fasiqun) and outside the circle of obedience to Allah's command" (إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ).

What was said in the above verse regarding the common traits of hypocrites is seen in every age and time. The hypocrites of our era, with the new faces they have adopted, are identical to the hypocrites of previous centuries in the aforementioned principles: they encourage corruption, hinder good deeds, are stingy and miserly, have forgotten Allah in all aspects of their lives, and are lawbreakers and rebellious. And strangely, despite all these traits, they also claim faith in Allah and a firm, urgent belief in religious and Islamic foundations!

In the next verse, their severe and painful punishment is stated in this short sentence: "Allah has promised the hypocrite men and hypocrite women and the disbelievers and faithless individuals the fire of Hell" (وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَ الْمُنافِقاتُ وَ الْكُفَّارَ نارَ جَهَنَّمَ).

[1]: Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, pp. 239 and 240.

The same blazing fire in which "they shall abide eternally" (خالِدِينَ فِيها), and "this one punishment, which encompasses all types of torments and penalties, is sufficient for them" (هِيَ حَسْبُهُمْ).

In other words, they need no other punishment, for in Hell there exists every kind of physical and spiritual torment.

And at the end of the verse, it adds: "Allah has distanced them from His mercy and an enduring punishment is theirs" (وَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ).

Indeed, this distance from Allah and being far from the Lord is itself considered the greatest torment and the most painful penalty for them.

History Repeating and a Lesson of Admonition

The next verse, in order to awaken this group of hypocrites, places the mirror of history before them. By comparing their lives with the hypocrites and tyrants of the past, it gives them the most cautionary lessons, saying: "You are like those before you," following the same path, program, and sinister fate (كَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ).

"Those who were mightier than you in power and more abundant in wealth and children" (كانُوا أَشَدَّ مِنْكُمْ قُوَّةً وَ أَكْثَرَ أَمْوالًا وَ أَوْلاداً).

"They enjoyed their portion and share in the world in the way of lusts, pollution, sin, corruption, and wickedness; you hypocrites of this Ummah have also enjoyed your portion and share just as the hypocrites before you enjoyed theirs" (فَاسْتَمْتَعُوا بِخَلاقِهِمْ فَاسْتَمْتَعْتُمْ بِخَلاقِكُمْ كَمَا اسْتَمْتَعَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ بِخَلاقِهِمْ). "Khalaq" (خلاق) linguistically means portion and share, and as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, it is derived from the root "Khalq" (خلق) (perhaps because a person receives their shares in this world in accordance with their character and disposition).

Then it says: "And you indulged in [falsehood] as they indulged" (وَ خُضْتُمْ كَالَّذِي خاضُوا) [1]. Finally, it states the end of the previous hypocrites' affair as a warning to the group of hypocrites contemporary with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and all the hypocrites of the world, in two sentences:

First: "They are those whose deeds have gone to waste in this world and the Hereafter, and no positive result shall accrue to them from them" (أُولئِكَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ).

Second: "They are the true losers and the real victims of ruin" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ).

They might gain "temporary" and "limited" benefits from their hypocritical actions, but if we look closely, we see they gain nothing from this path in the life of this world, nor do they have any share in the other world. Just as the history of previous nations clarifies this reality—how the calamities of hypocrisy overtook them and led them to decline and destruction—their sinister and evil end illuminates their fate in the other world.

When we see that they, with all those facilities, wealth, and children, reached nowhere, and their deeds were all nullified and destroyed due to being rootless under the influence of the factor of hypocrisy, you—who are at a lower level than them in terms of power and ability—will all the more be caught in such a sinister fate.

Then, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says by way of a rhetorical question: "Has there not reached this group of hypocrites the news of those before them: the people of Noah, and 'Ad, and Thamud, and the people of Abraham, and the dwellers of Madyan (the people of Shu'ayb), and the overturned cities of the people of Lot?" (أَ لَمْ يَأْتِهِمْ نَبَأُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَ عادٍ وَ ثَمُودَ وَ قَوْمِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ أَصْحابِ مَدْيَنَ وَ الْمُؤْتَفِكاتِ) [1]. These nations, which once controlled significant parts of the world, each fell victim to a type of Divine punishment due to wickedness, rebellion, defiance, fleeing from truth and justice, and engaging in various types of oppression, injustice, and corruption.

The people of Noah were destroyed by the crushing waves of the flood and drowning; the people of 'Ad (the people of Hud) by fierce and terrifying winds; the people of Thamud (the people of Salih) by devastating earthquakes; the people of Abraham by the deprivation of various blessings; the dwellers of Madyan (the people of Shu'ayb) by a rain of fire from the clouds; and the people of Lot by the overturning of their cities.

Only lifeless bodies and decayed bones under the soil or amidst the waves of water remained of them.

These are soul-shaking events, the study and investigation of which would shake any human being who has the slightest feeling in his heart.

Although Allah never deprived them of His grace, "and their messengers came to them with clear proofs to guide them" (أَتَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ).

But they did not listen to any of the sermons and advice of these men of God and did not value their exhausting efforts in the way of enlightening God's creation.

"Therefore, Allah never wronged them; it was they who used to wrong themselves!" (فَما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَ لكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ).

[1]: The phrase "كَالَّذِي خاضُوا" is actually in the sense of "as that which they indulged in," or in other words, it is a comparison of the action of today's hypocrites to the action of previous hypocrites, just as in the previous sentence, it compares their utilization of Divine blessings and favors in the way of lusts to the utilization by previous hypocrites. Therefore, it is not a comparison of person to person such that we would be forced to take "الذى" (singular) in the sense of "الذين" (plural); rather, it is a comparison of action to action.

[1]: "Mu'tafikat" (مؤتفكات) is from the root "I'tifak" (ائتفاك) meaning revolution and being overturned, and it refers to the cities of the people of Lot which were overturned by a severe earthquake.

Ayahs

وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَيُطِيعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَيَرْحَمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٧١)
But the faithful, men and women, are comrades of one another: they bid what is right and forbid what is wrong and maintain the prayer, give the zakat, and obey Allah and His Apostle. It is they to whom Allah will soon grant His mercy. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (71)
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَمَسَـٰكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِى جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ۚ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٧٢)
Allah has promised the faithful, men and women, gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and good dwellings in the Gardens of Eden. Yet Allah’s pleasure is greater [than all these]; that is the great success. (72)

Commentary

Signs of the True Believers

In the preceding verses, the common characteristics and aspects of the hypocritical men and women were discussed, which were summarized into five parts: "enjoining the evil," "forbidding the good," "withholding and stinginess," "forgetting Allah," and "disobeying the command of the Lord."

In the verses under discussion, the signs and characteristics of the believing men and women are stated, which are also summarized into five parts, and they are the exact opposite of each and every quality of the hypocrites.

The verse begins by stating: "The believing men and the believing women are allies, guardians, and helpers of one another" (وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ).

It is interesting to note that regarding the hypocrites, the word "Awliya" (allies/guardians) was not mentioned; rather, the phrase "some of them are from others" (بَعْضُهُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ) was seen, which indicates a unity of purpose and coordination in traits and actions. This suggests that although the "hypocrites" stand in a single rank and their various groups share characteristics and programs, the spirit of affection and guardianship (Wilayah) does not exist among them! Whenever their personal interests are endangered, they will even betray their own friends. For this reason, we read in verse 14 of Surah Al-Hashr: تَحْسَبُهُمْ جَمِيعاً وَ قُلُوبُهُمْ شَتَّى—"You think they are united, but their hearts are divided."

After stating this general principle, the verse proceeds to detail the specific qualities of the believers:

1- First, it says: "They invite people to good deeds" (يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ).

2- "They restrain people from ugliness, evils, and forbidden acts" (وَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ).

3- Unlike the hypocrites who had forgotten Allah, "they establish prayer," they are mindful of Allah, and through His remembrance and mention, they enlighten their hearts and keep their intellects awake and aware (وَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ).

4- Unlike the hypocrites who are stingy and miserly, they spend a portion of their wealth in the way of Allah, for the support of Allah's creation, and for the reconstruction of society, "and they pay the Zakat of their wealth" (وَ يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ).

5- The hypocrites are rebellious and defiant, stepping outside the command of Truth, but the believers "obey the command of Allah and His Messenger" (وَ يُطِيعُونَ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ).

At the end of this verse, referring to the primary distinction of the believers in terms of result and reward, it says: "Allah will soon encompass them in His mercy" (أُولئِكَ سَيَرْحَمُهُمُ اللَّهُ).

The word "Rahmah" (mercy) mentioned here has a very vast meaning that encompasses every kind of good, blessing, and prosperity in this world and the next. This sentence is, in fact, the opposite of the state of the hypocrites whom Allah has cursed and distanced from His mercy.

There is no doubt that the promise of mercy to the believers from Allah is certain and reassuring from every aspect, "for He is both All-Mighty and All-Wise." He does not make a promise without cause, nor is He unable to fulfill it once He has promised (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

The next verse explains a part of this vast Divine mercy that encompasses the believers, describing it in two aspects: "material" and "spiritual." First, it says: "Allah has promised the believing men and believing women gardens of Paradise beneath whose trees rivers flow" (وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

One of the characteristics of this great blessing is that decline, annihilation, and separation find no way into it, and "they shall abide therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها).

Another of the Divine favors to them is that Allah places "pure dwellings and comfortable abodes in the heart of the Garden of 'Adn' (Eden) at their disposal" (وَ مَساكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِي جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ).

"Adn" literally means to reside and remain in a place, and therefore the word "Ma'dan" (mine), which is the place where specific materials remain, is derived from this word. Thus, the concept of "Adn" is similar to eternity (Khulud). However, since the issue of eternity was mentioned in the previous phrase, it is inferred that the Gardens of Adn are a specific location within the Lord's Paradise that holds distinction over other gardens of Paradise.

In Islamic Hadiths and the words of commentators, this distinction has been described in various ways. In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "Adn is that abode of the Lord which no eye has seen and has not occurred to the heart of any human; none shall dwell in it except three groups: the Prophets, the Truthful (those who confirmed the Prophets and supported them), and the Martyrs." [1] In the book Al-Khisal, it is narrated from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: "Whoever is pleased to live my life and die my death, and to dwell in my Paradise which my Lord promised me—the Gardens of Adn... then let him take Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام and his progeny عليهم السلام after him as his masters (Wali)." [2] From this Hadith, it becomes clear that the Gardens of Adn are gardens of Paradise where the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and a group of his elite followers will be stationed.

This theme is also narrated in another Hadith from Ali عليه السلام, stating that the Gardens of Adn are the station of the Prophet of Islam.

Then, referring to their spiritual blessing and reward, it says: "The pleasure and satisfaction of Allah which reaches these true believers is greater and grander than all else" (وَ رِضْوانٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ).

No one can describe that spiritual pleasure and soulful sensation that comes to a human being because of the attention of Allah's pleasure and satisfaction with him. According to some commentators, even a fraction of this spiritual pleasure is superior to and higher than all of Paradise and its various, colorful, and endless blessings and favors.

Of course, we cannot visualize any of the blessings of the other world within this cage of the world and its limited life, let alone this great spiritual and soulful blessing.

However, we can visualize a faint representation of spiritual versus material differences in this world. For example, the pleasure we feel from meeting a kind and very intimate friend after a period of separation, or the specific spiritual feeling resulting from understanding a complex scientific problem we have pursued for months or years, or the exhilarating spiritual attraction we feel in a state of pure worship and a supplication mixed with presence of heart—none of these are comparable to the pleasure of any food or drink or similar material pleasures.

Here it also becomes clear that those who say the Quran, when describing the reward of the believers and the righteous, relies only on material blessings and there is no news of spiritual attractions in it, are in error. For in the above phrase, the pleasure of Allah—which is notably expressed with an "indefinite" noun (Ridwan)—refers to a portion of Allah's satisfaction being considered superior to all the material blessings of Paradise, and this shows to what extent that spiritual reward is valuable and precious.

The reason for this superiority is also clear, for the soul is in reality like a "gem" and the body is like a "shell." The "soul" is the commander and the "body" is the subordinate. The perfection of the soul is the ultimate goal, and the perfection of the body is a means. For this reason, all the radiations of the soul are vaster and more extensive than the body, and spiritual pleasures are not comparable to physical pleasures, just as spiritual pains are far more painful than physical pains.

At the end of the verse, referring to all these material and spiritual blessings, it says: "This is the great success" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ).

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [2]: Kitab al-Khisal, as narrated in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 241.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ جَـٰهِدِ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٧٣)
O Prophet! Wage jihad against the faithless and the hypocrites, and be severe with them. Their refuge shall be hell, and it is an evil destination. (73)

Commentary

The Struggle Against the Disbelievers and the Hypocrites

Finally, in this verse, the command is given for firmness of action against the disbelievers and the hypocrites, stating: "O Prophet! Strive against the disbelievers and the hypocrites" (يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ جاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَ الْمُنافِقِينَ‌).

"And be harsh against them" (وَ اغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ‌).

This is their punishment in this world, and in the Hereafter, "their abode is Hell, and wretched is the destination" (وَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَ بِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

Certainly, the method of Jihad against the "disbelievers" is clear; it is an all-encompassing struggle, and specifically an armed struggle. However, there is discussion regarding the method of Jihad against the "hypocrites," because the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله certainly did not engage in armed struggle against the hypocrites. This is because a hypocrite is someone who outwardly stands within the ranks of the Muslims and, according to outward appearances, is subject to all the rulings of Islam, even though they engage in sabotage inwardly. There are many individuals whom we know lack true faith, yet because of their profession of Islam, we cannot treat them as non-Muslims.

Therefore, as derived from Islamic narrations and the statements of commentators, it must be said: the meaning of Jihad against the hypocrites refers to other types and forms of struggle besides armed combat, such as condemnation, reprimand, threats, and exposing them. Perhaps the phrase "and be harsh against them" (وَ اغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ‌) is an allusion to this very meaning.

Of course, there is a possibility in the interpretation of the verse that as long as the status of the hypocrites is not manifest and their inner secrets are not revealed, they are subject to the rulings of Islam. However, once their status is clearly identified, they fall under the ruling of "combatant disbelievers" (Kuffar Harbi), and in this state, armed combat against them is also permissible.

However, what weakens this possibility is that in such a state, applying the word "hypocrite" to them would no longer be accurate; rather, they would be placed in the ranks of the "combatant disbelievers." For as we have said, a hypocrite is one whose outward appearance is Islam while their inward reality is disbelief.

Ayah

يَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ مَا قَالُوا۟ وَلَقَدْ قَالُوا۟ كَلِمَةَ ٱلْكُفْرِ وَكَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِسْلَـٰمِهِمْ وَهَمُّوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يَنَالُوا۟ ۚ وَمَا نَقَمُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ أَنْ أَغْنَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۚ فَإِن يَتُوبُوا۟ يَكُ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَتَوَلَّوْا۟ يُعَذِّبْهُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَمَا لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٧٤)
They swear by Allah that they did not say it. But they certainly did utter the word of unfaith and renounced faith after their Islam. They contemplated what they could not achieve, and they were vindictive only because Allah and His Apostle had enriched them out of His grace. Yet if they repent, it will be better for them; but if they turn away, Allah shall punish them with a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter, and they shall not find on the earth any guardian or helper. (74)

Occasion of Revelation

Various narrations have been cited regarding the occasion of revelation for this verse, all of which indicate that some of the hypocrites had uttered offensive remarks about Islam and the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. After their secrets were exposed, they swore false oaths that they had said nothing. Furthermore, they had hatched a conspiracy against the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله which was ultimately thwarted.

Among these accounts is that a hypocrite named "Julas," during the days of the Battle of Tabuk, vehemently denied and rejected some of the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله sermons. Upon returning to Medina, a person named "Amir ibn Qays," who had heard of this incident, came to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and reported Julas's words. However, when Julas himself came before the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, he denied the matter. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله commanded both of them to swear by the pulpit in the mosque that they were not lying. Both took the oath, but Amir pleaded: "O Allah! Reveal a verse to Your Prophet and identify the one who is truthful!" The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the believers said, "Amen."

Gabriel descended and conveyed the above verse to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. When he reached the phrase: "فَإِنْ يَتُوبُوا يَكُ خَيْراً لَهُمْ‌" (If they repent, it will be better for them), Julas said: "O Messenger of Allah, my Lord has offered me repentance; I am remorseful for my sin and I repent." The Noble Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله accepted his repentance.

Additionally, as previously mentioned, it is narrated that a group of hypocrites intended to startle the Prophet's camel at one of the mountain passes on the way back from the Battle of Tabuk, so that His Eminence would be thrown from the mountain into the valley. However, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله became aware of this plot through Divine revelation and neutralized their sinister plan. He entrusted the camel's halter to "Ammar" while "Hudhayfah" drove the camel from behind so that the mount was fully under control. He even ordered the people to take a different path so the hypocrites could not hide among the crowd to execute their plan. When, in the darkness of the night, he heard the sound of a group coming behind him at that pass, he ordered some of his companions to immediately turn them back. They—who were about twelve or fifteen people and had covered parts of their faces—fled when they saw the situation was unfavorable for their plan. However, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله recognized them and enumerated their names one by one for some of his companions [1]. As we shall see, the verse refers to two programs of the hypocrites: one being their improper speech, and the other being a conspiracy that was thwarted. Thus, it appears that both occasions of revelation are simultaneously correct.

Commentary

A Dangerous Conspiracy

The connection between this verse and the preceding verses is perfectly clear, as they all speak of the hypocrites. However, in this verse, another layer of their actions is unveiled: when they see their secrets exposed, they deny the facts and even resort to false oaths to prove their claims.

First, it states: "The hypocrites swear by Allah that they did not say such things [about the Prophet]" (يَحْلِفُونَ بِاللَّهِ ما قالُوا), whereas "they certainly uttered the word of disbelief" (وَ لَقَدْ قالُوا كَلِمَةَ الْكُفْرِ). Consequently, "they chose the path of disbelief after their acceptance and profession of Islam" (وَ كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِسْلامِهِمْ‌).

Of course, they were not true Muslims from the beginning to have become disbelievers; rather, they only made an outward profession of Islam. Therefore, they broke even this superficial and formal Islam by expressing disbelief.

[1]: Adapted from Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Manar, Ruh al-Ma'ani, and other commentaries.

Furthermore, "they meditated a dangerous plot which they could not attain" (وَ هَمُّوا بِما لَمْ يَنالُوا).

This "plot" may refer to the story of the conspiracy to destroy the Prophet at "Laylat al-Aqabah," as described in the occasion of revelation, or it may refer to all the actions and activities they undertook to disrupt the organization of Islamic society and create corruption, hypocrisy, and division—none of which ever reached their ultimate goal.

It is noteworthy that the vigilance of the Muslims in various events caused the hypocrites and their plans to be identified. The Muslims were always on the lookout for them so that if they heard anything from them, they would report it to the Prophet for prevention and necessary action. This alertness and timely action, followed by the revelation of verses and Allah's confirmation, led to the disgrace of the hypocrites and the neutralization of their conspiracies.

In the next sentence, to fully expose the ugliness and insolence of the hypocrites' activities and their ingratitude, it adds: In reality, they had seen no wrongdoing from the Prophet, and no harm had come to them from Islam. On the contrary, under the shadow of the Islamic government, they had attained all kinds of material and spiritual blessings. Therefore, "they were actually seeking revenge for the very blessings that Allah and His Messenger had bestowed upon them out of His grace until they were enriched" (وَ ما نَقَمُوا إِلَّا أَنْ أَغْناهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ‌) [1]. Undoubtedly, being enriched and having their needs met through the grace of the Lord and the services of the Prophet was not something to seek revenge for; rather, it called for appreciation and gratitude. Yet, these ungrateful people of ugly character responded to service and blessing with crime. This is a beautiful and expressive idiom used in many speeches and writings; for instance, to someone we have served for years and who then betrays us, we say: "Our only sin was that we gave you refuge, defended you, and showed you the utmost kindness."

Then, as is the method of the Quran, it opens the door of return to them, saying: "If they repent, it will be better for them" (فَإِنْ يَتُوبُوا يَكُ خَيْراً لَهُمْ‌).

This is a sign of the realism of Islam and its emphasis on education and reform, and its struggle against any form of undue harshness or severity. It leaves the path of reconciliation and repentance open even to hypocrites who conspired to destroy the Prophet and uttered blasphemous words and offensive insults; indeed, it invites them to repent.

This is truly the real face of Islam, yet how unfair are those who have portrayed Islam with such a face as a religion of pressure and violence.

In today's world, would any government—no matter how much it advocates for ultimate leniency—be willing to show such flexibility and kindness toward conspirators who plotted against it? And as we read in the occasion of revelation, one of the main executors of these hypocritical programs repented upon hearing this word, and the Prophet accepted his repentance.

At the same time, so they do not mistake this leniency for weakness, He warns them: "But if they turn away [from repentance and continue their way], Allah will punish them with a painful punishment in this world and the Hereafter" (وَ إِنْ يَتَوَلَّوْا يُعَذِّبْهُمُ اللَّهُ عَذاباً أَلِيماً فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ).

And if they imagine that someone might rush to their aid against Divine punishment, they are gravely mistaken, for "they shall have neither protector nor helper throughout the earth" (وَ ما لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَ لا نَصِيرٍ).

Of course, their punishments in the Hereafter are clear, but their punishments in this world consist of disgrace, loss of reputation, humiliation, misery, and the like.

[1]: It is noteworthy that in the above sentence, although "the grace of Allah and the Prophet" is mentioned, the pronoun in "من فضله" (from His grace) is singular, not dual. The reason for this expression is what we pointed out a few verses ago: such expressions are used to establish the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid)—that all affairs are in the hands of Allah, and if the Prophet does something, it is by His command and is not separate from His will.

Ayahs

۞ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ عَـٰهَدَ ٱللَّهَ لَئِنْ ءَاتَىٰنَا مِن فَضْلِهِۦ لَنَصَّدَّقَنَّ وَلَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٧٥)
Among them are those who made a pledge with Allah: ‘If He gives us out of His grace, we will surely give the zakat and we will surely be among the righteous.’ (75)
فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ وَتَوَلَّوا۟ وَّهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٧٦)
But when He gave them out of His grace, they begrudged it and turned away, being disregardful. (76)
فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفَاقًا فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُۥ بِمَآ أَخْلَفُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا وَعَدُوهُ وَبِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْذِبُونَ (٧٧)
So He caused hypocrisy to ensue in their hearts until the day they will encounter Him, because of their going back on what they had promised Allah and because of the lies they used to tell. (77)
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْوَىٰهُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (٧٨)
Do they not know that Allah knows their secret [thoughts] and [hears] their secret talks, and that Allah is knower of all that is Unseen? (78)

Circumstances of Revelation

It is well-known among commentators that these verses were revealed concerning one of the Ansar named "Thalabah bin Hatib." He was a poor man who frequently attended the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mosque and insisted that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) pray for Allah to grant him abundant wealth! The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said to him:

"A small amount for which you can fulfill its gratitude is better than a large amount that you cannot handle."

Is it not better for you to follow the example of Allah's Prophet and be content with a simple life? However, Thalabah would not let go and finally said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "I swear by the God who sent you with the truth, if Allah grants me wealth, I will fulfill all its rights." So, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) prayed for him.

Before long, according to one narration, a wealthy cousin of his passed away and a vast fortune reached him. According to another narration, he bought a sheep which soon multiplied so rapidly that keeping them in Medina became impossible. He was forced to move to the outskirts of Medina and became so preoccupied with material life that he no longer participated in the congregational prayers or even the Friday prayer.

After some time, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) sent a tax collector to him to collect the Zakat on his property. But this shallow-minded, newly-rich, and stingy man refused to pay the Divine right. Not only did he refuse, but he also objected to the very legislation of this ruling, saying: "This ruling is the brother of Jizyah! That is, we became Muslims to be exempt from paying Jizyah, so with the payment of Zakat, what difference remains between us and non-Muslims?!"

While he understood neither the concept of Jizyah nor the concept of Zakat—or perhaps he understood, but worldliness did not allow him to express the truth—regardless, when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) heard his words, He said: "Woe to Thalabah! Woe to Thalabah!" At this moment, the above verses were revealed.

Other circumstances of revelation have also been narrated for the above verses which are more or less similar to the story of Thalabah. From the sum of these narrations and the content of the verses, it is understood that the person or persons mentioned were not initially in the ranks of the hypocrites, but joined them because of such actions.

Commentary

The Hypocrites are Shallow-Minded

These verses, in fact, point to another of the ugly traits of the hypocrites: that at the time of weakness, inability, poverty, and distress, they speak so much of faith that no one would believe they would one day stand in the ranks of the hypocrites. They might even reproach those who possess vast means, asking why they do not use their resources for the benefit of the deprived.

However, as soon as they themselves acquire wealth, they lose their footing and become so drowned in worldliness that they consign all their covenants and promises with Allah to oblivion. It is as if they have completely changed their personality and gained a different perception and vision. This very shallowness—the result of which is worldliness, stinginess, parsimony, and selfishness—concentrates the spirit of hypocrisy within them so much that it closes the path of return to them! In the first verse, He says: "And among them are those who made a covenant with Allah, [saying], 'If He should give us from His bounty, we will surely give charity and be among the righteous.'" (وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ عاهَدَ اللَّهَ لَئِنْ آتانا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ لَنَصَّدَّقَنَّ وَ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

But they only said this when their hands were empty of everything. "But when He gave them from His bounty, they were stingy with it and turned away while they were averse." (فَلَمَّا آتاهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ بَخِلُوا بِهِ وَ تَوَلَّوْا وَ هُمْ مُعْرِضُونَ).

"This action, this breach of covenant, and this stinginess resulted in the spirit of hypocrisy taking root continuously and permanently in their hearts, continuing until the Day of Resurrection and the time they meet Allah." (فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفاقاً فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُ). This is because they broke the promise they made to Allah and because they used to lie. (بِما أَخْلَفُوا اللَّهَ ما وَعَدُوهُ وَ بِما كانُوا يَكْذِبُونَ).

Finally, He reproaches and rebukes them with this sentence: "Do they not know that Allah knows their secrets and their private conversations and that Allah is the Knower of the unseen?" (أَ لَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ سِرَّهُمْ وَ نَجْواهُمْ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ).

Here, several points should be noted

1- From the phrase "فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفاقاً فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ" (So He caused hypocrisy to follow in their hearts), it is clearly understood that many sins and ugly traits, and even disbelief and hypocrisy, are "cause" and "effect" of one another. For the above sentence explicitly states: their stinginess and breach of covenant caused hypocrisy to take root in their hearts. The same applies to other sins and wrongdoings; hence, we read in some expressions that sometimes major sins cause a person to leave this world without faith.

2- The meaning of "يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُ" (the day they meet Him), where the pronoun refers to "Allah," is the Day of Resurrection, because the expression "meeting Allah" and the like in the Quran usually refers to the Resurrection. It is true that with death, the period of action ends and the file of good and bad deeds is closed, but their effects will remain in the human soul until the Resurrection. Of course, the possibility has also been raised that the pronoun in "يلقونه" refers to "stinginess," meaning "until the time they find the result and punishment of their stinginess." It has also been suggested that the meeting with the Lord refers to the moment of death. However, all of these are contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse, and the apparent meaning is what we have stated. (Regarding what is meant by meeting the Lord, we have a discussion under verse 46 of Surah Al-Baqarah (Volume 1, page 156); please refer to it).

3- From the above verses, it is also understood that breaking covenants and lying are traits of the hypocrites. It is they who trample upon the covenant they made with Allah with great emphasis, and even lie to their Lord. A famous hadith narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) also emphasizes this reality, as He said:

"The hypocrite has three signs: when he speaks, he lies; when he promises, he breaks it; and when he is entrusted, he betrays!" [^1] Interestingly, in the aforementioned story (the story of Thalabah), all three signs are present: he lied, he broke his covenant, and he betrayed the wealth that Allah had entrusted to him! This hadith appears in a more emphasized form from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the book al-Kafi, where He says:

"There are three things which, if found in anyone, make him a hypocrite, even if he fasts and prays and claims to be a Muslim: one who betrays a trust, lies in speech, and breaks a promise." [^2] Of course, it is possible that these sins may rarely occur from people of faith who then repent, but their persistence is a sign of the spirit of hypocrisy and hypocritical behavior.

4- It is also necessary to mention that what we read in the above verses was not just a historical discussion related to the past; rather, it is the expression of a moral and social reality that has numerous examples in every age and time, and in every society, without exception.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the mentioned verse. [^2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, page 607.

If we look around us (and perhaps even if we look at ourselves!), we find examples of the actions of "Thalabah bin Hatib" and his way of thinking in various forms. How many people there are who, in normal conditions or when they are destitute, stand firmly and fervently in the ranks of the believers. They are present at all religious gatherings, support every reformist banner, harmonize with every caller to truth and justice, exert themselves for good deeds, and cry out against every corruption.

But when, so to speak, an opportunity arises and they gain some wealth, find a position or rank, and become someone of importance, they suddenly change their face and, more than that, change their essence. That burning passion and love for God and religion subsides in them; they are no longer seen in constructive gatherings, they are absent from any reform program, they no longer exert themselves for the truth, nor do they cry out against falsehood! Previously, when they had no status or position in society, they had made a thousand covenants and promises with Allah and His creation—that if they one day gained resources, they would do such and such. They even had a thousand criticisms and reproaches for the irresponsible wealthy. But the day their situation changed, they consigned all those covenants to oblivion, and all those criticisms and reproaches melted away like snow in summer.

Yes, this shallowness is one of the clear signs of the hypocrites. Is hypocrisy anything other than being double-faced or having a duality of personality? The life history of such individuals is the clearest example of a dual personality. Fundamentally, a person with depth and capacity does not become double-personalized.

There is no doubt that hypocrisy, like faith, has different stages. In some, this vile habit has so deeply permeated their soul that no trace of faith in Allah remains in their heart, even though they have inserted themselves into the ranks of the believers!

But another group, despite having weak faith and being truly Muslim, commit acts that suit the state of the hypocrites and bear the color of a dual personality. Is not the one who constantly lies while his appearance is one of truthfulness and honesty double-faced and a hypocrite?

Is not the one who is apparently trustworthy—and for this reason, people trust him and entrust their belongings to him—but in reality betrays them, afflicted with a duality of personality?

Likewise, do not those who make covenants and promises but never adhere to them have their actions counted as the actions of hypocrites?! Incidentally, one of the greatest social calamities and factors of backwardness is the existence of such hypocrites in human societies. If we do not close our eyes and do not lie to ourselves, how many of these "Thalabah-like" hypocrites can we count around us and in Islamic societies? And the wonder is that with all this defect, disgrace, and distance from the spirit of Islamic teachings, we still blame the sin of our backwardness on Islam!

Ayahs

ٱلَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ ٱلْمُطَّوِّعِينَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِى ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ فَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنْهُمْ ۙ سَخِرَ ٱللَّهُ مِنْهُمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٩)
Those who blame the voluntary donors from among the faithful concerning the charities—and as for those who do not find [anything] except [what] their means [permit], they ridicule them—Allah shall put them to ridicule, and there is a painful punishment for them. (79)
ٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لَا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِن تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٨٠)
Whether you plead forgiveness for them or do not plead forgiveness for them, even if you plead forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah shall never forgive them because they defied Allah and His Apostle; and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (80)

Occasion of Revelation

Multiple narrations regarding the occasion of the revelation of these verses have been cited in books of Tafsir and Hadith. From the sum of these, it is understood that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله intended to prepare the army of Islam to confront the enemy (likely for the Battle of Tabuk) and needed to seek assistance from the people. When He expressed His intention, those who had the means contributed significant amounts as Zakat or as unconditional aid to the Islamic army.

However, some low-income Muslim laborers, such as "Abu Aqil al-Ansari" or "Salim bin Umayr al-Ansari," by undertaking extra work and drawing water during the night, managed to provide two man (a unit of weight) of dates. They kept one man for their own families and brought the other man to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, offering what was outwardly a meager contribution to this great Islamic program.

But the fault-finding hypocrites criticized both groups: those who had given much were labeled as ostentatious (show-offs), and those who had contributed an outwardly insignificant amount were mocked and ridiculed. They would say, "Does the army of Islam really need such a contribution?"

The above verses were revealed, severely threatening them and warning them of Allah's punishment!

Commentary

The Sabotage of the Hypocrites

These verses point to another general characteristic of the hypocrites: they are stubborn, excuse-seeking, fault-finding, and obstructive individuals. They belittle every positive action with inappropriate labels and make it appear ugly, both to discourage people from performing good deeds and to sow the seeds of pessimism and suspicion in minds, thereby halting the wheels of useful and constructive activities in society.

The Holy Quran severely rebukes this inhumane method of theirs and alerts the Muslims to it, so they are not influenced by such evil suggestions, and so the hypocrites know that their tricks carry no weight in the Islamic society!

First, He says: "Those who criticize the contributors among the believers concerning their voluntary charities, and especially those who mock the faithful who are destitute and find nothing to give except their [meager] efforts—Allah mocks them, and a painful punishment awaits them!" (الَّذِينَ يَلْمِزُونَ الْمُطَّوِّعِينَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ فِي الصَّدَقاتِ وَ الَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ إِلَّا جُهْدَهُمْ فَيَسْخَرُونَ مِنْهُمْ سَخِرَ اللَّهُ مِنْهُمْ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

"Yalmizun" (يلمزون) is derived from the root "lamz" (rhyming with tanz), meaning fault-finding. "Muttawwi'in" (مطوعين) is from the root "taw'" (rhyming with mawj), meaning obedience, but this word is usually applied to virtuous individuals and those who perform recommended (Mustahabb) acts in addition to the obligatory ones.

From the above verse, it is understood that the hypocrites found fault with one group and mocked another. Clearly, the mockery was directed at those who were only capable of a small contribution to the Islamic army, while the fault-finding was directed at those who, conversely, provided abundant aid. They criticized the latter for ostentation and the former for insufficient effort.

In the next verse, greater emphasis is placed on the punishment of this group of hypocrites, and the final threat is mentioned. Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them—even if you ask forgiveness for them seventy times—Allah will never forgive them" (اسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ أَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ إِنْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ سَبْعِينَ مَرَّةً فَلَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ). "That is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger," and this very disbelief cast them into the abyss of hypocrisy and its sinister consequences (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ).

It is clear that Allah's guidance includes those who walk the path of seeking truth and are in search of reality, but "Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient, sinful, and hypocritical people" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ).

Here, several points should be noted

1- Importance lies in the quality of work, not the quantity

This truth is clearly derived from the verses of the Quran: that Islam has never emphasized the "magnitude of the quantity of an action," but has everywhere given importance to the "quality of the action." It places extraordinary value on sincerity and pure intention, of which the above verses are an example of this Quranic logic.

As we have seen, extraordinary importance is given to the modest act of a Muslim laborer who did not sleep from night until morning, working with a heart full of love for Allah, sincerity, and a sense of responsibility toward the problems of the Islamic society. Through this sleeplessness, he was able to help the Islamic army with one man of dates at a critical moment. Those who belittle such outwardly small but truly great deeds are severely rebuked and threatened, and told that a painful punishment awaits them.

From this subject, another truth becomes clear: in a healthy Islamic society, when problems arise, everyone must feel a sense of responsibility. Eyes should not be fixed only on the wealthy, because Islam belongs to everyone, and everyone must strive with heart and soul to preserve it.

The important thing is that no one withholds what they are capable of; it is not a matter of much or little, but a matter of responsibility and sincerity. It is noteworthy that we read in a Hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked: "Which charity and help is superior?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "The effort of the one with little means" (Juhd al-muqill).

2- The trait we read about regarding the hypocrites of the Prophet's era—

Like their other traits—is not exclusive to that group or that time. Rather, it is among the ugly traits of all hypocrites in every age and time. With their specific spirit of pessimism, they try to nullify every positive action with unfitting labels. They try to discourage every benefactor in some way, and even through mockery, ridicule, and belittling the sincere services of low-income individuals, they attempt to break and humiliate their character so that all positive activities in society are extinguished and they reach their sinister goals.

However, aware and vigilant Muslims in every age must be fully mindful of this sinister plot of the hypocrites and act exactly to the contrary. That is, they should encourage the servants of society and place great value on outwardly small services accompanied by sincerity, so that both the small and the great become heartened and interested in their work. They must also make everyone aware of this destructive plot of the hypocrites so they do not become discouraged.

3- The phrase "Allah mocks them" (سَخِرَ اللَّهُ مِنْهُمْ) does not mean that Allah performs actions similar to theirs. Rather, as commentators have said: it means He will give them the punishment of mockers, or He treats them in such a way that they are humiliated like those who are mocked!

4- There is no doubt that the number seventy (sab'in) in the above verses is for "multiplicity" (expressing a large, indefinite number) and not for "counting" (a literal limit). In other words, the meaning of the verse is that no matter how much you ask for their forgiveness, Allah will not forgive them. It is just like when someone says to another: "Even if you insist a hundred times, I will not accept." The meaning of this statement is not that if you insist a hundred and one times, I will accept; rather, the intention is that I will absolutely not accept.

This type of expression is actually for emphasizing the matter. Therefore, in Surah Al-Munafiqun, verse 6, this same subject is mentioned as an absolute negation where it says: "It is all the same for them whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them; Allah will never forgive them" (سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ). Another witness to this is the reason mentioned at the end of the verse: "That is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger, and Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient." It is clear that for such individuals, no amount of seeking forgiveness will cause salvation.

However, it is strange that in several narrations cited through Sunni sources, we read that after the revelation of the above verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "I will increase my seeking of forgiveness for them to more than seventy times!" hoping that Allah would forgive them. Then the verse was revealed: "It is all the same for them whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them; Allah will never forgive them." [^1]

[^1]: A large number of these narrations with this theme have been collected in Tafsir al-Tabari, Vol. 10, p. 138.

The implication of the above narrations is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله understood the number seventy in the verse under discussion as a literal "count," and therefore said He would ask forgiveness for them more than seventy times. However, as we have said, the verse under discussion—especially considering the reason given at its end—clearly informs us that the number seventy is for "multiplicity" and is a metaphor for absolute negation coupled with emphasis.

Therefore, since the above narrations contradict the Quran, they are not acceptable at all, especially since their chains of transmission are not considered authentic by us either.

The only justification that can be made for the above narrations (though it goes against the apparent meaning) is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said this sentence before the revelation of the above verses, and when the verses were revealed, He ceased seeking forgiveness for them.

Another narration has also been cited in this regard, which might be the original root of the above narrations that was altered during "transmission by meaning" (naql bi-l-ma'na), which is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "If I knew that if I increased [seeking forgiveness] beyond seventy times they would be forgiven, I would have done so."

The meaning of this statement (especially considering the word "law" [if], which is used for impossibility/counterfactuals) is: "I know Allah will not forgive them, but my heart is so filled with love for the guidance of Allah's servants and their salvation that if, hypothetically, seeking forgiveness more than seventy times would cause their salvation, I would do it."

In any case, the meaning of the above verses is clear, and any Hadith contrary to them must either be justified or set aside.

Ayahs

فَرِحَ ٱلْمُخَلَّفُونَ بِمَقْعَدِهِمْ خِلَـٰفَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَرِهُوٓا۟ أَن يُجَـٰهِدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَنفِرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَرِّ ۗ قُلْ نَارُ جَهَنَّمَ أَشَدُّ حَرًّا ۚ لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَفْقَهُونَ (٨١)
Those who were left behind boasted for sitting back against [the command of] the Apostle of Allah, and were reluctant to wage jihad with their possessions and persons in the way of Allah, and they said, ‘Do not go forth in this heat.’ Say, The fire of hell is severer in heat, should they understand. (81)
فَلْيَضْحَكُوا۟ قَلِيلًا وَلْيَبْكُوا۟ كَثِيرًا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٨٢)
So let them laugh a little; much will they weep as a requital for what they used to earn. (82)
فَإِن رَّجَعَكَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَىٰ طَآئِفَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ فَٱسْتَـْٔذَنُوكَ لِلْخُرُوجِ فَقُل لَّن تَخْرُجُوا۟ مَعِىَ أَبَدًا وَلَن تُقَـٰتِلُوا۟ مَعِىَ عَدُوًّا ۖ إِنَّكُمْ رَضِيتُم بِٱلْقُعُودِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ فَٱقْعُدُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَـٰلِفِينَ (٨٣)
If Allah brings you back [from the battlefront] to a group of them and they seek your permission to go forth, say, ‘You shall never go forth with me, and you shall not fight with me against any enemy. You were indeed pleased to sit back the first time, so sit back with those who stay behind.’ (83)

Commentary

Further Obstructionism by the Hypocrites

In these verses, the discourse continues to identify the hypocrites, their methods of action, behavior, and thoughts, so that Muslims may clearly recognize this group and not fall under the influence of their sinister plots.

First, He says: "Those who lagged behind (in Tabuk) from participating in Jihad, and sat in their homes with hollow excuses, and in their own imagination preferred safety over the dangers of the battlefield, are rejoiced at their sitting behind against the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله" (فَرِحَ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ بِمَقْعَدِهِمْ خِلافَ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ‌).

"And they were averse to striving with their wealth and their lives in the cause of Allah and attaining the great honors of the mujahideen" (وَ كَرِهُوا أَنْ يُجاهِدُوا بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُنِسِهِمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

They were not content with their own failure to participate in the field of Jihad; rather, with satanic whisperings, they attempted to discourage or dissuade others as well, "and they said: 'Go not forth in the heat' of this scorching summer to the battlefield" (وَ قالُوا لا تَنْفِرُوا فِي الْحَرِّ).

In reality, they wanted both to weaken the will of the Muslims and to provide more partners for their own crime.

Then the Quran turns its address to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and responds to them with a decisive and crushing tone: "Say to them: 'The fire of Hell is fiercer in heat,' if only they could understand!" (قُلْ نارُ جَهَنَّمَ أَشَدُّ حَرًّا لَوْ كانُوا يَفْقَهُونَ‌). But alas, due to weakness of faith and lack of sufficient understanding, they do not realize what a scorching fire awaits them—a fire whose smallest spark is more burning than all the fires of this world.

The next two verses point out that, imagining they have achieved a victory and reached a goal by lagging behind from Jihad and discouraging some of the mujahideen, they burst into laughter and laugh much, just as all hypocrites in every age and time do.

But the Quran warns them that "they should laugh a little and weep much" (فَلْيَضْحَكُوا قَلِيلًا وَ لْيَبْكُوا كَثِيراً).

Weeping for the dark future they have ahead, weeping for the painful punishments awaiting them, weeping because they have destroyed all bridges of return behind them, and finally, weeping because they have lost all this talent and the capital of their lives, and have purchased scandal, misery, and wretchedness for themselves.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "This is a recompense for what they used to earn" (جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ‌).

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the meaning is that this group should laugh little and weep more in this world, for they have painful punishments ahead; if they were aware of them, they would weep much and laugh little.

However, a group of commentators has offered another possibility for the meaning of this sentence: that no matter how much they laugh, given the short lifespan of this world, their laughter will be little, and in the Hereafter, they must weep so much that the weeping of this world is insignificant in comparison.

But the first commentary is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse and similar expressions found in speech and writing, especially since the second commentary necessitates that the imperative form (command) be interpreted as a report (news), which is contrary to the apparent meaning.
