The famous Hadith that many commentators have narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله where he said:

لو تعلمون ما اعلم لضحكتم قليلا و لبكيتم كثيرا

"If you knew what I know (of the terrifying punishments of the Resurrection), you would laugh little and weep much," also serves as evidence for the first meaning (reflect carefully).

In the last verse under discussion, it points to another calculated and dangerous method of the hypocrites: when they commit a wrong act openly, they apparently attempt to make amends to exonerate themselves, and with these fluctuations and contradictory actions, they hide their true face.

The verse says: "If Allah brings you back to a party of them and they ask your permission to go out (to another Jihad), say: 'Never shall you go out with me, and never shall you fight an enemy with me!'" (فَإِنْ رَجَعَكَ اللَّهُ إِلى‌ طائِفَةٍ مِنْهُمْ فَاسْتَأْذَنُوكَ لِلْخُرُوجِ فَقُلْ لَنْ تَخْرُجُوا مَعِيَ أَبَداً وَ لَنْ تُقاتِلُوا مَعِيَ عَدُوًّا).

That is, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله must make them despair forever and make it clear that their "henna no longer has any color" (their tricks no longer work) and no one will be deceived by them; it is better they pack up such traps and take them elsewhere, for here, no one else will fall into their snare!

It is also necessary to mention that the phrase "a party of them" (طائِفَةٍ مِنْهُمْ) shows that not all of them were ready to enter through this path and offer a proposal to participate in another Jihad, perhaps because some were so disgraced and ashamed that they were not even willing to appear in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to make such a proposal.

Then He explains the reason for rejecting their proposal as follows: "For you were content to sit still the first time, so sit with those who stay behind" (إِنَّكُمْ رَضِيتُمْ بِالْقُعُودِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ فَاقْعُدُوا مَعَ الْخالِفِينَ‌).

Here, several points should be noted

1- There is no doubt that if this group of hypocrites had felt regret after their failure to participate and had repented, and then offered to join other fields of Jihad to wash away their previous sin, Allah would have accepted it from them, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would not have rejected them. Therefore, it becomes clear that this proposal was also a kind of mischief and hypocritical act—a tactic, so to speak, to camouflage their ugly face and continue their previous actions.

2- The word "Khalif" (خالف) means one who lags behind, referring to those who did not participate in the field of Jihad, whether with or without an excuse. Some have also said that "Khalif" means "opponent" (مخالف), implying: go and join the group of opponents. This word has also been interpreted to mean "corrupt" (فاسد), as "Khuluf" (خلوف) means corruption and "Khalif" means corrupt in the language.

It is also possible that all the above meanings are intended by this word in the verse, as the group of hypocrites and their friends possessed all these vile traits.

3- We again find it necessary to remind that the Muslims of our era, in the face of the hypocrites in their own environment who use methods similar to the hypocrites of previous ages, must follow the same firm program of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Once they have fallen into their trap, they should not be deceived by them again nor pay any heed to their "crocodile tears," for "a Muslim man is never bitten from the same hole twice!"

Ayahs

وَلَا تُصَلِّ عَلَىٰٓ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُم مَّاتَ أَبَدًا وَلَا تَقُمْ عَلَىٰ قَبْرِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٨٤)
And never pray over any of them when he dies, nor stand at his graveside. They indeed defied Allah and His Apostle and died as transgressors. (84)
وَلَا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدُهُمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُعَذِّبَهُم بِهَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَتَزْهَقَ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٥)
Let not their possessions or their children impress you. Allah only desires to punish them with these in this world, and that their souls may depart while they are faithless. (85)

Commentary

A Firmer Approach Toward the Hypocrites

After the hypocrites tore away the veils and their true nature was exposed by their explicit refusal to participate in Jihad, Allah commands His Prophet to adopt a more direct and firmer stance against them. This was to ensure that the thought of hypocrisy and acting as a hypocrite would be uprooted from the minds of others forever, and so the hypocrites would realize there is no place for them within the Islamic society.

Therefore, He says: "And never offer prayer (Janazah) for any of them (the hypocrites) who dies" (وَ لا تُصَلِّ عَلى‌ أَحَدٍ مِنْهُمْ ماتَ أَبَداً).

"And do not stand by his grave" to seek forgiveness for him (وَ لا تَقُمْ عَلى‌ قَبْرِهِ‌).

In reality, this is a form of negative—yet effective—struggle against the group of hypocrites. For reasons previously mentioned, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) could not officially issue an order for their execution or to purge the Islamic environment through such means; however, negative struggles are sufficiently effective in stripping them of their credibility and isolating and ostracizing them from the Islamic community.

We know that a true believer is respected both in life and after death. For this reason, Islamic programs mandate washing (Ghusl), shrouding (Kafan), praying over, and burying the believer, so they are laid to rest with the utmost respect and specific ceremonies. Even after burial, people come to the graveside to seek Allah's forgiveness for their potential sins and lapses.

The refusal to perform these ceremonies for an individual signifies their expulsion from the Islamic community. If the one performing this expulsion is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself, it deals a severe and heavy blow to the reputation of such an outcast. In truth, this was a calculated program of struggle against the hypocrites of that time, and today, Muslims should utilize similar methods.

That is to say, as long as individuals profess Islam and adhere to its outward requirements, they must be treated as Muslims, even if their inner state is otherwise. However, if they tear away the veils and manifest their hypocrisy, they must be treated as strangers to Islam.

At the end of the verse, the reason for this command is clarified once more:

"This decree is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger" (إِنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ‌).

"And they died while they were rebellious (Fasiq) and defiant of Allah's command" (وَ ماتُوا وَ هُمْ فاسِقُونَ‌). They neither regretted their actions nor washed the stains of sin from their skirts with the water of repentance.

At this point, a question might have arisen for the Muslims: if the hypocrites are truly this far removed from Allah's mercy and Muslims should show them no affection or interest, then why has Allah granted them so much favor, providing them with such wealth and children (economic and human power)?

In the following verse, Allah addresses the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and answers this question: "And let not their wealth and their children impress you" (وَ لا تُعْجِبْكَ أَمْوالُهُمْ وَ أَوْلادُهُمْ‌).

For, contrary to what superficial people imagine, this wealth and these children are not a source of happiness for them; rather, "Allah only intends to punish them through these in this world, and that their souls should depart while they are disbelievers" (إِنَّما يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَنْ يُعَذِّبَهُمْ بِها فِي الدُّنْيا وَ تَزْهَقَ أَنْفُسُهُمْ وَ هُمْ كافِرُونَ‌).

This verse, which has a parallel in this same Surah (Verse 55), points to the reality that economic resources and human forces in the hands of unrighteous individuals do not create happiness; rather, they are often a source of trouble, calamity, and misery. This is because such people neither spend their wealth in ways that yield useful and constructive benefits, nor do they have righteous, faithful, and well-trained children who could be the "delight of their eyes" and solve their life's problems. Instead, their wealth is often spent on lethal and rebellious passions, producing corruption and strengthening the foundations of oppression, causing them to neglect Allah and the fundamental issues of life. Their children also end up in the service of oppressors and the corrupt, becoming afflicted with various moral deviations, and ultimately becoming a source of grief.

However, for those who consider wealth and human power as ends in themselves and do not care how they are utilized, the lives of such people present an enchanting vista from afar. But if we look closer at the core of their lives and consider the reality of how these resources are utilized, we will testify that they are never truly happy people.

Two points must be noted here

  1. Regarding the circumstances of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) for the first verse, several narrations have been recorded, which are not without discrepancies.

Some of these narrations suggest that when Abdullah ibn Ubayy (the famous hypocrite) died, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) prayed over him, stood by his grave and supplicated, and even gave his own shirt to be used as a shroud. Then, the above verse was revealed, forbidding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) from repeating such an act.

Meanwhile, other narrations indicate that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) intended to pray over him, but Gabriel descended and recited this verse to him, preventing him from doing so.

Others say that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) neither prayed over him nor intended to; rather, he only sent his shirt to encourage the tribe of "Abdullah" to shroud him in it. When asked why he did this for a man without faith, he replied: "My shirt will not save him from Allah's punishment, but I hope that because of this act, a large group will become Muslim." And so it was, as many from the Khazraj tribe became Muslim after this event.

Since these narrations differ greatly, we have refrained from mentioning them as a definitive circumstance of revelation, especially since, according to some great commentators, the death of Abdullah ibn Ubayy occurred in the 9th year of the Hijra, while the above verses were revealed around the 8th year (Al-Mizan, Vol. 9, p. 385).

However, what cannot be denied is that the tone of the verse suggests that before its revelation, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would pray over the hypocrites and stand by their graves, as they were outwardly Muslims [^1]. But after the revelation of this verse, this practice was definitively discontinued forever.

[^1]: Some narrations suggest that even after the revelation of this verse, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would pray over the hypocrites but would say only four Takbirs—meaning he would omit the final Takbir which pertains to supplicating for the deceased. This narration is acceptable only if the phrase "do not pray" (la tusalli) in the verse under discussion means "do not supplicate." However, if it means "do not perform the funeral prayer," then this narration contradicts the Quran and is unacceptable. It cannot be denied that the phrase "la tusalli" outwardly carries the second meaning. Therefore, we cannot perform the funeral prayer for such individuals whose hypocrisy has been exposed, nor can we abandon the apparent meaning of the verse for the sake of an ambiguous narration.

  1. It is also understood from the above verse that standing by the graves of believers and praying for them is permissible, because the prohibition in the verse is specific to the hypocrites. Therefore, the implication of the verse is that visiting the graves of believers—meaning standing by their graves and praying—is permissible.

However, the above verse is silent regarding whether one can seek intercession (Tawassul) through their graves or ask Allah for a need through their blessings, although the permissibility of this matter is established according to Islamic narrations.

Ayahs

وَإِذَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ مَعَ رَسُولِهِ ٱسْتَـْٔذَنَكَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلطَّوْلِ مِنْهُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ ذَرْنَا نَكُن مَّعَ ٱلْقَـٰعِدِينَ (٨٦)
When a surah is sent down [declaring]: ‘Have faith in Allah, and wage jihad along with His Apostle, the affluent among them ask you for leave, and say, ‘Let us remain with those who sit back.’ (86)
رَضُوا۟ بِأَن يَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَوَالِفِ وَطُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ (٨٧)
They are pleased to be with those who stay back, and their hearts have been sealed. So they do not understand. (87)
لَـٰكِنِ ٱلرَّسُولُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتُ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٨٨)
But the Apostle and the faithful who are with him wage jihad with their possessions and persons, and to such belong all the blessings, and it is they who are the felicitous. (88)
أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٨٩)
Allah has prepared for them gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great success. (89)

Commentary

The Low-Spirited and the True Believers

In these verses, the discourse once again turns to the hypocrites; however, their wicked deeds are contrasted here with the righteous actions of the true believers. Through this comparison, their deviation and wretchedness become more apparent.

In the first verse, it says: "Whenever a Surah is revealed regarding Jihad, inviting the people to believe in Allah (meaning to remain steadfast in their faith and to strengthen it) and to strive in Jihad alongside the Messenger of Allah ص in His way, at this moment, the powerful hypocrites—who possess sufficient physical and financial capability to participate in the battlefield—ask your permission to be excused from Jihad, saying: 'Leave us to be with those who sit behind (those who are excused from Jihad)'" (وَ إِذا أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ أَنْ آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ جاهِدُوا مَعَ رَسُولِهِ اسْتَأْذَنَكَ أُولُوا الطَّوْلِ مِنْهُمْ وَ قالُوا ذَرْنا نَكُنْ مَعَ الْقاعِدِينَ).

"Tawl" (طول) refers to means and financial capability. Therefore, "Ulul-Tawl" (أُولُوا الطَّوْلِ) refers to those who possess sufficient material power to be present on the battlefield, yet despite this, they preferred to remain in the ranks of the weak—those who lacked the physical or financial ability for Jihad.

The root of this word is derived from "Tul" (طول), which is the opposite of "Ardh" (width/breadth). The connection between these two meanings is clear, as financial and physical capability represents a kind of extension, continuity, and "length" of power.

In the following verse, the Quran reproaches and condemns them with this phrase: "They were content to remain with those who stayed behind" (رَضُوا بِأَنْ يَكُونُوا مَعَ الْخَوالِفِ). As we previously mentioned, "Khawalif" (خوالف) is the plural of "Khalifah," from the root "Khalf" (خلف) meaning "behind." For this reason, women who remain at home while men go out are called "Khalifah." In the verse under discussion, "Khawalif" refers to all those who were somehow excused from participating in the battlefield, whether they were women, the elderly, the sick, or children.

Some traditions narrated regarding the interpretation of this verse also point to this matter.

It then adds: "Due to sin and hypocrisy, these people have reached a stage where their hearts have been sealed; for this reason, they understand nothing" (وَ طُبِعَ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يَفْقَهُونَ).

At the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, we discussed the meaning of "sealing the heart." [^1]

In the next verse, mention is made of a group that stands in direct opposition to this category, whose qualities, spirit, and ultimate end are exactly the opposite: "But the Messenger and those who believed with him strove with their wealth and their lives in the way of Allah" (لكِنِ الرَّسُولُ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ جاهَدُوا بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ).

And the result of their work was that "all kinds of goodness, happiness, victories, and material and spiritual blessings in this world and the hereafter are their portion" (وَ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْخَيْراتُ), "and it is they who are the successful" (وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ).

The word "Al-Khayrat" (الْخَيْراتُ) is a plural form accompanied by the definite article "Al," from which universality is inferred. It is a comprehensive expression that includes every kind of success, victory, blessing, and good, whether material or spiritual.

The expressions in these two sentences, according to the rules of the science of rhetoric (Ma'ani wa Bayan), all indicate exclusivity. That is, the above expressions show that the successful ones are only this group, and similarly, those who deserve every kind of good and happiness are only this party—those who strive with their entire being and resources.

From this verse, it is clearly understood that if "faith" and "Jihad" are combined, they will bring every kind of good and blessing with them. Outside the shadow of these two, there is neither a path to prosperity and success, nor any share of material and spiritual goods and blessings.

It is also noteworthy that from the comparison of the qualities of these two groups, it is inferred that the hypocrites, due to their lack of faith and extreme pollution with sin, are ignorant and unaware individuals. For this reason, they are deprived of "high ambition" (uluww al-himmah), which is born of understanding, intellect, and awareness. They are content to stay with the sick and children, yet they refrain from participating in the field of Jihad with all its honors.

In contrast, the people of faith possess such insight, understanding, and high ambition that they have found the only way to overcome problems to be Jihad, and that too with all their resources.

This is the great lesson that the Quran has given us in many of its verses, yet we remain heedless of it!

In the final verse under discussion, it refers to a part of the otherworldly rewards for this group, saying: "Allah has prepared for them gardens of Paradise beneath which rivers flow" (أَعَدَّ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

And it emphasizes: This blessing and bounty is not borrowed or perishable; rather, "they shall abide therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها).

"And that is the great success" (ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ).

The expression "Allah has prepared for them" (أَعَدَّ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ) is a sign of the importance of the matter and the respect that Allah accords to this group, having prepared these blessings for them in advance.

[^1]: Volume 1, page 53.

Ayah

وَجَآءَ ٱلْمُعَذِّرُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ لِيُؤْذَنَ لَهُمْ وَقَعَدَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۚ سَيُصِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٩٠)
Some of the Bedouins who sought to be excused came, so that they may be granted leave [to stay back]; while those who lied to Allah and His Apostle sat back. Soon there shall visit the faithless among them a painful punishment. (90)

Commentary

In this verse, in connection with the previous discussions regarding the excuse-seeking and pretext-making hypocrites, reference is made to the situation of two groups of those who desisted from Jihad: first, those who were truly excused.

And second, the group who, without any excuse and out of rebellion and disobedience, refused to fulfill this great duty.

First, it says: "And those with excuses from the Bedouins came to you so that they might be granted permission and be exempted" (وَ جاءَ الْمُعَذِّرُونَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ لِيُؤْذَنَ لَهُمْ‌).

In contrast, "those who lied to Allah and His Messenger sat at home" without any excuse and did not go to the battlefield (وَ قَعَدَ الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ‌).

At the end of the verse, it severely threatens the second group, saying: "Soon those among them who disbelieved will be afflicted with a painful punishment" (سَيُصِيبُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ‌).

What we have stated in the commentary of the above verse is what is most consistent with the context and evidence present in the verse; because, on one hand, we see that these two groups are placed in opposition to one another, and on the other hand, the word "minhum" (among them) indicates that not all of these two groups were disbelievers. From the combination of these two pieces of evidence, it is inferred that the "Mu'adhdhirun" were those with genuine excuses.

However, in contrast to this interpretation, two other interpretations have also been mentioned:

First: That "Mu'adhdhirun" refers to those who fabricate hollow and false excuses to flee from Jihad, and the second group refers to those who do not even bother to make excuses and explicitly refuse to obey Allah's command regarding Jihad.

Second: That "Mu'adhdhirun" includes all groups who refrain from participating in Jihad by presenting an excuse, whether it be true or false.

However, the evidence indicates that the "Mu'adhdhirun" are indeed those with real excuses.

Ayahs

لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلضُّعَفَآءِ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرْضَىٰ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ مَا يُنفِقُونَ حَرَجٌ إِذَا نَصَحُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ مَا عَلَى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ مِن سَبِيلٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩١)
There is no blame on the weak, nor on the sick, nor on those who do not find anything to spend, so long as they are sincere to Allah and His Apostle. There is no [cause for] blaming the virtuous, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (91)
وَلَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا مَآ أَتَوْكَ لِتَحْمِلَهُمْ قُلْتَ لَآ أَجِدُ مَآ أَحْمِلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ تَوَلَّوا۟ وَّأَعْيُنُهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ حَزَنًا أَلَّا يَجِدُوا۟ مَا يُنفِقُونَ (٩٢)
Nor [is there any blame] on those to whom, when they came to you to provide them with a mount, you said, ‘I do not find any mount for you,’ and they turned back, their eyes flowing with tears, grieved because they did not find any means to spend. (92)
۞ إِنَّمَا ٱلسَّبِيلُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَـْٔذِنُونَكَ وَهُمْ أَغْنِيَآءُ ۚ رَضُوا۟ بِأَن يَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلْخَوَالِفِ وَطَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٩٣)
The blame lies only on those who ask leave of you [to stay behind] though they are well-off. They are pleased to be with those who stay back; Allah has set a seal on their hearts, so they do not know [the outcome of their conduct]. (93)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the first verse, it is narrated that one of the sincere companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "O Prophet of Allah! I am an old man, blind and weak; I do not even have anyone to take my hand and lead me to the field of Jihad. Am I excused if I do not participate in Jihad?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) remained silent, then the first verse was revealed, granting permission to such individuals.

From this occasion of revelation, it is understood that even the blind did not allow themselves to refrain from participating in Jihad without informing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). They sought his instruction, considering the possibility that their presence, even in that state, might be useful for encouraging the warriors or at least increasing the apparent size of the army.

Regarding the second verse, we read in narrations that seven poor men from the Ansar came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and requested that means for participating in Jihad be provided for them. However, since the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had no means at his disposal, he gave them a negative answer. They left his presence with eyes full of tears and later became famous as the "Bakka'un" (The Weepers).

Commentary

The Excused Who Shed Tears Out of Love for Jihad!

In these verses, to clarify the status of all groups regarding whether they are excused or not from participating in Jihad, a distinct classification is made. Five groups are mentioned, four of whom are truly excused, while one group consists of hypocrites who are not excused! The first verse states: "There is no blame on those who are weak and infirm (due to old age or physical defects such as loss of sight), nor on the sick, nor on those who do not find anything to spend (the necessary means to participate in the field of Jihad), if they do not participate in this obligatory Islamic program" (لَيْسَ عَلَى الضُّعَفاءِ وَ لا عَلَى الْمَرْضى‌ وَ لا عَلَى الَّذِينَ لا يَجِدُونَ ما يُنْفِقُونَ حَرَجٌ‌).

These three groups are exempt under any law, and reason and logic also endorse their exemption. It is certain that Islamic laws are never detached from logic and reason in any case.

The word haraj (حرج) originally means a center where things gather, and since a gathering or a dense crowd is associated with narrowness and a lack of space, this word has come to mean "narrowness," "distress," and "responsibility or obligation." In the verse under discussion, it carries the latter meaning, i.e., responsibility and obligation.

Then, an important condition for the ruling of their exemption is stated: "This is provided that they do not withhold any sincere goodwill toward Allah and His Messenger" (إِذا نَصَحُوا لِلَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ‌).

This means that although they lack the ability to take up arms and participate in the battlefield, they do have the ability to encourage the warriors through their speech and conduct, to hearten the fighters, and to strengthen their morale by recounting the fruits of Jihad. Conversely, as much as they are able, they should not fall short in weakening the enemy's morale and facilitating their defeat. This is because the word nush (نصح), which originally means sincerity, is a comprehensive term that includes every kind of goodwill and sincere action. Since the issue of Jihad is being discussed, it refers to the efforts and endeavors made in this regard.

Subsequently, to state the reason for this matter, He says: "Such individuals are doers of good, and for the doers of good, there is no way for blame, reproach, punishment, or calling to account" (ما عَلَى الْمُحْسِنِينَ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ‌).

At the end of the verse, He describes Allah with two of His great attributes as another reason for the exemption of these three groups, saying: "And Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

Ghafur (Forgiving) is derived from ghufran, meaning to veil or cover. That is, by virtue of this attribute, Allah casts a veil over the actions of the excused and weak individuals and accepts their excuse. Allah being Rahim (Merciful) necessitates that He does not impose a grueling or difficult task on anyone and exempts them. If these groups were forced to be present on the field, it would not be consistent with Allah's Forgiveness and Mercy; thus, the Forgiving and Merciful Allah will certainly exempt them.

From some narrations cited by commentators under this verse, it is understood that the excused groups are not only exempt from the obligation and free from punishment, but they also share in the rewards and honors of the warriors to the extent of their longing to participate in Jihad. As we read in a Hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله):

When he returned from the Battle of Tabuk and approached Medina, he said: "In this city, you have left behind men who were with you throughout the entire journey! Every step you took, every wealth you spent in this way, and every land you traversed, they were with you!" They asked: "O Messenger of Allah, how were they with us while they remained in Medina?"

He replied: "Because they were unable to participate in Jihad due to a valid excuse (but their hearts were with us)." [1]

Then He points to a fourth group who were also exempted from participating in Jihad, saying: "Nor is there any blame on those who, when they came to you so that you might provide them with mounts to participate in the field of Jihad, you said: 'I do not find anything to carry you upon.' They turned away while their eyes were overflowing with tears, and these tears were due to the sorrow that they did not find anything to spend in the way of Allah" (وَ لا عَلَى الَّذِينَ إِذا ما أَتَوْكَ لِتَحْمِلَهُمْ قُلْتَ لا أَجِدُ ما أَحْمِلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ تَوَلَّوْا وَ أَعْيُنُهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ الدَّمْعِ حَزَناً أَلَّا يَجِدُوا ما يُنْفِقُونَ‌).

Tafid (تفيض) is from the root fayadan, meaning to overflow due to being full. When a person becomes upset, if the distress is not very intense, the eyes fill with tears without flowing; however, when the distress reaches an intense stage, the tears flow.

This shows that this group of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) companions were so enamored, devoted, and in love with Jihad that they were not only not happy about being exempted, but they wept in the grief of this deprivation like someone who has lost their dearest loved ones.

Of course, there is no doubt that this fourth group is not separate from the third group mentioned in the previous verse, but because of the special distinction they have over them, and also to show appreciation for this group, their state has been depicted independently within a single verse.

[1] Al-Durr al-Manthur, as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 9, p. 386.

Their distinction lay in the following:

First, they did not content themselves with the fact that they lacked the necessary means to participate in Jihad; rather, they came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and insistently requested a mount from him.

Second, when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) gave them a negative answer, they were not only not joyful about being exempted, but they became extremely agitated, upset, and sorrowful. Because of these two reasons, Allah has mentioned them specifically and separately.

In the final verse, He describes the state of the fifth group—those who were not and will never be excused before Allah—saying: "The way of calling to account and punishment is only open against those who ask your permission to not participate in Jihad while they have sufficient resources and the necessary means for this task and are completely wealthy" (إِنَّمَا السَّبِيلُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَسْتَأْذِنُونَكَ وَ هُمْ أَغْنِياءُ).

Then He adds: "This disgrace is enough for them: that they were satisfied to remain in Medina with the weak, the sick, and the disabled, and to be deprived of the honor of participating in Jihad" (رَضُوا بِأَنْ يَكُونُوا مَعَ الْخَوالِفِ‌).

And this punishment is also enough for them: that Allah has taken away their power of thought and perception because of their ugly deeds, "and He has set a seal upon their hearts, and for this reason, they do not know" (وَ طَبَعَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

Here, several points should be noted

[The High Morale of the Soldiers of Islam]

  1. From these verses, the strong and high morale of the soldiers of Islam is clearly revealed—how the love for Jihad and martyrdom kept their hearts warm, such that they prioritized this honor over every other honor. From here, one of the important factors for the rapid progress of Islam in those days and our backwardness today becomes clear.

How can we expect those who shed tears like spring clouds upon being exempted from participating in Jihad to be equal to those who want to leave the ranks of the warriors with "a thousand and one excuses"?!

If that spirit of "faith," that "love for Jihad," and that "priding oneself on martyrdom" were revived among us Muslims today, victory and progress would be just as they were at the beginning of Islam.

The misfortune is that we have made Islam a superficial and outward covering for ourselves without it penetrating the depths of our being, and yet we still expect to occupy the position of the early Muslims!

[No One is Exempt from Jihad]

  1. From the preceding verses, it is also understood that no one is entirely exempt from cooperating with the warriors in the way of Allah—even those who are sick or blind and naturally unable to take up arms and participate in the battlefield. However, through their tongue, propagation, and actions, they can be encouragers of the warriors and supporters of their programs. They too must not forget this mission of theirs and should not withdraw entirely from the programs.

In reality, Jihad has stages, and being excused from one stage of it is not a reason for being excused from the other stages.

  1. The phrase "There is no way (to blame) the doers of good" (ما عَلَى الْمُحْسِنِينَ مِنْ سَبِيلٍ‌) has become the source of a broad legal principle in jurisprudential discussions, from which many rulings have been derived. For example, whenever a trust in the hands of a trustworthy person is destroyed without any excess or negligence, such a person should not be held liable. One of the proofs cited for this is the aforementioned verse, because he is a "Muhsin" (doer of good) and has not committed a wrongful act. For this reason, if we were to hold him responsible and liable, it would mean that doers of good can also be called to account.

Of course, there is no doubt that the above verse is about the warriors, but we know that the specific occasion of a verse does not diminish the generality of the ruling; in other words, the occasion is never a restrictor (mukhassis).

"End of the Tenth Part (Juz') of the Holy Quran"

Beginning of the 11th Part (Juz')

Ayahs

يَعْتَذِرُونَ إِلَيْكُمْ إِذَا رَجَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ قُل لَّا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكُمْ قَدْ نَبَّأَنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْ أَخْبَارِكُمْ ۚ وَسَيَرَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٤)
They will offer you excuses when you return to them. Say, ‘Do not make excuses; we will never believe you. Allah has informed us of your state of affairs. Allah and His Apostle will observe your conduct, then you will be returned to the Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, and He will inform you concerning what you used to do.’ (94)
سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ إِذَا ٱنقَلَبْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لِتُعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ فَأَعْرِضُوا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ رِجْسٌ ۖ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٩٥)
They will swear to you by Allah when you return to them, that you may leave them alone. So leave them alone. They are indeed filth, and their refuge shall be hell, a requital for what they used to earn. (95)
يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ لِتَرْضَوْا۟ عَنْهُمْ ۖ فَإِن تَرْضَوْا۟ عَنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَرْضَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٩٦)
They swear to you that you may be reconciled to them. But even if you are reconciled to them, Allah shall not be reconciled to the transgressing lot. (96)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators state that these verses were revealed regarding a group of hypocrites numbering over eighty individuals. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned from Tabuk, he commanded that no one should sit with them or speak to them. Finding themselves under severe social pressure, they came forward to offer excuses; subsequently, the above verses were revealed to clarify their situation.

Commentary

Do Not Pay Heed to Their False Excuses and Oaths

This series of verses continues to speak about the satanic deeds of the hypocrites, uncovering their actions one after another, and warning the Muslims not to be deceived by their hypocritical acts and seemingly pleasant words.

In the first verse, it says: "When you return (from the Battle of Tabuk) to Medina, the hypocrites will come to you and offer excuses" (يَعْتَذِرُونَ إِلَيْكُمْ إِذا رَجَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ‌).

From the use of the term ya’tadhirun (يَعْتَذِرُونَ), which is a present-tense verb, it appears that Allah had previously informed the Prophet and the Muslims that the lying hypocrites would soon come to them offering excuses; therefore, He also taught the Muslims how to respond to them.

Here, addressing His Prophet as the leader of the Muslims, He says:

"Tell the hypocrites: Make no excuses, for we will never believe you" (قُلْ لا تَعْتَذِرُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكُمْ‌).

"Because Allah has already informed us of your news," therefore, we are well aware of your satanic plots! (قَدْ نَبَّأَنَا اللَّهُ مِنْ أَخْبارِكُمْ‌).

"However, at the same time, the path of return and repentance remains open to you," and "soon Allah and His Messenger will observe your deeds" (وَ سَيَرَى اللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَ رَسُولُهُ‌). Another possibility suggested in the commentary of this verse is that this phrase does not refer to repentance, but rather its goal is to state that in the future as well, Allah and His Messenger (through divine revelation) will be aware of your actions and plots and will frustrate them; thus, you can achieve nothing today, nor tomorrow!

However, the first interpretation is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse.

Furthermore, note that regarding this phrase and the issue of all the deeds of the Ummah being presented to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we have a detailed discussion that will follow under verse 105 of this same Surah.

Then He says that all your deeds and intentions are recorded and archived today, "then you will be returned to the Knower of the unseen and the seen, and He will inform you of your deeds" and will give you their recompense (ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلى‌ عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

In the next verse, He refers once again to the false oaths of the hypocrites and says: "To deceive you, they will soon resort to oaths, and when you return to them, they will swear by Allah so that you may leave them alone, and if they have committed a mistake, you might include them in your forgiveness" (سَيَحْلِفُونَ بِاللَّهِ لَكُمْ إِذَا انْقَلَبْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ لِتُعْرِضُوا عَنْهُمْ‌).

In reality, they enter through every door; sometimes through offering excuses they want to portray themselves as innocent, and sometimes by confessing to sin, they request pardon and forgiveness, hoping that through one of these ways they might influence your hearts.

But you must not be influenced by them in any way and "turn away from them"—but as a sign of protest, anger, and rejection! Not as a sign of pardon, mercy, and forgiveness (فَأَعْرِضُوا عَنْهُمْ‌).

They request "turning away" in the sense of "forgiveness," and you should also "turn away," but in the sense of "denial and rejection." This use of two similar expressions with two completely opposite meanings possesses a particular subtlety and beauty that is not hidden from those of refined taste.

Then, as an emphasis, clarification, and statement of the reason, He says: "For they are an impurity (filth)," and one must turn away from such impure beings (إِنَّهُمْ رِجْسٌ‌), and because they are such, "their abode shall be Hell" (وَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ‌).

For Paradise is the place for the good and the pure, not the abode for the impure and the contaminated! However, "all of this is the result of the deeds they themselves have earned" (جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ‌).

In the last verse under discussion, another one of their oaths is mentioned, which is that "they swear to you with insistence so that you may be pleased with them" (يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ لِتَرْضَوْا عَنْهُمْ‌). The oath mentioned in the previous verse was so that the believers would not show any practical reaction against them, but the oath referred to in this verse is so that, in addition to the practical aspect, they might also be pleased with them in their hearts! Interestingly, in this case, Allah does not say: "Do not be pleased with them," rather—

With an expression that carries the scent of a threat, He says: "Even if you are pleased with them, Allah will never be pleased with the rebellious people" (فَإِنْ تَرْضَوْا عَنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَرْضى‌ عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ‌).

Doubtless, from a religious and moral perspective, they did not value the pleasure of the Muslims; rather, they wanted to wash away the Muslims' inner resentment through this path so that they would be safe from their future reactions. However, Allah, with the expression "He is not pleased with the rebellious people," warns the Muslims that these people are transgressors (fasiq) and there is no place for being pleased with them; these are deceptive traps they set in your path—be vigilant and do not get caught in them!

How good it is for Muslims in every age and time to be watchful of the well-known satanic plots of the hypocrites, so that they do not appear before them using those same past tricks and traps, and do not implement their sinister goals using these means.

Ayahs

ٱلْأَعْرَابُ أَشَدُّ كُفْرًا وَنِفَاقًا وَأَجْدَرُ أَلَّا يَعْلَمُوا۟ حُدُودَ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٩٧)
The Bedouins are more obdurate in unfaith and hypocrisy, and more apt to be ignorant of the precepts that Allah has sent down to His Apostle, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (97)
وَمِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مَن يَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنفِقُ مَغْرَمًا وَيَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمُ ٱلدَّوَآئِرَ ۚ عَلَيْهِمْ دَآئِرَةُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٩٨)
Among the Bedouins are those who regard what they spend as a loss, and they watch for a reversal of your fortunes. Theirs shall be an adverse turn of fortune, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (98)
وَمِنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَيَتَّخِذُ مَا يُنفِقُ قُرُبَـٰتٍ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَصَلَوَٰتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّهَا قُرْبَةٌ لَّهُمْ ۚ سَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩٩)
Yet among the Bedouins are [also] those who believe in Allah and the Last Day, and regard what they spend as [a means of attaining] nearness to Allah and the blessings of the Apostle. Now, it shall indeed bring them nearness, and Allah will admit them into His mercy. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (99)

Commentary

The Hardhearted and the Faithful Bedouins

In these three verses—in connection with the preceding discussions regarding the hypocrites of Medina—there is a discussion concerning the state of the hypocritical Bedouins, their signs and thoughts, as well as the sincere and truthful believers of the desert.

Perhaps this is to warn the Muslims not to imagine that the hypocrites are only those residing in the city; rather, the hypocritical Bedouins are harsher than them. As Islamic history bears witness, the Muslims were repeatedly attacked by this group; thus, the successive victories of the army of Islam should not lead them to overlook this danger.

In any case, the first verse states: "The Bedouin Arabs (by virtue of their distance from education and training, and not hearing the Divine verses and the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله) are more stubborn in disbelief and hypocrisy" (الْأَعْرابُ أَشَدُّ كُفْراً وَ نِفاقاً).

"And for this same reason, they are more likely to be ignorant of the limits of the commands and laws that Allah has sent down to His Messenger" (وَ أَجْدَرُ أَلَّا يَعْلَمُوا حُدُودَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ رَسُولِهِ‌).

"A'rab" (الأعراب) is a word that carries a collective meaning, but it has no singular form in the Arabic language. As lexicographers such as the authors of Al-Qamus, Al-Sihah, Taj al-Arus, and others have stated, this word refers specifically to the desert-dwelling Arabs. When they wish to express the singular meaning, they use the same word with the suffix of relation (ya al-nisbah), as "A'rabi" (اعرابى).

Therefore, contrary to what many imagine, A'rab is not the plural of Arab.

"Ajdar" (أجدر) is derived from the root jidar (جدار), meaning "wall." Subsequently, it was applied to anything elevated and worthy; for this reason, ajdar is usually used to mean "more worthy" or "more likely."

At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise." This means that if He judges the desert Arabs in this manner, it is based on the specific correlation that their environment has with such attributes (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ‌).

However, so that the misconception does not arise that all desert Arabs, or all Bedouins, possess such attributes, the next verse points to two different groups among them.

First, it says: "Among these Bedouin Arabs are those who, due to hypocrisy or weakness of faith, when they spend something in the way of Allah, they consider it a loss and a fine (penalty)" rather than a success, a victory, or a profitable trade (ومِنَ الْأَعْرابُ مَنْ يَتَّخِذُ ما يُنْفِقُ مَغْرَماً) [1]. Another of their attributes is that "they are always waiting for calamities and problems to surround you, and for misfortune and failure to come your way" (وَ يَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمُ الدَّوائِرَ).

[1]: Maghram (مغرم), as stated in Majma' al-Bayan, is derived from the root ghurm (غرم) (rhyming with jurm), which originally means being inseparable from something. Consequently, the "creditor" and "debtor" who do not leave each other and are inseparable are called gharim (غريم). Gharamat (غرامت - fine/indemnity) is also called so because it stays with a person and will not leave him until he pays it. Intense love is also called gharam because it penetrates the human soul such that it is inseparable. Maghram is equal in meaning to gharamat.

Dawa'ir (دوائر) is the plural of da'irah (دائرة), and its meaning is well-known. However, the Arabs also use da'irah (and in plural dawa'ir) to refer to severe and painful events that surround a person.

In reality, they are narrow-minded, stingy, and envious individuals. Their stinginess causes them to perceive any financial service in the way of Allah as a fine, and their envy causes them to always wait for the emergence of problems, difficulties, and calamities for others. Then He adds: "They should not wait for the emergence of problems and the descent of calamities upon you, for these problems, failures, and misfortunes only go toward this group of faithless, ignorant, narrow-minded, and envious hypocrites" (عَلَيْهِمْ دائِرَةُ السَّوْءِ) [1]. Finally, He ends the verse with the phrase: "And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing" (وَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ‌).

He hears their words and is aware of their intentions and the secrets of their hearts.

In the final verse, He refers to the second group, namely the sincere Bedouin believers, saying: "And among the Bedouin Arabs are those who believe in Allah and the Last Day" (وَ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ‌ الآخر).

For this reason, they never consider spending in the way of Allah as a fine or loss; rather, in view of the vast Divine rewards in this world and the Hereafter, "they consider this act a means of nearness to Allah and a cause for the blessings and prayers of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which is a great honor and blessing" (وَ يَتَّخِذُ ما يُنْفِقُ قُرُباتٍ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَ صَلَواتِ الرَّسُولِ‌).

Here, Allah confirms their way of thinking with great emphasis and

[1]: From the phrase "عَلَيْهِمْ دائِرَةُ السَّوْءِ", the meaning of restriction (hasr) is understood; that is, evil events exclusively overtake them. This use of restriction is because "عَلَيْهِمْ" (upon them), which serves as the predicate (khabar), has been placed before the subject (mubtada).

says: "Unquestionably, these spendings are certainly a means of nearness for them in the presence of Allah" (أَلا إِنَّها قُرْبَةٌ لَهُمْ‌).

And for this reason, "Allah will soon admit them into His mercy" (سَيُدْخِلُهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ‌).

And if any slips have occurred from them, He will forgive them because of their faith and pure deeds, "for Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

The successive emphases seen in this verse are truly remarkable: the words "Ala" (ألا) and "Inna" (إنّ), both of which are for expressing emphasis; then the sentence "سَيُدْخِلُهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ‌", especially considering the word "fi" (in) which implies entering and being immersed in Divine mercy; and finally the last sentence starting with "Inna" and mentioning two of Allah's merciful attributes (Forgiving and Merciful). All of these express the ultimate grace and mercy of God toward this group. Perhaps this is because this group, despite being deprived of education and lacking sufficient access to Divine verses and the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, still accepted Islam with heart and soul, and despite lacking financial means (as the condition of Bedouins necessitates), they do not withhold from spending in the way of Allah. Therefore, they are worthy of every appreciation and encouragement, even more than the well-to-do city dwellers.

It is particularly necessary to note that regarding the "hypocritical Bedouins," the phrase "عَلَيْهِمْ دائِرَةُ السَّوْءِ" (upon them is the circle of evil) was used, indicating the surrounding of misfortunes upon them. However, regarding the "faithful and self-sacrificing Bedouins," the phrase "فِي رَحْمَتِهِ" (in His mercy) is mentioned, which expresses the surrounding of Divine mercy upon them. One is surrounded by His mercy, and the other by misfortunes!

Key Points

1- Grand Assemblies

From the above verses, the importance that Islam gives to large societies and more populated centers becomes clear. Interestingly, Islam arose from a backward environment—an environment where no scent of civilization was smelled—yet it places special importance on the constructive factors of civilization and says: those who live in remote areas away from the city are even further behind in terms of faith and religious knowledge.

This is because they do not have sufficient facilities for education and training at their disposal.

Thus, we read in Nahj al-Balagha that Ali عليه السلام said: "وَ الْزَمُوا السَّوَادَ الْأَعْظَمَ فَإِنَّ يَدَ اللَّهِ مَعَ الْجَمَاعَةِ" "Adhere to the great mass (the large centers), for Allah’s hand is with the community" [1]. But the meaning of this statement is not that all people should turn toward the cities and leave the villages—which are the source of the cities' prosperity—to ruin. Rather, on the contrary, the science and knowledge of the city must be taken to the village, and efforts must be made to strengthen the principles of education, the foundations of religion and faith, and their awakening and awareness.

Undoubtedly, if the villagers are left to themselves and no window of urban sciences, knowledge, heavenly verses, and the teachings of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the great leaders is opened to them, disbelief and hypocrisy will quickly envelop them. They are especially more receptive to correct education because pure hearts and untouched thoughts are abundant among them, and the mischief and trickery of the city are less prevalent among them!

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 127.

2- Urban Bedouins

Although "A'rabi" means a desert-dweller, it has acquired a broader meaning in Islamic reports and traditions. In other words, its Islamic concept is not tied to a geographical region but rather to a way of thinking and a mental zone: those who are far from Islamic manners, traditions, and education—even if they are city dwellers—are "A'rabi." Conversely, desert-dwellers who are aware and familiar with Islamic manners and traditions are not "A'rabi."

The famous Hadith: "مَنْ لَمْ يَتَفَقَّهْ مِنْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ فَهُوَ أَعْرَابِيٌّ" "Whoever among you does not gain understanding of his religion is an A'rabi," which is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, is a clear witness to the above statement [1]. In another report, we read: "مِنَ الْكُفْرِ التَّعَرُّبُ بَعْدَ الْهِجْرَةِ" "To become an A'rabi after migration is a branch of disbelief."

Also, it is narrated from Ali عليه السلام in Nahj al-Balagha that he addressed a group of his rebellious companions, saying: "وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّكُمْ صِرْتُمْ بَعْدَ الْهِجْرَةِ أَعْرَاباً" "Know that after the migration, you have become A'rabi!" [2]. In the two aforementioned Hadiths, "becoming an A'rabi" is placed as the opposite of "migration" (Hijrah). Given that the broad concept of migration is also not merely spatial but is fundamentally the transition of thought from the axis of disbelief to the axis of faith, the meaning of being an A'rabi also becomes clear: it is the return from "Islamic manners and traditions" to "the manners and traditions of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah)."

3- In the above verses regarding the faithful Bedouins, we read that they consider their spending a means of "nearness" (qurb) to Allah. Specifically, this word appeared in the plural form as "qurubat" (قربات), which shows that they seek not just one nearness, but many nearnesses through it. There is no doubt that "nearness" (qurb and qurbat) before the Lord does not mean spatial proximity, but rather proximity of status—that is, moving toward Him who is Absolute Perfection, and casting a reflection of His attributes of Beauty and Majesty upon the canvas of one's thought and soul.

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 254. [2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon of al-Qasi'ah, p. 192.

Ayah

وَٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ وَٱلْأَنصَارِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُم بِإِحْسَـٰنٍ رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى تَحْتَهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٠٠)
The early vanguard of the Emigrants and the Helpers and those who followed them in virtue—Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. That is the great success. (100)

Commentary

The Pioneers of Islam

Although commentators have narrated various traditions regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verse, as we shall see, none of them constitute a specific "occasion of revelation" (sha'n al-nuzul); rather, they are in fact explanations of its instances and external existence.

In any case, following the previous verses which described the state of the disbelievers and the hypocrites, the above verse refers to different groups of true Muslims and divides them into three distinct categories:

First, those who were the pioneers in Islam and the Migration (وَ السَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ).

Second, those who were pioneers in supporting and aiding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his Migrant companions (وَ الْأَنْصارِ).

Third, those who came after these two groups and followed their programs, joining them by performing righteous deeds, accepting Islam, migrating, and aiding the religion of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (وَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُمْ بِإِحْسانٍ). [^1] From what we have said, it becomes clear that the meaning of "with righteousness" (bi-ihsan) is actually an expression of the deeds and beliefs in which they follow the pioneers of Islam. In other words, "righteousness" (ihsan) is a description of the programs that are followed.

However, another possibility has been suggested for the meaning of the verse: that "righteousness" is a description of the manner of following, meaning they follow in a worthy and proper manner (in the first case, the "with" (ba) means "in," and in the second case, it means "accompanied by").

But the apparent meaning of the verse accords with the first interpretation.

After mentioning these three groups, He says: "Allah is well-pleased with them, and they are well-pleased with Him" (رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَ رَضُوا عَنْهُ).

Allah's pleasure with them is due to the faith and righteous deeds they performed, and their satisfaction with Allah is due to the various, extraordinary, and significant rewards He has bestowed upon them.

[^1]: Many commentators have considered the word "from" (min) in the above phrase (وَ السَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ وَ الْأَنْصارِ) to be "partitive" (min al-tab'idiyyah), and the apparent meaning of the verse supports this, as the verse speaks of the pioneers among the Migrants and the Helpers, not all of them. In fact, the rest fall under the concept of the subsequent phrase, i.e., the followers.

In other words, they performed what Allah required of them, and He granted them what they requested of Him; thus, both Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Allah.

Although the previous sentence encompassed all Divine favors and blessings (material and spiritual, physical and psychological), as an emphasis and to provide "detail" after "brevity," He adds: "Allah has prepared for them gardens of Paradise beneath which rivers flow" (وَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي تَحْتَهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

One of the distinctions of this blessing is that it is eternal and "they will abide therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً).

"And the sum of these spiritual and material favors constitutes a great success for them" (ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ).

What success is greater than for a human to feel that his Creator, his Deity, and his Master is pleased with him and has signed his certificate of acceptance? And what success is higher than obtaining infinite eternal favors through limited deeds during the few days of a fleeting life?

Here, several important points must be noted

1. The Position of the Pioneers

In every vast social revolution that takes place against a disordered societal state, there are pioneers upon whose shoulders the foundations of the revolution and movement rest. They are, in fact, the most loyal revolutionary elements, for they gather around their leader at a time when he is alone in every respect. Despite being besieged from various sides and surrounded by all kinds of dangers from all four directions, they do not cease their aid and sacrifice.

Specifically, a study of the history of the dawn of Islam shows what difficulties the pioneers and the first believers faced. How they were tortured and harassed, insulted, accused, shackled, and destroyed.

Yet, despite all this, a group with iron will, burning love, firm resolve, and deep faith stepped onto this path and welcomed all kinds of dangers.

Among them, the share of the first Migrants (Muhajirun) was the greatest, followed by the first Helpers (Ansar)—those who welcomed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to Medina with open arms, housed his Migrant companions like their own brothers, defended them with their entire being, and even preferred them over themselves.

If we see that the above verse gives extraordinary importance to these two groups, it is because of this very reason.

Nevertheless, the Holy Quran—as is its constant method—has not ignored the share of others and mentions as "those who followed in righteousness" all the groups who joined Islam, migrated, or sheltered and supported the Migrants during the time of the Prophet or in later times, promising great rewards for all of them.

2. Who were the "Followers" (Tabi'un)?

The terminology used by a group of scholars is that the word "Followers" (Tabi'un) refers only to the students of the Companions (Sahaba), i.e., those who did not see the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله but came after him and expanded Islamic sciences—in other words, those who received their Islamic knowledge directly from the Companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

However, as we said above, the linguistic concept of the verse is not limited to this group; rather, the expression "those who followed in righteousness" includes all groups who, in any era or time, followed the program and goals of the pioneers of Islam.

To explain: contrary to what some think, the issue of "Migration" (Hijrah) as well as "Support" (Nusrah)—which are two constructive Islamic concepts—is not limited to the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Rather, today these two concepts exist in other forms, and they will exist tomorrow as well. Therefore, all those who in some way place themselves on the path of these two programs are included in the concept of "those who followed in righteousness."

However, it is important to note that by mentioning the word "righteousness" (ihsan), the Quran emphasizes that following the pioneers in Islam should not be confined to the realm of words and claims, or even faith without action; rather, this following must be intellectual, practical, and comprehensive.

3. Who was the first Muslim?

Here, most commentators, in connection with the discussion of the above verse, have raised the question: who was the first person to embrace Islam and have this great honor in history recorded in their name?

In response to this question, all have unanimously stated that the first woman to become Muslim was "Khadijah," the loyal and self-sacrificing wife of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. As for the men, all Shia scholars and a vast group of Sunni scholars consider "Ali" عليه السلام to be the first man to respond to the Prophet's call.

The fame of this matter among Sunni scholars is such that a group of them has claimed consensus (ijma') upon it.

Among them, "Hakim al-Nishapuri" in Mustadrak al-Sahihayn, in the book of "Knowledge," page 22, says: "I know of no disagreement among historians that Ali bin Abi Talib رضى اللَّه عنه was the first of them to embrace Islam; they only differ regarding his age of maturity at the time of accepting Islam." [^1] Ibn Abd al-Barr in al-Isti'ab (Vol. 2, p. 457) writes: "They agreed that Khadijah was the first to believe in Allah and His Messenger and to testify to what he brought, then Ali after her." [^2] Abu Ja'far al-Iskafi al-Mu'tazili writes: "People collectively have narrated Ali's pride in being the first to embrace Islam." [^3] Beyond this, numerous narrations from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, as well as from Ali عليه السلام himself and the Companions, have been narrated on this matter to the point of being mutawatir (frequently narrated). Below are a few hadiths as examples:

  1. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "The first of you to reach me at the Pond (al-Hawd) is the first of you to embrace Islam: Ali bin Abi Talib." [^4]

  2. A group of Sunni scholars has narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he took the hand of Ali عليه السلام and said: "Indeed, this is the first to believe in me, and this is the first to shake my hand [on the Day of Resurrection], and this is the Greatest Truthful One (al-Siddiq al-Akbar)." [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 5, p. 3075. [^2] and [^3]: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 3, pp. 238 and 237. [^4]: The above hadith, according to the report in Al-Ghadir, appears in Mustadrak al-Hakim, Vol. 2, p. 136; al-Isti'ab, Vol. 2, p. 457; and Sharh Ibn Abi al-Hadid, Vol. 3, p. 258.

  1. Abu Sa'id al-Khudri narrates from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he struck between the shoulders of Ali عليه السلام and said: "O Ali, you have seven qualities about which no one can argue with you on the Day of Resurrection: you are the first of the believers in Allah in faith, the most faithful of them to the covenant of Allah, and the most steadfast of them in the command of Allah..." [^2]

As we indicated, dozens of narrations in various books of history, commentary, and hadith have been narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and others regarding this. Those interested can refer to the third Arabic volume of Al-Ghadir, pages 220 to 240, and the book Ihqaq al-Haq, Volume 3, pages 114 to 120, for further explanation.

Interestingly, a group that has been unable to deny Ali's عليه السلام precedence in faith and Islam has attempted—for reasons that are obvious—to deny it in another way or to make it seem insignificant. Others have tried to place Abu Bakr in his stead as the first Muslim.

Sometimes they say Ali عليه السلام was ten years old at that time and naturally not yet an adult; therefore, his Islam, being the Islam of a child, had no impact on the strength and power of the Muslim front against the enemy (this statement was brought by Fakhr al-Razi in his commentary under the above verse).

This is truly strange and is, in fact, a criticism of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself. For we know that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله presented Islam to his kinsmen and tribe on the "Day of the House" (Yawm al-Dar), no one accepted it except Ali عليه السلام, who stood up and declared his Islam. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله accepted his Islam and even declared: "You are my brother, my legatee (wasi), and my successor (khalifa)!" This hadith, which a group of hadith masters from both Shia and Sunni have recorded in the Sahih and Musnad books, and which a group of Islamic historians have narrated and relied upon, shows that not only did the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله accept Ali's عليه السلام Islam at that young age, but he also introduced him as his brother, legatee, and successor. [^1]

Sometimes, by using the expression that Khadijah was the first Muslim among women, Abu Bakr the first among men, and Ali عليه السلام the first among children, they have sought to diminish its importance (this expression was mentioned by the famous and biased commentator, the author of Al-Manar, under the verse in question).

However, firstly, as we said, Ali's عليه السلام young age on that day in no way diminishes the importance of the matter, especially since the Quran explicitly says regarding Prophet Yahya عليه السلام: وَ آتَيْناهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا [^2]: "And We gave him wisdom while still a child."

[^1]: In the same source, this hadith is narrated from al-Tabarani, al-Bayhaqi, al-Haythami in al-Majma', Hafiz al-Ganji in Kifayah, Ikmal, and Kanz al-Ummal. [^2]: This hadith, according to the report in Al-Ghadir, is narrated in the book Hilyat al-Awliya, Vol. 1, p. 66. [^1]: This hadith has been narrated with various wordings; what we brought above is the wording narrated by Abu Ja'far al-Iskafi in the book Nahj al-Uthmaniyyah, Burhan al-Din in Anba' Nujaba' al-Anba', Ibn al-Athir in al-Kamil, and others (for more detail, see Vol. 2 of the Arabic Al-Ghadir, pp. 278 to 286). [^2]: Surah Maryam, Verse 12.

And regarding Jesus عليه السلام, we also read that he spoke while still an infant and said to those who were in doubt about him: إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ آتانِيَ الْكِتابَ وَ جَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا: "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet." [^1] When we combine such verses with the hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله above—that he appointed Ali عليه السلام as his legatee, caliph, and successor—it becomes clear that the statement in Al-Manar is nothing more than biased talk.

Secondly, it is not historically certain that Abu Bakr was the third person to accept Islam; rather, many books of history and hadith mention the conversion of another group before him.

We conclude this discussion by noting that Ali عليه السلام repeatedly referred in his speeches to the fact that "I am the first believer, the first Muslim, and the first to pray with the Prophet," clarifying his position, and this has been narrated from him in many books.

Furthermore, Ibn Abi al-Hadid narrates from the famous scholar Abu Ja'far al-Iskafi al-Mu'tazili that if the claim of some that Abu Bakr had precedence in Islam were true, why did he himself never use this as an argument for his own merit in any instance, nor did any of his supporters among the Companions make such a claim? [^2]

4. Were all the Companions righteous individuals?

We previously alluded to the fact that Sunni scholars generally believe that all the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were pure, upright, righteous, worthy, and destined for Paradise.

Here, in connection with the above verse—which some have taken as definitive proof for the above claim—we once again analyze this important issue, which is the source of many shifts in Islamic matters:

Many Sunni commentators have narrated this tradition under the above verse: Hamid bin Ziyad says: I went to Muhammad bin Ka'b al-Qurazi and said to him, "What do you say about the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله?" He said: "All the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) are in Paradise, the righteous among them and the wrongdoers among them!" I said, "On what basis do you say this?" He said, "Read this verse: وَ السَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ وَ الْأَنْصارِ ... until where it says: رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَ رَضُوا عَنْهُ." Then he said, "However, regarding the Followers (Tabi'un), He stipulated a condition, which is that they must follow the Companions only in good deeds (only in that case are they saved, whereas the Companions have no such condition)." [^1]

However, this claim is rejected and unacceptable for many reasons, because:

First—the ruling mentioned in the above verse also includes the Followers, and the "Followers" refers, as we indicated, to all those who follow the path of the first Migrants and Helpers and their programs. Therefore, the entire Ummah without exception would have to be saved! As for the answer given in the hadith of Muhammad bin Ka'b—that Allah mentioned the condition of "righteousness" (ihsan) for the Followers, meaning they must follow the good program and correct method of the Companions, not their sins—this is one of the strangest arguments.

For its implication is adding a "branch" to the "root." If the condition for the salvation of the Followers and those who follow the Companions is that they follow them in righteous deeds, then by priority (a fortiori), this condition must have existed for the Companions themselves.

In other words, Allah says in the above verse that His pleasure and satisfaction include all the first Migrants and Helpers who possessed a correct program, and all their followers—not that He wants to include the Migrants and Helpers in His pleasure whether they were good or bad, while accepting the Followers only with a specific condition.

Second—this matter is in no way compatible with the dictates of reason, for reason grants no privilege to the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله over others. What is the difference between the likes of Abu Jahl and those who first believed and then deviated from his religion?

And why should those who stepped into this world years and centuries after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and whose sacrifices and devotion in the path of Islam were no less than the first companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—rather, they had the distinction of recognizing and believing in him without seeing him—not be included in this Divine mercy and pleasure?

How can the Quran, which says: "Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you," accept this illogical discrimination? How can the Quran, which in its various verses curses the oppressors and the transgressors and considers them deserving of Divine punishment, accept this "illogical immunity of the Companions" against Divine penalty? Are these curses and threats of the Quran subject to exception, and is a specific group excluded from them? Why and for what reason?!

Above all, does such a ruling not amount to giving a "green light" to the Companions regarding any kind of sin or crime?

Third—this ruling is in no way compatible with the texts of Islamic history, for there were many who were once in the ranks of the Migrants and Helpers and then deviated from their path and became subjects of the wrath and anger of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which is coupled with the wrath and anger of Allah. Did we not read in the previous verses the story of Tha'labah bin Hatib al-Ansari and how he deviated and earned the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله displeasure?

To speak more clearly: if their meaning is that the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله generally committed no sin and were infallible and pure from every transgression, this is akin to denying the self-evident.

And if the meaning is that they committed sins and performed wrong actions yet Allah is still pleased with them, the implication is that Allah has expressed pleasure with sin! Who can acquit "Talhah" and "Zubayr"—who were initially among the special companions of the Prophet—as well as "Aisha," the wife of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, from the blood of seventeen thousand Muslims whose blood was shed in the Battle of the Camel? Was Allah pleased with this bloodshed?

Is opposing Ali عليه السلام, the successor of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—who, even if we hypothetically do not accept his divinely appointed caliphate, was at least chosen by the consensus of the Ummah—and drawing swords against him and his loyal companions something that Allah would be pleased and satisfied with?

The truth is that the proponents of the hypothesis of the "sanctification of the Companions," by insisting and persisting on this point, have made the pure face of Islam—which everywhere makes faith and righteous action the measure of a person's character—appear distorted and unappealing.

The final word is that the pleasure and satisfaction of Allah mentioned in the above verse is placed upon a general title, which is "Migration," "Support," "Faith," and "Righteous Action." All the Companions and Followers were subjects of Allah's pleasure as long as they remained under these titles, and the day they exited from under these titles, they also exited from under Allah's pleasure.

From what we have said, it becomes clearly evident that the words of the learned but biased commentator—the author of Al-Manar—who here reproaches and attacks the Shia for not believing in the purity and integrity of all the Companions, have the least value. The Shia have committed no sin except accepting the ruling of reason, the testimony of history, and the witness of the Quran, and refusing to listen to the hollow and incorrect privileges claimed by the biased.

[^1]: Surah Maryam, Verse 30. [^2]: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 3, p. 240. [^1]: Tafsir al-Manar and Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi under the above verse.

Ayah

وَمِمَّنْ حَوْلَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ مُنَـٰفِقُونَ ۖ وَمِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ ۖ مَرَدُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنِّفَاقِ لَا تَعْلَمُهُمْ ۖ نَحْنُ نَعْلَمُهُمْ ۚ سَنُعَذِّبُهُم مَّرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَذَابٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٠١)
There are hypocrites among the Bedouins around you and among the townspeople of Madinah, steeped in hypocrisy. You do not know them; We know them, and We will punish them twice, then they shall be consigned to a great punishment. (101)

Commentary

Once again, the Holy Quran directs the discussion toward the actions of the hypocrites and their various groups, stating: "Among those who are around your city (Medina), there are a group of hypocrites" (وَ مِمَّنْ حَوْلَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَعْرابِ مُنافِقُونَ‌).

This means you should not only focus your attention on the internal hypocrites; you must be vigilant, keep the external hypocrites under observation, and be wary of their dangerous activities.

The word "A'rab" (الْأَعْرابِ), as we have previously mentioned, usually refers to the desert-dwelling Arabs.

Then it adds: "And among the people of Medina itself, there are those who have reached the limits of rebellion and defiance in hypocrisy, and are firmly committed to it and experienced in it!" (وَ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْمَدِينَةِ مَرَدُوا عَلَى النِّفاقِ‌).

"Maradu" (مَرَدُوا) is derived from the root "Marad" (مرد), meaning rebellion, defiance, and absolute alienation. Originally, it carries the meaning of "nakedness and detachment." For this reason, boys who have not yet grown facial hair are called "Amrad" (امرد). "Shajarah marda'" (شجرة مرداء) refers to a tree that has no leaves, and "Marid" (مارد) refers to a rebellious person who has completely exited the bounds of obedience.

Some commentators and lexicographers have also defined this root as meaning "practice" or "training" (among them, Taj al-Arus and Al-Qamus mention "training" as one of its meanings).

This is perhaps because absolute detachment from something and complete exit from it is not possible without practice and exercise.

In any case, this group of hypocrites was so devoid of truth and reality, and so skilled in their craft, that they could insert themselves into the ranks of the true Muslims without anyone noticing them.

This difference in expression regarding the "internal" and "external" hypocrites in the above verse seems to point to the fact that the internal hypocrites are more skilled in their work and naturally more dangerous; thus, Muslims must be strictly watchful of them, although they should not lose sight of the external hypocrites either.

Therefore, immediately after this, He says: "You do not know them, but We know them" (لا تَعْلَمُهُمْ نَحْنُ نَعْلَمُهُمْ‌).

Of course, this refers to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله ordinary and human knowledge, but it in no way contradicts the fact that he could become fully aware of their secrets through revelation and Divine instruction.

At the end of the verse, He describes the severe punishment of this group as follows:

"We will punish them twice soon, and then they shall be returned to a great punishment" (سَنُعَذِّبُهُمْ مَرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّونَ إِلى‌ عَذابٍ عَظِيمٍ‌).

There is no doubt that the "great punishment" refers to the punishments of the Day of Resurrection. However, there is discussion among commentators as to what kind of punishments the other two are, and they have offered several possibilities.

However, it seems more likely that one of these two punishments is their social punishment through their disgrace and the uncovering of their inner secrets, following which they lose all their social status—evidence of which we read in previous verses. It is also mentioned in some hadiths that when the actions of this group reached dangerous stages, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would identify them by name and description, and even expel them from the mosque!

Their second punishment is the one mentioned in verse 50 of Surah Al-Anfal, where He says: وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْمَلائِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَ أَدْبارَهُمْ‌ ... "If you could but see when the angels take the souls of those who disbelieve, striking their faces and their backs..."

The possibility also exists that the second punishment refers to the internal distress and psychological torture that afflicted this group as a result of the comprehensive victory of the Muslims.

Ayah

وَءَاخَرُونَ ٱعْتَرَفُوا۟ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ خَلَطُوا۟ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا وَءَاخَرَ سَيِّئًا عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٠٢)
[There are] others who have confessed to their sins, having mixed up righteous conduct with other that was evil. Maybe Allah will accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (102)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verse, several narrations have been cited, in most of which we encounter the name of "Abu Lubabah al-Ansari." According to one narration, he and two or more other companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله refrained from participating in the Battle of Tabuk. However, when they heard the verses revealed in condemnation of those who stayed behind, they became deeply distressed and regretful. They tied themselves to the pillars of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله mosque. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned and inquired about their situation, they said: "They have sworn not to untie themselves from the pillars until the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله does so." The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: "I too swear that I shall not do such a thing unless Allah grants me permission."

The above verse was revealed, and Allah accepted their repentance, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله untied them from the pillars of the mosque.
