Out of gratitude for this, they offered all their wealth to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "This is the very wealth because of our attachment to which we refrained from participating in Jihad; accept all of it from us and spend it in the way of Allah!"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "No command has yet been revealed to me regarding this." Shortly thereafter, the next verse was revealed, commanding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to take a portion of their wealth, and according to some narrations, He accepted one-third of their property.

In some other narrations, we read that the above verse is about "Abu Lubabah" and concerns the story of "Banu Qurayza." The Banu Qurayza, who were a group of Jews, consulted him as to whether they should submit to the judgment of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or not. He said: "If you submit, he will slaughter all of you!" He then immediately regretted his words, repented, and tied himself to a pillar of the mosque. Subsequently, the above verse was revealed, and Allah accepted his repentance. [1]

Commentary

The Repentant

After referring to the state of the hypocrites inside and outside Medina in the previous verse, here it points to the condition of a group of sinful Muslims who took action to repent and compensate for their evil deeds, stating: "And [there are] others who have acknowledged their sins" (وَ آخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ).

"And they mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil" (خَلَطُوا عَمَلًا صالِحاً وَ آخَرَ سَيِّئاً).

Then He adds: "It may be that Allah will turn to them in forgiveness" and return His mercy to them (عَسَى اللَّهُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ).

"For Allah is Forgiving and Merciful," possessing vast and extensive mercy (إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the commentary of the above verse, and other commentaries.

The use of the expression "عسى" (it may be) in the above verse—which is usually used in cases of hope and the possibility of success combined with the possibility of failure—is perhaps intended to place them between fear and hope (khawf and raja'), which are two means of perfection and spiritual training.

There is also the possibility that the expression "عسى" indicates that, in addition to repentance, regret, and remorse, they must fulfill other conditions in the future and compensate for the past with their good deeds.

However, considering that He completes the verse by stating Divine forgiveness and mercy, the aspect of hope prevails within it.

It is also clear that the revelation of the verse regarding "Abu Lubabah" or other defaulters of the Battle of Tabuk does not restrict the broad meaning of the verse; rather, it encompasses all individuals who have mixed good and bad deeds and are regretful of their evil actions.

For this reason, it has been quoted from some scholars that they said the above verse is the most hopeful verse of the Quran, which has opened the doors to sinners and invites the repentant toward Himself.

Ayahs

خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ صَدَقَةً تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَتُزَكِّيهِم بِهَا وَصَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ إِنَّ صَلَوٰتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَّهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٠٣)
Take charity from their possessions to cleanse them and purify them thereby, and bless them. Indeed your blessing is a comfort to them, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (103)
أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ يَقْبَلُ ٱلتَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَأْخُذُ ٱلصَّدَقَـٰتِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٠٤)
Do they not know that it is Allah who accepts the repentance of His servants and receives the charities, and that it is Allah who is the All-clement, the All-merciful? (104)
وَقُلِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ فَسَيَرَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۖ وَسَتُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٠٥)
And say, ‘Go on working: Allah will see your conduct, and His Apostle and the faithful [as well], and you will be returned to the Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, and He will inform you concerning what you used to do. (105)

Commentary

Zakat as a Purifier of the Individual and Society

In the first verse under discussion, reference is made to one of the important rulings of Islam, namely the issue of Zakat. As a general law, it commands the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله: "Take alms (Zakat) from their wealth" (خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً).

The word "مِنْ" (from), which is used here to denote "partibility" (tab'id), indicates that Zakat always constitutes a portion of one's wealth, not all of it, nor even the major part of it.

Then, referring to two aspects of the ethical, psychological, and social philosophy of Zakat, He says: "By it, you purify them and cause them to grow" (تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَ تُزَكِّيهِمْ بِها). You purify them from moral vices, from world-worship, greed, and stinginess, and you nurture within them the seedlings of altruism, generosity, and regard for the rights of others.

Furthermore, through the performance of this divine obligation, you eliminate the corruptions and pollutions that arise in society due to poverty, class division, and the deprivation of certain groups, thereby cleansing the social arena of these impurities.

Moreover, social solidarity and economic growth and progress are secured under the shadow of such programs.

Therefore, the ruling of Zakat is both a "purifier of the individual and society" and a nurturer of the seeds of virtue in individuals, as well as a cause for the progress of society. This is the most eloquent expression that can be said regarding Zakat: on one hand, it washes away pollutions, and on the other, it creates perfection and evolution.

The possibility has also been suggested in the meaning of the verse that the subject of "تُطَهِّرُهُمْ" (purify them) is Zakat itself, and the subject of "تُزَكِّيهِمْ" (cause them to grow/sanctify them) is the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. According to this meaning, the verse would be: "Zakat purifies them, and by means of it, you nurture them." However, the more apparent (azhar) meaning is that the subject in both cases is the person of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, as we initially interpreted, although in terms of the ultimate result, there is little difference between these two interpretations.

Then He adds: "And pray for them when they pay Zakat" (وَ صَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ).

This shows that even for the performance of obligatory duties, people should be thanked and appreciated, and specifically encouraged through spiritual and psychological means. Thus, we read in narrations that when people brought their Zakat to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, he would pray for them with the phrase: Allahumma salli 'alayhim (O Allah, bless them).

He then adds that "your prayer and blessing is a source of tranquility for them" (إِنَّ صَلاتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَهُمْ).

This is because, through the light of this prayer, divine mercy descends upon their hearts and souls in a way that they can feel it. Additionally, the appreciation shown by the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, or those who stand in his place and accept the Zakat of the people's wealth, grants them a kind of spiritual and mental peace—knowing that if they have outwardly lost something, they have gained something far better.

It is interesting to note that we have never heard of tax collectors being duty-bound to thank the people; yet this command, as a recommended (mustahabb) ruling in Islamic programs, highlights the depth of the human dimensions in these instructions.

At the end of the verse, in accordance with the preceding discussion, He says: "And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing" (وَ اللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

He hears the prayer of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and is aware of the intentions of those who give Zakat.

Here, several points should be noted

  1. From the circumstances of revelation (shan al-nuzul) mentioned for the previous verses, it is clearly evident that this verse is closely linked to the preceding verse and the issue of the repentance of "Abu Lubabah" and his companions. This is because, in gratitude for the acceptance of their repentance, they brought their wealth to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, and he took only a portion of it.

However, this circumstance of revelation in no way contradicts the fact that the verse expresses a general and universal ruling regarding the Zakat of wealth. The notion held by some commentators that there is a conflict between these two is incorrect, as we have repeatedly stated regarding other verses of the Quran and their circumstances of revelation.

The only question remaining here is that, according to a narration, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله accepted one-third of the wealth of Abu Lubabah and his companions, whereas the amount of Zakat is never one-third in any case. For wheat, barley, dates, and raisins, it is sometimes one-tenth and sometimes one-twentieth; for gold and silver, it is approximately 2.5 percent; and for livestock (cows, sheep, and camels), it never reaches one-third.

However, this question can be answered as follows: the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله accepted a portion of their wealth as Zakat and the additional amount up to one-third as an expiation (kaffarah) for their sins.

Therefore, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله took their obligatory Zakat and accepted an additional amount for the cleansing of their sins, the total of both reaching one-third.

  1. The command "خُذْ" (Take) is clear evidence that the head of the Islamic government can collect Zakat from the people, rather than waiting to see if they wish to pay it themselves or not.

  2. The phrase "صَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ" (Pray for them), although addressed to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, clearly expresses a general ruling (since the general law is that Islamic rulings do not differ between the Prophet and others, and any specific characteristics of the Prophet regarding rulings must be proven by specific evidence).

Therefore, those in charge of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) in every age and time can pray for the Zakat payers with the phrase (Allahumma salli 'alayhim).

Nevertheless, it is surprising that some biased individuals among the Ahl al-Sunnah do not consider it permissible to send blessings (salawat) upon the Family of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله independently. That is, if someone says (Allahumma salli 'ala 'Ali Amir al-Mu'minin) or (Salli 'ala Fatimah al-Zahra), they deem it forbidden. Yet, the prohibition of such a prayer requires evidence, not its permissibility. Furthermore, as we said above, the Quran explicitly allows such a prayer for ordinary individuals, let alone for the Ahl al-Bayt, the successors of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the Wali. What can be done when prejudice sometimes blocks the understanding of Quranic verses?

Since some sinners, such as those who stayed behind from the Battle of Tabuk, were insistent that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله accept their repentance, the second verse under discussion points out that the acceptance of repentance is not the Prophet's work. "Do they not know that it is Allah alone who accepts repentance from His servants?" (أَ لَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ). Not only is He the acceptor of repentance, but "He also receives the Zakat or other charities given as expiation for sin and to seek nearness to the Lord" (وَ يَأْخُذُ الصَّدَقاتِ).

There is no doubt that the receiver of Zakat and charities is either the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, the Imam عليه السلام, the leader of the Muslims, or the needy individuals. In any case, Allah does not outwardly receive them. However, since the hand of the Prophet and the true leaders is the hand of Allah (as they are His representatives), it is as if Allah receives these charities. Similarly, the needy servants who accept such help by divine permission and command are, in reality, representatives of the Lord; thus, their hand is also the hand of Allah.

This expression is one of the most subtle expressions that visualizes the greatness and majesty of this Islamic ruling, namely Zakat. In addition to encouraging all Muslims toward this great divine obligation, it warns them to exercise the utmost etiquette and respect when paying Zakat and charities, because the Receiver is Allah. Let them not imagine, like narrow-minded individuals, that it is acceptable to humiliate a needy person, or to pay Zakat to them in a way that crushes their personality. On the contrary, like a humble servant before their Benefactor, they must observe the conditions of etiquette in paying Zakat and delivering it to those entitled to it.

In a narration quoted from the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, we read: "Indeed, charity falls into the hand of Allah before it reaches the hand of the beggar!" [1] In another hadith from Imam Sajjad عليه السلام, it is narrated: "Charity does not fall into the hand of the servant until it falls into the hand of the Lord" (it first reaches the hand of Allah and then the hand of the servant). [2] It is even explicitly stated in a narration that all the deeds of man are received by angels, except for charity, which reaches the hand of Allah directly! [3]

This theme, which we have read in various wordings in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, has also been narrated from the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله through Sunni sources with a different expression. In Sahih Muslim and Bukhari, it is stated: "None of you gives a charity from honest, lawful earnings—and Allah accepts nothing but the lawful—except that the All-Merciful takes it with His right hand, even if it is a single date, and it grows in the palm of the All-Merciful until it becomes greater than a mountain." [4]

[1] Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [2] Tafsir al-Ayyashi (as quoted in Tafsir al-Safi under the above verse). [3] Tafsir al-Ayyashi (as quoted in Tafsir al-Burhan under the above verse). [4] Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 11, p. 33 (This hadith has also been narrated from the Ahl al-Bayt by Imam Sadiq عليه السلام; refer to Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 96, p. 134, new edition).

This hadith, which is full of meaningful metaphors and allegories, clarifies the extraordinary importance of human service and helping the needy in Islamic teachings.

Other various expressions found in hadiths in this regard are so interesting and significant that they make those nurtured in this school so humble before the needy who receive financial aid, as if the needy person has placed a favor upon them and granted them an honor by accepting that help! For example, it is understood from some hadiths that the Infallible Leaders عليه السلام would sometimes kiss their own hand as a sign of respect and veneration before giving charity to a needy person, and then give it to them. Or they would first give it to the needy person, then take it back from them, kiss and smell it, and return it to them. Why? Because they were facing the hand of Allah!

But how far removed are those people who, when giving a small amount of help to their needy brothers and sisters, humiliate them or treat them with harshness and indifference, or even sometimes throw it toward them rudely.

Of course, as we have stated in its proper place, Islam exerts its utmost effort so that not a single poor or needy person is found in the entire Islamic society. However, undoubtedly, in every society, there are honorable disabled individuals, orphaned children, the sick, and the like who are unable to produce; they must be made self-sufficient through the public treasury and affluent individuals with the utmost etiquette and respect for their personality.

At the end of the verse, it says once more for emphasis: "And that Allah is the Acceptor of Repentance, the Merciful" (وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ).

Repentance and Compensation

As understood from numerous verses of the Holy Quran, repentance is not merely regret and remorse for sin; rather, it must be accompanied by reform and compensation. This compensation may specifically take the form of unconditional aid to the needy, as we read in the above verses and in the story of "Abu Lubabah."

It makes no difference whether the sin is a financial one or another sin, as we read in the case of those who stayed behind from the Battle of Tabuk. In fact, the goal is for the soul polluted by sin to be washed by a righteous and worthy deed, regaining its original and primordial (fitri) purity.

In the next verse, He emphasizes the previous discussions in a new form and commands the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله to announce this matter to all people: "And say: Act! For Allah will see your deeds, and so will His Messenger and the believers" (وَ قُلِ اعْمَلُوا فَسَيَرَى اللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَ رَسُولُهُ وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ).

This is an indication that no one should imagine that if they perform an act in seclusion or among a crowd, it remains hidden and concealed from the view of Allah's knowledge. Rather, in addition to Allah, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the believers are also aware of it.

Attention to and faith in this reality has a profound and extraordinary effect on the purification of deeds and intentions. Usually, if a person feels that even one person is watching them, they conduct themselves in a way that is beyond reproach, let alone if they feel that Allah, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, and the believers are aware of their deeds.

This awareness is a prelude to the reward or punishment awaiting them in the other world. Therefore, following this sentence, He adds: "And soon you will be returned to the Knower of the Unseen and the Seen, and He will inform you of what you used to do" and will judge accordingly (وَ سَتُرَدُّونَ إِلى‌ عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

Here, several points should be noted

1. The Issue of the Presentation of Deeds

Among the followers of the school of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, in light of the numerous reports received from the Imams, the well-known and famous belief is that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the Imams become aware of the deeds of the entire Ummah. That is, Allah presents the deeds of the Ummah to them through specific means.

The narrations quoted in this regard are very numerous and perhaps reach the level of tawatur (successive transmission). As an example, we quote a few parts below:

It is narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "The deeds of the servants are presented to the Messenger of Allah every morning, the deeds of the righteous and the wicked. So be cautious of them. And this is the word of Allah, the Almighty: وَ قُلِ اعْمَلُوا فَسَيَرَى اللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ وَ رَسُولُهُ (And say: Act! For Allah will see your deeds, and so will His Messenger). He said this and then remained silent." [1]

In another hadith from Imam Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Indeed, the deeds are presented to your Prophet every Thursday evening. So let one of you be ashamed that a foul deed be presented to his Prophet!" [2]

[1] Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 171 (Chapter on the Presentation of Deeds). [2] Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 158.

Again, in another narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read that a person said to him, "Pray for me and my family." He replied, "Do I not pray? By Allah, your deeds are presented to me every day and night."

The narrator of this hadith says, "This statement felt heavy upon me." The Imam noticed and said to me: "Do you not read the Book of Allah, the Almighty, which says: 'Act! For Allah will see your deeds, and so will His Messenger and the believers'? By Allah, the 'believers' refers to Ali ibn Abi Talib (and the other Imams from his descendants)." [1]

Of course, in some of these reports, only the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله is mentioned; in some, Ali عليه السلام; and in some, the Prophet and the Imams are all mentioned. Similarly, some consider only Thursday evening as the time for the presentation of deeds, some every day, some twice a week, some at the beginning of every month, and some at the time of death and being placed in the grave.

It is clear that these narrations do not contradict each other, and all of them can be correct. It is just as in many institutions where daily performance reports are given every day, weekly reports at the end of the week, and monthly or yearly reports at the end of the month or year to higher authorities.

Here, the question arises: can this subject be inferred from the verse itself, independent of the narrations entered in its interpretation? Or, as Sunni commentators have said, does the verse refer to an ordinary matter—that whatever deed a person performs will inevitably become manifest, and in addition to Allah, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and all believers will become aware of it through ordinary means?

In response to this question, it must be said: fairness dictates that we have evidence for this subject within the verse itself because:

Firstly: The verse is absolute (mutlaq) and includes all deeds. We know that not all deeds will be revealed to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the believers through ordinary means, as most wrongful acts are performed in private and secretly, and often remain hidden under a veil of secrecy; even many righteous deeds are similarly concealed and hidden. If we were to claim that all deeds, whether good or bad, or most of them, become clear to everyone, we would be making a very exaggerated claim. Therefore, the awareness of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the believers regarding the deeds of the people must be through extraordinary means and by divine teaching.

Secondly: At the end of the verse, we read: "فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ" (He will inform you of what you used to do). There is no doubt that this sentence includes all of man's deeds, whether hidden or manifest. The apparent expression of the verse is that the meaning of "deed" at the beginning and end of the verse is the same. Therefore, the beginning of the verse also includes all deeds, whether manifest or hidden, and there is no doubt that awareness of all these is not possible through ordinary means. In other words, the end of the verse speaks of the recompense for all deeds; the beginning of the verse also discusses the knowledge of Allah, the Prophet, and the believers regarding all deeds. One is the stage of awareness, and the other is the stage of recompense, and the subject in both parts is the same.

Thirdly: The emphasis on "the believers" is only correct if it refers to all deeds and through extraordinary means; otherwise, manifest deeds are seen by both believers and non-believers.

From here, this point also becomes clear: the meaning of "the believers" in this verse—as stated in numerous narrations—is not all individuals with faith, but a specific group of them who are aware of the secrets of the unseen by Allah's command, namely the true successors of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله.

An important point to be noted here is that, as previously mentioned, the issue of the presentation of deeds has an extraordinary educational effect on those who believe in it. For when I know that in addition to Allah, who is with me everywhere, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and my beloved leaders are aware every day or every week of every deed I perform, in every place and every location, whether good or bad, I will undoubtedly be more careful and watchful of my actions. It is just as employees of an institution would be if they knew that every day or every week, a report of all the details of their actions is given to higher authorities and they become informed of all of them.

Does "Seeing" Here Mean Vision?

  1. It is well-known among a group of commentators that "seeing" (ru'yah) in the sentence "فَسَيَرَى اللَّهُ عَمَلَكُمْ" (Allah will see your deeds) means "knowledge" (ma'rifah), not "vision," because it has taken only one object, and we know that if "seeing" means "knowing," it takes two objects.

However, there is no obstacle to taking "seeing" in its original sense, which is the observation of perceived things, rather than meaning "knowledge" or "cognition." This matter is not a subject of debate regarding Allah, who is present and observant everywhere and encompasses all perceived things. As for the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the Imams, there is also no obstacle to them seeing the deeds themselves when they are presented, for we know that human deeds do not perish but remain until the Day of Resurrection.

  1. There is no doubt that Allah is aware and informed of deeds before they are performed. The fact that the verse says with the phrase "فَسَيَرَى اللَّهُ" (Allah will soon see), it refers to the state of the deeds after their existence and realization.

Ayah

وَءَاخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٠٦)
[There are] others waiting Allah’s edict: either He shall punish them, or turn to them clemently, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (106)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators has stated that the above verse was revealed concerning three individuals who lagged behind during the Battle of Tabuk, named "Hilal ibn Umayyah," "Murarah ibn al-Rabi‘," and "Ka‘b ibn Malik." The account of their remorse and the manner of their repentance will come, Allah willing, under the commentary of verse 118 of this same Surah.

From some other narrations, it is understood that the above verse concerns certain disbelievers who, on the battlefields against the Muslims, martyred great personalities such as "Hamzah," the Master of Martyrs (سيد الشهداء), and his likes, but subsequently abandoned polytheism and turned to the religion of Islam.

Commentary

In this verse, reference is made to another group of sinners whose ultimate fate is not clearly defined; they are neither such that they are immediately entitled to Divine mercy, nor are they such that one can entirely despair of their forgiveness.

Therefore, the Quran says regarding them: "And [there are] others deferred for the command of Allah; whether He will punish them or whether He will forgive them" (وَ آخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللَّهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَ إِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ‌). "Murja’un" (مرجون) is derived from the root "Irja’" (ارجاء), meaning "delay" and "suspension." Originally, it is taken from "Raja’" (رجا), which means hope. Since a person sometimes delays something in the hope of a specific goal, this word has come to mean delay—but a delay that is coupled with a kind of hopefulness.

In reality, this group possesses neither such pure and firm faith and clear righteous deeds that they can be considered among the felicitous and the saved, nor are they so defiled and deviant that a red line can be drawn over them to categorize them as the wretched. Thus, it remains to be seen how Divine grace (certainly with regard to their spiritual predispositions and circumstances) will deal with them.

At the end of the verse, it adds: Allah does not deal with them without account; rather, He will deal with them according to His knowledge and the requirements of His wisdom, for "Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ‌).

Question

An important question arises here that few commentators have addressed comprehensively: what is the difference between this group and the group whose state was described in verse 102 of this same Surah?

Both groups were among the sinners, and both repented for their sins (as the first group expressed remorse by confessing their sins, and regarding the second group, it is understood from the phrase "whether He will forgive them" that they too have repented).

Furthermore, both groups are awaiting Divine mercy and stand between "fear" and "hope."

The answer to this question is that we can distinguish between these two groups in two ways:

1- The first group repented quickly and openly showed signs of remorse, such as Abu Lubabah, who tied himself to the pillar of the mosque. In short, they expressed their regret explicitly and declared their readiness for any form of physical or financial compensation.

However, the second group consisted of those who did not express their remorse at the beginning, even if they felt regret in their hearts, and they did not announce their readiness for compensation. In reality, they wanted to pass over their great sins simply. This group—of which the clear examples are the three individuals mentioned above whose story will soon follow—remained between fear and hope. Consequently, we see that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the people to distance themselves from them and cut off relations with them. This situation placed them under a severe social boycott, and eventually, they were forced to take the same path the previous group had taken. Since the acceptance of the repentance of such individuals at that time had to be announced through the revelation of a verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله remained waiting for revelation until their repentance was accepted after fifty days or less.

Thus, we see that at the end of the verse concerning the first group, the phrase "Indeed, Allah is Forgiving, Merciful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌) is mentioned, which is evidence of the acceptance of their repentance. But regarding the second group, as long as they had not changed their course, the phrase "And Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ‌) is used, which contains no sign of the acceptance of repentance.

Of course, it is not surprising that regarding major sins, especially during the era of the revelation of the Quranic verses, mere regret and remorse might not be sufficient for the acceptance of repentance; rather, taking action for compensation, making an open confession of the sin, and then the revelation of a verse regarding the acceptance of repentance might be a condition.

2- Another difference that can be made between these two groups is that the first group, although they had shirked a great Islamic duty like Jihad or had given some military secrets to the enemy, had not become tainted with monumental sins such as killing "Hamzah," the Master of Martyrs. Therefore, after repentance and readiness for compensation, Allah accepted their repentance. However, committing a sin like the murder of Hamzah was not something that could be compensated for; thus, the salvation of this group is dependent on the command of Allah—whether He includes them in His forgiveness or punishes them.

In any case, the first answer is consistent with those narrations regarding the occasion of revelation that link the verse under discussion to the three individuals who lagged behind in the Battle of Tabuk. Meanwhile, the second answer aligns with several narrations transmitted through the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام which state that this verse refers to the killers of Hamzah, Ja‘far, and their likes [1]. If we look closely, these two answers are not contradictory, and it is possible that both are intended in the interpretation of the verse.

[1]: For information on these narrations, refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 265, and Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 106.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مَسْجِدًا ضِرَارًا وَكُفْرًا وَتَفْرِيقًۢا بَيْنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَإِرْصَادًا لِّمَنْ حَارَبَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَلَيَحْلِفُنَّ إِنْ أَرَدْنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٠٧)
As for those who took to a mosque for sabotage and for defiance, and to cause division among the faithful, and for the purpose of ambush [used] by those who have fought Allah and His Apostle before—they will surely swear, ‘We desired nothing but good,’ and Allah bears witness that they are indeed liars. (107)
لَا تَقُمْ فِيهِ أَبَدًا ۚ لَّمَسْجِدٌ أُسِّسَ عَلَى ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ مِنْ أَوَّلِ يَوْمٍ أَحَقُّ أَن تَقُومَ فِيهِ ۚ فِيهِ رِجَالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَن يَتَطَهَّرُوا۟ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُطَّهِّرِينَ (١٠٨)
Do not stand in it ever! A mosque founded on Godwariness from the [very] first day is worthier that you stand in it [for prayer]. Therein are men who love to keep pure, and Allah loves those who keep pure. (108)
أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَـٰنَهُۥ عَلَىٰ تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٍ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَـٰنَهُۥ عَلَىٰ شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ فَٱنْهَارَ بِهِۦ فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠٩)
Is he who founds his building on Godwariness and [the pursuit of Allah’s] pleasure better-off or he who founds his building on the brink of a collapsing bank which collapses with him into the fire of hell? Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (109)
لَا يَزَالُ بُنْيَـٰنُهُمُ ٱلَّذِى بَنَوْا۟ رِيبَةً فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَّآ أَن تَقَطَّعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١١٠)
The building they have built will never cease to be [a source of] disquiet in their hearts until their hearts are cut into pieces, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (110)

Occasion of Revelation

The above verses concern another group of hypocrites who, in order to realize their sinister plots, took action to build a mosque in Medina, which later became known as the "Dhirar" (Harmful) Mosque.

All Islamic commentators and many books of Hadith and history have mentioned this event, although some differences are seen in the details.

The summary of the event, as derived from various commentaries and traditions, is as follows: A group of hypocrites came to the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله and said, "Permit us to build a mosque among the tribe of Bani Salim (near the Quba Mosque) so that the weak, the sick, and the disabled elderly may pray there, and also so that on rainy nights, when some people are unable to come to your mosque, they may perform their Islamic obligations there." This occurred while the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله was preparing for the Battle of Tabuk.

The Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله gave them permission, but they added, "Is it possible for you to personally come and pray in it?" The Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله replied, "I am currently departing on a journey, and upon my return, God willing, I will come to that mosque and pray in it."

When the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله returned from Tabuk, they came to him and said, "We now request that you come to our mosque and pray there, and ask Allah to bless us." This was while the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله had not yet entered the gates of Medina.

At this moment, the messenger of divine revelation descended and brought the above verses, unveiling the secrets of their work. Following this, the Prophet ordered the said mosque to be set on fire, its remains demolished, and its site to be turned into a place for dumping the city's refuse! If we look at the outward appearance of this group's action, we might initially be astonished by such an order. Is building a mosque—especially one intended to support the sick and elderly and for times of emergency, which is in truth both a religious and a humanitarian service—such a bad thing that such an order was issued regarding it?

However, when we examine the inner reality of the matter, we will see how calculated this command was.

To explain: During the Age of Ignorance, there was a man named "Abu 'Amir" who had accepted Christianity and entered the ranks of monks. He was considered among the worshippers and ascetics and held extensive influence within the "Khazraj" tribe.

When the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله migrated to Medina and the Muslims gathered around him and the cause of Islam rose, and when the Muslims triumphed over the polytheists in the Battle of Badr, Abu 'Amir—who himself was once among those who heralded the emergence of the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله—found himself abandoned. He rose in opposition to Islam and fled from Medina to the infidels of "Mecca," seeking their help to fight the Noble Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله and inviting the Arab tribes to join him.

He, who led part of the military strategies of the Battle of "Uhud" against the Muslims, ordered pits to be dug between the two army lines, into one of which the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله happened to fall, resulting in his forehead being wounded and his tooth being broken.

When the Battle of Uhud ended, and despite all the difficulties the Muslims faced on that field, the fame of Islam grew louder and spread everywhere, he fled from Medina and went to "Heraclius," the King of Rome, to seek his help and move with an army to crush the Muslims. It is also necessary to mention that due to these provocations and sabotages, the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله had given him the title "The Fasiq" (The Transgressor).

Some say death did not give him the chance to present his request to Heraclius, but in some other books, we read that he contacted Heraclius and was encouraged by his promises!

In any case, before he died, he wrote a letter to the hypocrites of Medina, promising them that he would come to their aid with an army from Rome. He specifically recommended and emphasized that they build a center for him in Medina to serve as the hub for his future activities.

However, since building such a center in Medina in the name of the enemies of Islam was practically impossible, the hypocrites saw it best to implement this plan under the mask of a mosque and under the pretext of helping the sick and the excused.

Eventually, the mosque was built, and it is even said that they chose a young man familiar with the Quran from among the Muslims named "Mujammi' ibn Harithah" (or Mujammi' ibn Jariyah) to lead the prayers.

But divine revelation pulled back the curtain on their work. Perhaps the reason the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله did not order severe action against them before going to Tabuk was so that the nature of their work would become clearer, and so he would not have another mental preoccupation from this side during the Tabuk journey.

Whatever the case, the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله not only did not pray in that mosque but, as we mentioned, commissioned some Muslims (Malik ibn Dukhshum, Ma'n ibn 'Adi, 'Amir ibn Sakan, or 'Asim ibn 'Adi) to burn and demolish the mosque. They did so; first, they burned the roof of the mosque with fire, then demolished the walls, and finally turned its site into a center for dumping garbage. [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Tafsir al-Manar, Tafsir al-Mizan, Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, and other books.

Commentary

An Idol-Temple in the Guise of a Mosque!

In the preceding verses, the condition of various groups of opponents was mentioned, and the verses under discussion introduce another group of them—a group that entered the field with a skillfully calculated plan. However, divine grace rushed to the aid of the Muslims, and this plan also vanished like water.

In the first verse, it says: "And [there are] those who took a mosque" in Medina, while sinister goals were hidden beneath this sacred name (وَ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مَسْجِداً) [1]. It then summarizes their goals into the following four parts:

  1. Their intention was to cause harm and loss to the Muslims (ضراراً - diraran). "Dirar" means intentional harming. In reality, exactly opposite to what they claimed—that their goal was to secure the interests of Muslims and help the sick and disabled—they wanted, through these preliminaries, to destroy the Prophet of Islam, crush the Muslims, and even, if successful, erase the name of Islam from the face of the earth.

  2. Strengthening the foundations of disbelief and returning people to the pre-Islamic state (وَ كُفْراً - wa kufran).

  3. Creating division among the ranks of the Muslims; because by gathering a group in this mosque, the "Quba" Mosque which was near it, or the Prophet's Mosque which was at a distance from it, would lose its prominence (وَ تَفْرِيقاً بَيْنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ - wa tafriqan bayna al-mu'minin).

From this phrase—as some commentators have inferred—it appears that the distance between mosques should not be so small that they affect each other's congregations. Therefore, those who build mosques next to one another based on ethnic prejudices or personal motives, thereby scattering the Muslim congregations such that their prayer rows become empty, lackluster, and soulless, are performing an act contrary to Islamic objectives.

  1. Their final goal was to build a center and a hub for someone who had previously fought against Allah and His Messenger and whose evil record was clear to everyone, so they could implement their programs from this base of hypocrisy (وَ إِرْصاداً لِمَنْ حارَبَ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ - wa irsadan liman haraba Allaha wa rasulahu min qabl).

But the wonder is that they wrapped all these evil intentions and sinister goals in a beautiful and deceptive outward appearance, "and they will surely swear, 'We intended nothing but good'" (وَ لَيَحْلِفُنَّ إِنْ أَرَدْنا إِلَّا الْحُسْنى).

And this is the way of the hypocrites in every age and time: in addition to concealing themselves behind seemingly beautiful veils, they resort to all kinds of false oaths to divert public opinion.

But the Quran adds: "And Allah—who is aware of all inner secrets and to whom the unseen and the seen are equal—bears witness that they are certainly liars" (وَ اللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ).

In this sentence, various types of emphasis are seen to refute them: first, it is a "nominal sentence" (jumlah ismiyyah); second, the word "inna" is for emphasis; also, the "lam" in "lakadhibun," which is the so-called lam al-ibtida for emphasis; and furthermore, the mention of "kadhibun" (liars) instead of a past-tense verb indicates the continuity and persistence of their lying. In this way, Allah refutes their heavy and solemn oaths in the strongest possible manner!

In the next verse, Allah places more emphasis on this vital matter, explicitly commanding His Prophet: "Never stand [for prayer] therein" (لا تَقُمْ فِيهِ أَبَداً). Rather, instead of this mosque, "A mosque founded on righteousness from the first day is more worthy for you to stand in" (لَمَسْجِدٌ أُسِّسَ عَلَى التَّقْوى‌ مِنْ أَوَّلِ يَوْمٍ أَحَقُّ أَنْ تَقُومَ فِيهِ).

Not this mosque whose foundation and basis from the first day were built on disbelief, hypocrisy, irreligion, and division.

The word "ahaqq" (more worthy), although it is a superlative (af'al al-tafdil), is not used here to compare two things in worthiness; rather, it compares the "worthy" and the "unworthy." There are many examples of this in Quranic verses, Hadiths, and everyday speech.

For example, we sometimes say to an impure person or a thief, "Purity and honesty are better for you." The meaning of this statement is not that theft and impurity are good but purity is better; rather, its meaning is that purity is good and theft is bad and unworthy.

Commentators have said that the mosque referred to in the above sentence, in which it is worthy for the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله to pray, is the "Quba" Mosque, near which the hypocrites had built the Dhirar Mosque.

Of course, the possibility has also been raised that it refers to the Prophet's Mosque or all mosques built on the basis of Taqwa (piety). However, considering the expression "from the first day" (أَوَّلِ يَوْمٍ) and given that the Quba Mosque was the first mosque built in Medina [1], the first possibility seems more appropriate, although this word is also consistent with mosques such as the Prophet's Mosque.

Then the Quran adds: In addition to this mosque being founded on the basis of Taqwa, "Within it are men who love to purify themselves; and Allah loves those who purify themselves" (فِيهِ رِجالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَتَطَهَّرُوا وَ اللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُطَّهِّرِينَ).

There is discussion among commentators as to whether this purification refers to outward and physical purity or spiritual and inward purity. In a narration cited in Tafsir al-Tibyan and Majma' al-Bayan under this verse, we read that the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله said to the people of the Quba Mosque: "What do you do in your purification, for Allah the Almighty has excelled in His praise of you?" They said, "We wash the traces of excrement with water."

Narrations with the same meaning have been related from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. However, as we have pointed out many times, such narrations are not evidence that the concept of the verse is limited to this instance. Rather, as the apparent generality of the verse testifies, "taharah" (purity) here has a broad meaning that includes every kind of spiritual cleansing from the effects of polytheism and sin, and physical cleansing from the effects of contamination by filth.

In the third verse under discussion, a comparison is made between two groups: the believers who establish mosques like the Quba Mosque on the foundation of Taqwa, and the hypocrites who place its foundation on disbelief, hypocrisy, division, and corruption.

First, it says: "Is he who founded his building on Taqwa (piety) from Allah and [His] pleasure better, or he who founded his building on the edge of a crumbling precipice beside Hell, so that it collapsed with him into the fire of Hell?" (أَ فَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيانَهُ عَلى‌ تَقْوى‌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رِضْوانٍ خَيْرٌ أَمْ مَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيانَهُ عَلى‌ شَفا جُرُفٍ هارٍ فَانْهارَ بِهِ فِي نارِ جهنم).

"Bunyan" is a verbal noun meaning "that which is built" (the building). "Shafa" means the edge of something. "Juruf" means the "bank" of a river or a well where the water has hollowed out the ground beneath it. "Har" means a weak person or building that is on the verge of collapsing.

The above analogy clarifies with the utmost lucidity and clarity the instability and weakness of the hypocrites' work and the strength and permanence of the work of the people of faith and their programs.

The believers are like someone who, to construct a building, chooses very solid ground and builds it from the foundation with durable and reliable materials. But the hypocrites are like someone who builds his structure on the edge of a river where the flood has completely hollowed out the ground beneath it and it is ready to collapse at any moment. Just as hypocrisy has an outward appearance but lacks substance, such a building also has an appearance without a base or foundation.

This building may collapse at any moment; the school of the people of hypocrisy may also reveal its inner reality at any moment and lead to disgrace.

Piety and seeking the pleasure of God—meaning harmony with reality and walking in step with the universe and its laws—is undoubtedly a factor for permanence and stability.

But hypocrisy—meaning alienation from realities and separation from the laws of creation—is undoubtedly a factor for decline and annihilation.

And since the group of hypocrites wrongs both themselves and society, at the end of the verse it says: "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

As we have said many times, divine guidance—meaning the provision of the preliminaries to reach the destination—only includes the group that possesses the merit, desert, and readiness for it. But the wrongdoers, who are far from this merit, will never be subject to such grace, for Allah is All-Wise, and His will and intention are based on wisdom.

In the last verse, referring to the stubbornness and persistence of the hypocrites, it says they are so stubborn in their work, so wandering in hypocrisy, and so bewildered in the darkness of disbelief that even "The building which they built will not cease to be a cause of doubt (or a result of doubt) in their hearts, until their hearts are cut into pieces [and they die]" (لا يَزالُ بُنْيانُهُمُ الَّذِي بَنَوْا رِيبَةً فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلَّا أَنْ تَقَطَّعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ).

They live in a state of constant bewilderment and wandering, and this center of hypocrisy and the Dhirar Mosque they had established remains in their souls as a factor of stubbornness and doubt, even though the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله burned and demolished that building; it is as if its impression is not erased from their doubt-filled hearts.

And at the end of the verse, it says: "And Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise" (وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ).

If He commanded His Prophet to fight and crush such a seemingly righteous building, it was because of His knowledge of the evil intentions of the builders and the inner reality of this structure. This command was issued as the essence of wisdom and according to the best interest and welfare of the Islamic society; it was neither a hasty judgment nor born of excitement or anger.

Here, several points must be noted

A Great Lesson

  1. The story of the Dhirar Mosque is a lesson for all Muslims throughout the history of their lives. The word of Allah and the action of the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله clearly show that Muslims must never be so superficial as to look only at righteous appearances and remain ignorant and detached from the primary goals.

A Muslim is one who recognizes hypocrisy and the hypocrite in every time, in every place, and in every garment and face—even if it be in the face of religion and sect, and in the garment of supporting the Quran and the mosque! Using "religion against religion" is nothing new; it has always been the way and method of colonialists, tyrannical apparatuses, and hypocrites in every society that if people have a specific inclination toward something, they use that very inclination to deceive and then colonize them, and even seek help from the power of religion against religion.

Fundamentally, the philosophy of creating fake prophets and false religions was to direct the religious inclinations of the people into their desired path.

It is obvious that in an environment like "Medina," especially in the era of the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله with that extraordinary influence of Islam and the Quran, open struggle against Islam was not possible. Rather, irreligion had to be wrapped in the veil of religion, and falsehood presented in the garment of truth, so that simple-hearted people would be attracted and their evil intentions could be put into practice.

But a true Muslim is not one who is so shallow as to be deceived by such appearances. One must, by carefully examining the factors and hands that begin to work for such programs and by investigating other clues, realize the true nature and see the inner face of individuals behind the outward face.

A Muslim is not one who accepts and responds to every call that arises from any throat just because it is seemingly righteous. A Muslim is not one who shakes every hand extended toward him, joins every seemingly religious movement he observes, beats his chest under every flag raised in the name of religion, or is attracted to every building constructed in the name of religion.

A Muslim must be vigilant, aware, realistic, forward-looking, and capable of analysis in all social issues.

He must recognize the demons in the garments of angels, distinguish the wolves in the garments of shepherds, and prepare himself to fight these seemingly friendly enemies.

A fundamental principle in Islam is that intentions must be examined before everything else, and the value of every action depends on its intention, not its appearance. Although intention is an internal matter, it is impossible for someone to have an intention in their heart without its effect appearing in the corners of their action, no matter how extraordinary a master and skilled they are at concealment.

And from here, the answer to this question becomes clear: why did the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله, with that greatness of station, order to burn a mosque—that is, the house of God—and demolish a mosque from which not even a pebble can be removed, and turn a place that must be immediately purified if contaminated into the city's garbage dump? The answer to all these questions is one thing: the Dhirar Mosque was not a mosque; in reality, it was an idol-temple. It was not a sacred place; it was a hub of division and hypocrisy. It was not the house of God but rather the house of Satan. And outward names, titles, and masks never change the reality of a thing.

This was the great lesson that the story of the Dhirar Mosque gave to all Muslims for all ages and centuries.

From this discussion, it also becomes clear that the importance of unity among the ranks of Muslims in the view of Islam is so great that even if building a mosque next to another mosque causes division, conflict, and a rift among the ranks of Muslims, that divisive mosque is unholy.

Negation Alone is Not Enough!

  1. The second lesson we learn from the above verses is that Allah, in these verses, commands the Prophet صلے الله عليه وآله: Do not pray in the Dhirar Mosque; rather, pray in the mosque whose foundation is built on the basis of Taqwa.

This "negation" and "affirmation"—which are manifestations of everything from the primary slogan of Islam "La ilaha illa Allah" to other large and small programs—express the reality that there must always be an affirmation alongside every negation for it to be implemented. If we forbid people from going to centers of corruption, we must, in contrast, build pure hubs for gathering and satisfying the spirit of group life. If we prevent unhealthy recreations, we must provide means for healthy recreations. If we forbid colonial schools, we must establish a healthy culture and pure centers for education and training. If we condemn immodesty, we must provide means for easy marriage for the youth.

Those who use all their power in "negation" and whose programs contain no news of "affirmation" should be certain that their negation will also achieve little.

For it is the law of creation that all instincts and feelings must be saturated through the correct path, and it is the definite program of Islam that "La" (No) must be coupled with "Illa" (Except) so that a life-giving monotheism (Tawhid) may be born from it.

And this is a lesson that, unfortunately, many Muslims have forgotten, and yet they complain why Islamic programs do not progress, while the program of Islam is not limited to negation as they imagine. If they had joined negation and affirmation together, their progress would have been certain.

Two Essential Conditions

  1. The third valuable lesson we learn from the event of the Dhirar Mosque and the above verses is that an active and positive religious and social hub is one formed from two positive elements: first, its foundation and goal must be pure from the beginning (أُسِّسَ عَلَى التَّقْوى‌ مِنْ أَوَّلِ يَوْمٍ).

And second, its supporters and guardians must be pure, righteous, faithful, and determined human beings (فِيهِ رِجالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَتَطَهَّرُوا).

The loss of either of these two fundamental pillars results in a lack of outcome and failure to reach the destination.

Ayahs

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱشْتَرَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلَهُم بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ ۚ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَقْتُلُونَ وَيُقْتَلُونَ ۖ وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا فِى ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَٱلْإِنجِيلِ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِعَهْدِهِۦ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَٱسْتَبْشِرُوا۟ بِبَيْعِكُمُ ٱلَّذِى بَايَعْتُم بِهِۦ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١١١)
Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls and their possessions for paradise to be theirs: they fight in the way of Allah, kill, and are killed. A promise binding upon Him in the Torah and the Evangel and the Quran. And who is truer to his promise than Allah? So rejoice in the bargain you have made with Him, and that is the great success. (111)
ٱلتَّـٰٓئِبُونَ ٱلْعَـٰبِدُونَ ٱلْحَـٰمِدُونَ ٱلسَّـٰٓئِحُونَ ٱلرَّٰكِعُونَ ٱلسَّـٰجِدُونَ ٱلْـَٔامِرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَٱلنَّاهُونَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظُونَ لِحُدُودِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١١٢)
[The faithful are] penitent, devout, celebrators of Allah’s praise, wayfarers, who bow [and] prostrate [in prayer], bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and keep Allah’s bounds—and give good news to the faithful. (112)

Commentary

A Peerless Trade

Since the previous verses discussed those who lagged behind and the subject of Jihad, these two verses describe the lofty station of the faithful Mujahideen by presenting a striking parable.

In this example, Allah introduces Himself as the "Buyer" and the believers as the "Sellers," stating: "Indeed, Allah has purchased from the believers their lives and their properties, and in exchange for this commodity, He gives them Paradise" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ اشْتَرى‌ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ أَمْوالَهُمْ بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ). Since every transaction essentially consists of "five fundamental pillars"—namely the buyer, the seller, the commodity, the price, and the deed of transaction—Allah has referred to all these pillars in this verse.

He has established Himself as the "Buyer," the believers as the "Sellers," their lives and wealth as the "Commodity," and Paradise as the "Price" (Thaman) for this transaction.

Furthermore, He describes the manner of delivering this commodity with a refined expression: "They fight in the way of Allah, so they kill the enemies of truth or are killed in this path and drink the draught of martyrdom" (يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ فَيَقْتُلُونَ وَ يُقْتَلُونَ‌). In reality, the hand of Allah is ready in the field of Jihad to receive this commodity, whether it be the life or the wealth spent in Jihad!

Following that, He refers to the valid and firm "Deed" of this transaction, which is the fifth pillar, saying: "This is a true promise binding upon Him, mentioned in three heavenly books: the Torah, the Gospel, and the Quran" (وَعْداً عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا فِي التَّوْراةِ وَ الْإِنْجِيلِ وَ الْقُرْآنِ‌).

Of course, considering the expression "in the way of Allah" (فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌), it becomes clearly evident that Allah is the buyer of lives, efforts, struggles, and strivings that take place in His path—meaning in the path of implementing truth, justice, and freedom, and saving humanity from the clutches of disbelief, oppression, and corruption.

Then, to emphasize this great transaction, He adds: "And who is more faithful to his covenant than Allah?" (وَ مَنْ أَوْفى‌ بِعَهْدِهِ مِنَ اللَّهِ‌).

This means that although the price of this transaction is not paid immediately, it carries none of the risks of a credit sale! For Allah, by virtue of His power, ability, and His needlessness of anyone, is the most faithful to His covenant and promise. He neither forgets, nor is He incapable of payment, nor does He do anything contrary to wisdom that He might regret, nor—God forbid—does He speak falsely. Therefore, no room remains for any doubt or hesitation regarding His faithfulness to His covenant and the payment of the price at the appointed time.

Most interestingly, after the formalities of this transaction are completed, just as is customary among merchants, He congratulates the other party and wishes for the transaction to be highly profitable for them, saying: "So rejoice in your transaction which you have contracted" (فَاسْتَبْشِرُوا بِبَيْعِكُمُ الَّذِي بايَعْتُمْ بِهِ‌) [1] "and that is the great success" (وَ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ‌).

A similar theme appears with different wording in Surah As-Saff, verses 10 and 11, where He says: "O you who have believed, shall I guide you to a transaction that will save you from a painful punishment? [It is that] you believe in Allah and His Messenger and strive in the cause of Allah with your wealth and your lives. That is best for you, if you should know. He will forgive for you your sins and admit you to gardens beneath which rivers flow and pleasant dwellings in gardens of perpetual residence. That is the great success." Here, man is submerged in wonder at all this grace and love from the Lord. Allah, who is the Owner of the entire universe and the absolute Ruler over all creation,

[1]: "Fastabshiru" is derived from the root "Bisharat," which originally comes from "Basharah" (skin/face), referring to the joy and happiness whose effects appear on a person's face.

and from whom everyone receives whatever they possess, assumes the position of a buyer for these very blessings He has bestowed upon His servants, purchasing His own gifts at a price a hundredfold.

Even more remarkable is that Jihad—which causes the dignity of man himself and the victory and honor of every nation and people, and whose fruits ultimately return to them—is counted as the payment of this commodity.

And while there should be an equivalence between the commodity and the price, He has disregarded this balance and placed eternal bliss in exchange for a transient commodity that is bound to perish anyway (whether on a sickbed or on the battlefield).

More importantly, even though Allah is the most truthful of all the truthful and needs no deed or guarantee, He has provided the most significant deeds and guarantees for His servants.

And at the end of this great transaction, He congratulates them and gives them glad tidings. Can any grace, love, or mercy higher than this be imagined? And does any transaction more profitable than this exist?

Thus, we read in a Hadith from Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari that when the above verse was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was in the mosque. He recited the verse aloud, and the people shouted "Allahu Akbar." A man from the Ansar came forward and asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in amazement: "Was this verse really revealed?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied, "Yes."

The Ansari man said: "A profitable sale! We will not seek to cancel it, nor would we accept if a cancellation were sought from us!" [1]

[1]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan).

As is the method of the Holy Quran—where it mentions something briefly in one verse and provides its explanation and detail in the next—in the second verse under discussion, it introduces the believers, who are the sellers of life and wealth to Allah, with nine prominent attributes:

  1. "They are the repentant" (التَّائِبُونَ‌), who wash their hearts and souls from the pollution of sin with the water of repentance.

  2. "They are the worshippers" (الْعابِدُونَ‌), who engage in self-building in the light of intimate prayer with Allah and the worship of His Pure Essence.

  3. "They are the praisers" (الْحامِدُونَ‌), who give thanks for the material and spiritual blessings of the Lord.

  4. "They are the wanderers" (السَّائِحُونَ‌), who move from one center of worship and service to another.

In this way, their self-building programs in the light of worship are not confined to a limited environment or a specific horizon; rather, everywhere is a center of servitude to the Lord, self-improvement, and education for them, and wherever there is a lesson in this regard, they are seekers of it.

"Sa'ih" is originally derived from the root "Sayh" and "Siyahat," meaning flowing and continuity.

There is discussion among commentators regarding what kind of "wandering," "flowing," or "continuity" is meant by "Sa'ih" in the above verse.

Some, as we mentioned above, have taken it to mean traveling between centers of worship. In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The wandering (Siyahat) of my Ummah is in the mosques." [1]

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, under the verse being discussed.

Others have taken "Sa'ih" to mean "Sa'im" (the fasting person), because fasting is a continuous act throughout the day. In another Hadith, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Indeed, the wanderers (Sa'ihun) are the fasting ones." [1] Other commentators have considered "Siyahat" to mean traveling and journeying across the earth to observe the signs of Allah's greatness, to understand human societies, and to become acquainted with the customs, traditions, and sciences of nations, which enlivens human thought and matures the mind.

Some other commentators consider "Siyahat" to mean moving toward the fields of Jihad and struggle against the enemy, citing the famous Prophetic Hadith: "Indeed, the wandering of my Ummah is Jihad in the way of Allah." [2]

And finally, some have interpreted it as the journey of the intellect and thought through various issues related to the universe, the factors of happiness and victory, and the causes of defeat and failure.

However, considering the attributes listed before and after it, the first meaning seems most appropriate, although it is entirely possible that all these meanings are intended by this word, as all these concepts are gathered within the concept of journeying and wandering.

  1. "Those who bow" (الرَّاكِعُونَ‌) before the greatness of Allah.

  2. "Those who prostrate" (السَّاجِدُونَ‌), placing their foreheads on His threshold.

  3. "Those who enjoin what is right" (الْآمِرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ‌), inviting people to goodness.

  4. "Those who do not suffice with the duty of inviting to good, but rather fight against every kind of corruption and evil" (وَ النَّاهُونَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ).

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn and many other commentaries. [2]: Tafsir al-Mizan and Tafsir al-Manar, under the verse being discussed.

  1. And "those who, after fulfilling the mission of enjoining good and forbidding evil, rise to their final and most important social duty, which is guarding the limits of Allah (وَ الْحافِظُونَ لِحُدُودِ اللَّهِ‌), implementing His laws, and establishing truth and justice."

After mentioning these nine attributes, Allah once again encourages such true believers and those trained in the school of faith and action, saying to His Prophet: "And give glad tidings to the believers" (وَ بَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Since the object of the glad tidings is not mentioned—or in other words, the glad tidings are given in an absolute sense—it conveys a broad meaning that encompasses every good and happiness; that is, give them glad tidings of every good, every happiness, and every kind of honor!

It is also necessary to note that a portion of these nine attributes (the first six) relates to the self-building and educational aspects of individuals, while another portion (the seventh and eighth attributes) refers to critical social duties and the purification of the social environment, and the final attribute speaks of collective responsibilities regarding the formation of a righteous government and active participation in positive political matters.

Ayahs

مَا كَانَ لِلنَّبِىِّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن يَسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَلَوْ كَانُوٓا۟ أُو۟لِى قُرْبَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١١٣)
The Prophet and the faithful may not plead for the forgiveness of the polytheists, even if they should be [their] relatives, after it has become clear to them that they will be the inmates of hell. (113)
وَمَا كَانَ ٱسْتِغْفَارُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَن مَّوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَهَآ إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُۥٓ أَنَّهُۥ عَدُوٌّ لِّلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ ۚ إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَأَوَّٰهٌ حَلِيمٌ (١١٤)
Abraham’s pleading forgiveness for his father was only to fulfill a promise he had made him. So when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of God, he repudiated him. Indeed Abraham was most plaintive and forbearing. (114)

Circumstances of Revelation

In Tafsir al-Mizan, a narration is cited regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the above verses, stating that a group of Muslims said to the Prophet of Islam: "Will you not seek forgiveness for our fathers who passed away during the era of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah)?" The above verses were revealed, warning them all that no one has the right to seek forgiveness for the polytheists (mushrikin).

Other accounts regarding the revelation of these verses have also been mentioned, which will be discussed after the completion of the commentary on the verse.

Commentary

The Necessity of Estrangement from Enemies

The first verse, using an eloquent and decisive expression, forbids the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the believers from seeking forgiveness for the polytheists, stating: "It is not fitting for the Prophet and those who believe to seek forgiveness for the polytheists" (مَا كَانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ يَسْتَغْفِرُوا لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ).

Then, for emphasis and generalization, it adds: "even if they be near of kin" (وَلَوْ كَانُوا أُولِي قُرْبَى).

Subsequently, it explains the reason for this matter within a sentence: "after it has become clear to them that they are the companions of the Hellfire," seeking forgiveness for them is meaningless (مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحَابُ الْجَحِيمِ).

This is a futile act and an out-of-place wish, for a polytheist is in no way forgivable, and for those who have tread the path of polytheism, no way of salvation can be imagined.

Furthermore, seeking forgiveness and asking for pardon is a form of expressing affection, connection, and attachment to the polytheists, and this is the very thing that has been repeatedly forbidden in the Quran.

Since the Muslims who were aware of and familiar with the Quran had read in the verses of this Heavenly Book that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) sought forgiveness for (his uncle) Azar, this question could immediately arise in their minds: "Was Azar not a polytheist? If this act is forbidden, why did this great Prophet of God perform it?!" Therefore, in the second verse, it addresses the answer to this question, saying: "And Ibrahim’s seeking forgiveness for his father (his uncle Azar) was only because of a promise he had made to him; but when it became clear to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he dissociated himself from him" and did not seek forgiveness for him (وَمَا كَانَ اسْتِغْفَارُ إِبْرَاهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَنْ مَوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَهَا إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ).

At the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, Ibrahim was one who was humble before Allah, fearful of the wrath and anger of the Lord, and a noble, clement, and forbearing man" (إِنَّ إِبْرَاهِيمَ لَأَوَّاهٌ حَلِيمٌ).

This sentence might be a reason for Ibrahim’s promise to Azar regarding seeking forgiveness; for his clemency and forbearance on one hand, and his being awwah—which according to some commentaries means being merciful—on the other, necessitated that he exert the utmost effort and endeavor for Azar's guidance, even if it were through the promise of seeking forgiveness and asking pardon for his past.

There is also the possibility that the above sentence is a reason for the fact that Ibrahim, due to the humility and submissiveness he possessed and his fear of disobeying the Lord, was never willing to seek forgiveness for the enemies of the Truth; rather, this act was specific to the time when he nurtured the hope of Azar's guidance in his heart. Thus, as soon as his enmity became apparent, he desisted from this act.

If it is asked: "How did the Muslims know that Ibrahim sought forgiveness for Azar?"

In response, we say: These verses of Surah At-Tawbah, as we mentioned at the beginning, were revealed toward the end of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) life. Previously, the Muslims had read in Surah Maryam, verse 47, that Ibrahim had promised Azar forgiveness with the phrase "سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي" (I will ask forgiveness for you from my Lord), and a Prophet of God does not make a promise in vain; whenever he makes a promise, he fulfills it. They had also read in Surah Al-Mumtahanah, verse 4, that Ibrahim said to him: "لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ" (I will surely ask forgiveness for you). Likewise, in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, which is among the Meccan surahs, Ibrahim’s seeking of forgiveness for his father is explicitly mentioned where he says: "وَ اغْفِرْ لِأَبِي إِنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ" (And forgive my father, for indeed he was of those astray) (Verse 86).

Here, several points must be noted

1- A Fabricated Narration

Many Sunni commentators have cited a fabricated hadith from Sahih Bukhari, Muslim, and other books from "Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib" from his father, stating that when the death of Abu Talib approached, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) entered upon him while Abu Jahl and Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah were with him. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said to him: "O uncle! Say 'There is no god but Allah' so that I may defend (and intercede for) you before the Lord." At this, Abu Jahl and Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah turned to Abu Talib and said: "Do you wish to turn away from the religion of (your father) Abdul Muttalib?" The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) repeatedly made this proposal to him, but Abu Jahl and Abdullah prevented him with the same statement. The last words Abu Talib spoke were: "Upon the religion of Abdul Muttalib!" and he refused to say 'There is no god but Allah.' At this moment, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: "I will seek forgiveness for you until I am forbidden from it." Then the above verse (مَا كَانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا...) was revealed [1]. However, signs of fabrication and falsehood are evident in this hadith because:

Firstly, it is well-known and famous among commentators and traditionists that Surah Al-Bara'ah (At-Tawbah) was revealed in the ninth year of the Hijrah; rather, according to some, it is the last surah revealed to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). Meanwhile, historians have written that the death of Abu Talib occurred in Mecca before the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) migration!

[1]: Tafsir al-Manar and other Sunni commentaries.

Because of this clear contradiction, some biased individuals, such as the author of Tafsir al-Manar, have struggled to find excuses. Sometimes they have said this verse was revealed twice! Once in Mecca, and once in Medina in the ninth year of the Hijrah! With this baseless claim, they intended to resolve the contradiction.

And sometimes they have said it is possible this verse was revealed in Mecca at the time of Abu Talib's death and was later placed in Surah At-Tawbah by the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) command, while this claim is also completely devoid of evidence.

Would it not have been better, instead of these undocumented justifications, to doubt the aforementioned narration and its authenticity?

Secondly, there is no doubt that before the death of Abu Talib, Allah had forbidden the Muslims from the friendship and love of polytheists in various verses of the Quran—and we know that seeking forgiveness is one of the clearest instances of expressing love and friendship. Given this, how is it possible that Abu Talib would die a polytheist and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) would swear, "I will continue to seek forgiveness for you until God forbids me"? What is strange is that Fakhr al-Razi, who is famous for bias in such matters, because he could not deny that this verse, like the rest of Surah At-Tawbah, was revealed in Medina toward the end of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) life, resorted to an astonishing justification: that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) continued to seek forgiveness for Abu Talib after his death until the revelation of Surah At-Tawbah, when the above verse was revealed and forbade him. He then says: "What prevents this from having been permissible for the Prophet and the believers until that time?!" If Fakhr al-Razi had freed himself from the bonds of bias, he would have realized the truth: that it is impossible for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to seek forgiveness for a polytheist for such a long duration, while numerous verses of the Quran revealed until then had condemned any form of affection, love, and friendship toward polytheists [1].

Thirdly, the only person who narrated this tradition is "Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib," and his enmity toward the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), is well-known. Therefore, his words regarding Ali (peace be upon him) or his father and son can never be relied upon.

The late "Allamah Amini," after pointing to the above matter, cites a statement from "Waqidi" that is noteworthy. He says: "Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib" passed by the funeral of "Imam Sajjad, Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him)" and did not pray over it, abstaining from this act (with a hollow excuse). Yet, when he was asked, according to Ibn Hazm, whether he would pray behind Hajjaj or not, he said: "We pray behind those worse than Hajjaj!"

Fourthly, as we stated in the fifth volume of this Tafsir, there is no doubt that Abu Talib believed in the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), and we presented clear documents and evidence for this matter. We proved that what has been said regarding Abu Talib's lack of faith is a great slander, which all Shia scholars and a group of Sunni scholars, such as Ibn Abi al-Hadid (in Sharh Nahj al-Balagha), Qastallani (in Irshad al-Sari), and Zayni Dahlan (in the margin of Tafsir al-Halabi), have explicitly stated.

We said that a meticulous researcher, considering the biased political wave that arose from the "Umayyad" rulers against Ali (peace be upon him), can easily guess that anyone connected to him did not remain safe from this malicious targeting. In reality, Abu Talib had no sin other than being the father of Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), the great leader of Islam. Did they not subject "Abu Dharr," that great mujahid of Islam, to all those accusations because of his love for Ali (peace be upon him) and his struggle against the school of Uthman?! (For more information on the faith of Abu Talib, who throughout his life was the supporter and defender of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and was obedient to his command, please refer to Volume 5 of this Tafsir, pages 191 to 198).

[1]: In Surah An-Nisa, which was certainly revealed before Al-Bara'ah, in verse 139, and in Surah Al-Imran, verse 38, which was also revealed before Al-Bara'ah, friendship and guardianship (wilayah) of the disbelievers are explicitly forbidden. In this very Surah At-Tawbah, in verses preceding the verse under discussion, Allah explicitly tells His Prophet that whether he seeks forgiveness for them (the disbelievers) or not, Allah will not forgive them.

2- Why did Ibrahim promise Azar to seek forgiveness?

Another question that arises here is how Ibrahim promised his uncle Azar to seek forgiveness and, according to the apparent meaning of the above verse and other verses of the Holy Quran, fulfilled this promise, despite the fact that he never believed and was in the ranks of the polytheists and idol-worshippers, and seeking forgiveness for such people is forbidden?

In response to this question, one must note that it is clearly understood from the above verse that Ibrahim expected that Azar might be drawn toward faith and monotheism through this means, and his seeking of forgiveness was, in reality: "O Allah, guide him and forgive his past sins."

However, when Azar passed away in a state of polytheism, and it became certain for Ibrahim that he had died in a state of enmity toward the Lord, and no room remained for his guidance, he ceased his seeking of forgiveness.

According to this meaning, Muslims may also seek forgiveness for their polytheist friends and relatives as long as they are alive and there is hope for their guidance—meaning they ask God for both their guidance and forgiveness—but after their death in a state of disbelief, there is no longer any room for seeking forgiveness.
