As for what has been mentioned in some narrations that Imam Sadiq (peace be upon him) said Ibrahim (peace be upon him) had promised to seek forgiveness for Azar if he became a Muslim (not before becoming a Muslim), and when it became clear to him that he was an enemy of God, he dissociated himself from him—and therefore Ibrahim's promise was conditional, and since the condition was not met, he never sought forgiveness.

This narration, besides being a mursal (disconnected) and weak narration, contradicts the apparent or explicit meaning of the Quranic verses. For the apparent meaning of the verse under discussion is that Ibrahim did seek forgiveness, and the explicit text of verse 86 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara is that Ibrahim requested forgiveness for him from Allah where he says: "وَ اغْفِرْ لِأَبِي إِنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ" (And forgive my father, for indeed he was of those astray).

Another witness to this statement is the famous saying narrated from Ibn Abbas, that Ibrahim repeatedly sought forgiveness for Azar as long as he was alive, but when he died in a state of disbelief and his enmity toward the true religion became certain, he desisted from this act.

And since a group of Muslims desired to seek forgiveness for their polytheist ancestors who had died in a state of disbelief, the Quran explicitly forbade them and clarified that Ibrahim's situation was completely different from theirs; he did such a thing during Azar's lifetime and in hope of his faith, not after his death!

3- Every bond with enemies must be severed

The verse under discussion is not the only verse that speaks of severing every relationship with polytheists; rather, from numerous verses of the Quran, it is well understood that every bond and solidarity—whether of kinship or otherwise—must be overshadowed by ideological bonds. This bond (faith in Allah and struggle against every form of polytheism and idolatry) must govern all relationships of Muslims, for this bond is a foundational one that governs all their social destinies.

Superficial and secondary bonds can never negate it. This was a lesson for yesterday, today, and all eras and centuries.

Ayahs

وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْمًۢا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يُبَيِّنَ لَهُم مَّا يَتَّقُونَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١١٥)
Allah does not lead any people astray after He has guided them until He has made clear to them what they should beware of. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (115)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۚ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (١١٦)
Indeed to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He gives life and brings death. And besides Allah you do not have any guardian or helper. (116)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have stated that a group of Muslims passed away before the revelation of the religious obligations and duties. A group came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and expressed concern regarding their fate, imagining that they might be subject to Divine punishment for not performing these obligations. The above verse was revealed and refuted this notion [1].

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

Other commentators have said that this verse was revealed regarding the Muslims seeking forgiveness for the polytheists and expressing affection for them before the explicit prohibition in the preceding verses. Since this matter had caused concern for a group of Muslims, the above verse was revealed to reassure them that their seeking of forgiveness prior to the Divine prohibition would not lead to reprimand or punishment.

Commentary

Punishment After Clarification

The first verse above refers to a general and universal law, which reason also confirms: that as long as Allah has not stated a command and no explanation has arrived in the Sharia regarding it, He will not punish anyone for it. In other words, obligation and responsibility always follow the clarification of rulings. This is the same principle referred to in the science of Usul (Principles of Jurisprudence) as the rule of "The Repugnance of Punishment Without Clarification" (Qubh al-Iqab bila Bayan).

Therefore, it begins by saying: "It is not for Allah to lead a people astray after He has guided them until He makes clear to them what they should avoid" (وَمَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْمًا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَاهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يُبَيِّنَ لَهُم مَّا يَتَّقُونَ).

The meaning of "yudilla" (to lead astray), which originally means misguiding, is either a "judgment of misguidance," as some commentators have suggested (similar to ta'dil and tafsiq, which mean judging someone to be just or sinful) [1].

[1]: Some imagine that only the Taf'il form sometimes carries the meaning of "judging," whereas this meaning is also seen in the If'al form, such as the famous poem narrated from Kumayt regarding his love for the family of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "And a group has akfaruni (judged me a disbeliever) because of my love for you."

Or it means leading them away from the path of reward and recompense on the Day of Resurrection, which in reality would mean punishing them.

Or the meaning of "idlal" is what we have encountered previously: the withdrawal of the grace of success (tawfiq) and leaving man to himself, the result of which is becoming lost and wandering aimlessly on the path of guidance. This expression is a subtle reference to the fact that sins are always the source of further misguidance and distancing from the path of guidance [1]. At the end of the verse, it says: "Indeed, Allah is Knowing of all things" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ).

This means that the knowledge and wisdom of Allah necessitate that until He has clarified something for His servants, He does not hold anyone responsible for it nor does He reprimand them.

Answer to a Question

Some commentators and traditionists have imagined that the above verse is evidence that "rational independent truths" (mustaqillat al-aqliyyah)—those things which man perceives through his own reason without a religious decree, such as the ugliness of injustice and the goodness of justice, or the evil of lying, theft, aggression, and murder—do not carry responsibility until they are clarified through the Sharia.

In other words, all dictates of reason must be confirmed by a religious decree to create obligation and responsibility for people; thus, before the arrival of the Sharia, people have no responsibility even toward rational independent truths.

However, the fallacy of this notion is clear. The phrase "until He makes clear to them" (حَتَّىٰ يُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ) provides the answer and clarifies that this verse—

[1]: For more explanation regarding the meaning of guidance (hidayah) and misguidance (dalalah) in the Quran, please study Volume 1, pages 101 to 103.

—and those like it are specific to matters that remain shrouded in ambiguity and require clarification and enlightenment. It certainly does not include rational independent truths, for the ugliness of injustice and the goodness of justice are not ambiguous subjects that require clarification.

Those who say such things do not realize that if this statement were correct, there would be no necessity for people to respond to the call of the Prophets or to investigate the truthfulness of a claimant to prophethood and his miracles. This is because the truthfulness of the Prophet and the Divine command would not yet be clear to them; therefore, there would be no necessity to examine their claim.

Thus, just as the necessity of investigating the call of claimants to prophethood is by the decree of reason and the command of intellect—being among the rational independent truths—other matters that the intellect and reason clearly perceive are also mandatory to follow.

Evidence for this is found in expressions seen in some hadiths narrated from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. In the book Al-Tawhid, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

That in the interpretation of this verse he said: "Until He makes known to them what pleases Him and what angers Him." This means Allah does not punish anyone until the time He makes them understand and introduces to them what things cause His pleasure and what things cause His wrath and anger [1]. In any case, this verse and its likes are considered the foundation for a general principle of Usul: as long as we do not have evidence for the obligation or prohibition of something, we have no responsibility toward it. In other words, everything is permissible for us unless evidence is established for its obligation or prohibition, and this is what is called the "Principle of Innocence" (Asl al-Bara'ah).

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 2, page 276.

In the next verse, it emphasizes and stresses the point that: "Indeed, to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ).

And the system of life and death is also in the hand of His power; He is the one who "gives life and causes death" (يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ).

And therefore: "And you have not besides Allah any protector or any helper" (وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ).

This is an indication that, considering all powers and all governments in the universe are in His hand and by His command, you must not rely on anyone other than Him. You should not take those alienated from Allah as your refuge or the object of your affection, nor should you establish or strengthen the bond of your love with these enemies of Allah through seeking forgiveness or otherwise.

Ayahs

لَّقَد تَّابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ وَٱلْأَنصَارِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ فِى سَاعَةِ ٱلْعُسْرَةِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا كَادَ يَزِيغُ قُلُوبُ فَرِيقٍ مِّنْهُمْ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِهِمْ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١٧)
Certainly Allah turned clemently to the Prophet and the Emigrants and the Helpers, who followed him in the hour of difficulty, after the hearts of a part of them were about to swerve. Then He turned clemently to them—indeed, He is most kind and merciful to them (117)
وَعَلَى ٱلثَّلَـٰثَةِ ٱلَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا۟ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَن لَّا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّآ إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١١٨)
—and to the three who were left behind. When the earth became narrow for them with [all] its expanse, and their own souls weighed heavily on them, and they knew that there was no refuge from Allah except in Him, then He turned clemently toward them so that they might be penitent. Indeed Allah is the All-clement, the All-merciful. (118)

Circumstances of Revelation: A Great Lesson!

Commentators have stated that the first verse was revealed regarding the Battle of Tabuk and the grueling hardships that befell the Muslims during this campaign. These difficulties were so severe that a group intended to turn back, but Divine grace and success encompassed them, and they remained steadfast.

Among those said to be the subject of this verse is "Abu Khaythama," who was a companion of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and not among the hypocrites; however, due to lethargy, he refrained from setting out toward the field of Tabuk alongside the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Ten days passed after this event. The weather was scorching and burning. One day, he came to his wives while they had arranged and prepared shaded shelters for him, provided cool water, and prepared fine food. Suddenly, he fell into deep thought and remembered his leader, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, who has no sin and for whom Allah has guaranteed his past and future, has taken up arms amidst the scorching winds of the desert and endured the suffering of this difficult journey, while Abu Khaythama sits in cool shade beside prepared food and beautiful women; this is not fair."

He then turned to his wives and said: "By Allah, I will not speak a single word to any of you, nor will I stay under this shade, until I join the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله." Having said this, he took up provisions, mounted his camel, and set out. No matter how much his wives tried to speak to him, he did not utter a word and continued his journey until he neared Tabuk.

The Muslims were saying to one another: "There is a rider passing by the side of the road." But the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "O rider! It would be better if you were Abu Khaythama."

When he drew near and the people recognized him, they said: "Yes, it is Abu Khaythama." He made his camel kneel, greeted the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and recounted his story. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله welcomed him and prayed for him.

  • In this way, he was among those whose hearts had inclined toward falsehood, but due to his spiritual readiness, Allah directed him toward the truth and made him steadfast.

Regarding the second verse, another circumstance of revelation has been narrated, the summary of which is as follows: Three Muslims named "Ka'b ibn Malik," "Murarah ibn al-Rabi'," and "Hilal ibn Umayyah" refrained from participating in the Battle of Tabuk and marching with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. However, this was not because they were part of the faction of hypocrites, but rather due to lethargy and laziness. It was not long before they felt regret.

When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned to Medina from the scene of Tabuk, they came to him and apologized, but the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not speak even a single sentence to them and commanded the Muslims that no one should speak to them.

They found themselves in a strange social boycott, to the extent that even their children and wives came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked for permission to separate from them. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not grant permission for separation but ordered that they should not go near them.

The atmosphere of Medina, despite all its vastness, became so constricted for them that they were forced to leave the city to escape this great humiliation and disgrace, seeking refuge on the peaks of the mountains surrounding Medina. Among the matters that dealt a severe blow to their morale was what Ka'b ibn Malik recounts: "One day I was sitting in the market of Medina in distress when I saw a Syrian Christian asking for me. When he recognized me, he handed me a letter from the King of Ghassan, in which it was written: 'If your master has driven you away, come to us.' My state was overturned, and I said: 'Woe to me! My situation has reached the point where enemies covet me!'"

In short, their relatives would bring food but would not speak a single word to them.

A period passed in this manner, and they constantly waited for their repentance to be accepted and for a verse to be revealed as proof of the acceptance of their repentance, but there was no news.

At this time, an idea occurred to one of them, and he said to the others: "Now that the people have cut ties with us, it is better that we also cut ties with one another (it is true that we are sinners, but we should not be pleased with another sinner)."

They did so, such that they did not speak even a single word to each other, and no two of them stayed together. Thus, finally, after fifty days of repentance and supplication before Allah, their repentance was accepted, and the above verse was revealed in this regard. [1]

Commentary

The Prison of Social Boycott for Sinners

These verses also continue to speak of the Battle of Tabuk and the various matters that occurred around this great Islamic event.

In the first verse, it refers to the inclusion of the Lord's infinite mercy toward the Prophet, the Muhajirun, and the Ansar during those sensitive moments, saying: "Allah's mercy encompassed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muhajirun and the Ansar—those who followed him in the hour of difficulty and crisis" (لَقَدْ تابَ اللَّهُ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ وَ الْمُهاجِرِينَ وَ الْأَنْصارِ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ فِي ساعَةِ الْعُسْرَةِ).

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Safinat al-Bihar, and Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi.

Then it adds: "This inclusion of Divine mercy was at a time when, due to the intensity of events and the pressure of hardships, a group of Muslims were close to turning back from the path of truth" (and deciding to return from Tabuk) (مِنْ بَعْدِ ما كادَ يَزِيغُ قُلُوبُ فَرِيقٍ مِنْهُمْ).

Once again, it emphasizes that "after this event, Allah encompassed them with His mercy and accepted their repentance, for He is Kind and Merciful toward the believers" (ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّهُ بِهِمْ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Not only did He grant His mercy to this great group that participated in the Jihad, but He also included those three individuals who had lagged behind from participating in the Jihad and whom the warriors had left behind (وَ عَلَى الثَّلاثَةِ الَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا).

However, this Divine grace did not encompass them easily; rather, it was when these three (Ka'b ibn Malik, Murarah ibn al-Rabi', and Hilal ibn Umayyah, whose story was mentioned in the circumstances of revelation) were placed under a severe social boycott, and all the people cut ties with them, "to the extent that the earth, with all its vastness, became narrow for them" (حَتَّى إِذا ضاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَرْضُ بِما رَحُبَتْ).

And their chests became so filled with grief that it was as if "they found no place within their own souls for themselves," to the point that they even cut ties with one another (وَ ضاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ).

In this way, all paths were closed to them, "and they became certain that there is no refuge from Allah's wrath except by returning to Him" (وَ ظَنُّوا أَنْ لا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ اللَّهِ إِلَّا إِلَيْهِ).

"Once again, Allah's mercy came to them and made true and sincere repentance and return easy for them, so that they might repent" (ثُمَّ تابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا).

For "Allah is the Acceptor of Repentance, the Merciful" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ).

Here, several points must be noted

1- What is meant by Allah's "Tawbah" (Repentance) upon the Prophet (ص)?

In the first verse under discussion, we read that Allah "turned in repentance" (taba) toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muhajirun and the Ansar and accepted their repentance.

Undoubtedly, the infallible Prophet had no sin from which he would need to repent and for Allah to accept his repentance (although some Sunni commentators have taken the above expression as evidence of a slip occurring from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the event of Tabuk).

However, careful reflection on the verse itself and other verses of the Quran testifies to the incorrectness of this interpretation. Firstly, the Lord's "Tawbah" means His return to mercy and attention toward His servants, and it does not imply sin or a slip in its concept. As in Surah An-Nisa, after mentioning a part of Islamic rulings, He says: يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ وَ يَهْدِيَكُمْ سُنَنَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (Allah intends to make clear to you [His rulings] and guide you to the worthy practices of those before you and to turn to you in mercy; and Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise).

In this verse and those before it, there is no mention of sin or a slip; rather, according to the explicit text of this verse, it speaks of clarifying rulings and guidance to the valuable traditions of the past, and this itself shows that "Tawbah" here means the inclusion of Divine mercy toward the servants.

Secondly, in lexicons as well, one of the meanings of "Tawbah" is mentioned as this very subject. In the famous book "Al-Qamus," one of the meanings of "Tawbah" is stated as: "He returned to him with His grace and acceptance."

Thirdly, in the verse under discussion, it attributes the deviation from the truth only to a group of believers, while it considers Divine "Tawbah" to include everyone. This itself shows that Allah's "Tawbah" here does not mean accepting the servants' excuse for a sin, but rather it is that specific Divine mercy that came to the aid of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and all believers without exception—from the Muhajirun and Ansar—during those difficult moments, making them steadfast in the matter of Jihad.

2- Why is the Battle of Tabuk referred to as the "Hour of Difficulty" (Sa'at al-'Usrah)?!

"Sa'ah" (Hour) linguistically means a portion of time, whether short or long; of course, very long periods are not called "Sa'ah." And "'Usrah" means hardship and difficulty.

The history of Islam shows that the Muslims were never under as much pressure and hardship as they were during the event of Tabuk.

For on one hand, the march toward Tabuk was during the peak of the summer heat.

On the other hand, drought had exhausted the people.

And on a third hand, it was the season when the people had to harvest whatever little produce was on the trees and prepare it for their year-long sustenance.

Beyond all this, the distance between Medina and Tabuk was very long.

And the enemy they were to face was the Eastern Roman Empire, one of the most powerful global powers of the day.

In addition to these, mounts and provisions among the Muslims were so scarce that sometimes ten people were forced to use one mount in turns. Some of the infantry did not even have shoes on their feet and were forced to cross the scorching sands of the desert barefoot. Regarding food and water, they were in such straits that sometimes several people would take turns putting a single date in their mouths and sucking it until only the pit remained, and several people would share a single gulp of water.

Yet, despite all these conditions, the Muslims generally possessed a strong and firm spirit and, despite all these problems, marched toward the enemy with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. With this extraordinary perseverance and steadfastness, they left behind a great lesson for all Muslims of the world across all centuries and ages—a lesson sufficient for all generations and a means of victory and triumph over large, equipped, and dangerous enemies.

There is no doubt that among the Muslims were individuals with weaker spirits, and it was they who harbored thoughts of returning, which the Quran describes as: مِنْ بَعْدِ ما كادَ يَزِيغُ قُلُوبُ فَرِيقٍ مِنْهُمْ (because "yazigh" is from the root "zaygh," meaning inclination and deviation from truth toward falsehood).

But as we saw, the high morale of the majority and the grace of the Lord also dissuaded them from this thought, and they joined the ranks of the mujahideen in the path of truth.

3- In the above verses, regarding those three lethargic and negligent Muslims, the expression "khullifu" is used, meaning "they were left behind." This expression is either because the Muslims, when such people showed lethargy, left them behind and proceeded toward the field of Jihad indifferent to their situation, or because when they came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to apologize, he did not accept their excuse and postponed the acceptance of their repentance.

4- A Great Lesson for All Time

Among the important matters derived from the above verses is the issue of punishing criminals and the corrupt through social boycott and the cutting of ties and bonds. We clearly see that this cutting of ties regarding the three who lagged behind in Tabuk put them under such pressure that it was harder for them than any prison, to the point that their lives reached their limits from the pressure of this social boycott, and they lost hope in everything.

This matter left such a wide reflection in the Muslim society of that day that afterward, few would dare to commit such sins.

This type of punishment has neither the headache and cost of prisons nor their quality of fostering laziness and bad habits, yet its impact is greater and more painful than any prison.

This is, in fact, a type of strike and negative struggle by society against corrupt individuals. If Muslims were to engage in such a struggle against "those who fail in sensitive social duties," victory would certainly be theirs in every age and time, and they could easily purge their society.

However, the spirit of compromise and sycophancy that has unfortunately become an almost epidemic disease in many Islamic societies today not only fails to stop such individuals but also encourages them in their ugly deeds.

5- The Battle of Tabuk and Its Achievements

"Tabuk" was the farthest point the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله stepped into during his battles. This word was originally the name of a firm and high fortress located on the border strip of Hijaz and Syria, and for this reason, that land was named the land of Tabuk.

The rapid spread of Islam in the Arabian Peninsula caused the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله fame to echo in all surrounding countries. Although they had not granted importance to Hijaz until then, the dawn of Islam and the power of the Prophet's army, which had mobilized Hijaz under one banner, made them fearful of their future.

Eastern Rome, which bordered Hijaz, thought it might be one of the first victims of the rapid progress of Islam; therefore, it gathered an army of about forty thousand men with sufficient and equipped weaponry, as befitted a powerful state like the Roman Empire at that time, and concentrated them on the border of Hijaz. This news reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله via travelers, and the Prophet, to give a lesson to Rome and other neighbors, immediately issued a call for mobilization. The Prophet's spokesmen in Medina and other places brought the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله voice to the people's ears, and it was not long before thirty thousand people were ready to fight the Romans, among whom were ten thousand cavalry and twenty thousand infantry.

The weather had become extremely hot, warehouses were empty of food supplies, and that year's agricultural products had not yet been harvested. Moving in such conditions was very difficult for the Muslims, but it was the command of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and in any case, they had to move and traverse the long and perilous desert between Medina and Tabuk!

This army, which became known as "Jaysh al-'Usrah" (the Army of Hardship) due to its many economic problems, the long route, the scorching poisonous winds, the deadly sandstorms, and the lack of sufficient mounts, endured all hardships and reached the land of Tabuk at the beginning of the month of Sha'ban in the ninth year of the Hijra. Meanwhile, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had left Ali عليه السلام in his place in Medina, and this was the only battle in which Ali عليه السلام did not participate.

This action by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was a very appropriate and necessary measure, as it was highly probable that some remnants of the polytheists or the hypocrites of Medina, who had refrained from participating in the field of Tabuk under various pretexts, would take advantage of the long absence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his soldiers to attack Medina, kill women and children, and destroy the city. However, the presence of Ali عليه السلام in Medina was a powerful barrier against their conspiracies.

In any case, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله reached Tabuk, he saw no sign of the Roman troops. It seems that when they learned of the movement of the great army of Islam with that extraordinary bravery and courage they had shown in battles—which had more or less reached the ears of the Romans—they saw it fit to recall their army into the country and pretend that the news of the Roman army's concentration on the borders with the intent to attack Medina was nothing more than a baseless rumor, for they were terrified of engaging in such a dangerous war that also lacked a pretext or justification.

However, the presence of the army of Islam with such speed in the field of Tabuk gave several lessons to the enemies of Islam:

Firstly—It was proven that the fighting spirit of the soldiers of Islam is so strong that they have no fear of clashing with the most powerful army of that time.

Secondly—Many tribes and emirs around Tabuk came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and signed non-aggression pacts with him, and the Muslims' minds became at ease regarding them.

Thirdly—The waves of Islam penetrated within the borders of the Roman Empire, and as an important event of the day, this voice echoed everywhere, preparing the ground for the Romans' attention toward Islam.

Fourthly—By traversing this path and enduring those hardships, the Muslims paved the way for the future conquest of Syria, and it became clear that this path was ultimately traversable.

And these great benefits were something worth the trouble of the military expedition.

In any case, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله consulted with his troops according to his custom as to whether they should continue the advance or return. The opinion of most of them was that returning was better and more consistent with the spirit of Islamic programs, especially since the soldiers of Islam had become exhausted and worn out due to the grueling hardship of the journey, and their physical resistance had weakened.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله approved this view, and the army of Islam returned to Medina.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَكُونُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (١١٩)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and be with the Truthful. (119)

Commentary

Be with the Truthful

In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned a group of those who lagged behind—defaulters who had broken their covenant with Allah and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and had practically belied their own claims regarding faith in Allah and the Day of Judgment. We saw how the Muslims punished them by severing ties with them! However, the verse under discussion points to their opposite and commands that:

They must strengthen their relationship with the truthful and those who stand by their covenant.

First, He says: "O you who have believed, fear Allah" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ).

And so that they may traverse the winding path of piety (Taqwa) without error or deviation, He adds: "and be with the truthful" (وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ).

Regarding who is meant by "the truthful" (al-Sadiqin), commentators have offered various possibilities.

However, if we wish to take the shortest path, we must refer to the Quran itself, which has interpreted "the truthful" in several verses.

In Surah Al-Baqarah, we read: "Righteousness is not that you turn your faces toward the east or the west, but [true] righteousness is [in] one who believes in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets and gives wealth, despite love for it, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveler, those who ask [for help], and for freeing slaves; [and who] establishes prayer and gives zakah; [those who] fulfill their promise when they promise; and [those who] are patient in poverty and hardship and during battle. Those are the ones who have been true (sadaqu), and it is those who are the righteous." (Al-Baqarah: 177).

In this verse, we see that after forbidding the Muslims from excessive talk regarding the change of the Qiblah, He interprets the reality of righteousness for them as: faith in Allah, the Resurrection, the angels, the Heavenly Books, and the Prophets; then spending in the way of Allah for the needy and deprived, establishing prayer, paying Zakat, fulfilling covenants, and steadfastness against hardships during Jihad. After mentioning all of these, He says: those who possess these qualities are the truthful and the pious.

Thus, a "truthful" person is one who possesses faith in all sacred matters and follows

that with action in all fields.

And in verse 15 of Surah Al-Hujurat, we read: "The believers are only those who have believed in Allah and His Messenger and then doubt not but strive with their properties and their lives in the cause of Allah. It is those who are the truthful."

This verse also introduces "truthfulness" (Sidq) as a collection of faith and action in which there is no room for doubt or deviation.

And in verse 8 of Surah Al-Hashr, we read: "[It is] for the poor emigrants who were expelled from their homes and their properties, seeking bounty from Allah and [His] approval and supporting Allah and His Messenger. It is those who are the truthful."

In this verse, the deprived believers who, despite all hardships, showed steadfastness and were driven from their homes and properties, having no goal but the pleasure of Allah and aiding the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), are introduced as the truthful.

From the sum of these verses, we conclude that "the truthful" are those who fulfill their obligations regarding faith in the Lord perfectly; they neither allow doubt to enter their hearts, nor do they retreat, nor do they fear a multitude of hardships; rather, through various sacrifices, they prove the truthfulness of their faith.

Undoubtedly, these qualities have levels; some may stand at the peak, whom we call the Infallibles (Ma'sumin), and some are at lower stages.

Does "the Truthful" Refer Only to the Infallibles?

Although the concept of "the truthful" is broad, as mentioned above, it is understood from many narrations that the intended meaning here refers specifically to the Infallibles.

Sulaym ibn Qays al-Hilali narrates that one day, the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), was speaking with a group of Muslims and said: "I adjure you by Allah, do you know that when Allah revealed 'O you who have believed, fear Allah and be with the truthful,' Salman asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, is this general or specific?'

The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) replied: 'Those commanded by this order are all believers, but the title "the truthful" is specific to my brother Ali (peace be upon him) and the successors after him until the Day of Resurrection.'"

When Ali (peace be upon him) asked this question, those present said: "Yes, we heard this from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)." [^1] Nafi' narrates from Abdullah ibn Umar regarding the interpretation of the verse that Allah first commanded the Muslims to fear Him, then said: "be with the truthful," meaning with Muhammad and his Household (peace be upon him and his family). [^2] Although some Sunni commentators, such as the author of Al-Manar, have narrated the end of the above report as "with Muhammad and his companions."

However, considering that the concept of the verse is general and encompasses all times, and we know that the companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) existed for a limited time, the phrasing narrated in Shia books from Abdullah ibn Umar seems more accurate.

The author of Tafsir al-Burhan has narrated a similar theme through Sunni channels, stating that Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad, with his chain of narrators from Ibn Abbas, narrated regarding the above verse:

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 170.

"It refers to Ali ibn Abi Talib."

He then says: "Abd al-Razzaq also mentioned this in the book Rumuz al-Kunuz." [^1] But the more important point is that in the above verse, two commands are given: first, the command to piety (Taqwa), and second, the command to be with the truthful. If the concept of "the truthful" in the verse were general and included all true and steadfast believers, it should have said "and be of the truthful" (wa kunu min al-sadiqin), not "be with the truthful" (wa kunu ma'a al-sadiqin) (Note this carefully).

This itself is a clear indication that "the truthful" in the verse refers to a specific group.

On the other hand, "being with" does not mean merely sitting with them; rather, it undoubtedly means following their path and being in step with them.

If someone is not infallible, can an unconditional command be issued to follow and be in step with them? Is this not itself evidence that this group consists only of the Infallibles?! Therefore, what we have derived from the narrations can also be derived from the verse itself through careful reflection.

It is noteworthy that the famous commentator Fakhr al-Razi, who is known for his bias and skepticism, has accepted this reality (even though most Sunni commentators have passed over this issue in silence!) and says: "Allah commanded the believers to be with the truthful; therefore, the verse indicates that those who are liable to error must follow someone who is infallible, so that under his light they may be protected from error. This meaning will exist in every age, and we have no reason to restrict it

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 170.

to the era of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)."

But he later adds: "We accept that this is the meaning of the verse and that there must be an infallible in every age, but we consider this 'infallible' to be the entire Ummah (community) collectively, not an individual! In other words, this verse is evidence for the authority of the consensus (Ijma') of the believers and the non-erroneous nature of the Ummah as a whole." [^1]

In this way, Fakhr al-Razi has traversed half the path well, but in the second half, he fell into error. If he had paid attention to one point within the text of the verse, he would have traversed the second half correctly as well: if the "truthful" meant the entire Ummah, then the "follower" himself is part of that whole, and in reality, the follower becomes part of the leader, leading to the union of the follower and the followed. However, the apparent meaning of the verse is that the followers are distinct from the leaders, and the subordinates from the superiors (Note this carefully).

The conclusion is that the above verse is among those that indicate the existence of an Infallible in every age and time.

The only remaining question is that "the truthful" (al-Sadiqin) is plural, and there should be multiple Infallibles in every age. The answer to this question is also clear: the addressees are not only the people of one age; rather, the verse addresses all ages and centuries. It is certain that the sum of the addressees of all ages will be with a group of "truthful ones." In other words, since there is an Infallible in every age, when we consider all centuries and ages together, the discussion will be about a group of Infallibles, not just one individual.

A clear witness to this subject is that in the era of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), there was no one

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 16, pp. 220-221.

whose obedience was obligatory except him, yet the verse certainly includes the believers of his time. Therefore, we understand that the plural does not refer to a group at one single time, but rather a group across the span of all times.

Ayahs

مَا كَانَ لِأَهْلِ ٱلْمَدِينَةِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَعْرَابِ أَن يَتَخَلَّفُوا۟ عَن رَّسُولِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَرْغَبُوا۟ بِأَنفُسِهِمْ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ لَا يُصِيبُهُمْ ظَمَأٌ وَلَا نَصَبٌ وَلَا مَخْمَصَةٌ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَطَـُٔونَ مَوْطِئًا يَغِيظُ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَلَا يَنَالُونَ مِنْ عَدُوٍّ نَّيْلًا إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُم بِهِۦ عَمَلٌ صَـٰلِحٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٢٠)
It is not fitting for the people of Madinah and the Bedouins around them to hang back behind the Apostle of Allah and prefer their own lives to his life. That is because they neither experience any thirst, nor fatigue, nor hunger, in the way of Allah, nor do they tread any ground enraging the faithless, nor do they gain any ground against an enemy but a righteous deed is written for them on its account. Indeed Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous. (120)
وَلَا يُنفِقُونَ نَفَقَةً صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً وَلَا يَقْطَعُونَ وَادِيًا إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُمْ لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٢١)
And neither do they incur any expense, big or small, nor do they cross any valley, but it is written to their account, so that Allah may reward them by the best of what they used to do. (121)

Commentary

The Hardships of the Mujahideen Do Not Go Unrewarded

In the preceding verses, discussions were held regarding the reprimand of those who refrained from the Battle of Tabuk. These two verses under discussion provide the final discourse on this subject as a general and universal law.

First, it states: "It is not for the people of Medina and the bedouins who live around them—which is the center and heart of Islam—to remain behind the Messenger of Allah" (ما كانَ لِأَهْلِ الْمَدِينَةِ وَ مَنْ حَوْلَهُمْ مِنَ الْأعرابِ أَنْ يَتَخَلَّفُوا عَنْ رَسُولِ اللَّهِ‌).

"Nor should they prefer their own lives to his life" (وَ لا يَرْغَبُوا بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ‌).

This is because he is the leader of the Ummah, the Prophet of Allah, and the symbol of the survival and life of the Muslim nation. Leaving him alone not only endangers the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله but also places the religion of God, and furthermore, the very existence and lives of the believers themselves, in serious peril.

In fact, by employing an emotional expression, the Quran encourages all people of faith to accompany the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and to support and defend him against hardships. It says:

Your lives are not dearer than his life, and your existence is not more valuable than his. Does your faith allow him—who is the most precious of human beings and was sent for your salvation and guidance—to fall into danger, while you, seeking safety, withhold sacrifice in his path to preserve your own lives?! It is certain that the emphasis on Medina and its surroundings is because, at that time, Medina was the center of Islam; otherwise, this ruling is neither exclusive to Medina and its environs nor limited to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

It is the duty of all Muslims across all centuries and eras to cherish their leaders as they do their own lives—indeed, even more—and to strive for their protection and not leave them alone in the face of harsh events, for a danger to them is a danger to the Ummah.

Then, it refers to the rewards of the Mujahideen (warriors) which they receive in exchange for every kind of hardship in the path of Jihad, pointing out seven types of these hardships and their rewards, saying:

"That is because no thirst afflicts them" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ لا يُصِيبُهُمْ ظَمَأٌ) "nor any fatigue or exhaustion" (وَ لا نَصَبٌ‌).

"And no hunger overtakes them in the way of Allah" (وَ لا مَخْمَصَةٌ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

"And they do not step on any dangerous ground or a battlefield that causes rage and distress to the disbelievers" (وَ لا يَطَؤُنَ مَوْطِئاً يَغِيظُ الْكُفَّارَ).

"And they do not receive any blow from the enemy" (وَ لا يَنالُونَ مِنْ عَدُوٍّ نَيْلًا) "except that in connection with it, a righteous deed is recorded for them" (إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُمْ بِهِ عَمَلٌ صالِحٌ‌).

And they will certainly receive the reward for each and every one of them from the Great Lord, "for Allah never wastes the reward of the doers of good" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

"Likewise, they do not spend any wealth, small or large, in the path of Jihad" (وَ لا يُنْفِقُونَ نَفَقَةً صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً) "and they do not cross any land to reach the battlefield or upon returning, except that all these steps and those expenditures are recorded for them" (وَ لا يَقْطَعُونَ وادِياً إِلَّا كُتِبَ لَهُمْ‌).

"So that ultimately Allah may reward these actions as the best of what they used to do" (لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Here, several points should be noted

1- Most commentators have interpreted the phrase "لا يَنالُونَ مِنْ عَدُوٍّ نَيْلًا" as mentioned above, stating it means that whatever blow the Mujahideen of Allah's path receive from the enemy—such as wounds, death, captivity, and the like—will be recorded in their book of deeds for divine reward, and they will have a recompense proportionate to each. Given that the verse enumerates the hardships of the Mujahideen, this meaning is appropriate.

However, if we rely on the grammatical structure of this phrase and interpret it according to the literal meaning of the words, the meaning would be that they do not strike any blow upon the enemy except that it will be written in their book of deeds, because the phrase "نال من عدوه" literally means to strike the enemy. Nevertheless, considering the context of the entire verse serves as evidence for the former interpretation.

2- Commentators have mentioned two types of interpretations for the phrase "أَحْسَنَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌". One is based on the word "ahsan" (best) being a description of their actions, and the other as a description of their reward.

In the first interpretation, which we chose above and is more consistent with the appearance of the verse, these actions of the Mujahideen are recognized as the best actions of their lifetime, and Allah gives the reward proportionate to that.

In the second interpretation, which requires assuming the word "min" (than) after the word "ahsan," the divine reward is introduced as better and higher than their actions, and the implied meaning of the sentence is: "That Allah may reward them better than what they used to do" (ليجزيهم اللَّه احسن مما كانوا يعملون).

3- The above verses were not only for the Muslims of yesterday, but for yesterday, today, and all centuries and eras. Undoubtedly, participating in any type of Jihad, whether small or large, involves various hardships and discomforts—physical, spiritual, financial, and the like. But whenever the Mujahideen illuminate their hearts and souls with faith in Allah and His great promises, and know that every breath, every word, and every step they take in this path is not lost, but rather the account of all of them is preserved without decrease or omission and with the utmost precision, and Allah gives the most worthy rewards from His boundless ocean of grace for them as the best of deeds—with this realization, they never refuse to endure these hardships and are not intimidated by their magnitude. They allow no weakness or lethargy to creep in, no matter how long, eventful, or arduous the Jihad may be.

Ayah

۞ وَمَا كَانَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ لِيَنفِرُوا۟ كَآفَّةً ۚ فَلَوْلَا نَفَرَ مِن كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ طَآئِفَةٌ لِّيَتَفَقَّهُوا۟ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَلِيُنذِرُوا۟ قَوْمَهُمْ إِذَا رَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ (١٢٢)
Yet it is not for the faithful to go forth en masse. But why should not there a group from each of their sections go forth to become learned in religion, and to warn their people when they return to them, so that they may beware? (122)

Occasion of Revelation

The late Tabarsi, in Majma' al-Bayan, narrates from Ibn Abbas that whenever the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) set out for the field of Jihad, all Muslims—except for the hypocrites and those with valid excuses—would march in his service. However, after verses were revealed censuring the hypocrites, specifically rebuking those who stayed behind from the Battle of Tabuk, the believers became more determined than ever to participate in the fields of Jihad. Consequently, even in battles where the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) did not personally participate (Sariyyah), everyone would head to the battlefield, leaving the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) alone. The above verse was revealed, declaring that unless necessary, it is not appropriate for all Muslims to go to the battlefield (rather, a group should remain in Medina to learn the knowledge and rulings of Islam from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and teach them to their mujahid companions upon their return).

The same great commentator also narrates another occasion of revelation to this effect:

A group of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) companions went among the desert-dwelling tribes to propagate Islam. The desert dwellers welcomed them and treated them with kindness. However, some criticized them, asking why they had left the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to come to them. They became upset and saddened by this and returned to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). The verse was revealed, approving their missionary program and relieving their anxiety.

A third occasion of revelation is also mentioned in the commentary Al-Tibyan for the above verse: when the desert dwellers became Muslim, they all moved toward Medina to learn the rulings of Islam. This caused an increase in the price of goods and provisions and created other difficulties and hardships for the Muslims of Medina. The verse was revealed, instructing them that it is not necessary for everyone to vacate their towns and lands and come to Medina to understand Islamic knowledge; rather, it is sufficient for a group to do so.

Commentary

Jihad against Ignorance and Jihad against the Enemy

The above verse, which is linked to the previous verses regarding Jihad, points to a reality that is of vital importance to Muslims: although Jihad is extremely significant and defecting from it is a disgrace and a sin, in cases where there is no necessity for all believers to participate in the field of Jihad—especially when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself remains in Medina—"everyone should not go to Jihad; rather, it is necessary for every population of Muslims to be divided into two groups: one group to perform the obligation of Jihad, and another group to stay in Medina and learn the knowledge and rulings of Islam."

(وَمَا كَانَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ لِيَنْفِرُوا كَافَّةً فَلَوْلَا نَفَرَ مِنْ كُلِّ فِرْقَةٍ مِنْهُمْ طَائِفَةٌ لِيَتَفَقَّهُوا فِي الدِّينِ)

"And when their mujahid companions return from the field, they should teach them the divine rulings and commands and warn them against disobeying them." (وَلِيُنْذِرُوا قَوْمَهُمْ إِذَا رَجَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ)

"Perhaps this program will cause them to avoid disobeying Allah's command and fulfill their duties." (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَحْذَرُونَ)

Several points should be noted here

1- What has been said in the commentary of the verse, in addition to being consistent with its well-known occasion of revelation, is more aligned with the apparent phrasing of the verse than any other interpretation. The only thing required is that the phrase "so that a group remains" must be assumed as implied after "a group from every division," meaning a group from every division should go, and another group should stay. Of course, given the existing context in the verse, this will not pose a problem (observe closely).

However, some commentators have suggested that there is no such implication in the verse, and the meaning is that a group of Muslims should go to the field of Jihad as a collective obligation (Wajib Kifa'i), and within the scene of Jihad, they should become acquainted with the teachings of Islam and witness with their own eyes the victory of the Muslims over the enemies—which is an example of the signs of the greatness and truth of this religion—and remind their friends upon their return. [1]

[1]: This interpretation was chosen by Tabari, as narrated by Qurtubi in his commentary, and a group of commentators have also mentioned it as a possibility under the verse.

A third possibility suggested by other commentators is that the verse states an independent ruling separate from the discussions of Jihad: that Muslims have a duty, as a collective obligation, for a number of people from every tribe and population to rise and go to the major Islamic centers to learn Islamic knowledge and teachings, and after completing their studies, return to their towns and lands to teach others. [1] However, as we said, the first interpretation is closer to the meaning of the verse, although intending all these meanings is not very far-fetched. [2]

[1]: This interpretation is consistent with the occasion of revelation mentioned by the late Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan.

[2]: Note that we consider the use of one word for multiple meanings to be permissible.

2- Some have imagined that there is a kind of contradiction between this verse and the previous verses, because in the previous verses, everyone was commanded to participate in the field of Jihad, and those who stayed behind were severely rebuked, whereas in the verse under discussion, it commands that not everyone should move toward the field of Jihad.

But it is clear that these two commands were issued considering different conditions. For example, in the case of the Battle of Tabuk and facing the powerful army of the Roman Empire, there was no choice but for all Muslims to prepare for Jihad. However, in the case of confronting smaller groups, it is not necessary for all Muslims to move, especially in cases where the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself stays in Medina; they should not leave Medina empty, ignore its potential dangers, or neglect learning the knowledge and rulings of Islam.

Therefore, there is no abrogation (Naskh) in the above verses, and the fact that some have imagined so is a mistake.

3- There is no doubt that the meaning of "Tafaqquh" (deep understanding) in religion is learning all Islamic knowledge and rulings, including both principles (Usul) and branches (Furu'), because the concept of Tafaqquh encompasses all these matters. Therefore, the above verse is clear evidence that a group of Muslims must always, as a collective obligation, engage in the pursuit of knowledge regarding all Islamic issues, and after completing their studies, return to various places—especially to their own people and population—to propagate Islamic rulings and familiarize them with Islamic matters.

Thus, the above verse is clear evidence for the obligation of teaching and learning in Islamic matters; in other words, it makes both studying and teaching obligatory. If today's world prides itself on compulsory education, the Quran, fourteen centuries ago, additionally imposed this duty upon teachers.

[The Quran and the Permissibility of Taqlid from a Faqih]

4- A group of Islamic scholars has used the above verse to argue for the permissibility of Taqlid (emulation), because learning Islamic teachings and conveying them to others in matters of the branches of religion, and the necessity for the listeners to follow them, is the very essence of Taqlid.

Of course, as we said, the above verse does not only discuss the branches of religion but also includes fundamental issues; however, in any case, it encompasses the branches of religion as well.

The only objection that might arise here is that in those days, there was no talk of Ijtihad and Taqlid, and those who learned Islamic issues and conveyed them to others were like the "narrators of rulings" (Mas'alah-gu) of our time, not like Mujtahids; meaning they took the ruling from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and narrated it exactly to others without any personal opinion.

However, considering that Ijtihad and Taqlid have a broad concept, the above objection can be answered.

To explain: there is no doubt that the science of Jurisprudence (Fiqh) with the breadth it has today did not exist at that time, and Muslims easily learned issues from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Nevertheless, it was not the case that all the great figures of Islam were merely like the narrators of rulings of our time, because many of them went to other places as judges or governors, and naturally, issues arose that they had not heard specifically from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), but which existed within the generalities and absolute statements of the verses of the Holy Quran. Surely, by applying general principles to specific cases—scientifically termed "returning the branches to the principles" and "applying principles to branches"—they understood their rulings, and this was a type of simple Ijtihad (observe closely).

Certainly, this work and its like existed during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and for this reason, the original root of Ijtihad existed among his companions and followers, even if not all companions were at this level.

And since the above verse has a general concept, it includes both accepting the word of the narrators of rulings and accepting the word of the Mujtahids. In this way, the permissibility of Taqlid can be argued through the generality of the verse.

[The Importance of "Teaching" and "Learning"]

5- Another important matter that can be derived from the verse is the special respect and importance that Islam has accorded to the issue of "teaching" and "learning," to the extent that it requires Muslims not to all participate in the field of war, but for a group to stay and learn Islamic knowledge.

That is, Jihad against ignorance is an obligation upon them just like Jihad against the enemy, and the importance of one is no less than the other; rather, until Muslims are victorious in the matter of Jihad against ignorance, they will not be victorious in Jihad against the enemy, because an ignorant nation is always doomed to defeat.

One contemporary commentator has an interesting point under this verse; he says:

"I was busy studying in Tripoli. One day, the governor there, who himself had considerable information about Islamic knowledge, said to me: 'Why does the government exempt scholars and students of religious sciences from military service, while this sacred service is religiously obligatory for everyone, and students of religious sciences are more worthy than all people to perform this religious obligation? Is this not a mistake?'

Without hesitation, I thought of the above verse and said: 'This practice has a root in the Holy Quran, where it says: a group should perform Jihad and a group should engage in the pursuit of knowledge!' He was very pleased with this answer, especially since it came from a beginner like me who had just started studying at that time." [1]

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْكُفَّارِ وَلْيَجِدُوا۟ فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٢٣)
O you who have faith! Fight the faithless who are in your vicinity, and let them find severity in you, and know that Allah is with the Godwary. (123)

Commentary

Deal with the Nearer Enemies!

In the above verse, in line with the discussions regarding Jihad previously mentioned in this Surah, two other instructions concerning this important Islamic subject are highlighted.

First, addressing the believers, it says: "O you who have believed, fight those at hand of the disbelievers" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا قَاتِلُوا الَّذِينَ يَلُونَكُمْ مِنَ الْكُفَّارِ).

It is true that one must struggle against all enemies, and there is no difference in this regard; however, in terms of tactics and the method of struggle, one must undoubtedly start with the nearer enemies. This is because the danger of nearer enemies is greater, just as when inviting toward Islam and guiding people to the religion of truth, one must start with those who are closer. By the command of Allah, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله began his invitation with his relatives, then preached to the people of Makkah, then sent missionaries throughout the Arabian Peninsula, and subsequently wrote letters to the monarchs of the world. Undoubtedly, this method is closer to victory.

Of course, every law has its exceptions. Extraordinary situations may arise where a distant enemy is significantly more dangerous, and one must hasten to repel them first; however, as we have stated, this is an exception and not a permanent law.

As for why addressing the nearer enemy is more necessary, the reasons are clear:

First: The danger of a near enemy is greater than the danger of distant enemies.

Second: Our awareness and information regarding nearer enemies are more extensive, and this itself aids in achieving victory.

Third: Addressing the distant and abandoning the near carries the risk that near enemies might attack from behind or crush the central hub of Islam when the center is vacated.

Fourth: The means and costs of struggling against the near are less and simpler, and maintaining control over the front is easier. For these and other reasons, repelling such enemies is more essential.

It also seems necessary to mention this point: at the time this verse was revealed, Islam had encompassed almost the entire Arabian Peninsula. Therefore, the nearest enemy at that time was perhaps the Eastern Roman Empire, toward whom the Muslims hastened to Tabuk to confront.

One must also not forget that although the above verse speaks of "armed struggle" and "physical distance," it is not unlikely that the spirit of the verse also governs logical struggles and spiritual distances. This means that when Muslims engage in logical and promotional struggles against enemies, they must first go after those whose danger to the Islamic society is greater and more immediate. For example, in our era, where the danger of atheism and materialism threatens all societies, the struggle against them must take precedence over the struggle against false sects—not that they should be forgotten, but the sharp edge of the attack should be directed toward the more dangerous group. Or, for instance, the struggle against intellectual, political, and economic colonialism must be placed in the first degree of importance.

The second instruction we read in the above verse regarding Jihad is the command for firmness of action. The verse says the enemies must feel a kind of harshness from you (وَ لْيَجِدُوا فِيكُمْ غِلْظَةً).

This indicates that internal courage, bravery, and psychological readiness for steadfastness and a tough struggle against the enemy are not enough; rather, you must demonstrate this readiness and toughness to the enemy so they know such a spirit exists within you. This very realization causes their retreat and the breaking of their morale. In other words, the existence of power is not sufficient; rather, there must be a display of power before the enemy.

Hence, we read in the history of Islam that when the Muslims came to Makkah for the pilgrimage to the House of Allah, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله commanded them to walk swiftly, or rather run, during the circumambulation (Tawaf) to show their strength, speed, and fitness to the enemies who were watching them.

Similarly, in the story of the Conquest of Makkah, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the Muslims to all light fires in the desert at night so that the people of Makkah would become acquainted with the greatness of the army of Islam. Coincidentally, this act had an effect on their morale. He also ordered that Abu Sufyan, the leader of Makkah, be kept in a corner while the powerful army of Islam paraded before him.

At the end of the verse, He gives the Muslims tidings of victory with this phrase: "And know that Allah is with the righteous (those who fear Him)."

In addition to what has been said, this expression may also point to the fact that resorting to violence and firmness of action must be coupled with Taqwa (piety/God-wariness) and must never exceed human boundaries.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُم مَّن يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زَادَتْهُ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنًا ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَزَادَتْهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَهُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (١٢٤)
Whenever a surah is sent down, there are some of them who say, ‘Which of you did it increase in faith?’ As for those who have faith, it increases them in faith, and they rejoice. (124)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ فَزَادَتْهُمْ رِجْسًا إِلَىٰ رِجْسِهِمْ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (١٢٥)
But as for those in whose heart is a sickness, it only adds defilement to their defilement, and they die while they are faithless. (125)

Commentary

The Impact of Quranic Verses on Prepared and Polluted Hearts

In connection with the discussions regarding the hypocrites and the believers, these two verses point to one of the prominent characteristics of these two groups.

First, it says: "Whenever a Surah is revealed, some of the hypocrites say to one another: 'Which of you has had his faith increased by the revelation of this Surah?'" (وَ إِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَقُولُ أَيُّكُمْ زادَتْهُ هذِهِ إِيماناً) [^1]. With these words, they intended to express the lack of impact of the Quranic Surahs and their own indifference toward them, suggesting that these verses contain nothing significant or remarkable, and are merely ordinary and trivial matters!

However, the Quran responds to them with a decisive tone, and while dividing people into two groups, it says: "As for those who believe, the revelation of these verses increases their faith, and the signs of joy and happiness become apparent on their faces" (فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فَزادَتْهُمْ إِيماناً وَ هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ‌).

"But as for those in whose hearts is the disease of hypocrisy, ignorance, enmity, and envy, it adds a new filth to their filth!" (وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَتْهُمْ رِجْساً إِلَى رِجْسِهِمْ‌).

[^1]: The word "ma" (ما) in the phrase "إِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ" is actually "ma za'idah" (extra) used for emphasis. Some have said it is "ma silah" which gives the conditional particle "idha" (اذا) authority over its response (jaza) and emphasizes the sentence.

"And finally, they shall die while they are in a state of disbelief and faithlessness" (وَ ماتُوا وَ هُمْ كافِرُونَ‌).

Key Points

1- In the two verses above, the Quran emphasizes the reality that the mere existence of life-giving programs and teachings is not sufficient for the salvation of an individual or a group; rather, the readiness of the ground must also be considered an essential condition.

The verses of the Quran are like life-giving drops of rain, which we know produce greenery in a garden and thorns in a salt marsh! Those who look upon them with a spirit of submission, faith, and love for reality learn a new lesson from every Surah—indeed, from every verse—which nurtures their faith and strengthens the prominent qualities of humanity within them.

But those who look at these verses through the dark glass of stubbornness, pride, and hypocrisy not only fail to benefit from them, but their disbelief and enmity are intensified.

In other words, they commit a new act of disobedience and rebellion against every new command, a new defiance against every instruction, and a new stubbornness against every truth. This causes an accumulation of sins, disobediences, and stubbornness within their being, and the roots of these ugly traits become so strong in their souls that they eventually die in a state of disbelief, and the path of return is completely closed to them! To put it another way: in any educational program, the "action of the actor" (fa'iliyyat al-fa'il) alone is not enough; rather, the spirit of acceptance and the "receptivity of the recipient" (qabiliyyat al-qabil) is also a fundamental condition.

2- "Rijs" (رجس) linguistically means a filthy and impure being. According to Raghib in the book "Al-Mufradat," this filth is of four types: sometimes in terms of instinct and nature, sometimes in terms of thought and intellect, sometimes in terms of Sharia, and sometimes from all aspects.

Undoubtedly, the filth resulting from hypocrisy, stubbornness, and persistence against the truth is a type of internal and spiritual filth whose effects eventually manifest in the person's entire being, speech, and actions.

[What is Rijs?]

3- The phrase "وَ هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ‌" (and they rejoice), considering the root of the word "bisharat" (glad tidings) which means a joy and happiness whose effects appear on a person's face (basharah), shows that the educational effect of the Quranic verses was so apparent in the believers that its signs immediately manifested on their faces.

4- In the above verses, "hypocrisy" and the ugly traits that accompany it are considered a disease of the heart. As we have said previously, "heart" (qalb) in such contexts means the soul and the intellect, and "heart disease" in these instances refers to moral vices and psychological deviations. This expression shows that if a human enjoys a healthy spirit, none of these ugly traits should take root in his being; such morals, like physical illness, are contrary to human nature. Therefore, being polluted with these traits is evidence of a deviation from the original natural path and a spiritual and psychological illness. [^1]

[Every Verse Gives a New Spirit]

5- The above verses provide a remarkable lesson to all of us Muslims, for they express the reality that the early Muslims found a new spirit and received a new upbringing with the revelation of every Surah of the Quran, such that its effects soon became apparent on their faces. Meanwhile, today we see individuals who are outwardly Muslim, yet not only does

[^1]: Regarding the disease of the heart and its concept in the Quran, we have had another discussion in Volume 1, page 64.

reading one Surah have no effect on them, but not even the slightest effect is seen in them after completing the entire Quran.

Have the Surahs and verses of the Quran lost their effect? Or has the pollution of thoughts, the disease of hearts, and the existence of veils resulting from our evil deeds given our hearts such a state of indifference and impenetrability? We must seek refuge in Allah from this state and ask His Holy Presence to grant us a heart like the hearts of the early Muslims.

Ayahs

أَوَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّهُمْ يُفْتَنُونَ فِى كُلِّ عَامٍ مَّرَّةً أَوْ مَرَّتَيْنِ ثُمَّ لَا يَتُوبُونَ وَلَا هُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٢٦)
Do they not see that they are tried once or twice every year? Yet they neither repent, nor do they take admonition. (126)
وَإِذَا مَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ نَّظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ هَلْ يَرَىٰكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ ثُمَّ ٱنصَرَفُوا۟ ۚ صَرَفَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُم بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (١٢٧)
Whenever a surah is sent down they look at one another: ‘Is anybody observing you?’ Then they slip away. Allah has turned aside their hearts, for they are a people who do not understand. (127)

Commentary

In these verses, the discourse regarding the hypocrites continues, rebuking and admonishing them by saying: "Do they not see that they are tried every year once or twice?" (أَ وَ لا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّهُمْ يُفْتَنُونَ فِي كُلِّ عامٍ مَرَّةً أَوْ مَرَّتَيْنِ‌).

And the wonder is that despite all these successive trials, "they do not turn back from their wrong path, nor do they repent, nor do they take heed" (ثُمَّ لا يَتُوبُونَ وَ لا هُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ‌).

Regarding what is meant by this annual trial that is repeated once or twice, there is discussion among the commentators.

Some consider it to be illnesses. Some consider it hunger and other hardships. Some consider it witnessing the signs of the greatness of Islam and the truthfulness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the fields of Jihad, in which the hypocrites participated due to the compulsion of their environment. And some consider it the lifting of the veil from their secrets.

However, considering that we read at the end of the verse that "they do not take heed," it becomes clear that the trial was of a nature that should have caused the awakening of this group.

Furthermore, it appears from the phrasing of the verse that this trial is distinct from the general trials that all people face in their lives.

With this in mind, it seems that the fourth interpretation—namely, the uncovering of their evil deeds and the manifestation of their inner reality—is closer to the meaning of the verse.

There is also the possibility that "trial and examination" (iftinan and imtihan) in the verse under discussion has a comprehensive meaning that includes all these subjects.

Then, it points to the defiant look they would adopt in the face of Divine verses, saying: "Whenever a Surah [of the Quran] is revealed, they look at one another with contempt and denial, and through the movements of their eyes, they manifest their levels of anxiety" (وَ إِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ نَظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌).

Their distress and anxiety stem from the fear that the revelation of that Surah might bring about a new scandal for them, or it is because, due to their blindness of heart, they understand nothing of it—and man is an enemy to what he does not know.

In any case, they decide to leave the gathering so as not to hear these heavenly melodies, but they fear that someone might see them as they exit. Therefore, they quietly ask one another: Is anyone noticing us? "Does anyone see you?" (هَلْ يَراكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ).

And as soon as they feel certain that the congregation is occupied with the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and is not paying attention to them, "they turn away [and leave the gathering]" (ثُمَّ انْصَرَفُوا).

The phrase "Does anyone see you?" (هَلْ يَراكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ) was either spoken verbally or conveyed through the signaling of their eyes. In the second case, the phrase "they look at one another" (نَظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ‌) expresses the same concept as this phrase; in fact, "Does anyone see you?" serves as an explanation for their looking at one another.

At the end of the verse, it mentions the reason for this behavior, saying: They are distressed by hearing the words of God because "Allah has turned their hearts away (due to their stubbornness, enmity, and sins) from the truth (and they have developed a state of hostility toward the truth), for they are a people who do not understand" (صَرَفَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ‌).

Regarding the phrase "Allah has turned their hearts away" (صَرَفَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ), commentators have offered two possibilities: first, that it is a declarative sentence as we interpreted above; second, that it is an optative sentence meaning a curse—i.e., "May Allah turn their hearts away from the truth." However, the first possibility seems closer to the intended meaning.

Ayahs

لَقَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ مَا عَنِتُّمْ حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُم بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢٨)
There has certainly come to you an apostle from among yourselves. Grievous to him is your distress; he has deep concern for you, and is most kind and merciful to the faithful. (128)
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُلْ حَسْبِىَ ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ ۖ وَهُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١٢٩)
But if they turn their backs [on you], say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me. There is no god except Him. In Him I have put my trust and He is the Lord of the Great Throne.’ (129)

Commentary

The Final Verses of the Holy Quran

The above verses, which according to some commentators are the last verses revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and with which Surah al-Bara'ah (Tawbah) concludes, are in fact an allusion to all the issues discussed throughout this Surah.

For on one hand, they remind all people—including believers, disbelievers, and hypocrites—that the strictness of the Prophet and the Quran, and the outward severities of which examples were mentioned in this Surah, are all due to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) love and passion for their guidance, education, and perfection.

On the other hand, they inform the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) not to be distressed or worried by the rebelliousness and disobedience of the people—many examples of which also passed in this Surah—and to know that in every circumstance, Allah is his Supporter, Ally, and Helper.

Therefore, in the first verse, addressing the people, He says: "A Messenger has come to you from among yourselves!" (لَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ).

Specifically, the use of "min anfusikum" (from your own souls/selves) instead of "minkum" (from among you) in this verse is an allusion to the intensity of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) connection with the people; it is as if a part of the people's soul and the spirit of society has manifested in the form of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

For this reason, He knows all their pains, is aware of their problems, and shares in their discomforts, griefs, and sorrows. Under such conditions, it is inconceivable that he would speak anything except for their benefit or take any step except in their path. This is, in fact, the first description mentioned for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the above verse.

It is strange that a group of commentators, influenced by racial and Arab prejudices, have said that the addressee in this verse is the Arab race! Meaning: a Prophet from this race has come to you!

In our view, this is the worst interpretation mentioned for the above verse, because we know that something not spoken of in the Quran is the issue of "race." Everywhere, the Quranic addresses begin with "O mankind" or "O you who believe" and the like, and in no instance does "O Arabs" or "O Quraysh" or similar terms exist.

Furthermore, the end of the verse which says: "kind and merciful to the believers" (بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ) clearly negates this interpretation, for it speaks of all believers, from whatever nation, people, or race they may be.

It is a matter of regret that some prejudiced scholars have brought the Quran down from its universal and human height and wish to confine it within small racial boundaries.

In any case, after mentioning this attribute (مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ), He points to four other distinct attributes of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) which have a profound effect on stirring people's emotions and attracting their feelings.

First, He says: "Any distress, harm, or loss that reaches you is grievous to him" (عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ ما عَنِتُّمْ).

That is, not only is he not pleased by your discomfort, but he will not be indifferent either; he suffers intensely from your sufferings. If he insists on your guidance and engages in arduous, exhausting wars, that too is for your salvation, for your liberation from the clutches of oppression, sin, and misery.

Another is that "he is deeply concerned for your guidance" and loves it (حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُمْ).

"Hirs" (greed/ardent desire) linguistically means intense attachment to something. Interestingly, in the verse under discussion, it speaks in absolute terms: "he is ardent for you," mentioning neither guidance nor anything else. This indicates that he loves every kind of good and prosperity for you, and every kind of progress, advancement, and happiness (and as the rule goes, the omission of the object indicates generality).

Therefore, if he dispatches you to the bitter fields of Jihad, and if he places the hypocrites under severe pressure, all of this is for the sake of love for freedom, honor, dignity, your guidance, and the purification of your society.

Then He points to the third and fourth attributes, saying: "He is kind and merciful toward the believers" (بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Thus, whatever difficult and exhausting command he gives (even traversing long and scorching deserts in the summer season, with hunger and thirst, to confront a powerful enemy in the Battle of Tabuk), that too is a form of affection and grace from his side.

Regarding the difference between "Ra'uf" (Kind) and "Rahim" (Merciful), there is discussion among commentators. However, it seems the best interpretation is that "Ra'uf" refers to specific affection and grace regarding the obedient, while "Rahim" refers to mercy toward sinners.

But it must not be forgotten that when these two words are used separately, they may be used with the same meaning, but when mentioned together, they occasionally convey two different meanings.
