In the next verse, which is the final verse of the Surah, He comforts the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) so that he does not become discouraged or worried by the rebelliousness and disobedience of the people. He says: "If they turn away from the truth, do not be worried and say: Allah is sufficient for me," for He is powerful over all things (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُلْ حَسْبِيَ اللَّهُ). "The same Allah besides whom there is no deity," and therefore He is his only refuge (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

Yes, "I have relied only upon such a Deity, and I am attached to Him, and I have entrusted my affairs to Him" (عليه توكلت).

"And He is the Lord of the Great Throne" (وَ هُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ).

When the Throne, the higher realm, and the metaphysical world with all the grandeur they possess are in the grasp of His power and under His protection and guardianship, how could He leave me alone and not help me against the enemy? Is there any power that can resist His power? Or can any mercy and kindness be imagined higher than His mercy and kindness?

O Allah, now as we conclude this Surah and write these lines, our enemies have surrounded us, and our brave and heroic nation has risen to uproot oppression, corruption, and tyranny. A unique solidarity and unity, which was never imagined, has emerged among all ranks and classes without exception; even children and youngsters are partners in this struggle, and no one withholds any kind of sacrifice.

O Lord, You know and see all of this, and You are the center of love and affection, and You have promised victory to the mujahideen. Bring Your victory and help near, and grant us the final victory, and quench the thirst of these seekers with the pure water of faith, justice, and freedom. Indeed, You are powerful over all things. [^1] "End of the Commentary of Surah al-Tawbah"

[^1]: This section was written before the Revolution and the establishment of the Islamic government, in the heat of the struggles against the Shah.

Surah Yunus (عليه السلام) This Surah was revealed in Makkah and has 109 verses.

In the Name of Allah, the Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful

Content and Virtue of this Surah

This Surah, which is among the Meccan Surahs and according to some commentators was revealed after Surah al-Isra and before Surah Hud, emphasizes several fundamental and foundational issues, the most important of which are the issues of "Origin" (the Beginning) and "Resurrection" (the Return), just like many Meccan Surahs.

However, it first speaks of the issue of revelation and the status of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), then addresses signs of the greatness of creation which are signs of the greatness of Allah. Afterward, it directs the people toward the instability of the material life of this world and the necessity of paying attention to the abode of the Hereafter and preparing for it through faith and righteous deeds.

In accordance with these issues, it recounts various parts of the lives of great Prophets, including Nuh, Musa, and Yunus (عليه السلام), and for this reason, it has been named Surah Yunus.

Furthermore, to reinforce the above discussions, it mentions the stubbornness and persistence of the idolaters and depicts the presence and witnessing of Allah everywhere for them. Specifically, to prove this issue, it seeks help from the depths of their innate nature (fitrah) which manifests during difficulties when they remember the One and Only God.

Finally, to complete the above discussions, it utilizes glad tidings and warnings in every appropriate instance—glad tidings of endless Divine blessings for the righteous, and warnings and frightening of the tyrants and the rebellious.

Thus, in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that he said:

"Whoever recites Surah Yunus every two or three months, it is not feared that he will be among the ignorant, and on the Day of Resurrection, he will be among the near-stationed (al-Muqarrabin)." [^1]

This is because the warning and awakening verses in this Surah are numerous, and if read with care and reflection, they remove the darkness of ignorance from the human soul, and their effect will remain in his being for at least a few months. Whenever, in addition to understanding the content of the Surah, he also acts upon it, he will certainly be placed among the near-stationed on the Day of Resurrection.

Perhaps it does not need reminding that the virtues of the Surahs, as we have said previously, are not attained merely by reciting the verses without understanding the meaning and without acting upon their content; for recitation is the prelude to understanding, and understanding is the prelude to action!

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 2, Page 290, and other commentaries.
